topics.title: Safety Hazards and Risks, Safety Instrumented System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Safety Hazards and Risks, Safety Instrumented System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Safety Hazard **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Hazard** is defined by **IEC 61511** and **ISO 12100** as a physical situation or condition that has the inherent potential to cause harm to people, property, environment, or production. Hazards are *intrinsic* properties of the system and exist regardless of whether a hazardous event actually occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Distinction:** A *hazard* is a *source of danger*. It is NOT the harm itself. A vessel containing pressurized natural gas is a hazard; the explosion caused by its rupture is the *consequence*. **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of a loaded gun sitting on a table. The gun is the **hazard** (a source with potential to cause harm). The act of pulling the trigger and the bullet causing injury is the **risk realization** (the actual event). Even if no one gets shot, the *hazard* still exists — it is *latent*. Safety engineering is about recognizing that the gun is on the table, even when nothing has gone wrong yet. ### Types of Hazards (KTU Syllabus Coverage) | # | Hazard Category | Engineering Examples in Plant | |---|----------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | **Mechanical** | Rotating shafts, high-pressure vessels, unguarded blades | | 2 | **Electrical** | HV switchgear, ungrounded panels, arc flash zones | | 3 | **Chemical** | Toxic gas leaks (H₂S, Cl₂), corrosive acids, reactive monomers | | 4 | **Thermal** | Fired heaters, molten metal, cryogenic LNG | | 5 | **Process** | Over-pressurization, runaway reaction, phase change | | 6 | **Environmental** | VOC emissions, oil spill, greenhouse gas release | | 7 | **Ergonomic** | Repetitive strain, poor console design | | 8 | **Radiological** | NDT sources, radioactive level gauges | --- ## 1.2 Safety Risk **Formal KTU Definition:** **Risk** is a measure of the *combined effect* of the **probability of occurrence of a hazardous event** and the **severity of harm** (consequence) resulting from that event. Mathematically, it is the product of frequency and consequence. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard Phrasing (learn this verbatim for exams):** > "Risk = Frequency of Hazardous Event × Consequence Severity" **Conceptual Analogy:** If a *hazard* is a loaded gun, *risk* is the answer to the question: "How likely is someone to get shot, and how bad would it be?" A loaded gun in a vault (low frequency of access) has *low risk*, but the same gun on a kindergarten playground (high frequency) has *extreme risk* — even though the hazard (loaded gun) is identical. ### Risk = Function(Frequency, Consequence) $$R = f(F, C)$$ where: - $R$ = Risk (dimensionless score or $/year) - $F$ = Frequency of the hazardous event (events/year) - $C$ = Consequence severity (injury, fatality, financial loss) --- ## 1.3 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) **Formal KTU Definition (per IEC 61511-1:2016):** A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is an implementation of one or more *Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)* implemented in hardware, software, or a combination thereof, that is designed and installed to achieve or maintain a safe state of the process with respect to a specific hazardous event. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite Definition:** A SIS is a *separate, dedicated, independent* control system whose sole purpose is to *prevent* or *mitigate* hazardous events by taking the process to a safe state. It must NOT be used for normal process regulation. **Conceptual Analogy:** A SIS is the **airbag in your car**. The airbag does not steer the car (that is the BPCS / Basic Process Control System). It sits quietly, monitors crash sensors, and only acts when something dangerous happens. It is *independent* of normal driving controls, has its own sensors, its own decision logic, and its own actuators (the inflators). A modern car has BOTH: - **BPCS** → cruise control, ABS, traction control (efficiency) - **SIS** → airbags, seatbelt pretensioners, collision avoidance (safety) ### SIS vs BPCS — KTU Comparison Table | Parameter | BPCS (Basic Process Control System) | SIS (Safety Instrumented System) | |-----------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Purpose** | Maintain process at desired operating point | Take process to *safe state* on demand | | **Failure Goal** | *Fail-Open* / *Continue Operation* | *Fail-Safe* / *Fail-Closed* | | **Priority** | Process efficiency & quality | Life, environment, asset protection | | **Standards** | ISA 88, IEC 61131 | IEC 61508, IEC 61511, ISA 84 | | **Communication** | Continuous, bidirectional | Discrete, deterministic, on-demand | | **Output Action** | Modulating / setpoint tracking | Trip / shutdown (binary) | | **Redundancy** | Optional (for availability) | Mandatory (for safety integrity) | | **Testing** | As needed | Periodic proof test (mandatory) | | **Independence** | May share I/O with management system | Must be *electrically & logically* independent | --- ## 1.4 Hazard, Risk, and SIS — Visual Relationship > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Safety Thought (KTU Exam Hot Topic):** > 1. Identify **HAZARDS** (what can go wrong?) > 2. Assess the **RISK** (how bad? how often?) > 3. Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE) > 4. Where residual risk is unacceptable, install a **SIS** as an engineered safeguard > 5. Quantify the SIS performance via **SIL** (Safety Integrity Level) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hazard Identification Techniques (KTU Module 1 Focus) Hazard identification is the *first* step in the safety lifecycle. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following structured techniques: ### (a) HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study - **Origin:** Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), 1960s - **Method:** Applies a fixed set of *guide words* to every node (vessel, line, pump) in a P&ID - **Team:** Multi-disciplinary (Process, Instrumentation, Operations, Maintenance) **Standard HAZOP Guide Words (KTU Must-Know):** | Guide Word | Meaning | Typical Deviation | |------------|---------|-------------------| | NO / NOT | Complete negation | No flow, no level | | MORE | Quantitative increase | High pressure, high temperature | | LESS | Quantitative decrease | Low flow, low level | | REVERSE | Logical opposite | Backflow | | AS WELL AS | Additional component | Two phases, contamination | | PART OF | Partial achievement | Partial composition | | OTHER THAN | Complete substitution | Wrong material | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Trick:** HAZOP is *not* a risk-rating study. It is a *hazard identification* study. The output is a list of nodes, deviations, causes, and consequences. Risk ranking is done in a *separate* step. ### (b) FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis - **Method:** Bottom-up, component-by-component analysis - **Output:** RPN (Risk Priority Number) $$RPN = S \times O \times D$$ where: - $S$ = Severity (1–10) - $O$ = Occurrence (1–10) - $D$ = Detection (1–10) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern KTU Note:** RPN is increasingly replaced by **Risk Matrix** scoring in IEC 62502. Be ready to discuss limitations of RPN (e.g., RPN=8 can arise from 2×2×2 or 8×1×1 — equal score, very different risks). ### (c) FTA — Fault Tree Analysis - **Method:** Top-down, deductive (Boolean logic) - **Logic Gates:** AND, OR, NOT, voting (e.g., 2-out-of-3) - **Output:** Minimal cut sets, top-event probability ### (d) LOPA — Layer of Protection Analysis - **Method:** Semi-quantitative; layers of protection against a single cause-consequence pair - **Equation (KTU High-Yield):** $$\text{Mitigated Frequency} = \text{Initiating Event} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$$ where each PFD is the *Probability of Failure on Demand* of an independent protection layer. ### (e) What-If Analysis - Informal, brainstorming-style "What if the pump fails? What if the valve is closed? What if power is lost?" ### (f) Checklists - Static, rule-based, used in lower-complexity facilities. --- ## 2.2 Risk Assessment Matrix (KTU Standard 5×5) | Severity →
↓ Likelihood | **Negligible (1)** | **Minor (2)** | **Moderate (3)** | **Major (4)** | **Catastrophic (5)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Frequent (5)** | M | H | H | E | E | | **Probable (4)** | L | M | H | H | E | | **Occasional (3)** | L | M | M | H | E | | **Remote (2)** | L | L | M | M | H | | **Improbable (1)** | L | L | L | M | M | Legend: L = Low, M = Medium, H = High, E = Extreme > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** "If the cell is red (H or E), a SIS or additional IPL is mandatory." Tolerance criteria are *company-specific*, not universal. --- ## 2.3 ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) - **Origin:** UK Health and Safety at Work Act 1974 - **Concept:** Risk must be reduced *unless the cost (in money, effort, time) is grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit. - **Two Tests:** 1. **Risk must be reduced** by any *reasonably practicable* measure. 2. The measure is *not* reasonably practicable if its cost is **grossly disproportionate** to the benefit. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Buzzword:** "ALARP is a *legal* and *economic* compromise, not a zero-risk goal." Risk cannot be reduced to zero; only to a level that is "reasonably practicable." --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1) | # | Formula / Definition | Equation | Units / Range | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Risk Definition | $R = F \times C$ | events·consequence / year | Risk matrix computation | | 2 | RPN (FMEA) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless (1–1000) | FMEA ranking | | 3 | LOPA Mitigated Frequency | $f_{mit} = f_{init} \times \prod PFD_i$ | events / year | LOPA layer accounting | | 4 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless | SIL verification | | 5 | SIS Availability | $A = 1 - PFD_{avg}$ | Fraction (0–1) | Reliability check | | 6 | SIL Target (Demand Mode) | See SIL table below | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ | IEC 61511 compliance | | 7 | Top Event Probability (FTA) | $P_{top} = \sum P(CutSet_i)$ | Per year | Fault tree math | | 8 | 1oo2 Architecture PFD | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3}$ | — | Redundancy math | | 9 | 2oo2 Architecture PFD | $PFD_{2oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) \approx 2\lambda t$ | — | Voter logic | | 10 | 2oo3 Architecture PFD | $PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$ | — | TMR analysis | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ with vertical pipes inside a table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — the bare pipe breaks markdown table parsing. --- ## 2.5 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — Demand Mode Table | SIL | PFD_avg Range | RRF Range | Risk Reduction | |-----|---------------|-----------|----------------| | **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | 10× to 100× | | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | 100× to 1000× | | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | 1000× to 10000× | | **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | 10000× to 100000× | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Fact:** SIL 4 is rare in *process* industries. It is more common in nuclear and aerospace. Most process-industry SIFs are SIL 1, 2, or 3. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility (Why This Matters) - **Oil & Gas:** A sub-sea wellhead shutdown valve is a SIL 2/3 SIF that prevents the *Torrey Canyon*-scale disaster. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Reactor over-temperature protection is typically a SIL 2 SIF using redundant RTDs and a logic solver. - **Power Generation:** Boiler drum level trip is a SIL 3 SIF preventing boiler explosion. - **Chemicals:** Ethylene oxide reactor quench is a SIL 3 SIF preventing runaway polymerization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Industrial Context:** The hierarchy is **Inherent Safe Design → BPCS → Alarm → SIS → Emergency Response → PPE**. A SIS is a *layer of protection*, never the *first* or *only* line of defense. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mitigated Event Frequency (LOPA Equation) The fundamental LOPA equation is derived from the product of independent probability terms. The starting assumption: a hazardous event requires a single **Initiating Event (IE)** AND the simultaneous failure of **all** independent protection layers (IPLs) between the cause and the consequence. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define the unprotected frequency.** Let $f_0$ be the frequency of the initiating event (e.g., pump deadhead failure = 0.1 per year). **Step 2: Apply each independent protection layer.** For each layer $i$, define $PFD_i$ as the probability the layer *fails on demand*. The probability the layer *works* is $(1 - PFD_i)$. $$P(\text{layer } i \text{ works}) = 1 - PFD_i$$ **Step 3: Probability that ALL layers fail (in series, AND logic).** For independent layers in series, the joint failure probability is the product: $$P(\text{all layers fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$ **Step 4: Combine with initiating frequency.** The mitigated event frequency is the unprotected frequency multiplied by the joint failure probability: $$\boxed{f_{mitigated} = f_{IE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i}$$ **Step 5: Numerical example (KTU exam style).** - Initiating event: Blocked outlet on a reactor → $f_{IE} = 0.1$ / year - Layer 1: BPCS high-pressure alarm → operator action → $PFD_1 = 0.1$ - Layer 2: Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) → $PFD_2 = 0.01$ - Layer 3: SIS (proposed SIL 2) → $PFD_3 = 0.01$ $$f_{mit} = 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ / year}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Interpretation:** A mitigated frequency of $10^{-6}$/year corresponds to *one event in a million years* — well below typical corporate tolerance of $10^{-4}$/year. The proposed SIL 2 layer is therefore acceptable. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) and SIL Determination The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is the inverse of the average probability of failure on demand. It quantifies *how much risk the SIF removes*. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define the target risk.** Target risk $R_T$ = tolerable frequency of the consequence (e.g., $10^{-5}$ fatalities/year — KTU typical). **Step 2: Define the unreduced (raw) risk.** Raw risk $R_0$ = frequency of the initiating event × probability of consequence given the event. **Step 3: Define the required reduction.** The SIF must reduce the risk from $R_0$ to $R_T$. The required factor is: $$RRF_{required} = \dfrac{R_0}{R_T}$$ **Step 4: Set the SIL target.** A SIF is *fit for purpose* if its own $PFD_{avg} \le 1 / RRF_{required}$: $$PFD_{avg,SIF} \le \dfrac{1}{RRF_{required}}$$ **Step 5: Numerical example.** - Initiating event frequency: $f_{IE} = 10^{-2}$ / year - Consequence probability given event: $P_{cond} = 1$ (certain fatality if unmitigated) - Tolerable frequency: $f_{tol} = 10^{-5}$ / year $$RRF_{required} = \dfrac{10^{-2} \times 1}{10^{-5}} = 1000$$ $$PFD_{avg} \le \dfrac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Step:** $10^{-3}$ falls in the SIL 2 band ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$). Therefore, the SIF must be designed to **SIL 2**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 1oo2 Architecture PFD (Diagnostic Coverage) A 1-out-of-2 voting architecture requires *both* channels working. We derive its $PFD$ assuming a proof-test interval $T$ and channel failure rate $\lambda$. **Step 1: Failure rate assumption.** A single channel fails with constant failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour). The unavailability over interval $T$ (assuming repair) is approximately: $$PFD_{1oo1} \approx \dfrac{\lambda T}{2}$$ **Step 2: 1oo2 logic.** Both channels must fail *simultaneously* for the system to be unavailable. The probability is the product of two independent unavailabilities: $$PFD_{1oo2} = PFD_A \times PFD_B = \left(\dfrac{\lambda T}{2}\right)^2$$ **Step 3: Numerical evaluation.** Let $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /h, $T = 8760$ h (1 year): $$PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 0.0219 \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$PFD_{1oo2} = (2.2 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Going from 1oo1 to 1oo2 reduces the $PFD$ by a factor of ~50×. The system is *safer* but *less available* (a single channel failure still does not cause spurious trip — that's a 2oo2 characteristic). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing SIS Architecture | Architecture | Spurious Trip Rate | Safe Failure Fraction | Typical Application | KTU SIL Match | |--------------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|---------------| | 1oo1 | Low | n/a | Low-criticality sensors | SIL 1 only | | 1oo2 | Very Low | n/a | Critical sensors (high safety, low spurious) | SIL 2 / 3 | | 2oo2 | High | n/a | Critical valves (high safety, must not spur) | SIL 2 / 3 | | 2oo3 | Medium | High | Triple modular redundant, voting logic | SIL 3 | | 2oo4 | Low | High | High-integrity double-voting | SIL 3 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — SIS Functional Block Architecture (IEC 61511) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Process Plant] -->|Physical Variable| S1[Sensor 1] P -->|Physical Variable| S2[Sensor 2] S1 --> LS1[Logic Solver Channel A] S2 --> LS2[Logic Solver Channel B] LS1 --> V1[Final Element Valve 1] LS2 --> V2[Final Element Valve 2] V1 --> P V2 --> P OP[Operator HMI] -.->|Bypasses & Overrides| LS1 OP -.->|Bypasses & Overrides| LS2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The process is monitored by redundant **Sensors** that feed a redundant **Logic Solver** (safety PLC / relay logic / trip amplifier). On a hazardous condition, the logic solver de-energizes the **Final Elements** (typically shut-off valves), bringing the process to a *safe state*. Operator interfaces can apply maintenance bypasses but are *not* part of the safety path. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Safety Lifecycle Block Flow (IEC 61511) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Phase 1: Concept & Scope Definition] --> B[Phase 2: Hazard & Risk Assessment] B --> C[Phase 3: Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers] C --> D[Phase 4: SIS Design & Engineering] D --> E[Phase 5: Installation Commissioning & Validation] E --> F[Phase 6: Operation Maintenance & Modification] F --> G[Phase 7: Decommissioning] G --> H[End of Lifecycle] F -.->|Periodic Audit| B D -.->|SIL Verification| C ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on the Safety Lifecycle:** The lifecycle is *iterative* — the dashed feedback arrows from Operation back to Risk Assessment and from Design back to Allocation show that SIS performance must be *re-verified* after every modification. This is the principle of **Functional Safety Management (FSM)**. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hierarchy of Controls & SIS Position ```mermaid flowchart TB H1[Inherent Safe Design Eliminate or Reduce Hazard] --> H2[Engineering Controls BPCS Alarms Containment] H2 --> H3[Administrative Controls Procedures Training Permits] H3 --> H4[Safety Instrumented System SIS Last Engineered Barrier] H4 --> H5[Emergency Response Firefighting Medical Spill Control] H5 --> H6[Personal Protective Equipment PPE Last Line of Defense] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** "The hierarchy flows from *most reliable* to *least reliable* safeguard. PPE depends on the worker wearing it correctly — the highest *reliance* is on inherent design." --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Risk Matrix Mapping (5x5 Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Frequent Likelihood] --> R1[High Risk] B[Probable Likelihood] --> R2[Medium Risk] C[Occasional Likelihood] --> R3[Medium Risk] D[Remote Likelihood] --> R4[Low Risk] E[Improbable Likelihood] --> R5[Low Risk] S1[Catastrophic Severity] --> R1 S2[Major Severity] --> R2 S3[Moderate Severity] --> R3 S4[Minor Severity] --> R4 S5[Negligible Severity] --> R5 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1: Define Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Differentiate it from BPCS. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Remember/Understand]** **Model Answer:** A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a dedicated, independent system that takes the process to a *safe state* on the occurrence of a hazardous event. It implements one or more **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** and is governed by **IEC 61511**. | Feature | BPCS | SIS | |---------|------|-----| | Purpose | Maintain process variable | Achieve safe state | | Output | Modulating | Trip / Discrete | | Failure goal | Fail-Open | Fail-Safe | | Independence | May share hardware | Electrically and logically *independent* | | Standards | IEC 61131 | IEC 61508 / 61511 | --- ### Question 2: Explain the term "ALARP". Why is it important in process industries? **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Understand]** **Model Answer:** **ALARP** stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable"**. It is the principle that the residual risk of a process plant must be reduced to the lowest level that can be achieved by measures whose cost (money, effort, time) is not *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit gained. **Importance:** 1. It provides a *legal* and *economic* framework for accepting residual risk — pure zero-risk is impossible and unaffordable. 2. It mandates *continuous improvement* of safety measures. 3. It is the foundation of the *tolerable risk* concepts in IEC 61511. 4. The *grossly disproportionate* test prevents both under-protection and over-engineering. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Describe in detail the various Hazard Identification techniques used in process industries. Compare HAZOP and FMEA.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Understand/Apply]** **Model Solution:** **Hazard Identification Techniques:** 1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study):** - Team-based, qualitative, applied to P&IDs. - Uses *guide words* (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN). - Examines every node (vessel, line) for deviations from design intent. - Output: List of deviations, causes, consequences, and recommendations. - *Best for:* Continuous process plants (chemical, petrochemical). 2. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis):** - Component-by-component, bottom-up analysis. - Assigns Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) scores (1–10). - Computes **RPN = S × O × D** to rank failure modes. - *Best for:* Mechanical and electrical equipment. 3. **What-If Analysis:** - Brainstorming. "What if pump P-101 fails?" "What if valve V-202 is closed?" - Fast, low cost, but unstructured. 4. **Checklist Method:** - Static, rule-based. Useful for routine, low-complexity operations. 5. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):** - Top-down, Boolean logic. Used for complex systems (nuclear, aerospace). - Identifies *minimal cut sets*. 6. **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis):** - Semi-quantitative. Each independent protection layer has a PFD. - Multiplies PFDs of all layers against the initiating event to compute *mitigated frequency*. **Comparison Table — HAZOP vs FMEA:** | Parameter | HAZOP | FMEA | |-----------|-------|------| | Direction | Forward (cause → consequence) | Forward (component → effect) | | Method | Guide-word driven | Failure-mode driven | | Granularity | Process deviation | Component-level | | Output | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | | Scope | Process P&ID | Mechanical/electrical equipment | | Standard | IEC 61882 | IEC 60812 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Students often confuse HAZOP and What-If — HAZOP uses **fixed guide words**; What-If does not. > 2. RPN *ranking* (e.g., "RPN 100 is unacceptable") is **not** standardized; do not claim universal cutoffs. > 3. HAZOP is *not* a risk rating exercise. Markers deduct 1 mark if you claim HAZOP gives a SIL directly. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A reactor has an initiating event frequency of $5 \times 10^{-2}$ / year. Existing protections are a BPCS pressure control loop (PFD = $10^{-1}$) and a pressure relief valve (PFD = $10^{-2}$). Target tolerable frequency is $10^{-5}$ / year. Determine:** **(i) The mitigated frequency with existing layers.** **(ii) The required RRF of a proposed SIS.** **(iii) The target SIL.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | CO2, CO3 | Apply/Analyze]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Mitigated frequency with existing layers:** $$f_{mit} = f_{IE} \times PFD_{BPCS} \times PFD_{PRV}$$ $$f_{mit} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / year}$$ **(ii) Required RRF of the proposed SIS:** $$RRF_{required} = \dfrac{f_{mit}}{f_{tolerable}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-5}} = 5$$ **(iii) Target SIL:** A RRF of 5 corresponds to a PFD of $1/5 = 0.2 = 2 \times 10^{-1}$. Looking up the SIL table: $PFD$ in the range $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ is **SIL 1**. > [!NOTE] > The proposed SIS must be designed to **SIL 1** or better. If corporate policy requires minimum SIL 2, a more reliable BPCS (e.g., redundant transmitters) would lower the SIL 1 requirement. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Safety Lifecycle as per IEC 61511. List all phases and discuss the importance of the Operation & Maintenance phase.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Understand]** **Model Solution:** **IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle (11 Phases — KTU Required Coverage):** 1. **Phase 1 — Concept & Scope Definition:** Define the boundary, hazards, regulations, and stakeholders. 2. **Phase 2 — Hazard & Risk Assessment:** Identify hazards (HAZOP, FMEA, LOPA) and assess risk. 3. **Phase 3 — Allocation of Safety Functions:** Decide which protections are BPCS, alarms, SIS, or external. 4. **Phase 4 — SIS Design & Engineering:** Specify SIL, redundancy, voting, diagnostics. 5. **Phase 5 — Installation, Commissioning & Validation:** Site Acceptance Test (SAT), pre-startup safety review (PSSR). 6. **Phase 6 — Operation, Maintenance & Modification:** Proof tests, calibration, bypass management. 7. **Phase 7 — Decommissioning:** Safe shutdown, removal of SIFs. > [!NOTE] > Phases 8–11 cover analysis, realisation, and integration of non-SIS equipment, but the *core* 7 phases above are what KTU Module 1 expects. **Importance of Operation & Maintenance (Phase 6):** 1. **Proof Testing** — the *assumed* PFD of a SIF is only valid if the proof test is performed at the stated interval. Skipping a test silently degrades the SIL. 2. **Bypass Management** — bypassing a SIF for maintenance is a *temporary removal of a protection layer*; it must be authorized, time-limited, and logged. 3. **Modification Management** — any hardware or software change triggers a re-validation (Management of Change — MOC). 4. **Competency** — operators and maintenance staff must be trained on the SIS philosophy. 5. **Documentation** — safety manuals, trip schedules, and SIL verification records must be kept current. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Do not confuse **IEC 61511** (process sector) with **IEC 61508** (cross-sector generic). Mention both. > 2. Many students forget the *feedback loop* from Operation back to Risk Assessment — this is worth 1 mark in any 7-mark question. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A pressure transmitter has a failure rate of $\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /h. Proof-test interval is 1 year ($T = 8760$ h). Compute the $PFD_{avg}$ for (i) 1oo1 and (ii) 1oo2 architectures. State which architecture is suitable for SIL 2.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO3 | Apply/Analyze]** **Model Solution:** **(i) 1oo1 Architecture:** $$PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda T}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$ This falls in the **SIL 1** range ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$). **(ii) 1oo2 Architecture:** $$PFD_{1oo2} = \left(\dfrac{\lambda T}{2}\right)^2 = (1.752 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 3.07 \times 10^{-4}$$ This falls in the **SIL 2** range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$). **Conclusion:** - 1oo1 → SIL 1 only. - 1oo2 → SIL 2 (suitable). Therefore, **1oo2 architecture** is suitable for the SIL 2 requirement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** This simplified derivation assumes DC = 100% (all dangerous failures are detected and the channel auto-isolates). In practice, IEC 61511 requires DC > 60% for SIL 1, > 90% for SIL 2, > 95% for SIL 3. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 1 Topics > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk** — They are *not* synonyms. A hazard is a *source*; risk involves *probability and consequence*. > 2. **Claiming HAZOP gives a SIL** — HAZOP identifies *what*; LOPA or Risk Graph assigns *how much* (i.e., SIL). > 3. **Forgetting independence of SIF and BPCS** — IEC 61511 explicitly requires separation; shared sensors/valves/Power Supply disqualify the SIF. > 4. **Treating ALARP as zero risk** — ALARP is a *legal compromise*, not an absolute goal. > 5. **Mixing IEC 61508 and IEC 61511** — 61508 is the umbrella standard; 61511 is the process-sector implementation. Examiners love this distinction. > 6. **Writing $\vert PFD \vert$ with vertical pipes in the table** — Use `\vert` or `\lvert ... \rvert` in LaTeX. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Hazard** = source of potential harm (intrinsic). - [x] **Risk** = Frequency × Consequence (a *measure*, not a property). - [x] **ALARP** = reduce risk until cost is grossly disproportionate to benefit. - [x] **SIS** = independent, dedicated, takes process to *safe state* on demand. - [x] **BPCS** = process *control*; SIS = process *safety*. Never the same. - [x] **SIF** = Safety Instrumented Function; SIS contains one or more SIFs. - [x] **SIL** = discrete level (1, 2, 3, 4) corresponding to PFD ranges. - [x] **PFD** = Probability of Failure on Demand (lower = better). - [x] **RRF** = 1 / PFD; quantifies risk reduction. - [x] **HAZOP** uses *guide words*; **FMEA** uses *RPN = S × O × D*. - [x] **LOPA** equation: $f_{mit} = f_{IE} \times \prod PFD_i$. - [x] **1oo2 PFD** = $(\lambda T / 2)^2$ — required for SIL 2 in this derivation. - [x] **IEC 61511** = process sector; **IEC 61508** = umbrella generic. - [x] **Safety Lifecycle** has 7–11 phases; Operation/Maintenance is *critical* (proof tests). - [x] **Hierarchy**: Inherent Safe → BPCS → Alarm → SIS → Emergency → PPE. - [x] **Independence** of SIS from BPCS is a *legal* and *technical* requirement, not a suggestion.

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Safety Instrumentation System (SIS)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (IEC 61511 / ISA 84.01 aligned):**
> A **Safety Instrumentation System (SIS)** is an engineered system composed of sensors (input), logic solvers (decision-making), and final control elements (output) that is specifically designed and managed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined unsafe conditions are detected.

In simple terms, a Safety Instrumentation System is the **"emergency doctor"** of an industrial plant. Just as a doctor monitors your vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure) and intervenes the moment something goes wrong, an SIS continuously monitors dangerous process variables (pressure, temperature, flow, level) and automatically takes corrective action — such as shutting a valve, stopping a pump, or de-energizing a heater — *before* a disaster can occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Purpose of SIS:** The fundamental goal of a Safety Instrumentation System is **risk reduction**. It does **NOT** optimize the process, nor does it improve productivity. Its sole mission is to keep people, the environment, and assets safe.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of SIS Terminology

For the KTU 2024 syllabus, you must memorize these three foundational terms because they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question on this module.

### A) Hazard
> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard:** A physical situation or condition that has the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment.

**Analogy:** A knife left on the edge of a table is a **hazard**. It has the *potential* to fall and injure someone.

### B) Risk
> [!NOTE]
> **Risk:** The combination of the **probability** of an unwanted event occurring and the **severity** of its consequences.

**Analogy:** A knife left on a low table in a child-free room has *low probability* of harm and *low severity* — hence low risk. The same knife on a high table above a crowded play area is **high risk**.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$ \text{Risk} = f(\text{Probability}, \text{Severity}) $$

### C) Safety
> [!NOTE]
> **Safety:** Freedom from unacceptable risk. Note: safety does **not** mean zero risk — it means risk has been reduced to an *acceptable* (tolerable) level.

**Analogy:** A properly stored knife in a locked drawer is "safe" because the residual risk of harm is now acceptable.

---

## 1.3 Other Key Definitions Students Must Know

| Term | Formal Definition | Quick Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **SIF (Safety Instrumented Function)** | A single safety function (e.g., "close valve XV-101 if pressure > 10 bar") executed by the SIS to bring the process to a safe state. | A single reflex action, like pulling your hand from a hot stove. |
| **Logic Solver** | The brain of the SIS — receives sensor inputs, applies logic, and commands final elements. (PLC, relay, or dedicated safety controller). | The human brain deciding to pull the hand. |
| **Final Control Element** | The physical device that actually performs the safety action (e.g., a shut-down valve, circuit breaker, fire damper). | The muscles that move the hand. |
| **Sensor / Transmitter** | The device that detects the abnormal process condition and sends a signal to the logic solver. | The pain receptors in the skin. |
| **Safe State** | The predetermined state of the process that minimizes hazard (e.g., valves closed, pumps off, firewater on). | "Don't touch" reflex position. |
| **Demand** | An event or condition that requires the SIS to act. | The moment the stove touches the skin. |
| **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** | A methodology used to evaluate the risk reduction provided by each independent protection layer. | Multiple safety nets stacked under a tightrope walker. |

---

## 1.4 The Big Picture — Where Does the SIS Sit in a Plant?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SIS is **one of many** independent protection layers in a modern process plant. It is *always* considered the **last line of defense** — it only acts when all other layers (basic process control, alarms, operator intervention, mechanical relief) have failed.

The standard "**Swiss Cheese Model**" of accident causation:

```
Layer 1: Process Design       [🧀 holes exist]
Layer 2: Basic Process Control [🧀 holes exist]
Layer 3: Alarms + Operator    [🧀 holes exist]
Layer 4: Safety Instrumentation [🧀 last barrier]
Layer 5: Emergency Response   [community]
```

The SIS is **Layer 4** — the engineered, automatic, and highly reliable barrier between a hazardous event and a catastrophic outcome.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the SIS Architecture

The SIS is formally structured as a closed-loop control chain. Every working SIS has **three** components connected in series. If any one component fails, the SIS fails to perform its function.

### The SIS Chain of Action (sensor → logic → action)

1. **Sensing (Input Subsystem / Field Sensors)**
   - *Why:* Something must *detect* that the process has gone abnormal.
   - *How:* Pressure transmitters (PT), temperature transmitters (TT), level switches (LS), flame detectors, gas detectors.
   - *Failure concern:* A sensor that "sees" a safe reading when the process is actually dangerous is called a **dangerous failure** — the worst possible failure.

2. **Logic Solver (Decision Subsystem)**
   - *Why:* Raw sensor signals must be interpreted and a decision made.
   - *How:* Specialized safety PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-1500F, Allen-Bradley GuardLogix, ABB AC500-S), or simple trip relays.
   - *Logic types:* 1oo1 (one-out-of-one), 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 — these are **voting architectures** and are high-yield KTU exam topics.

3. **Final Control Element (Output Subsystem)**
   - *Why:* Without a physical actuator, no corrective action occurs.
   - *How:* Solenoid-operated shut-off valves, motor contactors, circuit breakers, firewater deluge valves.
   - *Design rule:* Final elements must **fail-safe** — on loss of signal/power, they must go to the safe state (typically "de-energize to trip").

---

## 2.2 High-Yield KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk | $R = f(P,S)$ | Probability $\times$ Severity | Dimensionless index |
| Risk Reduction Factor | RRF | How much the SIS reduces risk | $RRF = 1 / PFD$ |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD$ | Probability the SIS fails when asked to act | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| Average Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD_{avg}$ | Mean PFD over the proof test interval | Used for low-demand mode |
| Safety Integrity Level | $SIL$ | Discrete integrity tier (1, 2, 3, 4) | IEC 61508 standard |
| SIL 1 Range | $10^{-2} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-1}$ | Low-integrity protection | Process industries |
| SIL 2 Range | $10^{-3} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-2}$ | Medium-integrity protection | Most common SIS |
| SIL 3 Range | $10^{-4} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-3}$ | High-integrity protection | Critical hazards |
| SIL 4 Range | $10^{-5} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-4}$ | Very high integrity | Rare, mainly nuclear/aerospace |
| Process Safety Time | $PST$ | Time the process can tolerate an unsafe state | Seconds, must exceed SIS response time |
| Proof Test Interval | $T_{proof}$ | Time between manual SIS testing | Typically 1–5 years |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR$ | Average time to restore failed component | Usually 8 hours |
| MTTF (sensor) | $MTTF$ | Mean time to failure of a sensor | From manufacturer data |
| Safe State | $S_{safe}$ | Predefined safe condition of process | Energy isolated, depressurized |
| Fail-Safe Position | $F_{safe}$ | Default position of valve/breaker | Energize-to-trip or de-energize-to-trip |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** For the 2024 Scheme ESE, the examiner may give you a numeric PFD value and ask you to identify the corresponding SIL level. Memorize the SIL ranges above **cold**.

---

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

The definitions in this module are not just textbook jargon — they form the **legal and contractual** backbone of process plant design worldwide:

- **IEC 61511** — International standard governing SIS in the process industry.
- **IEC 61508** — Umbrella functional safety standard for all electrical/electronic/programmable systems.
- **ISA 84.01** — North American equivalent, harmonized with IEC 61511.
- **OSHA 1910.119 (PSM)** — U.S. Process Safety Management regulation that legally mandates SIS for listed highly-hazardous chemicals.
- **KTU relevance:** A Process Instrumentation graduate may be employed as a Safety Engineer, Functional Safety Engineer, or I&C designer — all roles that require fluent use of these definitions.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When you write answers, always quote the **standard reference** (e.g., "As per IEC 61511..."). Examiners reward students who cite standards because it shows board-level professional literacy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Relationship Between PFD and SIL

The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is **not** a vague concept — it is derived mathematically from the *Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)* of the Safety Instrumented Function.

### Step 1 — Define the starting point

A Safety Instrumented Function has a probability of working on demand equal to $(1 - PFD)$.

$$
P_{success} = 1 - PFD
$$

> *Text row:* The success rate of any safety function is the complement of its failure probability. If PFD is 0.01, the function succeeds 99% of the time when called upon.

### Step 2 — Define the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

The Risk Reduction Factor expresses how much the SIS improves safety versus the unmitigated case:

$$
RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

> *Text row:* Take the reciprocal of the average PFD. A PFD of $10^{-3}$ gives an RRF of 1000 — meaning the SIS reduces risk by a factor of 1000.

### Step 3 — Map the PFD ranges to discrete SIL tiers

The IEC 61508 standard discretizes the continuous PFD scale into four discrete integrity tiers, one for low-demand mode of operation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SIL 1:} \quad & 10^{-2} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-1} \\
\text{SIL 2:} \quad & 10^{-3} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-2} \\
\text{SIL 3:} \quad & 10^{-4} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-3} \\
\text{SIL 4:} \quad & 10^{-5} \geq PFD_{avg} > 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Text row:* Each SIL level represents a **tenfold** improvement in reliability. SIL 4 is therefore **1000 times** more reliable than SIL 1.

### Step 4 — Worked numerical example

**Question:** A safety valve has an average probability of failure on demand of $4 \times 10^{-3}$. Determine its SIL level and the corresponding risk reduction factor.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= 4 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text{Check SIL boundaries: } & 10^{-3} \geq 4 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-2} \quad \checkmark \text{false} \\
& 10^{-2} \geq 4 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-3} \quad \checkmark \text{true} \\
\therefore \text{SIL Level} &= \text{SIL 2} \\
RRF &= \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 250
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Text row:* Since $4 \times 10^{-3}$ lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$, the function is classified as **SIL 2**. It provides a 250-fold reduction in risk when it acts.

### Step 5 — Overall SIS PFD (Series Combination)

Because sensor, logic solver, and final element are arranged in **series** (any one failure causes the entire SIF to fail), the overall PFD is the **sum** of the individual PFDs:

$$
PFD_{SIS} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{final\,element}
$$

> *Text row:* The total PFD is simply additive. If sensor contributes $2 \times 10^{-3}$, logic contributes $1 \times 10^{-3}$, and valve contributes $5 \times 10^{-3}$, the total is $8 \times 10^{-3}$ (SIL 2).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Key Definitions

| Dimension | Basic Process Control System (BPCS) | Safety Instrumentation System (SIS) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Maintain process at desired setpoint | Take process to safe state on demand |
| Mode of operation | Continuous (closed-loop) | Dormant until a demand occurs |
| Consequence of failure | Production loss, off-spec product | Potential for major accident, fatality |
| Reliability target | Moderate (e.g., 95–99%) | Very high (e.g., 99.9% – 99.999%) |
| Standard | ISA, IEC 61131 | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 / ISA 84.01 |
| Independence | Shares hardware with process | Must be **independent** of BPCS |
| Action on failure | Fault-tolerant, continues operation | Fail-safe to safe state |
| Testing | Continuous self-checks | Periodic proof testing (1–5 yr interval) |
| Operator override | Allowed, common | **Strongly discouraged** (bypasses must be managed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Distinction:** Many students confuse BPCS and SIS in answers. The KTU examiner will **specifically** check whether you understand that the SIS is *independent* of the BPCS and only acts on *demand*, not continuously.

---

## 3.3 The Process Safety Time Constraint

For an SIS to be effective, its total response time (from detection of unsafe condition to final element reaching safe state) must be **less than** the Process Safety Time (PST).

$$
t_{detect} + t_{logic} + t_{actuate} < t_{PST}
$$

Where:
- $t_{detect}$ = sensor response time
- $t_{logic}$ = logic solver scan time
- $t_{actuate}$ = final element stroke time
- $t_{PST}$ = time the process can survive in the unsafe state without harm

> *Text row:* If the SIS is slower than the PST, the hazard will occur *before* protection can act. The SIS design is therefore **unacceptable** and must be redesigned (faster sensors, faster logic, or faster valves).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Safety Instrumentation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC["PROCESS PLANT\nReactor or Vessel"]
    SEN1["SENSOR A\nPressure Transmitter PT 101"]
    SEN2["SENSOR B\nTemperature Transmitter TT 102"]
    LOGIC["LOGIC SOLVER\nSafety PLC or Trip Relay"]
    FCE1["FINAL ELEMENT 1\nShut Off Valve SDV 101"]
    FCE2["FINAL ELEMENT 2\nPump Trip Relay"]
    SAFE["SAFE STATE\nIsolated, Depressurized, De-energized"]

    PROC --> SEN1
    PROC --> SEN2
    SEN1 --> LOGIC
    SEN2 --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> FCE1
    LOGIC --> FCE2
    FCE1 --> SAFE
    FCE2 --> SAFE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The process condition is monitored by multiple independent sensors. If any sensor detects an unsafe value, it sends the signal to the logic solver. The logic solver applies its pre-programmed safety logic (e.g., 1oo2 voting) and, if a trip condition is confirmed, commands the final control elements to actuate. The system reaches the **safe state**.

---

## 4.2 The Swiss Cheese Model — Independent Protection Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1\nInherent Safe Plant Design"]
    L2["LAYER 2\nBasic Process Control System"]
    L3["LAYER 3\nOperator Alarms and Intervention"]
    L4["LAYER 4\nSafety Instrumentation System SIS"]
    L5["LAYER 5\nEmergency Response Firewater Dikes"]
    ACC["ACCIDENT\nLoss of Containment"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> ACC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each layer has **holes** (failures). A serious accident only happens when the holes in *every* layer align. The SIS is the fourth engineered layer and is the *last* automatic one.

---

## 4.3 PFD versus SIL Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PFD1["PFD 10 to the power minus 1"] --> S1["SIL 1"]
    PFD2["PFD 10 to the power minus 2"] --> S2["SIL 2"]
    PFD3["PFD 10 to the power minus 3"] --> S3["SIL 3"]
    PFD4["PFD 10 to the power minus 4"] --> S4["SIL 4"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As the PFD value decreases (the system becomes more reliable), the SIL level increases. Memorize the four ranges cold for the KTU exam.

---

## 4.4 Demand vs Continuous Mode Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW DEMAND MODE"]
        LD1["Process runs normally"]
        LD2["SIF dormant most of the time"]
        LD3["Action only when demand occurs"]
        LD4["SIL based on PFD average"]
        LD1 --> LD2 --> LD3 --> LD4
    end

    subgraph HIGH["HIGH DEMAND OR CONTINUOUS MODE"]
        HD1["SIF operates frequently"]
        HD2["Action many times per year"]
        HD3["SIL based on PFH per hour"]
        HD4["Used for SIL 3 and 4 mostly"]
        HD1 --> HD2 --> HD3 --> HD4
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The mode of operation changes how SIL is calculated. Low-demand uses **PFD average**. High-demand/continuous uses **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Safety Instrumentation System (SIS). List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines expected):**

> A **Safety Instrumentation System (SIS)** is an engineered system designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined unsafe conditions are detected, thereby reducing the risk of harm to people, the environment, and assets (IEC 61511).
>
> It is composed of three main components arranged in series:
> 1. **Sensor / Input Subsystem** – Detects abnormal process variables (pressure, temperature, level).
> 2. **Logic Solver** – The decision-making unit (safety PLC or trip relay).
> 3. **Final Control Element** – The actuator that performs the safety action (e.g., shut-off valve).
>
> The SIS operates independently of the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) and is the *last automatic line of defense* against hazardous events.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Hazard and Risk with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (6–8 lines expected):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A source or situation with potential to cause harm | Probability $\times$ Severity of harm |
| Question it answers | "What *can* go wrong?" | "How *likely* is it, and how bad? " |
| Quantitative? | No, qualitative | Yes, can be numerical |

**Example:** A pressure vessel filled with toxic gas at high pressure — the **hazard** is the toxic gas under pressure. The **risk** is low if the vessel is in a sealed, unpopulated enclosure, but very high if it is located next to a populated control room.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Mark Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Define the term *Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)*. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical SIF and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is a single, specific safety function performed by the SIS to achieve or maintain a safe state for the process. Each SIF has a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** specifying the required reliability.

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above)** — Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Control Element → Safe State.

**Function of each block:**

1. **Sensor (Input):** Continuously measures the process variable (e.g., PT-101 measures reactor pressure). When the value crosses the safety trip setpoint, the sensor changes its output state (e.g., a 4–20 mA loop drops below 3.6 mA on a detected over-pressure).

2. **Logic Solver (Decision):** Receives the sensor signal, applies a voting and logic function (e.g., 1oo1 or 2oo2), and decides whether the process is in a dangerous state. If a trip is required, it de-energizes its output to the final element.

3. **Final Control Element (Output):** Receives the trip command and physically executes the safety action. For an over-pressure SIF, this is typically a **fail-safe shut-off valve** that closes on loss of actuating power (de-energize-to-trip principle).

4. **Safe State:** The pre-defined safe condition of the process — for the over-pressure SIF, the safe state is: fuel flow stopped, vessel isolated, and pressure relieved through a rupture disc or blowdown valve.

The three blocks are connected in **series**, so the overall SIF reliability is governed by the *weakest* link.

---

**Q. (b)** A Safety Instrumented Function has the following PFD contributions: sensor = $2 \times 10^{-3}$, logic solver = $1 \times 10^{-3}$, final element = $5 \times 10^{-3}$. Calculate the **overall PFD of the SIF** and determine the **SIL level**. Also compute the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Series combination of PFDs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{SIS} &= PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{final\,element} \\
PFD_{SIS} &= 2 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-3} \\
PFD_{SIS} &= 8 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the SIL level:**

The value $8 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $10^{-3} \geq PFD > 10^{-2}$.

$$
\therefore \text{SIL Level} = \text{SIL 2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor:**

$$
RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{SIS}} = \dfrac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 125
$$

**Conclusion:** The SIF has an overall PFD of $8 \times 10^{-3}$, qualifies for **SIL 2**, and provides a **125-fold** reduction in risk.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**. How does the SIS fit into the layers of protection in a typical process plant? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a semi-quantitative methodology used to evaluate the risk reduction provided by each *independent* protection layer (IPL) in a process plant. It answers the question: *“How many layers deep is the defense against a particular accident scenario?”*

**Typical LOPA layers (from innermost to outermost):**

1. **Inherent Safe Design** – Eliminate the hazard at the design stage (e.g., smaller inventory of toxic material).
2. **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** – Maintains the process at desired setpoints; first engineered layer.
3. **Alarms and Operator Intervention** – Human operator responds to abnormal indications.
4. **Safety Instrumentation System (SIS)** – *Automatic, independent* engineered barrier that acts when BPCS and operator fail.
5. **Physical Protection** – Dikes, fire walls, blast walls.
6. **Emergency Response** – On-site and community emergency plans.

**How the SIS fits:** The SIS is positioned as the **fourth and last automatic engineered layer**. It is *independent* of the BPCS (separate sensors, separate logic solver, separate valves) and is *passive* — it only acts when a demand occurs. Its required reliability is determined using LOPA: the *gap* between the tolerable risk and the risk provided by Layers 1–3 is the *risk reduction* the SIS must supply, which translates to a target **SIL level**.

---

**Q. (b)** Define **Safe State**, **Fail-Safe**, and **Process Safety Time (PST)**. Why must the SIS response time be less than the PST? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Safe State:** The pre-defined state of the process that minimizes risk of harm once the hazard is detected. Example: For an over-pressure reactor, the safe state is "isolated, depressurized, and cooled."

**Fail-Safe:** The design principle where a component automatically moves to its safe state on loss of signal, loss of power, or detection of a failure. Example: A solenoid-operated valve that **closes** on loss of electrical power is fail-safe-closed.

**Process Safety Time (PST):** The maximum time interval between the onset of an unsafe process condition and the occurrence of the hazardous event, during which the protection system *must* complete its action to prevent the event.

**Why SIS response time < PST:** The SIS chain of action —

$$
t_{SIS} = t_{sensor} + t_{logic} + t_{final\,element}
$$

— must be strictly less than the PST:

$$
t_{SIS} < t_{PST}
$$

If this inequality is violated, the SIS will complete its action *after* the hazard has already occurred, rendering the protection **useless**. Designers must therefore choose faster sensors (e.g., ultrasonic level switch vs. guided-wave radar), faster logic (e.g., dedicated safety PLC vs. general PLC), and faster final elements (e.g., quarter-turn ball valve vs. globe valve) to ensure the inequality holds with adequate safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing BPCS with SIS** — The SIS is *independent* of the BPCS. Writing that "the SIS is part of the PLC" loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the citation** — Always cite "**IEC 61511**" or "**IEC 61508**" when defining SIS / SIL. Examiners reward this.
> 3. **Wrong SIL boundary** — A PFD of $1.2 \times 10^{-2}$ is **SIL 1**, not SIL 2. A PFD of $9.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is **SIL 2**. The boundary is *inclusive* on the higher-reliability side. Read the inequality carefully.
> 4. **Not showing the series addition** — For overall SIF PFD, you must write out: $PFD_{total} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{valve}$. A bare answer without the formula loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring units / scientific notation** — Write PFD values in standard form (e.g., $4 \times 10^{-3}$, not 0.004).
> 6. **Not drawing a block diagram** — A 7-mark sub-part in Part B almost always requires a labeled diagram. Skipping it = automatic deduction of 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 "Definition"**

- ✅ **SIS** = Engineered system (sensors + logic solver + final element) that takes the process to a safe state on demand.
- ✅ SIS is the **last automatic engineered protection layer** (Layer 4 in LOPA).
- ✅ **Hazard** = potential to cause harm. **Risk** = probability × severity. **Safety** = freedom from *unacceptable* risk.
- ✅ **SIF** = one specific safety function (e.g., close valve XV-101 on high pressure).
- ✅ **Safe State** = pre-defined safe condition of the process.
- ✅ **Fail-Safe** = on loss of power/signal, component goes to safe position.
- ✅ SIS must be **independent** of BPCS.
- ✅ Modes: **Low-demand** (use $PFD_{avg}$, SIL 1–4). **High-demand/continuous** (use $PFH$, SIL 1–3).
- ✅ **SIL ranges (low-demand):** SIL 1: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 2: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$, SIL 3: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$, SIL 4: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$.
- ✅ **Overall SIF PFD** = sum of sensor + logic + final element PFDs (series architecture).
- ✅ **RRF** = $1 / PFD_{avg}$.
- ✅ **Process Safety Time** must exceed the total SIS response time: $t_{SIS} < t_{PST}$.
- ✅ Key standards: **IEC 61508** (umbrella), **IEC 61511** (process industry), **ISA 84.01** (US equivalent).
- ✅ **LOPA** = Layer of Protection Analysis — used to determine target SIL for the SIS.
- ✅ Always cite the **standard reference** (IEC 61511 / IEC 61508) in your answers.
- ✅ Always **draw a labeled block diagram** for 7-mark sub-parts.
- ✅ Always show **formula + substitution + final answer** in PFD / SIL numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a specially engineered, independent control system designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined, unsafe operating conditions are detected, thereby protecting personnel, environment, and assets from the consequences of hazardous events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEC 61511 Standard):** "An instrumented system used to implement one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) to achieve or maintain a safe state for the process under control."

The SIS operates as the **last layer of automated defense** and is fundamentally different from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS). The BPCS maintains the process *at* the desired operating point, whereas the SIS acts *only* when the process deviates into an unsafe region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airbag Analogy"

Imagine driving a modern car at **80 km/h**:

- The **accelerator and steering wheel** (BPCS) keep the car on the road, at the right speed, navigating traffic.
- The **seatbelt** is a passive, procedural safeguard.
- The **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** is an active safeguard (alarm + operator action).
- The **Airbag** is the SIS — it does nothing during **99.99%** of the drive and only deploys in the *fraction of a second* when a crash is detected.

Likewise, an SIS in a refinery, chemical plant, or nuclear facility sits silently in the background. It performs **no economic control function**. It waits, monitors, and when a dangerous deviation (overpressure, overtemperature, gas leak) is detected, it executes a pre-defined **emergency shutdown (ESD)** action.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Introduced in Module 1

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hazard** | A physical situation with potential for human injury, asset damage, or environmental harm. |
| **Risk** | A measure of the combination (frequency × consequence) of a hazardous event. |
| **Safety** | Freedom from unacceptable risk. |
| **SIF** | Safety Instrumented Function — a single function performed by the SIS to mitigate a specific hazard. |
| **SIL** | Safety Integrity Level — discrete levels (1, 2, 3, 4) defining the required risk reduction. |
| **PFD** | Probability of Failure on Demand — the likelihood that the SIS fails when called upon. |
| **RRF** | Risk Reduction Factor — the inverse of PFD. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU Module 1, the student must clearly distinguish between **Safety** and **Reliability**. A system can be highly *reliable* (low failure rate) but still *unsafe* (failure leads to severe consequences). Safety is a *risk-based* concept, not a pure availability concept.

## 1.4 Why Safety Instrumentation? — The Engineering Imperative

The catastrophic industrial disasters of the 20th century forced the engineering community to formalize safety:

- **Flixborough Disaster, UK (1974)** — Nylon caprolactam plant exploded due to a temporary bypass pipe failure; **28 fatalities**.
- **Bhopal Gas Tragedy, India (1984)** — Methyl isocyanate leak from a Union Carbide plant; **thousands of fatalities**.
- **Texas City Refinery, USA (2005)** — Raffinate splitter explosion; **15 fatalities, 170+ injuries**.

These events led directly to standards like **IEC 61508 (1998)** and **IEC 61511 (2003)**, which form the backbone of modern functional safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A BPCS that performs a control function *cannot* be used as the sole layer of safety, because its failure mode is the same as the initiating event. **Independence** between BPCS and SIS is a *non-negotiable* design principle under IEC 61511.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Functional Safety Triad

Functional safety is built upon three integrated pillars:

1. **Identification of the Hazard** — HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study), FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis), or What-If Analysis.
2. **Determination of the Risk** — quantified using event frequency × consequence severity.
3. **Reduction of the Risk** — achieved through a **Layer of Protection Architecture (LOPA)** until residual risk is "as low as reasonably practicable" (ALARP).

## 2.2 The Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)

LOPA is a semi-quantitative methodology used to evaluate the effectiveness of each protective layer between the process and the catastrophic event. Each layer is assumed to be **independent** (a failure in one layer does not affect another).

| Layer | Description | Typical PFD Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Layer 1** | Process Design (high pressure rating, relief devices) | $\geq 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 2** | Basic Process Control System (BPCS) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 3** | Operator Intervention via Alarm & Human Action | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 4** | Safety Instrumented System (SIS) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| **Layer 5** | Physical Protection (bunds, dikes, blast walls) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 6** | Community Emergency Response (fire brigade) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |

The **objective** of LOPA is to determine whether the cumulative probability of the unwanted event, after all IPLs, is below a target tolerable frequency.

## 2.3 Core Mathematical Framework

### 2.3.1 Frequency of an Unwanted Event (LOPA Equation)

$$F_{final} = F_{initiating} \times PFD_{BPCS} \times PFD_{Alarm} \times PFD_{SIS} \times PFD_{Physical} \times \ldots$$

Where:

- $F_{initiating}$ = frequency of the initiating event (per year).
- $PFD_{X}$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of layer X.

### 2.3.2 Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$$

### 2.3.3 Required Risk Reduction (Tolerable Risk)

The target PFD for the SIS is computed by:

$$PFD_{SIS}^{required} = \frac{F_{tolerable}}{F_{initiating} \times PFD_{other\,IPLs}}$$

### 2.3.4 SIL Mapping to PFD (IEC 61511)

| SIL | PFD Range (Demand Mode) | RRF Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10 to 100 |
| **2** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100 to 1 000 |
| **3** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 1 000 to 10 000 |
| **4** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10 000 to 100 000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Demand mode operation (low/medium demand) uses PFD. Continuous mode (high demand) uses PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour). The $10^{-5}$ lower bound for SIL 4 reflects practical hardware limitations.

## 2.4 Components of a Safety Instrumented System

A complete SIF chain consists of three sub-systems, all contributing to the overall PFD:

1. **Sensor Subsystem** — detects the process deviation (pressure transmitter, level switch, gas detector).
2. **Logic Solver** — typically a Safety PLC, sometimes a hardwired relay system. Receives sensor input, executes the safety logic, and generates output.
3. **Final Element** — executes the safety action (isolation valve, circuit breaker, dump valve, fire suppression).

$$\boxed{PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{LogicSolver} + PFD_{FinalElement}}$$

This is a **simplified approximation** valid only for low PFDs. The rigorous formula is:

$$PFD_{SIF} = 1 - (1 - PFD_S)(1 - PFD_L)(1 - PFD_{FE})$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety Instrumented Systems are mandated in:

- **Oil & Gas** — Offshore platforms, LNG terminals, refineries (e.g., emergency shutdown ESD-0, ESD-1, ESD-2).
- **Chemical Plants** — Overpressure protection, toxic gas detection, boiler protection.
- **Power Plants** — Turbine overspeed trip, nuclear reactor protection systems.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Sterilization, fermentation process containment.
- **Transportation** — Railway signaling (ATP — Automatic Train Protection), aviation (TCAS).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Tolerable Frequency to Required SIL

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question pattern**. Let us walk through the full LOPA calculation.

### Problem Setup

A hydrocarbon separator operates at 5 barg. The relief valve (Layer 1) is inspected yearly. The BPCS pressure controller (Layer 2) has a $PFD = 10^{-1}$. Operator response to a high-pressure alarm (Layer 3) has a $PFD = 10^{-1}$. The company has set a tolerable event frequency of $10^{-5}$ per year. The initiating event "blockage of outlet line" occurs with frequency $F_{init} = 0.1$ per year.

**Determine the required SIL for the SIF.**

### Step 1 — Write the LOPA Frequency Equation

We model the chain of independent protection layers as a product of probabilities. The frequency reaching the SIF is:

$$F_{SIF} = F_{init} \times PFD_{RV} \times PFD_{BPCS} \times PFD_{Alarm}$$

Plug in numerical values:

$$F_{SIF} = 0.1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1}$$

Group the powers of ten:

$$F_{SIF} = 0.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

Convert the $0.1$ into a power of ten for uniform handling:

$$0.1 = 10^{-1}$$

So:

$$F_{SIF} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Required PFD of the SIF

The remaining tolerable risk must be absorbed by the SIF:

$$PFD_{SIF}^{required} = \frac{F_{tolerable}}{F_{SIF}}$$

Substitute:

$$PFD_{SIF}^{required} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-4}} = 10^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Map to the SIL Table

Compare $PFD_{SIF}^{required} = 10^{-1}$ to the SIL bands:

| SIL | PFD Range | Match? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SIL 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | ✓ Yes |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | ✗ No |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | ✗ No |

**The required SIF must be designed to at least SIL 1.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Composite PFD for Redundant Sensors (1oo2 Architecture)

In a 1-out-of-2 voting, **either** sensor can trip the SIF. The system fails to danger only if **both** sensors fail dangerously at the same time (within the proof test interval). For **identical** sensors with failure rate $\lambda_D$ and proof test interval $T_i$:

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \lambda_{D}^{2} \times \frac{T_{i}^{2}}{3}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{3}$ comes from integrating the unavailability over the test interval (triangular distribution assumption for low demand). The full derivation:

**Step 1** — Unavailability of a single sensor over interval $[0, T_i]$:

$$U(t) = \lambda_D \cdot t$$

**Step 2** — Average unavailability (integrate and divide by $T_i$):

$$\overline{U} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i}\lambda_D t\, dt = \frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}$$

**Step 3** — For 1oo2 voting, both must fail. Assuming independent failures:

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \overline{U}^2 = \left(\frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\lambda_D^{2}\, T_i^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 4** — IEC 61508 simplified formula introduces a factor of $\frac{4}{3}$ for proof test coverage (typical 100% coverage gives the $1/3$ form in the standard's archive):

$$PFD_{1oo2} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^{2}\, T_i^{2}}{3}$$

This is the formula used in IEC 61508 Tables A.5 to A.14.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The factor $\frac{1}{3}$ is **NOT** $\frac{1}{2}$ as students often misremember. The $\frac{1}{2}$ applies to *single* channel PFD, while the redundancy-derived factor becomes $\frac{1}{3}$ after integrating for two channels with an additional multiplier for proof test imperfections.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common SIS Architectures

| Architecture | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** (single channel) | $PFD = \lambda_D T_i / 2$ | Lowest hardware cost, highest PFD. |
| **1oo2** (parallel) | $PFD \approx \lambda_D^{2} T_i^{2} / 3$ | Higher availability, used for SIL 2/3. |
| **2oo2** (parallel) | $PFD \approx \lambda_D T_i / 2$ | Same as 1oo1 in PFD but eliminates spurious trips. |
| **2oo3** (two-out-of-three voted) | $PFD \approx \lambda_D^{2} T_i^{2} / 3$ | High integrity + high availability; used in critical boilers and turbines. |
| **1oo2D** (with diagnostics) | $PFD \approx \lambda_{DU}^{2} T_i^{2} / 3$ | Diagnostics cut *dangerous undetected* failure rate. |

Where $\lambda_{DU}$ is the *dangerous undetected* failure rate (much smaller than $\lambda_D$ when diagnostics are in place).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Hazard Analysis PHA] --> B[Risk Assessment and SIL Determination]
    B --> C[SIF Design and SIL Verification]
    C --> D[Installation and Commissioning]
    D --> E[Operation and Maintenance]
    E --> F[Proof Testing and Modification]
    F --> G[Decommissioning]
    G --> H[End of Life Audit]

    A:::phaseNode
    B:::phaseNode
    C:::phaseNode
    D:::phaseNode
    E:::phaseNode
    F:::phaseNode
    G:::phaseNode
    H:::phaseNode

    classDef phaseNode fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Layer of Protection Architecture (LOPA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Variables Pressure Temperature Flow Level] --> L1
    L1[L1 Process Design IPL] --> L2
    L2[L2 Basic Process Control System BPCS] --> L3
    L3[L3 Operator Alarm and Intervention] --> L4
    L4[L4 Safety Instrumented System SIS] --> L5
    L5[L5 Physical Protection Bunds Blast Walls] --> L6
    L6[L6 Community Emergency Response] --> O[Final Consequence Reduced to Tolerable]

    I1[Initiating Event Blockage Pump Failure Valve Stuck]:::initEvent --> P1

    classDef initEvent fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Single Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Pressure Sensor PIT-100] --> S1[1001 Redundancy Voter]
    TS[Temperature Sensor TIT-200] --> S1
    S1 --> LS[Logic Solver Safety PLC SIL 3 Certified]
    LS --> V1[Final Element Emergency Shutdown Valve ESDV-001]
    LS --> V2[Final Element Trip Signal to Compressor]
    V1 --> AO[Achieve Safe State Isolated Depressurized Section]

    PS:::sensorNode
    TS:::sensorNode
    S1:::logicNode
    LS:::logicNode
    V1:::finalNode
    V2:::finalNode
    AO:::safeStateNode

    classDef sensorNode fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef logicNode fill:#a16207,stroke:#713f12,color:#ffffff
    classDef finalNode fill:#9d174d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff
    classDef safeStateNode fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — BPCS vs SIS Functional Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Basic Process Control System Layer]
        B1[DCS Controller Regulates Pressure to 5.0 barg]
        B2[Operator HMI Displays Trends]
    end

    subgraph SAFE[Safety Instrumented System Layer]
        S1[SIS Logic Solver Monitors Independently]
        S2[SIS Pressure Transmitter Separate from BPCS]
        S3[SIS Final Element Shutoff Valve]
    end

    PROC[Process Reactor] --> B1
    PROC --> S1
    B1 --> PROC
    S1 --> S3
    S3 --> PROC

    B1:::bpcsNode
    S1:::sisNode
    S3:::sisNode

    classDef bpcsNode fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef sisNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** In the KTU board exam, students are expected to clearly draw a **functional block diagram** of an SIS showing sensors → logic solver → final element, with separate independence between BPCS and SIS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**. How is it functionally different from a Basic Process Control System (BPCS)?

**Model Answer:**
A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is an engineered control system designed to take a process to a safe state when unsafe operating conditions are detected, thereby protecting personnel, environment, and equipment. As per IEC 61511, an SIS implements one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs).

The functional differences from a BPCS are:

1. **Purpose:** BPCS maintains the process at a desired operating setpoint (e.g., flow = 100 L/min). SIS brings the process to a safe state only when a hazardous deviation is detected.
2. **Independence:** IEC 61511 mandates that the SIS must be **physically, electrically, and functionally independent** from the BPCS. A common-mode failure affecting the BPCS must not affect the SIS.
3. **Operation Mode:** BPCS operates continuously in closed-loop; SIS is dormant and acts only on-demand (or continuously for very high SIL functions).
4. **Priority:** In the event of a conflict, the **SIS always overrides** the BPCS. This is a mandatory design rule.
5. **Failure Philosophy:** BPCS failure is *degraded production*; SIS failure is *catastrophic* and demands proof testing.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** List and briefly explain the **four levels of Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** as defined by IEC 61511.

**Model Answer:**
IEC 61511 defines four discrete SILs (1 to 4), each corresponding to a specific Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) range when the SIF operates in low/medium demand mode:

- **SIL 1** — $PFD$ between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$. Achieved by a single, well-proven sensor and valve loop. Used for low-consequence hazards.
- **SIL 2** — $PFD$ between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$. Requires redundancy (typically 1oo2 sensors) and certified Safety PLCs. Most common in oil & gas.
- **SIL 3** — $PFD$ between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-4}$. Demands 1oo2D or 2oo3 voting, extensive diagnostics, and high proof-test coverage.
- **SIL 4** — $PFD$ between $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-5}$. Used in critical applications (e.g., nuclear reactor shutdown). Hardware implementation becomes extremely stringent.

The higher the SIL, the lower the tolerable probability of dangerous failure, and hence the greater the engineering rigor and cost.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — University Question Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** methodology in detail. Discuss any **four** independent protection layers (IPLs) commonly used in a chemical process plant with their typical PFD values.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A reactor has the following protection layers:
- Initiating event frequency: **0.5 per year**
- BPCS: $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
- Operator alarm: $PFD = 10^{-1}$
- Relief valve: $PFD = 10^{-2}$

The tolerable event frequency is $10^{-5}$ per year. Determine the **required SIL** for the SIF.

#### Model Solution for Q. A(a)

**Step 1 — Define LOPA:**
LOPA is a semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology defined in IEC 61511. It evaluates each protection layer as an Independent Protection Layer (IPL) that mitigates the consequence of a single initiating event. The risk of the unwanted consequence is computed as a product of the initiating event frequency and the PFD of each IPL.

**Step 2 — List four common IPLs and their typical PFDs:**

1. **Process Design (Layer 1):** Inherent safety achieved through high-pressure ratings, oversized vessels, and material selection. Typical $PFD$ in the range of $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$.
2. **Basic Process Control System (Layer 2):** The DCS controller acting as a continuous control function that responds to deviations. Typical $PFD$ around $10^{-1}$.
3. **Safety Instrumented System (Layer 4):** Independent, dedicated hardware that performs emergency shutdown. Typical $PFD$ from $10^{-2}$ down to $10^{-5}$ depending on SIL.
4. **Physical Protection (Layer 5):** Dikes, bunds, blast walls, and drainage systems. Typical $PFD$ around $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$.

**Step 3 — Conclude:**
The product of all IPL PFDs, combined with the initiating event frequency, must be less than the tolerable event frequency. If not, additional layers or stricter SILs are mandated.

#### Model Solution for Q. A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the frequency reaching the SIF:**

$$F_{SIF} = F_{init} \times PFD_{BPCS} \times PFD_{Alarm} \times PFD_{RV}$$

Substitute values:

$$F_{SIF} = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}$$

Compute step by step:

$$F_{SIF} = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{SIF} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required PFD of the SIF:**

$$PFD_{SIF}^{required} = \frac{F_{tolerable}}{F_{SIF}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.4$$

In scientific notation:

$$PFD_{SIF}^{required} \approx 4 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Map to SIL bands:**

| SIL | PFD Range | Match? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SIL 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | ✓ Yes ($0.4 = 4 \times 10^{-1}$ falls in this range) |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | ✗ No |

**Conclusion:** The SIF must be designed to at least **SIL 1**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Safety Instrumented System**. Distinguish clearly between the **sensor subsystem**, **logic solver**, and **final element**. State two examples of each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 1oo2 redundant sensor subsystem uses identical pressure transmitters with a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T_i = 1$ year (8760 hours). Compute the PFD of the sensor subsystem.

#### Model Solution for Q. B(a)
