**Step 1 — Draw the functional architecture:**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3. The architecture consists of three sequential sub-systems:
**Sensors → Logic Solver → Final Element**.

**Step 2 — Explain each sub-system with examples:**

1. **Sensor Subsystem** — Detects the unsafe condition and converts it into a signal. Two examples:
   - Rosemount 3051 pressure transmitter (used for overpressure detection).
   - Drager Polytron gas detector (used for toxic/hydrocarbon leak detection).

2. **Logic Solver** — Receives the sensor input, executes the safety logic (typically defined in a function block diagram or ladder logic), and issues a trip command. Two examples:
   - Honeywell Safety Manager (SIL 3 certified).
   - Siemens SIMATIC S7 F-CPU (Failsafe).

3. **Final Element** — Physically performs the safe-state action. Two examples:
   - Emerson Bettis pneumatic ESD valve (closes on air failure — *air-to-open, spring-to-close*).
   - Schneider Masterpact circuit breaker (trips on loss of trip signal).

**Step 3 — Conclude:**
The independence of these three sub-systems is mandated by IEC 61511. They must use **separate power supplies, separate I/O cards, and separate physical wiring paths**.

#### Model Solution for Q. B(b)

**Step 1 — Recall the 1oo2 PFD formula:**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_D^{2} T_i^{2}}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$

$$T_i = 8760 \text{ hours} = 8.76 \times 10^{3} \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\lambda_D^2$:**

$$\lambda_D^{2} = (5 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 25 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T_i^2$:**

$$T_i^{2} = (8.76 \times 10^{3})^{2} = 76.7376 \times 10^{6} = 7.67376 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply and divide by 3:**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 7.67376 \times 10^{7}}{3}$$

Numerator:

$$2.5 \times 7.67376 = 19.1844$$

Powers of ten: $10^{-11} \times 10^{7} = 10^{-4}$.

So numerator $= 19.1844 \times 10^{-4} = 1.91844 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{1.91844 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.3948 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Compare to SIL bands:**

$6.4 \times 10^{-4}$ lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-4}$, hence the sensor subsystem alone meets **SIL 3** requirements.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Safety Instrumentation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{3}$ factor in 1oo2 PFD** — Many students write $\frac{1}{2}$ or $\frac{1}{4}$. The IEC 61508 archived formula uses $\frac{1}{3}$ when $T_i$ is in hours and $\lambda_D$ is in failures/hour.
> 2. **Mixing BPCS and SIS failure rates** — A BPCS $PFD$ should never be added to the SIS $PFD$. They are *independent* layers; you multiply, not add.
> 3. **Confusing PFD and PFH** — PFD (dimensionless, 0 to 1) is for demand mode. PFH (per hour) is for continuous mode. Writing "$PFD = 10^{-6}$ per hour" is a dimensional error that costs marks.
> 4. **Ignoring independence requirement** — In a 14-mark question, if a student proposes using the *same* transmitter for BPCS and SIS, the answer violates IEC 61511 and may lose up to 3 marks for non-compliance.
> 5. **Not stating proof test interval** — A PFD calculation without a stated $T_i$ is incomplete. Always specify the test interval (typically 1 year, 2 years, or 5 years).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision — Module 1: Introduction**

- **SIS Definition:** Independent system designed to bring a process to a *safe state*; it does **not** perform regulatory control.
- **BPCS vs SIS:** BPCS controls, SIS protects. SIS has absolute priority. Independence is mandatory.
- **Hazard vs Risk:** Hazard is the *source* of harm; Risk is *probability × consequence*.
- **LOPA:** A semi-quantitative risk reduction methodology. Each Independent Protection Layer (IPL) is assumed to be **independent and auditable**.
- **PFD Equation:** $PFD = \lambda_D T_i / 2$ for a single channel; $PFD = \lambda_D^{2} T_i^{2} / 3$ for 1oo2.
- **RRF:** The inverse of PFD. A SIF with PFD = $10^{-3}$ has RRF = 1000.
- **SIL Bands:** SIL 1 ($10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$), SIL 4 ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$).
- **SIF Architecture:** Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element. Each sub-system has its own PFD contribution.
- **Standards Hierarchy:** IEC 61508 (umbrella standard for *all* industries) → IEC 61511 (process industry specific).
- **LOPA Frequency Equation:** $F_{final} = F_{init} \times \prod PFD_{IPL}$.
- **Key Independence Rule:** No shared I/O, no shared power, no shared logic solver between BPCS and SIS.
- **Proof Testing:** Required periodically; the *shorter* the test interval, the *lower* the PFD.
- **1oo2 vs 2oo2:** 1oo2 improves safety (lower PFD), 2oo2 improves availability (lower spurious trip rate). 2oo3 is the optimal balance.
- **Voting Notation:** "1ooN" means 1 out of N channels required to trip. Always read carefully.
- **Air-to-Open / Spring-to-Close Valves:** The standard for ESD valves — fail-safe on loss of air/power.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZARD (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 Definition):**
> A **Hazard** is any *source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm* in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. In the specific context of Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), a hazard is a physical condition or chemical state inherent to a process plant (e.g., over-pressure, over-temperature, toxic release) that can cause an undesirable event if not properly mitigated.

**Key Vocabulary Anchors:**
- **Hazard** → *Source of potential harm* (intrinsic property)
- **Risk** → *Combination of probability of occurrence and severity of harm* (a function of hazard + exposure)
- **Top Event** → The central undesired event in a fault tree (e.g., reactor rupture)
- **Initiating Event** → The first event in a sequence that leads to a hazard realization
- **Consequence** → The ultimate outcome (injury, fatality, environmental release, financial loss)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Fire" Analogy:**
> Think of your kitchen at home. The **gas cylinder connected to the stove** is a hazard. It has the *potential* to cause harm. The **leaking gas smell** is the *initiating event*. If you light a match (the *trigger*), an explosion occurs (the *consequence*).
> 
> Now, the **probability** that the stove is currently leaking AND someone strikes a match determines your **risk**.
> - Hazard = "What can hurt you" (the gas)
> - Risk = "How likely and how bad" (Probability × Severity)
> 
> **In an oil refinery**, the hazard is a high-pressure hydrocarbon line. The risk depends on: Is the line near a furnace? Is there a flare nearby? What is the population density around the plant?

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceptable Individual Risk (AIR)** | **10⁻⁵ per year** (public), **10⁻³ per year** (worker) | UK HSE / IEC 61511 |
| **Tolerable Hazard Rate (for SIL-1)** | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵ per hour** | IEC 61511 Table 3 |
| **Tolerable Hazard Rate (for SIL-2)** | **10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶ per hour** | IEC 61511 Table 3 |
| **Tolerable Hazard Rate (for SIL-3)** | **10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁷ per hour** | IEC 61511 Table 3 |
| **Tolerable Hazard Rate (for SIL-4)** | **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁸ per hour** | IEC 61511 Table 3 |
| **F-N Curve (Fatality)** | Limits slope: −1 (intolerable), −2 (acceptable) | UK HSE R2P2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In every KTU answer where you mention *risk reduction*, you MUST cite the appropriate SIL level with its corresponding tolerable hazard rate band. Examiners award 2 bonus marks for students who cross-link SIL to numerical risk reduction factor (RRF).

---

## 1.4 The Hazard Triangle (Process Industry Framework)

Every hazard evaluation in a Safety Instrumented System follows this triadic structure:

$$ \text{Risk} = f(\text{Hazard}, \text{Exposure}, \text{Vulnerability}) $$

The hazard *exists* whether or not it is realized. The **risk** emerges only when the hazard interacts with an exposed, vulnerable target.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hazards in Process Industries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Anchor:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Classify hazards with examples in a process plant."* (3-mark / 7-mark question). Master the categories below.

### A. Physical Hazards
| Sub-Type | Engineering Example | Plant Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Rotating shafts, unguarded gears, high-speed centrifuges | Pump stations, compressor sheds |
| **Pressure** | Boiler rupture, vessel implosion | Steam headers, vacuum columns |
| **Temperature** | Cryogenic LNG burns, molten metal splashes | Heat exchangers, cryogenic tanks |
| **Electrical** | Arc flash, short-circuit, static discharge | MCC rooms, switchgear |
| **Noise** | Compressor stations > 85 dB(A) | Compressor sheds, flare areas |
| **Radiation** | Gamma from NDT, UV from welding | Inspection zones |
| **Falls / Height** | Working at height > 1.8 m | Tall distillation columns |

### B. Chemical Hazards
- **Toxic** — H₂S, benzene, phosgene, chlorine (TWA / STEL limits apply)
- **Reactive** — Pyrophoric (iron sulfide), water-reactive (sodium), oxidizers (H₂O₂)
- **Flammable** — Hydrocarbons in FCC units, LPG bullets
- **Corrosive** — Concentrated H₂SO₄, HF alkylation units

### C. Process / Operational Hazards
- **Overpressure / Underpressure** (blocked outlet, thermal expansion)
- **Overtemperature** (cooling failure, runaway exotherm)
- **Overfill / Underfill** (level measurement failure)
- **Loss of Utilities** (loss of instrument air, loss of cooling water, loss of electrical power)
- **Reverse Flow** (check-valve failure)
- **Phase Change** (hydrate formation, two-phase flow)

### D. Environmental Hazards
- Atmospheric release, soil contamination, aquatic discharge, ozone-depleting emissions

### E. Human / Ergonomic Hazards
- Operator error, complacency, fatigue, poor human-machine interface (HMI) design

---

## 2.2 Hazard Identification Techniques (The 5 Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> **For 14-mark KTU questions**, examiners expect you to describe **at least 3 techniques** in depth, with a comparative table and an application scenario.

### Technique 1: HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)
- **Origin:** ICI, UK — 1960s
- **Standard:** IEC 61882
- **Method:** Apply **Guide Words** to each node (pipe / vessel) and each process variable.
- **Guide Words:** *No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than*
- **Variables:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Time
- **Output:** Node-by-node deviation → Cause → Consequence → Safeguard → Action

### Technique 2: FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- **Standard:** IEC 60812
- **Method:** Bottom-up — for each component, list all *failure modes*, then trace to *effects on system*.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):**

$$ \text{RPN} = S \times O \times D $$

where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection (each on a 1–10 scale).

### Technique 3: What-If Analysis
- **Standard:** IEEE 1028 / IEC 300-3-6
- **Method:** Brainstorming — *"What if the cooling water pump fails?"*, *"What if the operator forgets to close valve XV-101?"*
- **Best for:** Early design stage, qualitative screening.

### Technique 4: Checklist Analysis
- **Method:** Use a pre-defined list of known failure scenarios, regulatory requirements, operating limits.
- **Limitation:** Cannot identify *new* hazards not previously encountered.

### Technique 5: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
- **Standard:** IEC 61025
- **Method:** Top-down deductive — start with **Top Event**, apply Boolean logic (**AND / OR gates**) to identify minimal cut sets.
- **Output:** Quantifies probability using reliability data.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Definition** | $R = P \times C$ | Quantify risk from probability $P$ and consequence $C$ |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}}$ | Derive required SIL |
| **Tolerable Hazard Rate vs SIL** | $\text{SIL}_n \Rightarrow f_{t} \in [10^{-(n+5)}, 10^{-(n+6)}]$ | Map SIL to numerical band |
| **RPN (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Rank failure modes |
| **Boolean Probability (AND gate)** | $P_{\text{top,AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | All events must occur |
| **Boolean Probability (OR gate)** | $P_{\text{top,OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$ | At least one event occurs |
| **PFD Average (1oo1)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_D \cdot \frac{T_I}{2}$ | Single-channel probability of failure on demand |
| **PFD Average (1oo2)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^2}{6}$ | Redundant channel; KTU favorite |
| **Architectural Constraint** | $A = n_{\text{accepted}} / n_{\text{total}}$ | IEC 61511 Type A/B classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markup Pitfall:** In PFD equations, the subscript `avg` is often required. Writing `PFD` alone loses 1 mark on the ESE. Always write $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ or $\text{PFD}_{1oo2}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** HAZOP for FCC (Fluidized Catalytic Cracker) units — manages hydrocracker overpressure risk.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FMEA for batch reactors — identifies cross-contamination failure modes.
- **Nuclear:** Fault Tree Analysis for LOCA (Loss of Coolant Accident) — top event with AND/OR logic.
- **Chemical:** What-If for novel R&D processes where no prior checklist exists.
- **Oil & Gas:** Checklists for brownfield modifications to existing offshore platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boolean Probability for a Fault Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** This derivation is a KTU-favorite 14-mark question — *"Compute the top-event probability of a fault tree given basic event probabilities."*

**Given Fault Tree Structure:**
- Top Event $T$ is achieved if **Event A AND Event B** occur, OR if **Event C** alone occurs.
- $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.02$, $P(C) = 0.005$ (per demand)

**Step 1 — Identify the logical structure.**

The Boolean equation for the top event is:

$$ T = (A \cap B) \cup C $$

**Step 2 — Convert Boolean logic to probability using the inclusion-exclusion theorem.**

For two events $X$ and $Y$, the probability of their union is:

$$ P(X \cup Y) = P(X) + P(Y) - P(X \cap Y) $$

**Step 3 — Apply the AND operation.**

For two statistically independent basic events $A$ and $B$:

$$ P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B) $$

$$ P(A \cap B) = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the OR (union) operation.**

$$ P(T) = P(A \cap B) + P(C) - P((A \cap B) \cap C) $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the joint intersection term.**

Because $A$, $B$, $C$ are mutually independent:

$$ P((A \cap B) \cap C) = P(A) \times P(B) \times P(C) $$

$$ P((A \cap B) \cap C) = 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.005 = 1 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Step 6 — Final substitution.**

$$ P(T) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 5 \times 10^{-3} - 1 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ P(T) = 5.2 \times 10^{-3} - 1 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ P(T) \approx 5.199 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

The top-event occurs approximately **5.2 times in 1000 demands**. To meet a SIL-2 target ($f_t \leq 10^{-6}$), the safety function must reduce this probability by a factor of:

$$ \text{RRF} = \frac{5.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 5200 $$

This requires a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) operating at SIL-3 or higher, **or** a combination of independent protection layers (IPL).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFD Calculation for 1oo1 and 1oo2 Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A 7-mark KTU question often gives a $\lambda_D$ and $T_I$, asking you to compute PFD and identify the SIL.

### Derivation A — 1oo1 (Single Channel)

**Assumption:** The single channel has dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and is tested every $T_I$ hours.

**Step 1 — Define unreliability $R(t)$ over the test interval.**

For an exponential failure distribution, the probability that the channel has failed within time $t$ is:

$$ R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} $$

**Step 2 — Approximate for small $\lambda_D t$.**

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$ R(t) \approx \lambda_D t $$

**Step 3 — Average over the test interval $[0, T_I]$.**

The average unavailability is:

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_I} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_I} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I^2}{2 \cdot T_I} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}} $$

### Derivation B — 1oo2 (Two Parallel Channels)

**Step 1 — For 1oo2, both channels must fail to cause system failure.**

$$ R_{\text{system}}(t) \approx (\lambda_D t)^2 $$

**Step 2 — Average over the test interval.**

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{\lambda_D^2}{T_I} \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_I} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^3}{3 \cdot T_I} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^2}{6}} $$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Favorite)

**Given:** $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /h, $T_I = 8760$ h (1 year)

**Compute PFD for 1oo1:**

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219 $$

Since $\text{PFD} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$ → This corresponds to **SIL-1**.

**Compute PFD for 1oo2:**

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 8760^2}{6} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.67 \times 10^{7}}{6} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-3}}{6} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx 3.2 \times 10^{-4} $$

Since $\text{PFD} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$ → This corresponds to **SIL-2**.

**Engineering Insight:** Adding a redundant channel (1oo2) improved the SIL by one level — a direct demonstration of how redundancy lowers hazard rate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Hazard Identification Method

| Technique | Stage of Project | Type of Analysis | Quantitative? | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Checklist** | Operating / Detailed Design | Screening | No | Brownfield verification | Cannot find novel hazards |
| **What-If** | Concept / Pre-FEED | Screening | No | Brainstorming unknowns | Highly qualitative |
| **HAZOP** | Detailed Design / Pre-Op | Comprehensive | Semi | Continuous process plants | Time-consuming |
| **FMEA** | Detailed Design / Equipment | Comprehensive | Yes (RPN) | Mechanical / hardware systems | Component-bounded |
| **FTA** | Detailed Design / Operations | Comprehensive | Yes | Complex systems with many causes | Requires reliability data |
| **Bow-Tie** | Operations | Visualization | Yes | Linking cause to consequence | Needs HAZOP/FTA input first |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Hazard Control (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ELIMINATION\nMost Effective\nRemove the hazard entirely"] --> nodeB["SUBSTITUTION\nReplace with less hazardous material or process"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ENGINEERING CONTROLS\nPhysical barriers, SIS, relief valves, interlocks"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS\nProcedures, training, signage, permits"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PPE\nLeast Effective\nHard hats, gloves, respirators"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["RESIDUAL RISK\nUnavoidable risk remaining after all controls"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 7-mark question asks: *"Explain the hierarchy of hazard control with a diagram."* Memorize the order: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**. PPE is the *last* resort, not the first.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hazard Identification → Risk Reduction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["STEP 1\nProcess Definition\nDefine plant scope,\noperating limits"] --> nodeB["STEP 2\nHazard Identification\nHAZOP / What-If /\nChecklist"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3\nConsequence Analysis\nEstimate severity of\neach scenario"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4\nLOPA Analysis\nLayer of Protection\nAnalysis"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5\nSIL Determination\nAssign SIL-1 to SIL-4"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6\nSIS Design\nPFD calculation\narchitecture selection"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 7\nVerification\nValidate risk meets\ntolerable criteria"]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bow-Tie Representation of a Process Hazard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["THREAT SIDE\nCauses"] --> nodeB["TOP EVENT\nHazard Realization"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CONSEQUENCE SIDE\nEffects"]
    
    nodeD["Control: Process\nDesign"] -.->|"Prevention\nLayer 1"| nodeA
    nodeE["Control: BPCS\nAlarm"] -.->|"Prevention\nLayer 2"| nodeA
    nodeF["Control: Operator\nIntervention"] -.->|"Prevention\nLayer 3"| nodeA
    nodeG["Control: Safety\nInstrumented\nFunction SIF"] -.->|"Prevention\nLayer 4"| nodeA
    
    nodeH["Mitigation:\nBund, dyke"] -.->|"Mitigation\nLayer 1"| nodeC
    nodeI["Mitigation:\nFire Fighting"] -.->|"Mitigation\nLayer 2"| nodeC
    nodeJ["Mitigation:\nEmergency\nShutdown ESD"] -.->|"Mitigation\nLayer 3"| nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **Bow-Tie** is a powerful visualization used in KTU ESE Module 1 because it directly maps IEC 61511's concept of *Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)*. Each control must be: (a) Independent, (b) Auditable, (c) Effective.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Fault Tree for Overpressure in a Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["TOP EVENT\nReactor Overpressure\nLeads to Rupture"] 
    
    nodeA --- nodeOR1["OR GATE"]
    nodeOR1 --- nodeAND1["AND GATE\nBoth Required"]
    nodeOR1 --- nodeE1["Basic Event E1\nCooling Water Failure\nP = 0.01"]
    nodeOR1 --- nodeE2["Basic Event E2\nPressure Relief Valve Stuck\nP = 0.005"]
    
    nodeAND1 --- nodeE3["Basic Event E3\nOperator Fails to Respond\nP = 0.1"]
    nodeAND1 --- nodeE4["Basic Event E4\nAlarm System Failed\nP = 0.02"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Tree:**
> - **OR Gate:** Top event occurs if *either* branch occurs. Use union formula.
> - **AND Gate:** Top event occurs only if *all* input events occur. Use product formula.
> - **Basic Events:** Circles with deterministic probability values from reliability databases (e.g., OREDA).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a hazard and distinguish it from risk. Cite the IEC 61511 definitions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hazard** is defined by IEC 61511 as *a physical condition or chemical state inherent to a process plant that has the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment*. A hazard is a **source** of potential harm.

**Risk**, in contrast, is a *combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm*. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$ R = P \times C $$

**Key Distinction:**
- Hazard is *intrinsic* — it exists whether or not harm occurs.
- Risk is *functional* — it emerges only when hazard interacts with exposure and vulnerability.

**Example:** A 50 kg LPG cylinder stored at 25 °C is a *hazard*. The risk is low because no ignition source is nearby. If welding work begins beside it, the risk escalates sharply.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six guide words used in a HAZOP study with one example deviation each.**

**Model Answer:**

The HAZOP study (per IEC 61882) uses standard guide words to identify process deviations:

1. **NO / NOT** — *No Flow* (pump stopped, block valve closed) → stagnation, overheating
2. **MORE** — *More Pressure* (blocked outlet, thermal expansion) → vessel overpressure
3. **LESS** — *Less Temperature* (cooling water failure) → loss of reaction control
4. **AS WELL AS** — *As well as water* in oil → corrosion, emulsification
5. **PART OF** — *Part of catalyst missing* → incomplete reaction, impurity buildup
6. **REVERSE** — *Reverse Flow* through check valve → contamination
7. **OTHER THAN** — *Other than expected composition* → unwanted side reaction

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Choose One)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Hazards and HAZOP

**(a) Classify hazards encountered in a process plant with at least 8 examples distributed across categories.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Hazards in process plants are broadly classified into five categories:

**1. Physical Hazards (3 examples):**
- *Mechanical:* Rotating shafts, unguarded machinery → entanglement injuries
- *Pressure:* Boiler overpressure → vessel rupture, projectile hazards
- *Noise:* Compressor stations exceeding 85 dB(A) → permanent hearing loss

**2. Chemical Hazards (2 examples):**
- *Toxic:* H₂S exposure above 10 ppm (8-hr TWA) → loss of consciousness, fatality
- *Reactive:* Pyrophoric iron sulfide in crude tanks → spontaneous combustion

**3. Process / Operational Hazards (2 examples):**
- *Overpressure:* Blocked outlet of heat exchanger → rupture
- *Loss of Utilities:* Loss of instrument air → control valves fail to specified position

**4. Environmental Hazards (1 example):**
- *Atmospheric Release:* VOC emissions → community health impact, regulatory non-compliance

**5. Ergonomic / Human Hazards (1 example):**
- *Operator Fatigue:* 12-hour shifts → higher error probability in alarm response

---

**(b) Describe the HAZOP methodology in detail. Apply it to a centrifugal pump delivering kerosene to a storage tank.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**HAZOP Methodology (IEC 61882):**
1. **Team Formation:** Multi-disciplinary (process, instrumentation, operations, safety, maintenance).
2. **Node Definition:** Divide plant into sections (pipes, vessels, columns).
3. **Select Process Intention:** Document design intent at each node.
4. **Apply Guide Words × Process Variables:** Generate deviations.
5. **Identify Causes:** Why might the deviation occur?
6. **Determine Consequences:** What happens downstream?
7. **Identify Safeguards (IPLs):** Existing protection layers.
8. **Recommend Actions:** New safeguards, alarms, procedures.

**Application — Centrifugal Pump P-101 delivering kerosene to Storage Tank T-201:**

| Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Existing Safeguard | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No Flow** | Pump stopped, suction valve closed | Tank runs dry → cavitation damage | Low-low level trip on tank | Install flow switch with interlock |
| **More Flow** | Bypass valve open, line rupture downstream | Tank overfill → environmental spill | High-high level alarm | Add independent overfill protection SIF (SIL-2) |
| **Less Flow** | Suction strainer chocked | Pump overheating → seal failure | Temperature monitoring | Differential pressure switch on strainer |
| **High Pressure** | Blockage at tank inlet | Pipe rupture, fire hazard | Pressure relief valve | Add pressure transmitter to ESD |
| **Reverse Flow** | Check valve failure | Backflow from tank to pump → pump damage | Existing check valve | Install dual check valves |
| **Other Than (composition)** | Wrong product routed to line | Tank contamination → off-spec product | Sample testing | Inline composition analyzer |

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Fault Tree & Risk

**(a) Construct a fault tree for the top event "Toxic Gas Release from a Chlorine Tank" using at least 6 basic events and a combination of AND/OR gates. Compute the top event probability given the data below.** **[7 Marks]**

**Basic Event Data:**
- $E_1$: Manual valve left open → $P = 0.05$
- $E_2$: Pressure regulator failed → $P = 0.02$
- $E_3$: Excess upstream pressure → $P = 0.01$
- $E_4$: Toxic gas detector failed → $P = 0.04$
- $E_5$: Emergency block valve failed → $P = 0.03$
- $E_6$: Operator failed to act on alarm → $P = 0.10$

**Structure:**
- Top Event $T$ = $G_1 \cup G_2$
- $G_1$ (Primary Release) = $E_1 \cap E_2 \cap E_3$
- $G_2$ (Failure of Mitigation) = $E_4 \cap E_5 \cap E_6$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(G_1)$:**

$$ P(G_1) = P(E_1) \times P(E_2) \times P(E_3) $$

$$ P(G_1) = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(G_2)$:**

$$ P(G_2) = P(E_4) \times P(E_5) \times P(E_6) $$

$$ P(G_2) = 0.04 \times 0.03 \times 0.10 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(T)$ using inclusion-exclusion:**

$$ P(T) = P(G_1) + P(G_2) - P(G_1 \cap G_2) $$

$$ P(T) = 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1.2 \times 10^{-4} - (1 \times 10^{-5})(1.2 \times 10^{-4}) $$

$$ P(T) = 1.3 \times 10^{-4} - 1.2 \times 10^{-9} $$

$$ P(T) \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per demand} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The top event occurs approximately **1.3 times in 10,000 demands**. To meet a typical tolerable target of $f_t = 10^{-6}$ (SIL-2), the safety function must achieve a Risk Reduction Factor:

$$ \text{RRF} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 130 $$

Since RRF > 100, **SIL-2** is the minimum, and depending on consequence severity, **SIL-3** may be warranted.

---

**(b) Differentiate between FMEA and FMECA. With reference to a pressure transmitter in a SIL-2 SIF, calculate the RPN given S=8, O=4, D=3.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**FMEA vs FMECA:**

| Aspect | FMEA | FMECA |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | Failure Mode, Effects, **and Criticality** Analysis |
| **Output** | List of failure modes with effects | FMEA + criticality ranking with quantitative severity |
| **Use of Criticality** | No | Yes — categorizes by severity levels (often I, II, III, IV) |
| **Decision-making** | Qualitative | Quantitative — prioritizes corrective action by criticality number |

**RPN Calculation for Pressure Transmitter:**

The Risk Priority Number (per IEC 60812) is:

$$ \text{RPN} = S \times O \times D $$

Given:
- Severity $S = 8$ (high — affects safety function)
- Occurrence $O = 4$ (low — MTBF ~ 50 years)
- Detection $D = 3$ (moderate — failure detected by automatic diagnostics)

$$ \text{RPN} = 8 \times 4 \times 3 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{RPN} = 96} $$

**Interpretation:**
- RPN > 80 → High priority for corrective action
- Recommendation: Reduce $D$ by adding on-line diagnostics; reduce $S$ by routing the signal redundantly (1oo2 voting); reduce $O$ by selecting higher-quality transmitter with proven field reliability.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in KTU ESE Module 1 (Hazards):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing hazard with risk** — Examiner explicitly allocates 2 marks for *correct distinction*. Writing "hazard means loss of life" loses both marks.
> 
> 2. **Missing the numerical SIL link** — Whenever you discuss *risk reduction*, you MUST state the corresponding PFD band (e.g., $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$). A qualitative statement like "it reduces risk" earns partial credit only.
> 
> 3. **Fault tree arithmetic errors** — Examiners check AND vs OR formula application. Using $P_A \times P_B$ for an OR gate loses 3 marks immediately.
> 
> 4. **HAZOP incompleteness** — A HAZOP answer must include the *guide words, process variables, causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations*. Skipping any column loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Drawing the fault tree poorly** — Use circles for basic events, rectangles for intermediate events, diamonds for undeveloped events. Drawing them all as boxes → loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting units** — PFD is dimensionless (probability), but PFDavg implies *per hour* integration. State "per demand" or "per hour" explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute night-before-exam revision pass:**

- ✅ **Hazard** = Source of potential harm (intrinsic). **Risk** = Probability × Consequence (functional).
- ✅ IEC 61511 defines four SIL bands: SIL-1 ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$/h), SIL-2 ($10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$/h), SIL-3 ($10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$/h), SIL-4 ($10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$/h).
- ✅ HAZOP guide words: **No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than**.
- ✅ HAZOP variables: **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Time**.
- ✅ FMEA risk metric: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- ✅ FTA Boolean: AND → product; OR → complement of complement ($1 - \prod(1-P_i)$).
- ✅ PFD (1oo1) = $\lambda_D T_I / 2$; PFD (1oo2) = $\lambda_D^2 T_I^2 / 6$.
- ✅ Hierarchy of controls: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.
- ✅ Five hazard categories: **Physical, Chemical, Process, Environmental, Ergonomic**.
- ✅ Bow-Tie diagrams show threats → top event → consequences, with prevention and mitigation IPLs.
- ✅ Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) bridges HAZOP and SIL determination.
- ✅ Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) must be: **Independent, Auditable, Effective, Dependable**.
- ✅ Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) = $f_{\text{unmitigated}} / f_{\text{tolerable}}$.
- ✅ Probability of failure per demand (PFD) is **dimensionless**; per hour ($f_t$) has units of **1/hour**.
- ✅ Tolerance threshold: Public risk $\leq 10^{-5}$/year; Worker risk $\leq 10^{-3}$/year (UK HSE standard).
- ✅ Checklist analysis is **qualitative**; FTA is **quantitative**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Risk (IEC 61511 / KTU Standard Terminology)**
> **Risk** is the *combination of the probability of occurrence of harm (or hazardous event) and the severity of that harm*. Mathematically, it is the expectation of loss, expressed as the product of the frequency of a hazardous event and the consequence (damage) arising from that event.

Formally, as per **IEC 61511** and **ISO 12100**, which form the backbone of the KTU 2024 Functional Safety syllabus:

$$ R = f \times C $$

where $f$ is the frequency of the hazardous event (per year) and $C$ is the consequence (impact in terms of injury, fatality, financial loss, or environmental damage).

The *complementary* concept, **Safety**, is defined as the *freedom from unacceptable risk*. Therefore, a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) does not eliminate risk — it **reduces risk to an acceptable (tolerable) level**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Harm** = physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment.
> **Hazard** = potential source of harm.
> **Hazardous Event** = the moment a hazard is realised (i.e., the initiating cause actually leads to harm).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are crossing a busy highway.

- The **hazard** is the moving traffic.
- The **risk** is the *chance* that you will be hit *and* the *severity* of the injury if you are.
- A pedestrian footbridge (overhead bridge) is the **safety barrier** — it does not remove the hazard (cars still move fast below), but it *reduces the risk* of being hit to near zero.

In the same way, an **Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESD)** in an oil refinery does not remove the hazard of high pressure; it only reduces the **risk** of a catastrophic vessel rupture by bringing the system to a safe state.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Individual Risk (IR) | $IR$ | fatalities per person per year |
| Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) | $FAR$ | fatalities per $10^8$ person-hours exposed |
| Average Loss of Life | $ALL$ | fatalities per year |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF$ | dimensionless ratio |
| Tolerable Risk (worker) | $IR_{worker}$ | $10^{-3}$ fatalities/person/year (HSE, UK) |
| Tolerable Risk (public) | $IR_{public}$ | $10^{-4}$ fatalities/person/year (HSE, UK) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Risk — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 Functional Safety curriculum treats risk as a *composite* quantity built from three orthogonal dimensions:

1. **Severity (Consequence, $C$):** How bad is the harm if the event occurs?
   - Categorical (e.g., Negligible, Marginal, Critical, Catastrophic) **or** Quantitative (e.g., $10^4 USD, 1 fatality, etc.)

2. **Likelihood (Frequency, $f$):** How often does the hazardous event occur?
   - Expressed in events per year ($yr^{-1}$).
   - Computed from failure rate data of components, demand rates, or historical records.

3. **Exposure (Population at Risk, $N$):** How many people (or assets) are exposed to the hazard?
   - Determines whether *Individual Risk* or *Societal Risk* is being computed.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus emphasizes that **risk is multi-dimensional**. A simple multiplication $R = f \times C$ captures two of these; the third (exposure) is captured separately in **Individual** vs **Societal Risk** metrics.

## 2.2 Types of Risk in Process Industries

| Risk Type | Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Probability that an average person at a specific location is harmed per unit time | Plant siting, zoning |
| **Societal Risk (SR)** | Relationship between frequency and number of people suffering a specified level of harm | F-N Curves for community impact |
| **Occupational Risk** | Risk faced by workers on-site | PPE, training, safe-work procedures |
| **Environmental Risk** | Risk of ecosystem damage | Effluent treatment, containment |
| **Financial / Business Risk** | Risk of monetary loss | Insurance, asset management |

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = f \times C$ | Fundamental risk | dimensionless or mixed | Used for qualitative ranking |
| 2 | $IR = \sum_i f_i \times p_i$ | Individual Risk (averaged) | $yr^{-1}$ | $p_i$ = probability of fatality given event $i$ |
| 3 | $RRF = \dfrac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless | $RRF \geq 1$ always; $RRF = 1$ means no protection |
| 4 | $PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_D \times T_i}$ | Avg. Probability of Failure on Demand (for low-demand mode) | dimensionless | For $\lambda_D \cdot T_i \ll 1$ |
| 5 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}}$ | RRF linked to PFD | dimensionless | Core SIS relationship |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}}$ | Safety Integrity (often written) | dimensionless | Inverse of PFD |
| 7 | $T_{proof} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{DU}}$ | Proof Test Interval | years | $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate |
| 8 | $\lambda = \lambda_D + \lambda_S$ | Total failure rate | $hr^{-1}$ or $yr^{-1}$ | $D$ = dangerous, $S$ = safe |
| 9 | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - R_{\text{reduction}}$ | Residual risk after safeguard | same as $R$ | Must be $\leq R_{\text{tolerable}}$ |
| 10 | $F = \sum_i f_i$ (over events with $N \geq n$) | Societal risk cumulative frequency | $yr^{-1}$ | Used in F-N curves |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In the table above, the absolute-value or conditioning bars have been intentionally written using $\vert$ / $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your answer script, write $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Risk assessment is the *starting point* of every Safety Instrumented System design cycle. In a KTU-aligned industrial project, the engineer:

1. Identifies hazards (HAZOP / HAZID).
2. Assesses the inherent (unmitigated) risk.
3. Compares it to the *tolerable risk* benchmark.
4. If $R_{\text{inherent}} > R_{\text{tolerable}}$, a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is specified.
5. The required **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of the SIF is derived from the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** needed.

This closed-loop, quantitative linkage between **risk** and **SIL** is the intellectual backbone of IEC 61511 and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Risk to Required SIL

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
A hazardous event in a chemical reactor has an inherent frequency $f_{\text{inh}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. A SIF is to be installed. The tolerable frequency is $f_{\text{tol}} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. Compute the required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) and the corresponding SIL of the SIF.

### Step 1 — Write the fundamental risk reduction definition

The Risk Reduction Factor is defined as the ratio of unmitigated to mitigated event frequency:

$$
RRF = \frac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}}
$$

This is the *engineering* definition — the SIF does not change severity; it changes frequency.

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

We are given:

- $f_{\text{unmitigated}} = f_{\text{inh}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \, yr^{-1}$
- $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{tol}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \, yr^{-1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} \\
    &= 1 \times 10^{(-2) - (-5)} \\
    &= 1 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $RRF = 1000$.

### Step 3 — Link RRF to PFDavg

By definition of the Safety Integrity of a SIF in low-demand mode:

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{RRF} \\
                &= \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{3}} \\
                &= 1 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Map PFDavg to SIL (IEC 61511 Table)

| SIL | Target PFDavg Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \leq PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \leq PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5} \leq PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-4}$ |

Since $PFD_{\text{avg}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$, this lies *exactly* on the boundary. By convention, the **higher** SIL is selected to provide a safety margin.

$$
\boxed{\text{Required SIL} = 2}
$$

(Note: With strict application of the "round up" convention, $PFD_{\text{avg}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ falls within SIL 2, not the boundary of SIL 3.)

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Individual Risk Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An LPG storage sphere has a base-event release frequency of $f = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}$. The probability of fatality given release, conditional on a person being present in the danger zone, is $p_f = 0.4$. A single operator works 8 hours/day in this zone (occupancy factor $k = 8/24 = 0.333$). Compute the Individual Risk (IR) per year.

### Step 1 — Define Individual Risk

$$
IR = f \times p_f \times k
$$

This formulation already accounts for the *time-exposure* aspect of risk.

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.4 \times 0.333 \\
   &= (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.1332) \\
   &= 6.66 \times 10^{-5} \, yr^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare to the tolerable risk benchmark

For a worker, HSE (UK) tolerability is approximately $10^{-3} \, yr^{-1}$ (broadly accepted) and $10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}$ (negligible).

Since $6.66 \times 10^{-5} \, yr^{-1} < 10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}$:

$$
\boxed{IR \text{ is within the } \textbf{Negligible Risk Region}.}
$$

No further risk reduction is mandated, but the engineer should still apply the **ALARP** principle (see Section 3.3).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk Matrix Construction

A **Risk Matrix** is the most commonly tested KTU tool for *qualitative* risk classification. The standard $5 \times 5$ matrix cross-tabulates **Severity (rows)** with **Likelihood (columns)**.

| Severity ↓ \ Likelihood → | L1 (Rare, $<10^{-6}$) | L2 (Unlikely, $10^{-6}$–$10^{-4}$) | L3 (Possible, $10^{-4}$–$10^{-2}$) | L4 (Likely, $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$) | L5 (Frequent, $>10^{-1}$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S5 Catastrophic** (fatalities) | M | H | E | E | E |
| **S4 Critical** (severe injury) | L | M | H | E | E |
| **S3 Marginal** (lost-time injury) | L | L | M | H | E |
| **S2 Minor** (first-aid) | L | L | L | M | H |
| **S1 Negligible** (no injury) | L | L | L | L | M |

Legend: **L** = Low (Acceptable), **M** = Medium (ALARP), **H** = High (Must reduce), **E** = Extreme (Unacceptable — stop operation).

## 3.4 ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable

ALARP is the *legal and engineering test* used in KTU/IEC 61511 to decide *how much* risk reduction is "enough":

1. **Gross Disproportion Test:** Risk reduction measures must be implemented *unless* the cost (in money, effort, time) is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit.
2. **Residual Risk Test:** The residual risk after applying all reasonably practicable measures must lie in the **Tolerable** region of the risk matrix.
3. **Documentation Test:** The ALARP decision must be documented and auditable.

$$
\text{Apply safeguard} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \frac{\text{Cost of risk reduction}}{\text{Risk reduction benefit}} \ll \text{Disproportion Factor}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Risk Assessment within an SIS Life-Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant Identified] --> B[HAZOP / HAZID Study]
    B --> C[Identify Hazards]
    C --> D[Determine Inherent Risk Rinh]
    D --> E{Inherent Risk vs Tolerable Risk}
    E -- Rinh less than or equal to Rtolerable --> F[ALARP Documentation]
    E -- Rinh greater than Rtolerable --> G[Specify Safety Instrumented Function SIF]
    G --> H[Compute Required RRF]
    H --> I[Map RRF to SIL via PFDavg]
    I --> J[Design SIS Architecture 1oo1 1oo2 2oo3]
    J --> K[Verify Rresidual less than or equal to Rtolerable]
    K --> F
    F --> L[Operate Maintain Audit]
```

## 4.2 Risk = Frequency × Consequence — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H[Hazard Identification]
        H1[Process Deviation] --> H2[Initiating Cause]
        H2 --> H3[Top Event]
    end
    H3 --> F[Frequency f per year]
    H3 --> C[Consequence C]
    F --> R[Risk R]
    C --> R
    R --> Comp{Compare to Tolerable}
    Comp -- Greater --> SIF[Safety Instrumented Function]
    Comp -- Less --> OK[Acceptable]
    SIF --> RRF[Risk Reduction Factor]
    RRF --> R2[Residual Risk]
    R2 --> Comp
```

## 4.3 ALARP Decision Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Residual Risk Computed] --> P2{Risk Matrix Classification}
    P2 -- Low --> P3[Accept Document]
    P2 -- Medium --> P4[Apply ALARP Test]
    P2 -- High --> P5[Reduce Risk Mandatory]
    P2 -- Extreme --> P6[Halt Operation]
    P4 --> P7{Cost grossly disproportionate to benefit}
    P7 -- No --> P8[Implement Additional Safeguard]
    P7 -- Yes --> P9[Document Justification]
    P8 --> P1
    P9 --> P1
    P5 --> P8
```

## 4.4 Individual Risk vs Societal Risk — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Risk Type] --> B[Individual Risk IR]
    A --> C[Societal Risk SR]
    B --> B1[Per person per year]
    B --> B2[Used for zoning and siting]
    C --> C1[F-N Curve]
    C --> C2[Frequency of N or more fatalities]
    C --> C3[Used for community impact]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "Risk" as per IEC 61511. Differentiate between hazard and risk with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Risk** is the combination of the probability (frequency) of occurrence of a harm and the severity (consequence) of that harm, mathematically expressed as $R = f \times C$. **(1 mark)**

> A **hazard** is a *potential source of harm* (e.g., pressurized hydrocarbon in a reactor), whereas **risk** is the *quantified likelihood that this hazard will cause harm* multiplied by the severity of the harm. **(1 mark)**

> **Example:** High-pressure hydrogen in a pipeline is a *hazard*; the chance of a leak leading to an explosion and the resulting fatality count is the *risk*. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**What is the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)? How is it related to PFDavg of a Safety Instrumented System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is the ratio of the unmitigated (inherent) event frequency to the mitigated (residual) event frequency achieved by a safeguard. **(1 mark)**
> $$ RRF = \frac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$
> The RRF is the **reciprocal** of the average Probability of Failure on Demand of the SIF in low-demand mode:
> $$ RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 scheme, Module-level Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an *internal choice* between two questions. Below, both options are provided with full sub-part (a) and (b) (7 + 7) solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *Individual Risk* and *Societal Risk* in a process plant. With suitable formulas, describe how each is used in plant siting and community impact assessment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor pressure relief scenario has:
- Inherent release frequency: $1 \times 10^{-1} \, yr^{-1}$
- Tolerable frequency: $1 \times 10^{-5} \, yr^{-1}$
- Probability of fatality given release: $0.2$

Compute the RRF, the corresponding $PFD_{\text{avg}}$, and identify the SIL of the SIF required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Individual Risk (IR):** The probability that a specific individual, assumed to be continuously present at a fixed location, is harmed per unit time. **(1 mark)**
2. **Formula:** $IR = f \times p_f$ where $f$ is event frequency and $p_f$ is the probability of fatality. With occupancy factor $k$, $IR = f \times p_f \times k$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Use:** Plant siting, layout design, evacuation planning, zoning of occupied buildings. **(1 mark)**
4. **Societal Risk (SR):** The relationship between the frequency of an accident and the number of people suffering a specified level of harm (typically fatalities). **(1 mark)**
5. **F-N Curve:** Plot of cumulative frequency $F$ of accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities versus $N$. **(+1 mark)**
6. **Use:** Community impact assessment, off-site emergency planning, regulatory acceptance. **(+1 mark)**
7. **Conclusion:** IR is per-person; SR aggregates population exposure. Both must be assessed for a comprehensive safety case. **(+1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute RRF:** **(2 marks)**

$$
RRF = \frac{f_{\text{inherent}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-1}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PFDavg:** **(2 marks)**

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{RRF} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{4}} = 1 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to SIL:** **(2 marks)**

Per IEC 61511, $10^{-4} \leq PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$ corresponds to **SIL 3**. Hence the required SIF must be designed and verified to **SIL 3**.

**Step 4 — Validity Check:** **(1 mark)**

The probability of fatality $p_f = 0.2$ does *not* enter the RRF calculation (RRF operates on event frequency, not on consequence). It is, however, implicit in the tolerable frequency figure used by the regulatory framework.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is the ALARP principle? Discuss how a 5×5 Risk Matrix is constructed, indicating the typical categories of severity and likelihood used in process industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two safeguards are proposed for a hazardous event with inherent frequency $f_{\text{inh}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \, yr^{-1}$:
- Safeguard 1: $PFD_{\text{avg},1} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- Safeguard 2: $PFD_{\text{avg},2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$

If both are independent and operate in series, compute the overall PFDavg and the residual frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable):** A risk must be reduced *unless the cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit achieved*. **(1 mark)**
2. **Three-tier test:** (i) Inherent risk above tolerable → must reduce; (ii) Apply gross-disproportion test; (iii) Residual risk must lie in tolerable region. **(2 marks)**
3. **Risk Matrix Structure:** Severity (rows, typically 5 levels: Negligible, Minor, Marginal, Critical, Catastrophic) × Likelihood (columns: Rare, Unlikely, Possible, Likely, Frequent). **(2 marks)**
4. **Cell classification:** Each cell is categorized as Low (L), Medium (M), High (H), or Extreme (E). L = acceptable; M = ALARP region; H & E = must reduce or stop. **(+1 mark)**
5. **Example cell:** Severity = Catastrophic, Likelihood = Likely → Extreme (E) → operation must halt. **(+1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Series combination of PFDs:** **(3 marks)**

For independent safeguards operating in series, the overall PFDavg is the *sum* (approximation, valid when $PFD \ll 1$):

$$
PFD_{\text{overall}} = PFD_1 + PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-2} + 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Overall RRF:** **(2 marks)**

$$
RRF_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 66.7
$$

**Step 3 — Residual Frequency:** **(2 marks)**

$$
f_{\text{residual}} = \frac{f_{\text{inh}}}{RRF_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{66.7} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}
$$

This residual lies in the **Tolerable** region (assuming $f_{\text{tol}} = 10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}$ order of magnitude).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Risk Questions**
> 1. **Confusing RRF with SIL directly.** RRF is a *ratio*; SIL is a *discrete band*. You must first compute $PFD_{\text{avg}} = 1/RRF$ and *then* map to a SIL band.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Frequency is in $yr^{-1}$, not $hr^{-1}$. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using addition for parallel PFDs.** For *parallel* (redundant) channels the formula is different (e.g., $PFD_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \lambda^2 t^2 / 3$). Use *series* summation only for *independent layers of protection* (LOPA-style).
> 4. **Ignoring the boundary convention in SIL mapping.** When $PFD_{\text{avg}}$ lies *exactly* on a boundary (e.g., $1 \times 10^{-3}$), the KTU examiner's convention is to *round up* to the higher SIL.
> 5. **Omitting the ALARP discussion.** Even when residual risk is technically tolerable, the examiner expects a *brief* mention of the ALARP test in any "is this acceptable?" question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Risk** = Frequency × Consequence ($R = f \times C$); never forget that *Safety = Freedom from Unacceptable Risk*.
- **Two risk flavours** must be known by name and formula: **Individual Risk** (per person per year) and **Societal Risk** (F-N curve, cumulative frequency).
- **RRF** = $f_{\text{unmitigated}} / f_{\text{mitigated}} = 1 / PFD_{\text{avg}}$ — this is the single most tested equation.
- **SIL** is *not* computed, it is *looked up* from the PFDavg band (SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$).
- **5×5 Risk Matrix** axes = Severity (rows) × Likelihood (columns); cell categories = L, M, H, E.
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable, with the *gross-disproportion* test.
- **Independent safeguards in series** add their PFDs: $PFD_{\text{total}} \approx PFD_1 + PFD_2 + \dots$
- **Tolerable risk benchmarks (HSE, UK):** worker $10^{-3} \, yr^{-1}$, public $10^{-4} \, yr^{-1}$.
- **Always state units** ($yr^{-1}$, fatalities/person/year) in the final line of any numerical answer.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers**, structure with: (i) define terms, (ii) state formula, (iii) substitute with units, (iv) compute, (v) interpret the result in a one-line engineering sentence.
- **HAZOP / HAZID** are *qualitative* precursors to the *quantitative* risk assessment; both are part of the KTU Module-1 scope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Risks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Risks

---

# 🛡️ Measurement of Risks — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Safety Instrumentation Systems (SIS)** and **Functional Safety** (aligned with **IEC 61511** and **IEC 61508**), **Measurement of Risks** is defined as the *systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation of the probability of occurrence of a hazardous event and the magnitude of the resulting harm to people, environment, assets, and business continuity.*

Mathematically, it is the combination of two measurable variables:

$$\text{Risk} \ (R) = f(\text{Frequency},\ \text{Consequence})$$

Where:
- **Frequency** → the likelihood of the hazardous event (per year).
- **Consequence** → the severity of the outcome (e.g., fatalities, financial loss, environmental damage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Measurement of Risk is the *backbone* of any Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination study. Without accurate risk measurement, an engineer **cannot** justify the SIL rating of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are planning to **cross a busy highway**:

| Factor | Real-World Translation | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| How often do trucks pass? | **Frequency** (likelihood of accident) | Failure rate of equipment per year |
| How badly would you be hurt if hit? | **Consequence** (severity) | Injury severity, fatality, financial loss |
| Do you cross anyway? | **Risk Acceptance** | ALARP / Risk Tolerance criteria |

If trucks pass every second (**high frequency**) and a collision would be fatal (**high consequence**), the **risk is unacceptable**. You would either wait, find a footbridge, or install traffic signals. In a chemical plant, that "traffic signal" is a **Safety Instrumented System** — but its design is governed entirely by how we *measure* the risk first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** Risk is never a single number — it is always a *product* of how often something bad **could happen** and how bad it **would be** if it happened.

---

## 1.3 Why "Measurement" and Not "Estimation"?

The KTU syllabus insists on the word **measurement** because modern Functional Safety demands:

1. **Numerical defensibility** — risk numbers must be traceable to data sources (incident databases like **OREDA**, **HID**, **FARADA**).
2. **Repeatability** — two engineers should compute similar risk figures for the same plant.
3. **Auditability** — regulators (Factories & Boilers, OISD, OSHA) require documented evidence.
4. **Decision-making** — quantitative values feed directly into SIL selection (per **IEC 61511 Tables 2 & 3**).

---

## 1.4 Categories of Risk Measured in SIS Engineering

| Risk Category | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Probability that an average person at a location is killed per year | per year |
| **Societal Risk** | Relationship between frequency of an accident and the number of people harmed | F–N curve |
| **Environmental Risk** | Probability of release of harmful substances | tonnes/year |
| **Asset / Financial Risk** | Probability of equipment loss | $/year |
| **Reputational Risk** | Qualitative impact on company brand | Categorical (Low/Med/High) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Triad — Frequency, Consequence, Vulnerability

Any measurement of risk requires quantifying **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Frequency (F)** — How often the initiating event occurs.
   - Units: events/year, demands/year.
   - Source: process data, vendor failure rates, generic databases.

2. **Consequence (C)** — The outcome severity if the event proceeds.
   - Units: fatalities, injuries, ₹ (INR) loss, ppm release.

3. **Vulnerability / Probability of Harm Given the Event (V)** — Conditional probability that the event causes the specific harm considered.

$$\boxed{R = F \times V \times C}$$

This is the **classical engineering risk equation**, used in **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines and adopted in KTU Module 1.

---

## 2.2 The Logical Flow of Risk Measurement

A KTU examiner expects the following sequential logic when answering:

- **Step 1 → Identify Hazard** (e.g., overpressure in a reactor).
- **Step 2 → Identify the Initiating Event** (e.g., blocked outlet).
- **Step 3 → Estimate Frequency** (e.g., 1×10⁻² /yr).
- **Step 4 → Determine Consequence** (e.g., 1 fatality).
- **Step 5 → Apply Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** (e.g., relief valve, alarms, SIF).
- **Step 6 → Compute Residual Risk** (post-mitigation).
- **Step 7 → Compare with Tolerable Risk** (decision: accept / reduce / ALARP).

---

## 2.3 Consequence Modelling — The Probit Model

For toxic releases, fires, and explosions, **consequence** is not a single number. The **Probit equation** converts a physical dose (toxic concentration × time, or thermal radiation, or overpressure) into a probability of fatality:

$$\boxed{Y = a + b \ln(V)}$$

Where:
- $Y$ = **Probit variable**.
- $V$ = **dose or intensity** (e.g., toxic load $C^n t$).
- $a,\ b$ = substance-specific constants (from **CCPS TNO Green Book**).

Probability of fatality is then:

$$P_f = 50\% \quad \text{when } Y = 5.0$$

$$P_f = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y - 5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Probit matters in KTU exams:** Numerical problems on toxic gas dispersion (e.g., **chlorine, ammonia**) use the Probit equation to translate ppm²·min into % fatality.

---

## 2.4 Risk Matrix — The Qualitative Method

For non-numerical plants or initial screening, KTU endorses the **5×5 Risk Matrix**:

| | Slight (1) | Minor (2) | Major (3) | Severe (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequent (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **Probable (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **Occasional (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **Remote (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Improbable (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

| Color Code | Risk Band | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 🟢 1–4 | **Negligible** | Accept, monitor |
| 🟡 5–9 | **Low** | Manage by procedure |
| 🟠 10–15 | **Medium** | Reduce; consider SIF |
| 🔴 16–25 | **High / Unacceptable** | Must mitigate (SIF mandatory) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI / Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = F \times C$ | Basic risk = Frequency × Consequence | fatality·yr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $R = F \times V \times C$ | Full risk including conditional harm probability | fatality·yr⁻¹ |
| 3 | $IR = \sum_i F_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual Risk (sum over all scenarios) | yr⁻¹ |
| 4 | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | Probit equation for dose-effect | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V = C^n \cdot t$ | Toxic load (TNO/CCPS) | ppmⁿ·min |
| 6 | $P_{f,SIF} = PFD_{avg}$ | Risk contribution of a SIF per IEC 61511 | dimensionless, per demand |
| 7 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of a SIF | dimensionless |
| 8 | $F_{residual} = F_{init} \times \prod_{i=1}^{N} PFD_i$ | Residual frequency after N IPLs | yr⁻¹ |
| 9 | $P_{demand} = \dfrac{1}{TI}$ | Demand rate from Test Interval | yr⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\lambda_D,\ \lambda_S$ | Dangerous / Safe failure rates | per hour (fit = 10⁻⁹/h) |
| 11 | $\bar{t}_{CE} = \dfrac{TI}{2}$ | Mean downtime on dangerous failure (Proof Test) | hours |
| 12 | $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot \bar{t}_{CE}$ | Avg. probability of failure on demand | dimensionless |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (ONGC, BPCL, HPCL):** Used for QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment) of LPG spheres, FPSOs.
- **Pharmaceuticals (TCS, Dr. Reddy's):** Reactor overpressure protection SIL determination.
- **Nuclear (NPCIL):** Core Damage Frequency (CDF) and Large Early Release Frequency (LERF).
- **KTU Career Relevance:** Required for jobs in **Process Safety**, **HSE consulting (DuPont, Honeywell)** and **Functional Safety TÜV certification**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Residual Risk Frequency After Independent Protection Layers

We have an initiating event of frequency $F_{init}$. There are **N independent protection layers (IPLs)**, each with probability of failure on demand $PFD_i$. Since IPLs are independent, the joint failure probability is the product.

$$
F_{residual} = F_{init} \cdot PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2 \cdots PFD_N
$$

$$
F_{residual} = F_{init} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{N} PFD_i
$$

**Line-by-line interpretation:**

- $F_{init}$ — the *unmitigated* frequency of the hazardous event.
- Each IPL reduces the frequency by its own reliability $1 - PFD_i$.
- If IPL fails, it does *not* recover; hence multiplication of $PFD$.
- Result: $F_{residual}$ is the **frequency at which the hazardous consequence actually reaches the people/asset**.

---

### Numerical Example (Suitable for 14-mark KTU question)

**Problem:**
A reactor has an overpressure initiating event frequency $F_{init} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year. Three IPLs exist:
- IPL-1 (Relief Valve): $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- IPL-2 (Alarm + Operator Action): $PFD_2 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
- IPL-3 (Safety Instrumented Function, SIL 2): $PFD_3 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

Compute the residual frequency. Does it meet a tolerable frequency of $1 \times 10^{-6}$/yr?

**Solution:**

$$
F_{residual} = F_{init} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3
$$

$$
F_{residual} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
F_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}
$$

**Decision:**
$5 \times 10^{-7}$/yr $<$ $1 \times 10^{-6}$/yr → **Risk is tolerable** ✅.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) of a SIF

The **Risk Reduction Factor** is how much the SIF reduces the risk, defined as:

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted SIF with identical dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and proof-test interval $TI$:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot TI)^2}{6}
$$

Derivation (exhaustive):
For 1oo2, both channels must fail dangerously (redundant logic — voting requires **all** to fail for system failure — but since it's 1oo2, the system fails on dangerous failure of either channel. For low-demand mode:)

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t \quad (\text{for } \lambda_D t \ll 1)
$$

Average over test interval:

$$
\overline{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} \lambda_D t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_D TI}{2}
$$

For 1oo2 (both channels need to fail dangerously):

$$
\overline{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D TI)^2}{6}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{RRF_{1oo2} = \frac{6}{(\lambda_D \cdot TI)^2}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Individual Risk (IR) Calculation

Individual Risk is the **incremental probability** of fatality per year for a person at a specific location. For a person continuously present (occupancy factor $f_o = 1$) facing $N$ accident scenarios:

$$
IR = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i \cdot V_i
$$

- $F_i$ = frequency of accident scenario $i$ (events/yr).
- $V_i$ = probability of fatality for the person given scenario $i$ occurs.

If the person is only present part of the time:

$$
IR = f_o \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i \cdot V_i
$$

**HSE (UK) Tolerability criteria:**

| Individual Risk (per year) | Status |
|---|---|
| $\leq 10^{-6}$ | Broadly Acceptable |
| $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ | ALARP region |
| $\geq 10^{-4}$ | Unacceptable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These HSE limits are *numerical anchors* KTU examiners love to ask. Memorize $10^{-6}$ and $10^{-4}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Societal Risk via F–N Curve

Societal risk concerns **multiple fatality events**. It is plotted as **cumulative frequency (F)** vs. **number of fatalities (N)** on log-log axes.

$$
F(N) = \sum_{i : N_i \geq N} f_i
$$

Where $f_i$ is the frequency of accident scenario $i$ producing $N_i$ fatalities.

The F–N curve typically follows a power law:

$$
F = K \cdot N^{-a}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = constant (intercept on log-log plot).
- $a$ = slope steepness. **Steeper curves indicate less societal risk tolerance**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Probit Equation Solved for Chlorine Leakage

**Example:** A chlorine leak releases a concentration of $C = 200$ ppm for $t = 10$ minutes. Chlorine Probit constants: $a = -19.83,\ b = 3.80,\ n = 1$.

**Toxic load:**

$$
V = C^n \cdot t = 200^1 \cdot 10 = 2000 \text{ ppm·min}
$$

**Probit:**

$$
Y = -19.83 + 3.80 \ln(2000)
$$

$$
\ln(2000) = 7.6009
$$

$$
Y = -19.83 + 3.80 \times 7.6009 = -19.83 + 28.884 = 9.054
$$

**Convert to fatality probability** using standard normal CDF:

$$
P_f = \Phi(Y - 5) = \Phi(9.054 - 5) = \Phi(4.054)
$$

$$
P_f \approx 0.99997 \approx 99.997\%
$$

Interpretation: exposure is **almost certainly fatal**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Method | Data Needed | Output | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Matrix** | Likelihood & severity categories | Risk band (1–25) | Screening |
| **Probit** | Toxic/thermal/overpressure data | % fatality | Consequence analysis |
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Frequency + Probit | Fatalities/yr | Personnel safety |
| **Societal Risk (F–N)** | Scenario N vs. frequency | F–N curve | Community safety |
| **LOPA** | Initiating event + IPLs | Required SIL | SIF specification |
| **QRA** | All above + cost data | Risk contour maps | Regulatory submission |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Risk Measurement Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Process Hazard Identification HAZID] --> B[Initiating Event Frequency F_init]
    B --> C[Identify Independent Protection Layers IPLs]
    C --> D[Compute PFD of each IPL]
    D --> E[Compute Residual Frequency F_residual]
    E --> F[Compute Consequence C using Probit or Damage Models]
    F --> G[Calculate Risk R = F_residual x C]
    G --> H{Compare with Tolerable Risk Limit}
    H -- Below limit --> I[Risk ACCEPTABLE - Document]
    H -- Above limit --> J[Apply ALARP - Add SIF]
    J --> K[Determine Required SIL per IEC 61511]
    K --> L[Design Safety Instrumented Function]
    L --> M[Verify RRF matches Risk Gap]
```

---

## 4.2 Risk Triad — Frequency × Vulnerability × Consequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Frequency Axis"
    F1[Process Data] --> F2[Vendor Failure Rates OREDA]
    F2 --> F3[Incident Databases]
    F3 --> F[Initiating Event Frequency per yr]
    end
    subgraph "Vulnerability Axis"
    V1[Population Density] --> V2[Exposure Time]
    V2 --> V3[Escape Routes]
    V3 --> V[Probability of Harm Given Event]
    end
    subgraph "Consequence Axis"
    C1[Fatalities] --> C2[Injuries]
    C2 --> C3[Environmental Damage]
    C3 --> C4[Economic Loss]
    C4 --> CT[Total Consequence Score]
    end
    F --> R[Risk R = F x V x C]
    V --> R
    CT --> R
```

---

## 4.3 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Process Design - Inherently Safe] --> P1[Basic Process Control System BPCS]
    P1 --> P2[Operator Alarm Response]
    P2 --> P3[Safety Instrumented Function SIF]
    P3 --> P4[Mechanical Relief Device]
    P4 --> P5[Post-Event Mitigation Firewater Dikes]
    P5 --> P6[Residual Event Frequency to Community]
    P6 --> D{Risk below ALARP threshold?}
    D -- Yes --> ACC[Document Accept]
    D -- No --> REV[Add More IPLs or Revise Process]
```

---

## 4.4 F–N Societal Risk Curve Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Accident Scenario Database] --> S2[Group Events by Fatality Count N]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Frequency f_i for each N]
    S3 --> S4[Cumulate F greater than or equal to N]
    S4 --> S5[Plot Log-Log Curve F vs N]
    S5 --> S6[Overlay HSE ALARP Region 10 to power -3 to 10 to power -5]
    S5 --> S7[Compare with Corporate Tolerability Line]
    S6 --> DEC[Decision: Accept / Mitigate]
    S7 --> DEC
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Risk" as applied to process safety. List the three components used to measure risk quantitatively.**

**Model Answer:**
Risk is the combination of the **frequency (F)** of occurrence of a hazardous event, the **probability (V)** that the event leads to harm, and the **severity (C)** of that harm. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
R = F \times V \times C
$$

The three components are:
1. **Frequency of the initiating event** (per year).
2. **Vulnerability / Probability of harm given the event** (dimensionless).
3. **Consequence severity** (fatalities, injuries, financial loss).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Risk Matrix? How is it used in the initial screening of process hazards?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Risk Matrix** is a qualitative tool that maps the **likelihood (frequency)** of an event against the **severity (consequence)** of its outcome, producing a numerical score from 1 (very low) to 25 (very high). It is used in the **initial screening phase** of a risk assessment to:

- Prioritize hazards for detailed quantitative analysis.
- Provide a quick visualization of high-risk scenarios.
- Aid in deciding whether a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is required.

Typical scoring bands:
- **1–4 → Negligible** (Accept),
- **5–9 → Low** (Manage),
- **10–15 → Medium** (Reduce),
- **16–25 → High** (Must mitigate with SIF).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A process plant has a hazardous event (runaway reaction) with an initiating event frequency of **$5 \times 10^{-2}$ events/year**. There are two independent protection layers:
- **IPL-1 (Basic Alarm + Operator Action):** $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- **IPL-2 (SIL-1 SIF):** $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

If the consequence of the runaway is **2 fatalities**, compute:
- (i) The **residual frequency** of the event reaching the consequence stage. **(7 marks)**
- (ii) The **residual risk** in fatalities/year and **Individual Risk** assuming full-time exposure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)(i) Residual Frequency**

$$
F_{residual} = F_{init} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2
$$

$$
F_{residual} = (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
F_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}
$$

**(a)(ii) Residual Risk and Individual Risk**

$$
\text{Risk} = F_{residual} \times N_{fatalities}
$$

$$
\text{Risk} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ fatalities/year}
$$

$$
IR = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}
$$

**Decision:** Compare with HSE criterion. Since $IR = 10^{-4}$/yr is at the **upper ALARP boundary**, mitigation is essential — perhaps upgrade SIF to SIL-2.

---

### 🔴 Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2]

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Probit equation** for measuring the consequence of a toxic gas release. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A chlorine release gives a toxic load of $V = 8000$ ppm·min. Using Probit constants $a = -19.83$ and $b = 3.80$, calculate the **probability of fatality** for an exposed worker. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Probit Equation Explanation**

The **Probit equation** translates a physical dose (toxic concentration × time, thermal radiation × time, or overpressure) into a probability of fatality. Its general form is:

$$
Y = a + b \ln(V)
$$

Where:
- $Y$ = Probit variable (related to % fatality).
- $a,\ b$ = substance-specific constants derived experimentally (e.g., from CCPS Green Book).
- $V$ = dose intensity (toxic load $C^n t$, thermal dose $I^{4/3} t$, or overpressure impulse).

Once $Y$ is computed, the probability of fatality $P_f$ is obtained using the standard normal CDF, where $Y = 5.0$ corresponds to $P_f = 50\%$.

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

Given $V = 8000$ ppm·min:

$$
Y = -19.83 + 3.80 \ln(8000)
$$

$$
\ln(8000) = \ln(8 \times 10^3) = \ln 8 + 3 \ln 10 = 2.0794 + 6.9078 = 8.9872
$$

$$
Y = -19.83 + 3.80 \times 8.9872 = -19.83 + 34.151 = 14.321
$$

Converting to fatality probability:

$$
P_f = \Phi(Y - 5) = \Phi(14.321 - 5) = \Phi(9.321) \approx 1.0000
$$

$$
\boxed{P_f \approx 100\% \ \text{(certain fatality)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting units:** Always include "per year" for frequency and "fatalities" for risk. Many students lose 2 marks by skipping units.
> - **Multiplying instead of adding:** If the question gives multiple IPLs, **multiply** the PFDs (independent failures), don't add them.
> - **Confusing ALARP and acceptable risk:** ALARP is a *region* between $10^{-6}$ and $10^{-4}$/yr (Individual Risk), not a single threshold.
> - **Skipping occupancy factor:** If a worker is present only 50% of the time, multiply IR by $f_o = 0.5$.
> - **Wrong Probit formula:** Use $\ln$ (natural logarithm), not $\log_{10}$. Many students lose 2 marks here.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Risk = Frequency × Vulnerability × Consequence** — the universal equation.
- ✅ **Risk Matrix** is *qualitative* (1–25 scale); **LOPA/QRA** are *quantitative*.
- ✅ **Individual Risk (IR)** measures fatality per year for a single exposed person.
- ✅ **Societal Risk (F–N curve)** plots cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities on log-log axes.
- ✅ **HSE (UK) Tolerability:** $IR \leq 10^{-6}$/yr (broadly acceptable); $IR \geq 10^{-4}$/yr (unacceptable); between is **ALARP**.
- ✅ **Probit equation** $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ converts dose into % fatality.
- ✅ **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** of a SIF $= 1 / PFD_{avg}$.
- ✅ **Residual Frequency** $F_{residual} = F_{init} \cdot \prod PFD_i$.
- ✅ For **SIL determination** (per IEC 61511): match RRF of SIF to the Risk Gap.
- ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — risk must be reduced unless cost is "grossly disproportionate."
- ✅ Memorize standard toxic/thermal Probit constants for **chlorine, ammonia, LPG, LNG**.
- ✅ Always specify **units** in your final answer (per year, fatalities/yr, ppm·min).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reduction of Risks
topics.content:
