**Step 1: Define the events.**
Let:
- $D_1$ = Negative Ancestry/Social Environment
- $D_2$ = Personal Fault
- $D_3$ = Unsafe Act / Condition
- $D_4$ = Accident
- $D_5$ = Injury

**Step 2: Express injury as a conditional chain.**

$$
P(I) = P(D_1 \cap D_2 \cap D_3 \cap D_4 \cap D_5)
$$

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule of probability.**

$$
P(I) = P(D_1) \cdot P(D_2 \mid D_1) \cdot P(D_3 \mid D_1 \cap D_2) \cdot P(D_4 \mid D_1 \cap D_2 \cap D_3) \cdot P(D_5 \mid D_1 \cap D_2 \cap D_3 \cap D_4)
$$

**Step 4: Apply Heinrich's assumption of *strong causation* — each domino is the direct cause of the next, so conditional dependence reduces to a serial product.**

$$
P(I) = \prod_{i=1}^{5} P(D_i \mid D_{i-1}) \quad \text{with } D_0 = \text{universe}
$$

**Step 5: Derive the *prevention* condition.**

If **any single domino $D_k$ is removed** (i.e., its probability is forced to zero), then:

$$
P(I) = P(D_1) \cdot P(D_2 \mid D_1) \cdots \underbrace{P(D_k = 0)}_{=\, 0} \cdots P(D_5 \mid \cdot) = 0
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion of derivation.**

$$
\boxed{\; \text{If } P(D_k) = 0 \text{ for any } k \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}, \text{ then } P(I) = 0 \;}
$$

> This is the **formal mathematical proof** of Heinrich's qualitative claim: removing one causal link completely breaks the chain and prevents injury.

### 3.2 Heinrich's 88:10:2 Ratio — Numerical Verification

Suppose a plant records **N = 600** accidents in a year. Using Heinrich's 88:10:2 ratio:

**Step 1:** Compute the count of each severity class.

$$
N_{\text{no-injury}} = 0.88 \times N
$$

$$
N_{\text{minor}} = 0.10 \times N
$$

$$
N_{\text{major}} = 0.02 \times N
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N = 600$.

$$
N_{\text{no-injury}} = 0.88 \times 600 = 528
$$

$$
N_{\text{minor}} = 0.10 \times 600 = 60
$$

$$
N_{\text{major}} = 0.02 \times 600 = 12
$$

**Step 3: Verify the sum.**

$$
528 + 60 + 12 = 600 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Derive the ratio of major injuries to total accidents.**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_{\text{major}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{12}{600} = \frac{1}{50} = 2\%
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Heinrich vs. Bird

| Parameter | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1969 — Updated) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin of chain | Ancestry & Social Environment | **Lack of Management Control** |
| Number of dominos | **5** | **5** (refined labels) |
| Data sample | ~75,000 accidents | ~1,753,498 accidents (90 companies) |
| Minor:Major ratio | $30 : 1$ | $10 : 1$ |
| Most effective intervention | Remove unsafe act | **Remove management failure** |
| Modern application | Basic safety training | Modern PSM / EHS systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Heinrich's Domino Chain (Linear Causation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Domino 1: Ancestry and Social Environment] --> B[Domino 2: Fault of Person]
    B --> C[Domino 3: Unsafe Act or Mechanical Hazard]
    C --> D[Domino 4: Accident - Energy Transfer]
    D --> E[Domino 5: Injury to Person or Property]
    S[SIS Intervention: Remove Domino 3] -.-> C
    M[SIS Intervention: Remove Domino 4] -.-> D
    classDef domino fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef intervention fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E domino
    class S,M intervention
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bird's Updated Domino Model (with Management Root)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Domino 1: Lack of Management Control] --> A2[Domino 2: Basic Causes - Personal or Job Factors]
    A2 --> A3[Domino 3: Immediate Causes - Substandard Acts or Conditions]
    A3 --> A4[Domino 4: Incident - Contact with Energy]
    A4 --> A5[Domino 5: Losses - People, Property, Process]
    I1[Intervention: Strong Management Oversight] -.-> A1
    I2[Intervention: Engineering Controls] -.-> A3
    classDef base fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#e63946,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 base
    class I1,I2 int
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Mapping the Domino Chain to a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hazard Source - Domino 3 Trigger] --> S1[Sensor - Detects Unsafe Condition]
    S1 --> S2[Logic Solver - Evaluates vs Threshold]
    S2 --> S3{Final Element - Execute Shutdown}
    S3 --> P1[Process Brought to Safe State - Domino 4 Prevented]
    P1 --> P2[Injury Averted - Domino 5 Avoided]
    classDef sis fill:#90be6d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class H1,P1,P2 process
    class S1,S2,S3 sis
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** The SIS is essentially an *engineered removal* of Domino 4 from the chain. When asked to "relate Domino Theory to SIS," draw a parallel between **Domino 4 (accident)** and the **SIS's safe shutdown action** that interrupts it.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*State Heinrich's Domino Theory of accident causation. List the five factors in the correct sequence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Domino Theory, proposed by **H.W. Heinrich** (1931), explains accidents as the result of a chain reaction of **five sequential factors**, each acting as a "domino" that triggers the next:
1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** (Domino 1)
2. **Fault of Person** (Domino 2)
3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition (Mechanical Hazard)** (Domino 3)
4. **Accident** (Domino 4)
5. **Injury** (Domino 5)

The theory's central idea is that **injury never occurs alone** but is the final outcome of a predictable causal chain. If any one domino in the sequence is removed, the chain breaks and injury is prevented. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Explain Heinrich's 88:10:2 ratio. What is its significance in industrial safety?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Based on the analysis of approximately **75,000 accident reports**, Heinrich concluded that for every **100/600 accidents**:
- **88%** result in **no injury / minor property damage only**
- **10%** cause **minor injury** (first-aid / medical-aid cases)
- **2%** cause **serious / disabling injury** or fatality

**Significance:** The ratio implies that **no-injury events and minor injuries share the same root causes** as serious injuries. By analyzing and preventing the high-volume minor events, organizations can systematically prevent the rare but catastrophic major injuries. This justifies the proactive investigation of **near-misses** as precursors. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain in detail **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation with a neat diagram. Discuss its **limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe **Bird's updated Domino Theory** and state **three key differences** from Heinrich's original model. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Heinrich's Domino Theory (7 marks):**

Heinrich's model proposes that **industrial accidents occur as a result of a chain of five sequential factors**, each of which triggers the next, much like a row of standing dominoes. The five factors in order are:

1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — Inherited traits (intelligence, temperament) and social conditions (poverty, education) that influence behavior.
2. **Fault of Person** — Personal deficiencies such as recklessness, nervousness, or improper motivation developed from ancestry.
3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Mechanical or Physical Condition** — Behaviors like operating without authority, failure to wear PPE, or physical hazards like unguarded machinery.
4. **Accident** — The contact point where energy is transferred to a person or property (e.g., a worker struck by a falling object).
5. **Injury** — The final outcome — bodily harm or property damage.

**Key principle:** The injury (Domino 5) is **never the first event**; it is the consequence of all preceding factors. **Prevention** is achieved by removing any one of the middle dominos — most effectively Domino 3 (the unsafe act/condition).

**Limitations (3 of many):**
- Assumes a **strictly linear, single-cause** relationship; in reality, accidents have **multiple parallel causes**.
- Places excessive emphasis on **human error** while underestimating **systemic and management factors**.
- The **88:10:2 ratio** is not universally observed across all industries and geographies (e.g., Bird's 1969 study found different ratios).
- Fails to address **latent conditions** and **organizational culture** — gaps later addressed by Reason's Swiss Cheese Model.

*(7 marks: 5 for description + diagram, 2 for limitations)*

**Part (b) — Bird's Updated Domino Theory (7 marks):**

In **1969**, **Frank Bird** updated Heinrich's theory using data from **~1.75 million accidents across 90 companies**. The five updated factors are:

1. **Lack of Management Control** (replaces Ancestry)
2. **Basic Causes** — Personal factors or job factors
3. **Immediate Causes** — Substandard acts or conditions
4. **Incident / Contact with Energy or Substance**
5. **Losses** — to people, property, or business processes

**Three key differences from Heinrich:**

| # | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1969) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Originates from **Ancestry/Social Environment** | Originates from **Lack of Management Control** |
| 2 | Minor-to-major injury ratio = **30 : 1** | Minor-to-major injury ratio = **10 : 1** (more realistic) |
| 3 | Focuses on **worker-level unsafe acts** | Focuses on **systemic management responsibility** |

**Significance:** Bird's model places the **root cause of accidents squarely on management** rather than blaming the worker's ancestry or character — a major philosophical shift toward modern safety accountability.

*(7 marks: 4 for description + 3 for differences table)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** With a suitable **neat diagram**, explain **Heinrich's Domino Theory**. Show how the theory is applied to **prevent accidents** in a process plant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A refinery recorded **600 accidents** last year. Using **Heinrich's 88:10:2 ratio**, calculate the expected number of no-injury, minor-injury, and major-injury accidents. Comment on the practical implications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Application to Process Plant (7 marks):**

Refer to the diagram shown in **Section 4.1** (Heinrich's Domino Chain).

**Step-by-step application in a process plant:**

| Domino | Plant Example | Preventive Measure |
|---|---|---|
| D1 — Ancestry/Social | Worker with poor hazard perception training | Improved recruitment, aptitude testing |
| D2 — Personal Fault | Carelessness due to fatigue | Fatigue management, rest breaks |
| D3 — Unsafe Act/Condition | Bypassing a pressure-relief valve | **Engineering control (SIS)**, lock-out/tag-out |
| D4 — Accident | High-pressure release of toxic gas | **SIS initiates emergency shutdown (ESD)** |
| D5 — Injury | Burns, fatality | PPE, evacuation systems |

**Key insight:** In a modern process plant, the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** acts by *removing Domino 4* — when the sensor detects an unsafe condition (rising pressure), the logic solver commands a shutdown, preventing the energy transfer to the worker. This is the practical engineering embodiment of "removing a domino."

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram/description, 4 for process plant application)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N_{total} = 600$ accidents; ratio = $88 : 10 : 2$

**Step 1: Compute no-injury events.**

$$
N_{\text{no-injury}} = \frac{88}{88+10+2} \times 600 = \frac{88}{100} \times 600 = 528
$$

**Step 2: Compute minor-injury events.**

$$
N_{\text{minor}} = \frac{10}{100} \times 600 = 60
$$

**Step 3: Compute major-injury events.**

$$
N_{\text{major}} = \frac{2}{100} \times 600 = 12
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

$$
528 + 60 + 12 = 600 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Practical implications (4 marks distributed):**
- The **528 no-injury events** are early warning indicators of underlying process/behavioral problems and should be investigated thoroughly.
- The **60 minor injuries** consume first-aid and medical-aid resources; root-cause analysis can prevent escalation.
- The **12 major injuries** represent the highest human/economic cost; even one is unacceptable in modern safety practice.
- **Conclusion:** Heinrich's ratio justifies investing disproportionately in **near-miss reporting systems** — fixing the 528 "free lessons" can prevent the 12 catastrophic events.

*(7 marks: 3 for computation steps, 4 for implications discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong sequence of dominos:** Many students write the chain as *Accident → Injury → Unsafe Act* (reverse order). The correct order is **Ancestry → Fault → Unsafe Act → Accident → Injury**. Marks deducted: ~2 per swap.
> 2. **Confusing Heinrich with Bird:** If the question says "updated" or "Bird," the first domino is **Management**, **not** Ancestry. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 3. **Forgetting the diagram:** In 7-mark Part B sub-questions, a **neat labelled diagram of the five dominos** is compulsory. Omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 4. **88:10:2 miscalculation:** Ensure denominators sum to 100. A common error is using $88+10+2=100$ correctly but applying it to the wrong $N$ value.
> 5. **Not stating Heinrich's full name (H.W. Heinrich) and year (1931):** Examiners reward this exactness — 1 mark reserved for it in many papers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Domino Theory in 60 Seconds"**

- **Proposer:** **H.W. Heinrich**, **1931**, in *"Industrial Accident Prevention: A Scientific Approach."*
- **Core Idea:** Accidents are the **result of a chain of 5 sequential factors (dominos)**, each triggering the next.
- **The 5 Dominos (Heinrich):**
  1. **A**ncestry and Social Environment
  2. **F**ault of Person
  3. **U**nsafe Act / Mechanical Hazard
  4. **A**ccident (Energy Transfer)
  5. **I**njury
- **Mnemonic:** *"**A** **F**isherman **U**nfortunately **A**te **I**njured Salmon"* (A → F → U → A → I).
- **Key Ratio:** **88 : 10 : 2** (no-injury : minor : major) — based on ~75,000 accident reports.
- **Prevention Principle:** Remove **any one domino** in the chain → injury probability becomes **zero** (proven via probability product rule).
- **Most Effective Removal Point (Heinrich):** Domino 3 — the unsafe act/condition.
- **Bird's Update (1969):** First domino is **Lack of Management Control**; ratio revised to **10 : 1** for minor-to-major.
- **SIS Mapping:** An SIS **removes Domino 4** by executing a safe shutdown before energy reaches the person.
- **Modern Descendants:** Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**; **Bow-Tie Analysis**; **Tripod-Delta** model.
- **Limitations to Mention:** Linear assumption, ignores multi-causation, blames worker not system, ratio not universal.
- **Exam-Ready Phrase:** *"Injury is not the first event — it is the last of five."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bird's Law Causation Model, Tye-Pearson Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bird's Law Causation Model, Tye-Pearson Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bird's Law Causation Model (Multi-Causation Theory)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Bird's Law Causation Model**, formally known as the **Bird Multi-Causation Theory of Accident Causation**, was developed by **Frank E. Bird Jr.** in **1969** as an extension and statistical refinement of **H.W. Heinrich's Domino Theory**. It is a **systemic, management-oriented accident causation model** that rejects the single-cause accident philosophy and demonstrates that accidents are the *visible end-result* of a chain of pre-existing organizational, situational, and behavioral failures. The model has five sequential elements connected by a **feedback loop** from the final "Loss" back to the initial "Lack of Control."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bird's model is the **dominant** accident causation model in modern Process Safety Management (PSM) and is the foundation of the **Loss Causation Model (LCM)** used in Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) and Incident Prevention programs across the process industries (Refineries, Petrochemicals, Pharma, Oil & Gas).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a **river flooding your house**. You would not just blame the rain (single-cause thinking). The actual cause is a chain:  
🏠 *Dam upstream was not maintained (Lack of Control)* → 🪨 *Silt built up and cracks formed (Basic Causes)* → 💧 *Cracks widened over time (Immediate Causes)* → 🌊 *Heavy rain overwhelmed the structure (Incident)* → 🏘️ *Houses destroyed, lives lost (Loss)* → 🔄 *Government sets up an inspection authority (Feedback Loop)*.  
That **multi-stage chain with a corrective feedback loop** is exactly what Bird's model says about industrial accidents.

---

## 1.2 Tye-Pearson System Causation Model (Hazard Chain Model)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Tye-Pearson System Causation Model**, proposed by **J.M. Tye** and **K. Pearson** in the **1970s–1980s** for the **UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE)**, is a **system-safety causation model** that mathematically conceptualizes accidents as the **alignment of three independent vectors**: a pre-existing **Hazard**, an **Initiating Event**, and a set of **Ensuing/Contributing Events**. It is sometimes called the **"Hazard Triangle Model"** or **"System Causation Sequence."** The model is foundational to **Hazard Analysis and Operability (HAZOP)**, **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**, and **Major Hazard Control legislation (CIMAH/Seveso Directives).**

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Picture a **road intersection** 🚦. The **hazard** is the intersection itself (a road design that makes collisions *possible*). The **initiating event** is the moment a car runs the red light. The **ensuing events** are: (a) the second car is exactly at that point, (b) the brakes are wet, (c) the driver is sleepy. The **incident** is the crash. **No single factor causes the crash; the accident occurs only when all three elements align in space and time.** That alignment condition is the Tye-Pearson concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Favourite):** Bird's model is **domino-sequential (chronological)**. Tye-Pearson is **vector-convergent (spatial-temporal alignment)**. Bird asks *"How did we get here historically?"*; Tye-Pearson asks *"What must align RIGHT NOW for an accident to occur?"*

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Bird's Law Model | Tye-Pearson Model |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Year / Authors** | 1969, F.E. Bird Jr. | 1970s, J.M. Tye & K. Pearson |
| **Theoretical Basis** | Multi-causation / Statistical | System Safety / Probabilistic |
| **Shape** | 5-level vertical chain with feedback | Triangle of converging vectors |
| **Key Output** | Loss | Incident / Major Hazard |
| **Application Domain** | BBS, OSHA, PSM | HAZOP, LOPA, Seveso, HSE |
| **Feedback Loop** | ✅ Yes (Loss → Lack of Control) | ❌ No explicit loop |
| **Statutory Use in SIS** | Indirect (behavioral) | Direct (SIL/performance) |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Exam:** Bird = **M-anagement driven (top-down)** ; Tye-Pearson = **H-azard driven (bottom-up)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bird's Law — Five Sequential Levels (Detailed)

The model is read **top-down (causation)** and **bottom-up (loss reversal)**. Each lower level is the *symptom* of the level above it.

**Level 1 — Lack of Control (ROOT / MANAGEMENT DEFICIENCY)**
- Definition: Inadequate *Management Control Systems* — the foundational reason accidents exist.
- Sub-elements: (i) Inadequate *Program Standards*, (ii) Inadequate *Program Compliance*, (iii) Inadequate *Program Implementation*.
- Engineering examples: No Safety Integrity Level (SIL) policy, missing Management of Change (MOC) procedure, no Permit-to-Work system.

**Level 2 — Basic Causes (ORIGINATING FACTORS)**
- Two parallel sub-branches:
  - **Personal Factors** (≈ 80–90% statistically per Bird's 1974 study of 1.7 million incidents):
    - Physical/physiological (fatigue, illness, hearing loss)
    - Mental/Psychological (poor judgment, distraction, panic)
    - Knowledge/Skill gap (untrained operator)
  - **Job/Systemic Factors**:
    - Inadequate engineering/ergonomic design
    - Poor maintenance, inadequate tools, wear & tear
    - Unreasonable production pressure (management culture)

**Level 3 — Immediate Causes (SYMPTOMS / DIRECT TRIGGERS)**
- **Substandard Acts (Unsafe Behaviors):** operating equipment without authority, bypassing safety interlocks, improper lifting, taking shortcuts, poor housekeeping.
- **Substandard Conditions (Unsafe States):** unguarded machinery, defective tools, congestion, fire/explosion atmospheres, hazardous chemical storage.

**Level 4 — Incident (THE VISIBLE EVENT)**
- The *witnessed* mishap. Bird separates:
  - **Incident** — minor, no lost-time injury (LT-free).
  - **Accident** — serious, lost-time injury, fatality, or major property damage.

**Level 5 — Loss (THE BUSINESS / HUMAN CONSEQUENCE)**
- **People:** injury, illness, fatality, reduced morale.
- **Property:** damage to equipment, infrastructure, environment.
- **Production:** downtime, loss of customer trust, regulatory penalties.

**THE FEEDBACK LOOP (BIRD'S DISTINCTIVE FEATURE):**
The loss feeds back upward and **forces Management to re-examine Lack of Control**, creating organizational learning. This is the empirical origin of the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) safety cycle** in **ISO 45001:2018** and **OHSAS 18001**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Statistic:** Bird's 1974 study found a ratio of **1 Serious injury : 29 Minor injuries : 300 Near-misses (No-injury incidents)** — the famous *1-29-300 ratio*. This is the basis of Heinrich's Triangle update.

---

## 2.2 Tye-Pearson Model — Three Converging Vectors (Detailed)

The Tye-Pearson model is a **probabilistic system-safety model** that requires three independent elements to **coincide** for an incident to occur.

**Vector 1 — HAZARD (H):**
- An **inherent physical, chemical, or operational property** of the system that has the *potential* to cause harm. Hazards are *always present* in any process plant — they cannot be eliminated, only **controlled**.
- Examples: High-pressure hydrocarbon gas, flammable solvent, rotating impeller, energized 11 kV busbar.
- Notation: $H$ exists with **always-true probability = 1** in the system, but harm is potential, not actual.

**Vector 2 — INITIATING EVENT (I):**
- The **first unplanned event** in the chain that *releases* the hazard or *creates the unsafe condition*. A single, identifiable failure.
- Examples: Pump seal failure → hydrocarbon release; corrosion of a pipe wall; operator misreading a level gauge.
- Notation: $I$ has occurrence frequency $f_I$ (events/year).

**Vector 3 — ENSUING / CONTRIBUTING EVENTS (E):**
- A *set* of secondary failures or conditions that *allow the initiating event to propagate* to the incident. Tye-Pearson explicitly identified these as **"the missing control"** elements — the independent protection layers (IPLs) that failed.
- Examples: Block valve fails to close on demand; flame detector inoperative; ESD logic un-tested; operator absent from field.

**THE ALIGNMENT CONDITION (CRITICAL):**
An **Incident (Inc)** occurs **if and only if**:  
$$\text{Hazard} \; \cap \; \text{Initiating Event} \; \cap \; (\text{All Contributing Events Align})$$  
In Boolean / probabilistic notation:
$$\text{Incident} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; H \;\land\; I \;\land\; \bigwedge_{k=1}^{n} E_k$$

This is the **mathematical origin of LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** in **IEC 61511**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables | Units / Domain |
|---|---------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Bird's Loss Ratio (Heinrich-Bird Update) | $R = 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 : 3000$ | Serious : Minor : Near-miss : Property : Vehicle | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Bird's Incident Frequency (1950s-70s data) | $f_{inc} = 5.7 \times 10^5$ | Disabling injuries per $10^8$ man-hours | $\text{injuries} \cdot \text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Tye-Pearson Combined Failure Probability (PFD model) | $P_{inc} = f_I \times \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$ | $f_I$ = initiating freq., $PFD_k$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of k-th layer | $\text{events} / \text{year}$ |
| 4 | Probability of Aligned Event (AND gate) | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Independent events | $[0, 1]$ |
| 5 | Probability of OR gate (Tye-Pearson redundant) | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$ | Independent | $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | Risk Definition (Tye-Pearson derived) | $\mathcal{R} = P_{inc} \times C$ | $P_{inc}$ = incident prob., $C$ = consequence | [loss units / year] |
| 7 | Layer of Protection (LOPA) | $PFD_{SIF} = \dfrac{P_{inc,\;target}}{f_I \times \prod PFD_{IPL}}$ | Target SIL derivation | $[10^{-2}, 10^{-5}]$ per demand |
| 8 | IEC 61511 SIL Band | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | SIL 1 to SIL 4 | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Safety Instrumentation

- **Bird's Model → Drives Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) Audits, OSHA 1910 Process Safety Management (PSM), ISO 45001 OHSMS.** Every modern incident investigation report (e.g., **Texas City Refinery 2005, BP Deepwater Horizon 2010**) uses Bird's five-level structure as the *spine* of root-cause analysis (RCA).
- **Tye-Pearson Model → Direct input to SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination in IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**, HAZOP node scoring, and **LOPA worksheets**. Every Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) in a refinery's Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system is *quantitatively* designed using the Tye-Pearson alignment logic.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mental Shortcut:** **"Bird = Why did it happen historically? (Cause-side, top-down)"** and **"Tye-Pearson = What must align right now? (Risk-side, statistical)".**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tye-Pearson Combined Probability of Incident

We will derive, in full algebraic detail, the **fundamental equation of Tye-Pearson system causation** as it is used in **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** and **SIL determination in IEC 61511**.

---

### Step 1 — Define the basic events

Let the system contain:
- One **Hazard** $H$ — *always present* in the system (inherent property of the process).
- One **Initiating Event** $I$ — first deviation from design intent.
- $n$ **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ — each with its own Probability of Failure on Demand, $PFD_k$.

In Boolean terms, the **Incident (Inc)** event occurs only when:
$$Inc = H \;\land\; I \;\land\; \overline{E_1} \;\land\; \overline{E_2} \;\land\; \dots \;\land\; \overline{E_n}$$

The "$\overline{E_k}$" denotes that *each* IPL must *fail* simultaneously with the others for the incident to propagate.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Product Rule for Independent Events

Since the IPLs are designed as **independent** (this is the central engineering requirement of IEC 61511 §11.4), the joint probability equals the product of the individual probabilities:

$$P_{inc} = P(H) \cdot P(I) \cdot P(\overline{E_1}) \cdot P(\overline{E_2}) \cdots P(\overline{E_n})$$

Recognize that $P(\overline{E_k}) = PFD_k$ (Probability of Failure on Demand of layer $k$).

$$P_{inc} = P(H) \cdot P(I) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$$

---

### Step 3 — Recognize the Hazard as "always-true"

For an *inherent* process hazard, the *presence* of the hazard is certain in the process unit. Therefore $P(H) = 1$ and drops out of the equation:

$$P_{inc} = P(I) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$$

Or, expressing the initiating event as a *frequency* $f_I$ (events per year), we convert the probability equation into a frequency equation:

$$f_{inc} = f_I \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$$

This is **Equation (3) of the KTU Formula Sheet**.

---

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A hydrocarbon storage sphere has:
- Initiating event: $f_I = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year (one major leak every 10 years from flange failure).
- Layer 1 (BPCS high-level alarm): $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$.
- Layer 2 (Operator intervention): $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$.
- Layer 3 (SIF — overfill protection): $PFD_3 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$.

**Find:** The resulting incident frequency, and identify the SIL band of the SIF.

---

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

Substitute into the Tye-Pearson–derived equation:
$$f_{inc} = f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3$$

$$f_{inc} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})$$

Group the powers of ten:
$$f_{inc} = 1 \times 10^{-(1+1+1+2)} \text{ per year}$$

$$f_{inc} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

This is **once every 100,000 years** — extremely low. The SIF's $PFD = 10^{-2}$ is consistent with **SIL 2** in IEC 61511 (since $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$).

---

### Step 5 — Reverse derivation: SIL target for the SIF

Suppose the **target tolerable incident frequency** is $f_{inc,\;target} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year (corporate HSE policy). Solve for the SIF $PFD_{SIF}$:

$$PFD_{SIF} = \frac{f_{inc,\;target}}{f_I \times PFD_{alarm} \times PFD_{operator}}$$

$$PFD_{SIF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{(1 \times 10^{-1})(1 \times 10^{-1})(1 \times 10^{-1})}$$

$$PFD_{SIF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$PFD = 10^{-3}$ falls in the **SIL 2** band of IEC 61511. The SIF must be designed, installed, and proof-tested to maintain $PFD \le 10^{-3}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Bird's vs. Tye-Pearson (Side-by-Side Derivation Logic)

| Analytical Lens | Bird's Law Model | Tye-Pearson Model |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Question it answers** | Why did this *specific* accident occur? | What is the *probability* of any accident occurring? |
| **Time orientation** | Retrospective (past → present) | Prospective (present → future) |
| **Mathematical form** | Causal chain (logical dependency) | Probability product (statistical independence) |
| **Output quantity** | Categorical (Loss / No-Loss) | Numerical ($f_{inc}$ or $P_{inc}$) |
| **Used in** | Incident investigation, BBS audits | LOPA, SIL determination |
| **Standards linked** | OSHA 1910, ISO 45001 | IEC 61508, IEC 61511, Seveso III |
| **Failure mode assumed** | Sequential cascade | Concurrent alignment |
| **How it treats the human** | Personal factors (80-90% Bird) | Operator as an IPL with $PFD \approx 10^{-1}$ |
| **Feedback considered** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Stochastic?** | No (deterministic categories) | Yes (probabilistic) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bird's Law Causation Model — Full Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LEVEL 1 LACK OF CONTROL]:::root --> L2[LEVEL 2 BASIC CAUSES]
    L2 --> PFa[Personal Factors]:::branch
    L2 --> JFa[Job or Systemic Factors]:::branch
    PFa --> L3
    JFa --> L3
    L3[LEVEL 3 IMMEDIATE CAUSES] --> SA[Substandard Acts]:::sympt
    L3 --> SC[Substandard Conditions]:::sympt
    SA --> L4
    SC --> L4
    L4[LEVEL 4 INCIDENT OR ACCIDENT] --> L5[LEVEL 5 LOSS]:::loss
    L5 --> LP[Loss to People]
    L5 --> LPR[Loss to Property]
    L5 --> LPD[Loss to Production]
    LP --> FB
    LPR --> FB
    LPD --> FB
    FB[FEEDBACK LOOP TO MANAGEMENT]:::fb -.-> L1
    classDef root fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef sympt fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#e7d6ff,stroke:#60c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Engineering Reading of the Flowchart (Step-by-step Interpretation):**
- The red **"Lack of Control"** node is the **root** — it is the management-level origin of all accidents.
- The yellow **"Basic Causes"** node splits into two parallel human-and-system vectors: personal deficiency (training, fatigue) and job deficiency (design, tools).
- The blue **"Immediate Causes"** node is the *visible* deviation — the moment an operator takes an unsafe action or a machine is left in an unsafe state.
- The purple **"Loss"** node branches into the **three P's: People, Property, Production** (the standard SIS consequence classification).
- The green **"Feedback Loop"** is the **defining feature of Bird's model** — every loss must retroactively tighten the Lack of Control at the top. This is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** safety management cycle.

---

## 4.2 Tye-Pearson System Causation Model — Convergent Vector Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hazard inherent always present]:::hz --> A
    I[Initiating Event]:::init --> A
    E1[Ensuing Event 1]:::en --> A
    E2[Ensuing Event 2]:::en --> A
    En[Ensuing Event n]:::en --> A
    A[ALIGNMENT CONDITION]:::align --> INC[INCIDENT OR MAJOR ACCIDENT]:::out
    INC --> C[Consequence People Property Environment]
    classDef hz fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef init fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#b80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef align fill:#dcd0ff,stroke:#609,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffd2f5,stroke:#909,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Engineering Reading of the Tye-Pearson Flowchart (Step-by-step):**
- The **red Hazard node** is *always-on* — it represents the *intrinsic* danger of the process (e.g., hydrocarbon + air mixture).
- The **yellow Initiating Event** is the *single trigger* — e.g., a flange leak.
- The **blue Ensuing Event nodes** represent the *failed protection layers* (failed block valve, inoperative alarm, absent operator).
- All four must **converge at the Alignment Condition** (purple AND-gate) for the **Incident** (pink) to occur.
- The **Consequence** node is the SIS outcome — used to compute Risk $\mathcal{R} = P_{inc} \times C$.

---

## 4.3 Combined Causation Stack — Bird + Tye-Pearson in SIS Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BIRD[Bird Lack of Control and Basic Causes]:::bird --> BEE
    BEE[Bird Immediate Causes]:::bird --> BIRD_INC
    BIRD_INC[Bird Incident]:::bird --> BLOSS
    BLOSS[Bird Loss]:::bird --> BLM
    BLM[Feedback to Management System]:::fb
    BLM --> SIL
    TP_H[Tye-Pearson Hazard]:::hz --> SIL[LOPA Worksheet SIL Determination IEC 61511]:::lopa
    TP_I[Initiating Event Frequency]:::init --> SIL
    TP_E[PFD of each IPL]:::en --> SIL
    SIL --> SIF[Safety Instrumented Function SIF design]:::sif
    SIF --> TUV[Proof Test Interval and Verification]:::sif
    classDef bird fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hz fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef init fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#b80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef lopa fill:#dcd0ff,stroke:#609,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sif fill:#d2ffd2,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Combined Diagram:**
This is the **unified architecture used in modern SIS design**. Bird's model provides the **management and behavioral foundation** (organizational learning after every loss). Tye-Pearson provides the **mathematical foundation** of LOPA, which in turn drives the **SIL assignment** of the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) in **IEC 61511**. The SIF is then **proof-tested** at intervals calculated from $PFD = \lambda \cdot T/2$ (for detected failures, where $\lambda$ is the dangerous failure rate and $T$ is the proof test interval).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the diagram is structured this way:** The Tye-Pearson inputs (Hazard, Initiating Event, PFD of IPLs) are *quantitative* (numerical, used in LOPA). The Bird's model outputs (Loss → Feedback) are *qualitative* (used for management review). Modern Safety Instrumentation systems **integrate both** — quantitative LOPA on the engineering side, qualitative Bird's review on the management side.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEICT862 Module 1]
**Differentiate between Bird's Law Causation Model and Tye-Pearson Causation Model. Highlight the role of the feedback loop in each.**

**Model Answer (3-mark KTU pattern):**
Bird's Law (1969) is a *chronological, multi-causation model* with **five sequential levels** — Lack of Control, Basic Causes, Immediate Causes, Incident, Loss — and an **explicit feedback loop** from Loss back to Lack of Control that drives organizational learning. Tye-Pearson (1970s) is a *probabilistic, system-safety model* where an incident requires the **simultaneous alignment** of three independent vectors — Hazard, Initiating Event, and Ensuing/Contributing Events — combined by the product of their probabilities. Tye-Pearson **does not have a feedback loop**; instead, the consequence feeds into **LOPA (IEC 61511)** for SIL determination.

> **[Valuation key — 1 mark each for: definition of Bird, definition of Tye-Pearson, statement of feedback loop role.]**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEICT862 Module 1]
**Explain the significance of the "Alignment Condition" in the Tye-Pearson system causation model. How does this concept lead to SIL determination in IEC 61511?**

**Model Answer (3-mark KTU pattern):**
The **Alignment Condition** states that an incident occurs only when the **Hazard, Initiating Event, and all Ensuing Events (failed IPLs) are simultaneously present** — a Boolean AND condition expressed mathematically as:
$$P_{inc} = f_I \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$$
This equation is the **core of LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** in IEC 61511. By setting a corporate *target tolerable incident frequency* $f_{inc,\;target}$ and dividing by the product of the non-SIF IPLs, the engineer solves for the **$PFD$ required of the SIF**, which is then mapped to a **SIL band** (SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$).

> **[Valuation key — 1 mark: AND-gate logic; 1 mark: equation; 1 mark: link to SIL bands.]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Bird's Law Focus** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **Bird's Law Multi-Causation Model**. List the **five sequential levels** and the **two sub-branches** at Level 2. Discuss the role of the **feedback loop** in modern OHS management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process plant records the following incident data over 10 million man-hours of work: 5 fatalities, 25 lost-time injuries, 750 first-aid cases, and 15,000 near-misses. Verify whether the data satisfies the **Bird-Heinrich statistical ratio** ($1 : 10 : 30 : 600 : 3000$). Comment on the management-level implications if the ratio is **skewed toward fatalities**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**
- **Diagram** (3 marks): Five-level vertical chain with a feedback loop returning from Loss to Lack of Control. Show sub-branches at Level 2 (Personal Factors, Job Factors) and Level 3 (Substandard Acts, Substandard Conditions). *(See SECTION 4.1 for the reference diagram.)*
- **Five levels with definitions** (2 marks): Lack of Control (management), Basic Causes (origin), Immediate Causes (symptom), Incident (event), Loss (people/property/production).
- **Feedback loop role** (2 marks): Loss forces management to re-examine Lack of Control — this is the *Plan-Do-Check-Act* cycle of ISO 45001 and forms the empirical basis of Behavior-Based Safety (BBS).

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**
- **Given data:** Fatalities = 5, Lost-time = 25, First-aid = 750, Near-miss = 15,000. *(We assume the standard 5-level Heinrich-Bird ratio starts from Fatality = 1.)*
- **Normalized to "1" fatality:**
   - Fatality ratio: $5/5 = 1$
   - Lost-time: $25/5 = 5$
   - First-aid: $750/5 = 150$
   - Near-miss: $15{,}000/5 = 3000$
- **Standard Bird-Heinrich ratio:** $1 : 10 : 30 : 600 : 3000$
- **Comparison:**
   - Fatality-to-Lost-time: Plant has $5$ vs standard $10$ → **plant is over-reporting fatalities relative to lost-time injuries** (severity skew).
   - Fatality-to-First-aid: Plant has $150$ vs standard $30$ → fatalities **5× higher than the safe ratio**.
   - Fatality-to-Near-miss: $3000$ matches the standard (good near-miss reporting culture).
- **Management implications (2 marks):** The *severity skew* indicates a **broken management control system** — likely insufficient hazard identification, inadequate Permit-to-Work, poor MOC, or absence of effective IPLs. **Bird's feedback loop demands immediate management review** to address Lack of Control, re-baseline procedures, intensify BBS audits, and consider a HAZOP revalidation. The correct corporate action is **not** to reduce near-miss reporting (which is healthy) but to investigate *why severity is rising* — typically pointing to an aging asset, deferred maintenance, or a toxic management culture.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tye-Pearson Focus** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **Tye-Pearson System Causation Model** with a neat labeled diagram. Define the **Hazard, Initiating Event, and Ensuing Events**. State and derive the **alignment equation** for incident frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor has the following protection layers: (i) BPCS high-pressure trip with $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$, (ii) Operator manual intervention with $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$, (iii) Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) with $PFD_3 = ?$. The **initiating event frequency** is $f_I = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year. The **corporate target incident frequency** is $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year. Calculate the **$PFD$ required of the SIF** and identify the **SIL band** per IEC 61511. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**
- **Diagram** (2 marks): Three vectors (Hazard, Initiating Event, Ensuing Events) converging at an AND-gate, output is the Incident. *(See SECTION 4.2.)*
- **Definitions** (2 marks):
   - **Hazard:** inherent property of the system with *potential* to cause harm; $P(H) = 1$.
   - **Initiating Event:** the *first* unplanned event that releases the hazard; quantified by $f_I$.
   - **Ensuing Events:** the *set of failed IPLs* that allow the chain to propagate; each characterized by $PFD_k$.
- **Derivation** (3 marks):
   Starting from Boolean AND:
   $$Inc = H \land I \land \overline{E_1} \land \dots \land \overline{E_n}$$
   Since IPLs are independent:
   $$P_{inc} = P(H) \cdot P(I) \cdot \prod PFD_k$$
   With $P(H) = 1$ and replacing $P(I)$ with $f_I$:
   $$f_{inc} = f_I \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$$

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Write the LOPA equation:**
   $$f_{inc,\;target} = f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_{SIF}$$
- **Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
   $$1 \times 10^{-6} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times PFD_{SIF}$$
- **Step 3 — Multiply the known PFDs:**
   $$(1 \times 10^{-1})^3 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$
- **Step 4 — Solve for $PFD_{SIF}$:**
   $$PFD_{SIF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$
- **Step 5 — Identify the SIL band per IEC 61511 Table:**
   $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2**.
- **Step 6 — Engineering action (1 mark):** The SIF must be designed, installed, and proof-tested per IEC 61511 §16 to maintain $PFD \le 10^{-3}$. Using $PFD = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T/2$ (for detected dangerous failures), with a typical $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h, the maximum proof-test interval is $T = 4{,}000$ hours (≈ 6 months).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing "Hazard" with "Risk"** in Tye-Pearson — Hazard is the *source*; Risk = Probability × Consequence.
> 2. **Forgetting the AND-multiplication rule** in the alignment equation — many students wrongly *add* PFDs instead of *multiplying* them.
> 3. **Dropping the $P(H) = 1$ step silently** in derivations — KTU award a full mark for the *recognition* that the hazard is *always present*; do not skip.
> 4. **For Bird's model, missing the "Feedback Loop"** — it is the *distinguishing* feature of Bird's model; answer without it loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing the "10" in Heinrich's 1:10:30:600:3000 with the SIL bands of 10⁻¹⁻⁻⁻⁻⁰·⁵** — these are different scales. Heinrich's ratio is dimensionless; SIL $PFD$ is per-demand probability.
> 6. **Forgetting the **dimensions** of $f_{inc}$** — write "per year" explicitly; it is the most-skipped unit in KTU scripts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU One-Page Revision Notes (Must memorize before exam):**

- ✅ **Bird's Law = Multi-Causation Model (1969, F.E. Bird Jr.)** with **5 levels**: Lack of Control → Basic Causes → Immediate Causes → Incident → Loss. **Feedback loop** is the signature feature.
- ✅ **Level 2 (Basic Causes)** branches into **Personal Factors** and **Job/Systemic Factors** (Bird's 1974 study: 80–90% personal).
- ✅ **Level 3 (Immediate Causes)** branches into **Substandard Acts** and **Substandard Conditions**.
- ✅ **Level 5 (Loss)** is the **3-P model: People, Property, Production**.
- ✅ **Bird-Heinrich Ratio = 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 : 3000** (Fatality : Lost-time : First-aid : Property : Near-miss).
- ✅ **Tye-Pearson = System Causation Model** based on **alignment of Hazard + Initiating Event + Ensuing Events**.
- ✅ **Tye-Pearson Master Equation:** $f_{inc} = f_I \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} PFD_k$.
- ✅ **Hazard is always present** → $P(H) = 1$ → drops out of equation.
- ✅ **LOPA equation for SIF:** $PFD_{SIF} = \dfrac{f_{inc,\;target}}{f_I \times \prod PFD_{IPLs}}$.
- ✅ **SIL Bands (IEC 61511):** SIL 1 → $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$, SIL 2 → $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$, SIL 3 → $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$, SIL 4 → $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$.
- ✅ **Bird's Model uses** → ISO 45001, OSHA PSM, BBS, Incident Investigation (RCA).
- ✅ **Tye-Pearson uses** → IEC 61508/61511, LOPA, HAZOP, Seveso Directive, Major Hazard Control.
- ✅ **Mental Hook:** *Bird = Past/Why (Qualitative, Sequential)* ; *Tye-Pearson = Future/How-likely (Quantitative, Probabilistic)*.
- ✅ **Equation Type:** Bird's is *categorical/Boolean cascade*; Tye-Pearson is *probabilistic product* (independent events).
- ✅ **Consequence Triplet:** People, Property, Environment (modern extension of Bird's 3-P Loss).
- ✅ **Always state units** in numerical problems: $f_{inc}$ in **per year**, $PFD$ is **dimensionless per demand**, $\lambda$ in **per hour**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Standards, Safety Terminology, Critical Control Systems, Safety Shutdown Systems, Protective Instrumented Systems, Equipment Protection Systems, Emergency Shutdown Systems, Safety Critical Systems, Interlocks, Safety Instrumented Systems, ISA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Standards, Safety Terminology, Critical Control Systems, Safety Shutdown Systems, Protective Instrumented Systems, Equipment Protection Systems, Emergency Shutdown Systems, Safety Critical Systems, Interlocks, Safety Instrumented Systems, ISA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety Standards — The Governing Framework

**Formal Definition:**
Safety Standards are codified technical documents (published by international bodies like **IEC**, **ISA**, **ISO**, and **ANSI**) that specify the minimum functional, performance, and procedural requirements for designing, operating, and maintaining safety instrumented systems in process industries. They provide the legal and engineering framework ensuring that Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) achieve a defined **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** consistent with tolerable risk criteria.

The three foundational standards governing functional safety are:

| Standard | Full Name | Scope |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **IEC 61508** | Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems | Generic / Cross-industry (umbrella standard) |
| **IEC 61511** | Functional Safety – Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector | Process industries (refineries, chemical, petrochemical) |
| **ISA 84.00.01 / IEC 61511** | Application of Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industries | Equivalent USA adoption of IEC 61511 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** IEC 61508 is the **umbrella (generic) standard**; IEC 61511 is the **process-industry-specific implementation**. For PEICT862, IEC 61511 is the most frequently examined standard.

### Conceptual Analogy — Safety Standards are the "Rulebook"

Imagine driving a car without traffic rules — chaos, accidents, no accountability. **Safety Standards are exactly the traffic rulebook for industrial plants.** They don't *build* the brakes (that's the engineering); they tell the engineer *how good* the brakes must be (stopping distance, response time, failure rate). Just as rules differ between highways and city streets, **IEC 61508** sets the broad rules for all electronics, while **IEC 61511** narrows the rules for oil refineries, chemical plants, and LNG terminals.

---

## 1.2 Safety Terminology — The Language of Functional Safety

Safety has a **rigorous vocabulary** — misusing a term can mean the difference between a safe plant and a disaster. Below are the **KTU-mandated definitions**:

> [!NOTE]
> **SIL — Safety Integrity Level**
> A discrete level (1 to 4) corresponding to a range of values for the probability of failure on demand (PFD) or the probability of a dangerous failure per hour (PFH). SIL is the **target reliability band** for a safety function.

| Term | Expansion | Meaning (Plain English) |
|------|-----------|-------------------------|
| **PFD** | Probability of Failure on Demand | Chance that the safety system fails *when asked to act* |
| **PFH** | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour | Dangerous failure rate during continuous operation (high-demand mode) |
| **RRF** | Risk Reduction Factor | How much the safety system *reduces* the risk (1/PFD) |
| **SIF** | Safety Instrumented Function | A single safety task (sensor + logic + final element) |
| **SIS** | Safety Instrumented System | The complete hardware/software implementing one or more SIFs |
| **SIL** | Safety Integrity Level | Reliability target (1, 2, 3, or 4) |
| **HFT** | Hardware Fault Tolerance | Ability to function despite N faults (e.g., 1oo2D, 2oo3) |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Average uptime before failure |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Average downtime for repair |
| **λ** | Failure Rate | Failures per hour (λD = dangerous, λS = safe, λDD = dangerous detected) |
| **Tproof** | Proof Test Interval | Time between manual tests that reveal hidden failures |
| **SFF** | Safe Failure Fraction | Proportion of failures that lead to a *safe* (non-dangerous) state |
| **DC** | Diagnostic Coverage | Fraction of failures detected by automatic self-diagnostics |
| **MooN** | M out of N voting | N channels installed; M must agree to trip (e.g., 2oo3) |

### Conceptual Analogy — SIL is the "Safety Class"

Think of an airplane, a car, and a bicycle. Airplanes demand the **highest safety** (SIL 3–4 equivalent in aviation DO-178C), cars are middle (SIL 2 in automotive ISO 26262), and bicycles are low (no SIL). **SIL is the engineering "trust grade"** assigned to a safety function based on how dangerous its failure would be.

---

## 1.3 Critical Control Systems (CCS)

**Formal Definition:**
A Critical Control System is a control system whose failure can directly cause an immediate, unacceptable hazard to human life, the environment, or major capital assets. It continuously modulates process variables (temperature, pressure, flow) within strict safety envelopes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English:** Critical Control Systems are the "guardians of the steady state" — they keep the process *inside* safe operating limits using continuous feedback (PID control, advanced process control).

**Example:** A reactor temperature controller preventing runaway exothermic reaction.

---

## 1.4 Safety Shutdown Systems (SSD)

**Formal Definition:**
A Safety Shutdown System is a protective system that detects a developing hazardous condition and takes the process to a **safe state** by de-energizing energy sources (closing valves, stopping pumps, tripping motors) upon detection of a pre-defined trip condition.

**Difference from CCS:**
- CCS = **prevents** excursions (continuous control)
- SSD = **responds to** excursions (discrete trip action)

---

## 1.5 Protective Instrumented Systems (PIS)

**Formal Definition:**
A Protective Instrumented System is the complete instrumentation package (sensors, logic solver, final elements, valves) designed to bring the process to a safe state when predetermined abnormal conditions are detected. It is essentially the *instrumentation realization* of a safety function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern KTU/IEC terminology, **PIS ≈ SIS** — both implement Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs). The term "PIS" emphasizes the *protective* purpose (vs. purely informational control).

---

## 1.6 Equipment Protection Systems

**Formal Definition:**
Equipment Protection Systems are dedicated safety systems designed to prevent **mechanical or electrical damage** to critical rotating and static equipment (turbines, compressors, pumps, motors, transformers). They protect *assets*, not necessarily human life, although the two often overlap.

**Examples:**
- Turbine overspeed trip
- Compressor surge protection
- Motor overcurrent relay
- Pump low-flow protection
- Bearing temperature trip

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** This is the **"equipment health insurance policy"** — it pays out (trips) when the machine is about to die, even if human life is not directly threatened.

---

## 1.7 Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD / ESDS)

**Formal Definition:**
An Emergency Shutdown System is a **highly reliable, independent** protective system that takes rapid, decisive action to bring an entire process unit (or the whole plant) to a safe state during a major emergency such as fire, gas release, or major equipment failure. ESD is activated either automatically by sensors or manually by emergency push-buttons.

**ESD Levels (KTU Frequently Asked):**

| Level | Action | Speed | Examples |
|-------|--------|-------|----------|
| **ESD-0** | Total plant shutdown | < 1 s | Catastrophic fire, total power loss |
| **ESD-1** | Unit shutdown | 1–5 s | Major gas leak, reactor runaway |
| **ESD-2** | Equipment shutdown | 5–30 s | Compressor trip, pump failure |
| **ESD-3** | Equipment local shutdown | 30–120 s | Sub-system trips |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESD systems are SIL 2 or SIL 3** in nearly all KTU-syllabus examples. They are *always* physically and logically separate from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS).

---

## 1.8 Safety Critical Systems (SCS)

**Formal Definition:**
A Safety Critical System is any system whose **failure could result in loss of life, serious injury, or significant environmental/economic damage**. The term is broader than SIS — it includes emergency response, fire & gas detection, BMS, and even some IT systems that support safety functions.

**KTU Distinction:**
- **Safety Critical System (SCS)** = broad category (philosophy, governance, lifecycle)
- **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** = specific implementation of safety functions using instrumentation (per IEC 61511)

---

## 1.9 Interlocks

**Formal Definition:**
An Interlock is a **logic-based protective mechanism** that prevents an operation from being performed in an unsafe sequence or until predefined permissive conditions are satisfied. Interlocks can be implemented mechanically, electrically (relay logic), electronically (solid-state), or via software (PLC/SIS).

**Types of Interlocks:**

| Type | Function | Example |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Permissive Interlock** | Allows action *only if* conditions are met | Pump start requires lube oil pressure |
| **Preventive Interlock** | Prevents action if conditions are unsafe | Valve cannot open if upstream pump is OFF |
| **Sequencing Interlock** | Enforces correct operational order | Compressor start: cooling → lube → main motor |
| **Mechanical Interlock** | Physical hardware (cams, keys) | Kirk-key interlock in switchgear |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interlocks are the "guardrails" of operations** — they don't drive the process; they *block* incorrect actions.

---

## 1.10 Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)

**Formal Definition:**
A Safety Instrumented System is an **independent** system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements designed to:
1. Take the process to a safe state
2. Maintain the safe state
3. Permit the safe state to be exited only when process conditions allow

SIS implements one or more **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)**, each with a target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. Per IEC 61511, the SIS must be **separate from the BPCS** (Basic Process Control System) both physically and functionally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:** *"SIS = Sensor + Logic Solver + Final Element, designed to a target SIL, independent of BPCS."*

---

## 1.11 ISA — The International Society of Automation

**Formal Definition:**
The **International Society of Automation (ISA)**, formerly the Instrument Society of America, is a globally recognized non-profit technical society that develops standards, certifies professionals, and publishes technical resources for automation and control engineering.

**ISA Standards Relevant to KTU PEICT862:**

| Standard | Title | Relevance |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **ISA 84.00.01** | Application of SIS for the Process Industries | Harmonized with IEC 61511 |
| **ISA 88** | Batch Control Models | Reference for batch process safety |
| **ISA 95** | Enterprise-Control System Integration | Defines BPCS–SIS interface |
| **ISA 101** | Human-Machine Interfaces for Process Automation | HMI design for safety systems |
| **ISA 18.2** | Management of Alarm Systems for the Process Industries | Alarm rationalization (related to operator response in SIL calculations) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** ISA is the **"trade guild"** of instrumentation engineers. It sets the *vocabulary* (terminology), the *rules* (standards), and the *credentials* (ISA 84 SIS SIL certification, CAP, CCST).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511 Phases)

The standards mandate a **lifecycle approach** to safety. Memorizing these phases is essential for 14-mark KTU questions.

**Phase-by-Phase Logic:**

1. **Concept & Scope Definition** — Identify the process, hazards, scope of study.
2. **Hazard & Risk Assessment (HRA)** — Identify hazards, estimate frequency & consequence.
3. **Risk Reduction Specification** — Decide *how much* risk must be reduced → assign **target SIL** to each SIF.
4. **SIS Design & Engineering** — Specify sensors, logic solver, final elements; calculate achieved PFD.
5. **Installation, Commissioning & Validation** — Verify SIS meets the SIL specification.
6. **Operation & Maintenance** — Proof testing, calibration, bypass management.
7. **Modification** — Manage changes via Management of Change (MOC) procedure.
8. **Decommissioning** — Safe shutdown and disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trigger Phrase:** *"Explain the functional safety lifecycle as per IEC 61511"* — this lifecycle is the **complete 14-mark answer skeleton**.

---

## 2.2 Risk Graph Method for SIL Determination

The **Risk Graph** (IEC 61511 Annex A) is the most common method for assigning SIL in process plants. It uses four parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Options |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Consequence (C) | C | C1 (minor injury), C2 (serious injury/death), C3 (multiple fatalities), C4 (catastrophe) |
| Occupancy (F) | F | F1 (rare), F2 (frequent) |
| Avoidance (P) | P | P1 (possible under specific conditions), P2 (impossible) |
| Demand Rate (W) | W | W1 (< 0.1/year), W2 (0.1–1/year), W3 (> 1/year) |

**Logic Flow:**

$$
\text{Risk} = f(C, F, P, W) \rightarrow \text{Reference Table} \rightarrow \text{SIL (1, 2, 3)}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

1. Start at parameter **C** (most severe consequence).
2. Adjust based on **F** (how often people are exposed).
3. Adjust based on **P** (can they escape?).
4. Adjust based on **W** (how often the demand occurs).
5. Map to a cell in the risk graph → outputs **Required SIL**.

---

## 2.3 SIL Probability Targets — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three KTU Mandatory Tables** — write them verbatim in exams.

**Table A — Low Demand Mode (≤ 1 demand/year) — PFD basis**

| SIL | PFDavg Target | RRF Target |
|-----|---------------|------------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10–100 |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100–1,000 |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000–10,000 |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10,000–100,000 |

**Table B — High Demand / Continuous Mode — PFH basis**

| SIL | PFH Target (per hour) |
|-----|------------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ |

**Table C — Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) Requirements**

| SFF | HFT = 0 | HFT = 1 | HFT = 2 |
|-----|---------|---------|---------|
| < 60% | SIL 1 | SIL 1 | SIL 2 |
| 60–90% | SIL 2 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| 90–99% | SIL 3 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |
| ≥ 99% | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | SIL 4 |

---

## 2.4 Core Formulas — PFD Calculations

### Formula 1: PFD for a Single 1oo1 Channel

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_{DD} \cdot T_{repair})^2}{3}
$$

where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (failures/hour)
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous detected failure rate
- $T_{proof}$ = proof test interval (hours)
- $T_{repair}$ = mean time to repair (hours)

**Derivation logic:** The first term models a *uniform distribution* of undetected failures over the test interval; the second term models the brief period during which a detected failure is being repaired.

### Formula 2: PFD for a 1oo2 Channel (Two sensors, one trip)

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2}{3} + \lambda_{DD} \cdot T_{repair}
$$

**Logic:** A 1oo2 system fails *only if both* channels fail dangerously — hence the **square** of the failure probability. This gives much lower PFD (higher SIL) than 1oo1.

### Formula 3: PFD for a 2oo3 Channel (Voted, highest availability)

$$
PFD_{2oo3} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2 + \lambda_{DD} \cdot T_{repair}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **2oo3 is used where both high safety AND high availability (avoiding spurious trips) are required**, e.g., turbine trips, fire & gas detection.

### Formula 4: Total PFD of a SIF (Sensor + Logic + Final Element)

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{Final\,Element}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Killer Fact:** The final element (e.g., shutdown valve) typically contributes **50–70%** of total PFD. Designers must give it the most attention.

### Formula 5: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

### Formula 6: Achieved vs. Required SIL

$$
\text{SIS is acceptable if } PFD_{SIF\,achieved} \le PFD_{SIF\,required}
$$

### Formula 7: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) Relation

$$
MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Formula 8: Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}
$$

### Formula 9: Diagnostic Coverage (DC)

$$
DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D\,total}} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}
$$

### Formula 10: Equivalent Average Unavailability

$$
t_{CE} = PFD_{avg} \cdot T_{mission} \quad \text{(where } T_{mission} = 1 \text{ year in low-demand mode)}
$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|----------------------|
| SIL assignment via Risk Graph | Refinery, LNG terminal safety design |
| PFD calculation for SIF | Petrochemical batch reactor emergency cooling |
| SFF/HFT tables | Selection of redundant transmitters (1oo2D, 2oo3) |
| Proof test interval optimization | Offshore platform maintenance cost reduction |
| Independence from BPCS | Compliance audit for API RP 14C, IEC 61511 |

---

## 2.6 SIS Architecture Block Identification

A typical SIF in **low-demand** mode (per IEC 61511) comprises:

$$
\text{SIF} = \underbrace{S}_{\text{Sensor (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3)}} + \underbrace{L}_{\text{Logic Solver (PLC/SIS)}} + \underbrace{FE}_{\text{Final Element (valve, breaker)}}
$$

**Voting Notation:** $\text{MooN}$ — e.g., $\text{1oo2}$ means "1 out of 2 must trip."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *1oo1 = simple, low SIL. 1oo2 = high SIL, more spurious trips. 2oo3 = high SIL + high availability.*

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: PFD for 1oo1 Channel

We model a single channel operating in **low-demand mode**. Failures occur at rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (per hour) and remain undetected until the next proof test.

**Step 1 — Uniform Hazard Assumption**

We assume failures are equally likely to occur at any moment during the test interval $T_{proof}$. This is a standard conservative assumption.

**Step 2 — Average Unavailability Over Test Interval**

For a failure occurring at time $t$ after a successful proof test, the channel remains unsafe from $t$ until $t = T_{proof}$ (end of test). The average downtime contribution is:

$$
\text{Average downtime} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t \cdot \lambda_{DU} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Integral**

$$
= \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Detected Failure Downtime**

The second term accounts for **detected** dangerous failures, which are repaired with average downtime $T_{repair}$:

$$
\text{Detected downtime} = \lambda_{DD} \cdot \frac{T_{repair}^2}{3}
$$

(The $1/3$ comes from the triangular distribution of repair times over $T_{repair}$.)

**Step 5 — Final Expression**

$$
\boxed{
PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} + \frac{\lambda_{DD} \cdot T_{repair}^2}{3}
}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

Given:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /h
- $\lambda_{DD} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /h
- $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (1 year)
- $T_{repair} = 8$ h

**Compute Term 1:**

$$
\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Compute Term 2:**

$$
\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8^2}{3} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 64}{3} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} + 1.07 \times 10^{-4} \approx 8.87 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Check SIL Band:** $8.87 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ → **SIL 1**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: PFD for 1oo2 Architecture

In 1oo2 voting, the SIF **trips** if **any** of the two channels trips. Hence the SIF **fails** only if **both** channels fail dangerously.

**Step 1 — Failure Logic**

The 1oo2 system is unavailable if channel A *and* channel B both have undetected dangerous failures simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Joint Probability (assuming independent failures)**

The fraction of time channel A is in failed state is approximately $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$. The probability that channel B is also failed at the same instant is the *square* (independent events):

$$
PFD_{1oo2} \approx (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2
$$

**Step 3 — Add the Detected Term**

The detected failure term is similar to 1oo1 but doubled (two channels can each be in repair):

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2 + \lambda_{DD} \cdot T_{repair}
$$

(For simplicity, IEC 61511 uses the more conservative 6 simplified formulas — the one above is the standard $T_{proof}$ model.)

**Step 4 — Compare with 1oo1**

Using the previous numerical data:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = (2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^2 + 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8
$$

$$
= (1.752 \times 10^{-2})^2 + 4 \times 10^{-5} = 3.07 \times 10^{-4} + 4 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.47 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Check SIL Band:** $3.47 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ → **SIL 2 or 3** (a clear improvement over 1oo1).

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Total SIF PFD (Sensor + Logic + Final Element)

**Step 1 — Series Model**

A SIF fails if **any one** of its three sub-systems (sensor, logic solver, final element) fails. Failures are independent.

**Step 2 — Apply Probability Addition Rule**

For small probabilities, the union of independent events is approximately the sum:

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 1 - (1 - PFD_{Sensor})(1 - PFD_{Logic})(1 - PFD_{FE})
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate (because PFD values are small)**

$$
PFD_{SIF} \approx PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{FE}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

| Sub-system | PFD |
|------------|-----|
| Sensor (1oo2) | $3.47 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Logic Solver (1oo1D) | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Final Element (1oo1) | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 3.47 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 9.47 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Check SIL Band:** $9.47 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ → **SIL 2 (borderline SIL 3)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The **final element (5×10⁻⁴)** contributes ~53% of the total PFD — confirming the KTU golden rule: *final elements dominate SIF unreliability*.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: RRF and Risk Tolerance Check

**Step 1 — Compute RRF from Achieved PFD**

$$
RRF_{achieved} = \frac{1}{PFD_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{9.47 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1056
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RRF Required from Tolerable Risk**

If tolerable event frequency is $10^{-5}$/year and unmitigated event frequency is $10^{-1}$/year:

$$
RRF_{required} = \frac{f_{unmitigated}}{f_{tolerable}} = \frac{10^{-1}}{10^{-5}} = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compare**

$$
RRF_{achieved} = 1056 < RRF_{required} = 10{,}000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Design is INADEQUATE}
$$

**Step 4 — Action:** Upgrade sensor to 2oo3, or shorten $T_{proof}$, or add a second final element.

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — System Comparison Table

| Architecture | PFD Expression | Availability | Spurious Trip Rate | Use Case |
|--------------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|----------|
| 1oo1 | $\frac{\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}{2}$ | Low | Low | Low SIL (SIL 1) |
| 1oo2 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2$ | Low | High | High SIL, budget allows down time |
| 2oo2 | $\frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2}{3}$ | High | Medium | High SIL + high availability |
| 2oo3 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2$ | Highest | Medium | Fire & Gas, turbine trip |
| 1oo2D | $\frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2}{3} + \lambda_{DD} T_{repair}$ | High | Low | Most common modern SIS design |

(Where D = with diagnostic, using a de-energize-to-trip principle.)

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical Example — Comprehensive SIL Verification

**Problem:** A reactor high-pressure SIF uses:
- 1oo2 pressure transmitters: $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h
- 1oo1D logic solver: $PFD_{Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$
- 1oo2D shutdown valves: $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/h each, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h

Required SIL: **2** → PFD $\le 10^{-2}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sensor PFD:**

$$
PFD_{Sensor} = (1 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^2 = (8.76 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.67 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — Logic Solver PFD:**

$$
PFD_{Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Element PFD (1oo2D):**

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760)^2}{3} = \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Total:**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-5} + 6.4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.33 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify against SIL 2 limit ($10^{-2}$):**

$$
1.33 \times 10^{-4} < 10^{-2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SIL 2 achieved (with margin of ~75x)}.
$$

**Step 6 — Achieved RRF:**

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{1.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 7518
$$

This meets any reasonable RRF up to ~7,500.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1 Concept and Scope] --> B[Phase 2 Hazard and Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Phase 3 Risk Reduction Specification SIL Assignment]
    C --> D[Phase 4 SIS Design and Engineering]
    D --> E[Phase 5 Installation Commissioning and Validation]
    E --> F[Phase 6 Operation and Maintenance]
    F --> G[Phase 7 Modification Management of Change]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Phase 8 Decommissioning]
    H --> I[End of Lifecycle]
    D -.Feedback Loop.-> B
    F -.Proof Testing.-> E
```

**Explanation:** The diagram shows the standard IEC 61511 lifecycle. Notice the feedback loops: operation triggers proof testing (re-validation), and any modification re-enters the risk assessment phase. This is the **"cradle-to-grave"** safety philosophy demanded by KTU examiners.

---

## 4.2 SIS Block Diagram — Sensor to Logic to Final Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable e.g. Pressure] --> B[Sensor 1oo2 Transmitter]
    B --> C[Logic Solver PLC or Safety PLC]
    C --> D[Final Element 1oo2D Shutdown Valve]
    D --> E[Safe State e.g. Isolated Vessel]
    C --> F[Alarm and HMI to Operator]
    F --> G[Operator Action Manual Backup]
    H[Proof Test Interval Timer] -.Periodic Test.-> B
    H -.Periodic Test.-> D
    I[BPCS Basic Control System] -.Independent From.-> C
```

**Explanation:** This is the **canonical SIS block diagram** used in every KTU answer on "Explain the architecture of a Safety Instrumented System." Key points:
- The SIS is **independent** of the BPCS.
- The final element is **separately proof-tested** (the dotted lines indicate proof-test inputs).
- The operator is **informed** but does not normally control SIS action.

---

## 4.3 ESD Hierarchy in a Refinery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire and Gas Detection F and G] --> B[ESD 0 Total Plant Shutdown]
    A --> C[ESD 1 Unit Shutdown]
    C --> D[ESD 2 Equipment Shutdown]
    D --> E[ESD 3 Local Equipment Trip]
    F[Operator Emergency Push Button] --> B
    F --> C
    G[Process Trip e.g. High Pressure] --> C
    G --> D
    H[Vibration or Temperature Trip] --> E
```

**Explanation:** The cascading structure of ESD levels. **ESD-0 is the most drastic** (used for total fire/gas), **ESD-3 is the most local** (single equipment). The hierarchy prevents over-reaction — a local fault should not shut down the entire plant.

---

## 4.4 Risk Graph Decision Tree (SIL Determination)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Hazard Identification] --> B{Consequence Severity}
    B -->|C1 Minor| C[Possible SIL 1]
    B -->|C2 Serious| D{Frequency of Exposure}
    B -->|C3 Catastrophic| E{Avoidance Possibility}
    B -->|C4 Multiple Fatalities| F[At least SIL 3]
    D -->|F1 Rare| C
    D -->|F2 Frequent| G[Increase SIL]
    E -->|P1 Possible| H{Demand Rate}
    E -->|P2 Impossible| I[SIL upgraded]
    H -->|W1 Low| J[Reference Table Lookup]
    H -->|W2 Medium| J
    H -->|W3 High| K[SIL 2 minimum]
    J --> L[Final SIL 1 2 or 3]
```

**Explanation:** This mirrors the IEC 61511 Risk Graph method. In KTU answers, students should always mention: *"SIL is determined by traversing the four parameters — Consequence, Occupancy, Avoidance, and Demand Rate — to a reference cell."*

---

## 4.5 Independence of SIS from BPCS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BPCS[Basic Process Control System]
        A1[DCS Controller] --> A2[Control Valve]
        A1 --> A3[Operator HMI]
    end
    subgraph SIS[Safety Instrumented System]
        B1[Safety PLC] --> B2[Shutdown Valve]
        B1 --> B3[SIS HMI Minimal]
    end
    subgraph Field[Process Plant]
        X[Reactor] --> A2
        X --> B2
    end
    A1 -.Cannot Read.-> B1
    B1 -.Cannot Read.-> A1
```

**Explanation:** The double-wall separation. The BPCS controls the plant under normal conditions; the SIS takes over **only** when a trip condition is detected. The two systems are **physically and logically independent** — this is a fundamental IEC 61511 requirement.

---

## 4.6 MooN Voting Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voting Architecture] --> B[1oo1 Single Channel]
    A --> C[1oo2 One of Two]
    A --> D[2oo2 Two of Two]
    A --> E[2oo3 Two of Three]
    B -->|Lowest SIL| F[Use only for SIL 1]
    C -->|Highest SIL| G[Use for SIL 2 3]
    D -->|High Availability| H[Use where spurious trips costly]
    E -->|Best Compromise| I[Use for Fire Gas Turbine]
```

**Explanation:** Voting architectures are a **favourite KTU 7-mark question**. The key trade-off: **higher safety (1oo2, 2oo3) costs more and increases spurious trip rate; higher availability (2oo2, 2oo3) reduces nuisance shutdowns.**
