# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) and list the four SILs with their PFD ranges in low-demand mode. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete reliability target (1, 2, 3, or 4) assigned to a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) that specifies the permissible probability of failure on demand (PFDavg) in low-demand mode, or the permissible probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFH) in high-demand/continuous mode.
>
> **PFD Ranges in Low-Demand Mode (IEC 61511):**
>
> | SIL | PFDavg Target | RRF |
> |-----|---------------|-----|
> | 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |
> | 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 |
> | 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 |
> | 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 |
>
> **Conclusion:** Higher SIL → lower PFD → higher reliability requirement → more redundancy and shorter proof-test intervals.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between a Safety Shutdown System (SSD) and a Basic Process Control System (BPCS). `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> | Parameter | BPCS | SSD (SIS) |
> |-----------|------|-----------|
> | Purpose | Maintain process *within* normal operating window | Bring process to *safe state* on demand |
> | Action type | Continuous (PID control) | Discrete (trip/no-trip) |
> | Failure consequence | Process upset, not catastrophic | Catastrophic hazard if it fails |
> | Independence | Not required from anything | Must be independent of BPCS |
> | Target reliability | Normal (e.g., 99%) | High (SIL 1–4) |
> | Operating mode | Active 100% of time | Dormant until demand |
> | Action on failure | Annunciate and operator intervenes | Fail-safe (de-energize to trip) |
> | Standards | ISA 95, IEC 61131 | IEC 61508, IEC 61511, ISA 84 |
>
> **Key Statement:** *"The BPCS keeps the process under control; the SIS protects the process when control is lost."*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive on SIS & PFD

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Functional Safety Lifecycle as per IEC 61511. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a 1oo2 pressure transmitter, a 1oo1D logic solver, and a 1oo2D shutdown valve. The following data are given:
> - Pressure transmitter: $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_{DD} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h
> - Logic solver: $PFD_{Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$
> - Shutdown valve (each): $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/h, $T_{proof} = 4380$ h
>
> Calculate the total PFD of the SIF, determine the achieved SIL, and verify whether the design meets a target SIL of 2. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Functional Safety Lifecycle (7 Marks)

The IEC 61511 functional safety lifecycle consists of **eight phases** executed in a cradle-to-grave approach:

**Phase 1 — Concept and Scope Definition:** Identify the process unit, the study boundary, the applicable standards, and the relevant stakeholders.

**Phase 2 — Hazard and Risk Assessment (HRA):** Identify all hazards (HAZOP, FMEA, What-If), estimate the unmitigated event frequency, and evaluate the consequences (fatality, injury, environmental release).

**Phase 3 — Risk Reduction & SIL Assignment:** Determine the tolerable risk target and compute the required risk reduction factor (RRF) for each hazard. Use the Risk Graph or LOPA method to assign a target SIL (1, 2, 3, or 4) to each SIF.

**Phase 4 — SIS Design and Engineering:** Select the architecture (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3) for each sub-system; specify sensors, logic solver, and final elements; calculate the achieved PFD using failure-rate data; verify $PFD_{achieved} \le PFD_{required}$.

**Phase 5 — Installation, Commissioning, and Validation:** Factory Acceptance Test (FAT), Site Acceptance Test (SAT), and Site Integration Test (SIT). Validate that the installed SIS meets the SIL specification.

**Phase 6 — Operation and Maintenance:** Manage bypasses, perform proof tests at the specified interval, calibrate instruments, log every event, and train operators.

**Phase 7 — Modification:** Every change — software, hardware, or procedural — must follow Management of Change (MOC), which re-enters the lifecycle at an appropriate phase.

**Phase 8 — Decommissioning:** Safe shutdown, isolation, and disposal of the SIS at end-of-life, with full documentation handover.

> **Key points to remember:** Phases 1–3 are *before* the SIS is built; Phases 4–5 are *during* design and construction; Phases 6–7 are *operational*; Phase 8 is *end-of-life*.

#### Part (b) — PFD Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — PFD of the 1oo2 pressure transmitter:**

$$
PFD_{Sensor} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2 = (1 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^2 = (8.76 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.67 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — PFD of the logic solver (given):**

$$
PFD_{Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — PFD of the 1oo2D shutdown valve:**

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^2}{3} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380)^2}{3}
$$

$$
= \frac{(2.19 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \frac{4.80 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = 1.60 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFD of the SIF:**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{FE} = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-5} + 1.60 \times 10^{-6} = 1.28 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — SIL Determination:**

The achieved PFD = $1.28 \times 10^{-4}$ falls in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ → **SIL 2 achieved**.

**Step 6 — Verification against target SIL 2:**

Target SIL 2 requires $PFD \le 10^{-2}$. Since $1.28 \times 10^{-4} \le 10^{-2}$, the design **comfortably meets** SIL 2 (with a margin of ~78×).

**Step 7 — Achieved Risk Reduction Factor:**

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{1.28 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7813
$$

> **Conclusion:** The SIF achieves SIL 2 with a healthy design margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the architectures of a Critical Control System (CCS), a Protective Instrumented System (PIS), and an Emergency Shutdown System (ESD). Include in your answer the typical SIL, independence, response speed, and example applications. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Discuss in detail the role and characteristics of the **sensor sub-system**, the **logic solver**, and the **final element sub-system**. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — CCS vs PIS vs ESD (7 Marks)

| Feature | Critical Control System (CCS) | Protective Instrumented System (PIS) | Emergency Shutdown System (ESD) |
|---------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Continuous safe operation within design envelope | Take process to safe state on demand | Rapid shutdown of unit/plant in major emergency |
| **Mode of action** | Continuous (modulating) | Discrete (on/off trip) | Discrete (on/off trip, often cascaded) |
| **Target SIL** | Usually no SIL (control grade) | SIL 1–3 | SIL 2–3 (highest priority) |
| **Independence from BPCS** | Not required | Required by IEC 61511 | Strictly independent, sometimes triple-redundant |
| **Response time** | Slow (seconds to minutes) | Medium (1–5 s) | Fast (< 1 s for ESD-0) |
| **Activation** | Automatic (continuous feedback) | Automatic on trip condition | Automatic + manual (push-button) |
| **Example** | Temperature PID loop on reactor | High-pressure shutdown of compressor | Plant-wide gas leak / fire shutdown |
| **Standard** | ISA 88, ISA 95 | IEC 61511 | API RP 14C, IEC 61511 |
| **Failure action** | Annunciate, let operator correct | Fail-safe (de-energize) | Fail-safe, often with redundancy |

**Diagrammatic Relationship:**

- **CCS** is the *baseline controller* (DCS, PLC).
- **PIS** sits *above* CCS — it is the protective layer for individual equipment.
- **ESD** is the *highest layer* — for plant-level emergencies.

This is the **"layer of protection"** concept.

#### Part (b) — SIS Structure and Sub-systems (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram:**

$$
\text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Logic Solver} \rightarrow \text{Final Element} \rightarrow \text{Safe State}
$$

**1. Sensor Sub-system (Input):**

- **Role:** Detect the process variable (pressure, temperature, flow, level) and convert it to an electrical/pneumatic signal.
- **Typical devices:** Pressure transmitters, RTDs, thermocouples, level switches, flame detectors, toxic gas sensors.
- **Characteristics:**
  - High **SFF** (Safe Failure Fraction) ≥ 60% for SIL 2.
  - Redundant configuration (1oo2, 2oo3) for higher SIL.
  - Periodic **proof testing** to detect dangerous undetected failures.
  - Diagnostic coverage (DC) ≥ 60% for SIL 2; ≥ 90% for SIL 3.
- **Contribution to PFD:** Often 20–30% of total.

**2. Logic Solver (Brain):**

- **Role:** Receive sensor signals, apply trip logic (1oo2, 2oo3 voting), and generate trip commands to final elements.
- **Typical devices:** Safety PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-F, Honeywell FSC, ABB AC 800M HI), solid-state logic, or relay logic.
- **Characteristics:**
  - Must be **certified to IEC 61508** for the target SIL.
  - **Independent** of BPCS controller.
  - Usually 1oo1D (single channel with diagnostics) for SIL 2.
  - Performs automatic self-diagnostics (DC > 90%).
- **Contribution to PFD:** Usually the *lowest* (~10–15%).

**3. Final Element Sub-system (Actuator):**

- **Role:** Physically bring the process to a safe state — close a valve, stop a motor, open a vent, de-energize a heater.
- **Typical devices:** Shutdown valves (ball, gate, globe), motor control centres, circuit breakers, vent valves.
- **Characteristics:**
  - **De-energize-to-trip** (DETT) is the standard for safety (solenoid vents air, spring closes valve).
  - **Partial-stroke testing** for pneumatically actuated valves — allows online testing without full closure.
  - Often the **weakest link** in the SIF — contributes 50–70% of total PFD.
- **Reliability improvement:** Use 1oo2D architecture, shorten $T_{proof}$, and use MOV (motor-operated valve) backups.

> **Conclusion:** The three sub-systems operate *in series*; the total PFD is their sum. The final element is the dominant contributor and must be designed with the highest attention.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Conflating SIL with PFD:** Many students write "SIL = 10⁻³" — this is **wrong**. SIL is a *band* (1, 2, 3, 4); PFD is the *numerical value*. Always say "SIL 2 requires PFD between 10⁻³ and 10⁻²."
> 2. **Forgetting the square in 1oo2 PFD:** The most common PFD-derivation error is missing the squared term $(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2$. Mark loss: 2–3 marks.
> 3. **BPCS-SIS confusion:** Stating that BPCS is a "safety system" is a major mark-losing error. BPCS has *no SIL*.
> 4. **Skipping the proof-test interval:** Every PFD calculation must state the value of $T_{proof}$. Omitting it → 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Wrong units:** $\lambda$ is per *hour*, not per year. Students writing $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/year lose 1 mark.
> 6. **No independence statement:** In SIS questions, the phrase *"SIS must be independent of BPCS"* is mandatory.
> 7. **Mishandling the Risk Graph:** Always list all four parameters (C, F, P, W) in SIL-determination answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2**

### A. Standards
- ☐ **IEC 61508** = generic functional safety (umbrella).
- ☐ **IEC 61511** = process-industry implementation of IEC 61508.
- ☐ **ISA 84.00.01** = US equivalent of IEC 61511.
- ☐ ISA also publishes 88, 95, 101, 18.2 for related topics.

### B. Terminology (must-know abbreviations)
- ☐ **SIL** (1–4), **PFD**, **PFH**, **RRF**, **SIF**, **SIS**, **HFT**, **SFF**, **DC**, **MTTF**, **MTTR**, **Tproof**, **MooN voting**.

### C. SIL Bands — Low-Demand Mode
- ☐ SIL 1: PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$
- ☐ SIL 2: PFD $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$
- ☐ SIL 3: PFD $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
- ☐ SIL 4: PFD $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$

### D. SIL Bands — High-Demand / Continuous Mode
- ☐ SIL 1: PFH $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$/h
- ☐ SIL 2: PFH $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$/h
- ☐ SIL 3: PFH $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$/h
- ☐ SIL 4: PFH $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$/h

### E. PFD Formulas (must memorize)
- ☐ $PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_{DD} T_{repair})^2}{3}$
- ☐ $PFD_{1oo2} = (\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2 + \lambda_{DD} T_{repair}$
- ☐ $PFD_{2oo3} = (\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^2 + \lambda_{DD} T_{repair}$
- ☐ $PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{FE}$
- ☐ $RRF = 1 / PFD$

### F. System Hierarchy (Layers of Protection)
- ☐ Layer 1: **CCS** (BPCS, continuous control).
- ☐ Layer 2: **PIS / SIS** (alarm + trip).
- ☐ Layer 3: **ESD** (plant-level emergency).
- ☐ Layer 4: **Relief valves, dikes** (physical protection).
- ☐ Layer 5: **Community emergency response** (last line).

### G. Equipment Protection vs Personnel Safety
- ☐ **Equipment Protection Systems** (EPS) protect *machines* (turbines, compressors, motors).
- ☐ **SIS** protects *people, plant, and environment*.
- ☐ They often overlap but have different SIL targets.

### H. Interlock Types
- ☐ Permissive, Preventive, Sequencing, Mechanical (Kirk-key).
- ☐ Implemented in relays, PLCs, or mechanical cams.

### I. ESD Levels
- ☐ ESD-0 (plant), ESD-1 (unit), ESD-2 (equipment), ESD-3 (local).
- ☐ Manual activation always available via push-button.

### J. ISA & Standards
- ☐ ISA = global professional body; **ISA 84** is the safety SIS standard harmonized with IEC 61511.
- ☐ Mention "per IEC 61511 / ISA 84.00.01" in KTU answers for full marks.

### K. Validation Checklist
- ☐ PFD achieved ≤ PFD required (target SIL met).
- ☐ SFF and HFT consistent with target SIL.
- ☐ Diagnostic coverage (DC) satisfies IEC 61511 minimums.
- ☐ Proof test interval is specified and manageable.
- ☐ Independence from BPCS demonstrated.
- ☐ Total SIF PFD = sum of three sub-system PFDs.

### L. KTU "Examiner-Words" — High-Yield Phrases
- ☐ "Per IEC 61511, the SIS shall be independent of the BPCS."
- ☐ "The final element contributes the largest share of the total PFD."
- ☐ "SIL assignment is based on the Risk Reduction Factor derived from tolerable risk criteria."
- ☐ "Proof testing is essential to reveal dangerous undetected failures."

> **Final Exam Tip:** A 14-mark KTU question on this module can almost always be answered using a **3-part structure**:
> 1. **Define the system** (SIS/SIF/SIL/PFD with table).
> 2. **Show the architecture** (block diagram with three sub-systems).
> 3. **Compute the PFD** (formula + numerical substitution + SIL band check).
> 
> Practising this structure will earn you 12–14 marks in most Module 2 questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEC Standards: IEC 61508 - Fundamental Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IEC Standards: IEC 61508 - Fundamental Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IEC 61508** is the international umbrella standard titled *"Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems (E/E/PE SRS)"*. It provides a generic, risk-based, and lifecycle-oriented framework for designing, integrating, validating, operating, and maintaining safety instrumented systems across all industries (process, nuclear, railway, machinery, medical).

The standard is published in **seven parts** (IEC 61508-1 through IEC 61508-7) and forms the foundational reference for sector-specific derivatives such as:
- **IEC 61511** — Functional Safety for the Process Industry Sector (SIS)
- **IEC 62061** — Functional Safety for Machinery
- **IEC 61513** — Nuclear Power Plant I&C Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** A viva/lab/ESE question will almost always begin with *"What is IEC 61508 and why is it called an umbrella standard?"* — answer both halves to secure full marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aircraft Maintenance Manual"

Imagine you own a passenger aircraft. You must guarantee that the engine, hydraulics, sensors, and software all work safely **from design to retirement**. You cannot rely on one component being perfect; you must prove, with documented evidence, that:

1. Every failure mode has been **identified**.
2. The probability of a dangerous failure is **quantified**.
3. The system has **multiple layers** (redundancy, diagnostics, proof tests).
4. The software controlling the plane is developed under **strict lifecycle discipline**.

IEC 61508 is exactly that — but for any E/E/PE safety system. It tells engineers **what to do, what to prove, and what records to keep** throughout the entire life of the safety function. It shifts safety from *intuition* to *evidence*.

---

## 1.3 The Seven Parts of IEC 61508 (At a Glance)

| Part | Title | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| **Part 1** | General Requirements | Sets out the overall framework, conformance criteria |
| **Part 2** | Requirements for E/E/PE Safety-Related Systems | Hardware & architecture requirements |
| **Part 3** | Software Requirements | Software safety lifecycle (V-model) |
| **Part 4** | Definitions and Abbreviations | Glossary of 100+ terms |
| **Part 5** | Determination of SIL Methods | Risk-graph, risk-matrix, calibrated layer methods |
| **Part 6** | Guidelines on Application | Worked examples and engineering guidance |
| **Part 7** | Bibliography | Supporting references and techniques |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Part 2 and Part 5 are the most frequently tested modules in PEICT862 Module 2.

---

## 1.4 Key Definitions You Must Memorize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Block — Memorize Word-for-Word for KTU ESE**

- **Functional Safety** — Part of overall safety that depends on a system or equipment operating correctly in response to its inputs.
- **Safety Function** — A function to be implemented by an E/E/PE system, other technology safety-related system, or external risk reduction facility, that is intended to achieve or maintain a safe state.
- **Safety Integrity** — The probability that a safety-related system will perform its required safety function under all stated conditions within a stated period of time.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — A discrete level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety functions to be allocated to the E/E/PE safety-related systems.
- **Safe State** — A state of the process/equipment where the hazard is avoided or removed.
- **Dangerous Failure** — A failure that has the potential to put the safety-related system in a hazardous or loss-of-function state.
- **Safe Failure** — A failure that does not put the safety-related system into a hazardous or loss-of-function state.
- **Proof Test** — A periodic test performed to detect failures in a safety-related system so that the system can be restored to an "as new" condition.

---

## 1.5 The Two Modes of Operation

IEC 61508 distinguishes **two distinct operation modes** because the failure probability metric differs:

1. **Low Demand Mode** — Safety function is demanded **no more than once per year**. Metric: **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** per demand.
2. **High Demand / Continuous Mode** — Safety function is demanded **more than once per year** (often continuously). Metric: **Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)**.

This distinction is critical because the SIL boundary values are completely different between the two modes (see Section 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Safety Life Cycle (Overall Structure)

IEC 61508 prescribes a **16-phase safety lifecycle** (Part 1, Figure 2). The life cycle is split into three sub-cycles:

1. **Overall Safety Life Cycle (Phases 1–5)** — Risk assessment, allocation, and planning.
2. **E/E/PE System Safety Life Cycle (Phases 6–13)** — Realisation phase.
3. **Operation, Maintenance & Modification (Phases 14–16)** — Post-installation phase.

The lifecycle is **iterative** — if any phase reveals new hazards, the engineer must loop back to Phase 1.

### Why the Lifecycle Matters
- It forces **front-loaded risk analysis** (HAZOP, FMEA, LOPA).
- It enforces **verification at each gate** (design review, integration test, site acceptance test).
- It guarantees **traceability** from hazard → SIL → design → operation.

---

## 2.2 The Risk Reduction Concept (Phase 1–5 Logic)

The most powerful idea in IEC 61508 is the **risk reduction equation**:

$$R_{acceptable} = R_{intrinsic} - R_{reduced}$$

Where:
- **R_intrinsic** = Risk inherent to the process (before any safety layer).
- **R_reduced** = Risk removed by layers of protection (control, alarm, SIS, fire protection).
- **R_acceptable** = Residual risk — must equal the organization's *tolerable risk*.

The standard explicitly identifies the **EUC (Equipment Under Control)** and assigns each protective layer a **Tolerable Hazard Rate (THR)** or **Tolerable PFD**. The risk reduction required from the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is then translated into a **SIL target**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Think of risk as a stack of Swiss cheese slices. Each layer has holes (failures), but stacking them ensures no single hazard can pass through. IEC 61508 sizes each slice based on the size of the holes it must plug.

---

## 2.3 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 → SIL 4)

SILs are **discrete confidence levels** representing the probability that a safety function will operate as intended. **Higher SIL = Lower failure probability = Higher Reliability.**

The IEC 61508-1 risk-graph and risk-matrix techniques are used to derive the SIL from a **four-parameter risk score**:

| Parameter | Categories |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Consequence (C)** | Minor, Serious, Hazardous, Catastrophic |
| **Frequency of Exposure (F)** | Rare, Occasional, Frequent, Continuous |
| **Possibility of Avoidance (P)** | Avoidable, Not Avoidable |
| **Demand Rate (W)** | Very low, Low, High |

Each parameter is assigned a numerical weighting (typically 0–7), summed, and the sum maps onto a **risk graph that yields SIL 1, 2, 3, or 4**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** SIL 4 is reserved for extremely hazardous applications (e.g., nuclear reactor shutdown). **Process industry SIS applications under IEC 61511 are limited to SIL 1, 2, and 3 only.**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — IEC 61508 Quantitative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — Required for Every Quantitative Question**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Fraction (0–1) or % |
| 3 | Hardware Failure Budget | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Failures/hour |
| 4 | Total Dangerous Failure Rate | $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Failures/hour |
| 5 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| 6 | PFDavg — 1oo1 Architecture | $PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2}$ | Low demand |
| 7 | PFDavg — 1oo2 Architecture | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2}{3}$ | Low demand |
| 8 | PFDavg — 2oo2 Architecture | $PFD_{2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T$ | Low demand |
| 9 | PFDavg — 2oo3 Architecture | $PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2$ | Low demand |
| 10 | Mean Down Time for Undetected Failure | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T}{2}$ | Hours |
| 11 | PFH for 1oo1 (High Demand) | $PFH_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}$ | Failures/hour |
| 12 | PFH for 1oo2 | $PFH_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}$ | Failures/hour |

---

## 2.5 Failure Rate Classification (Per IEC 61508-4)

| Symbol | Failure Category | Effect on System |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| $\lambda_S$ | Safe Failure | Trips system safely (no hazard) |
| $\lambda_D$ | Dangerous Failure | Prevents system from performing safety action |
| $\lambda_{DD}$ | Dangerous Detected | Found by automatic diagnostics |
| $\lambda_{DU}$ | Dangerous Undetected | Hides until next proof test |

Where:
$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

---

## 2.6 SIL Boundary Values — Memorize This Table

### Low Demand Mode (PFDavg per demand)
| SIL | Lower Bound | Upper Bound |
|-----|-------------|-------------|
| **SIL 1** | $\geq 10^{-2}$ | $< 10^{-1}$ |
| **SIL 2** | $\geq 10^{-3}$ | $< 10^{-2}$ |
| **SIL 3** | $\geq 10^{-4}$ | $< 10^{-3}$ |
| **SIL 4** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ | $< 10^{-4}$ |

### High Demand / Continuous Mode (PFH per hour)
| SIL | Lower Bound | Upper Bound |
|-----|-------------|-------------|
| **SIL 1** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ | $< 10^{-5}$ |
| **SIL 2** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ | $< 10^{-6}$ |
| **SIL 3** | $\geq 10^{-8}$ | $< 10^{-7}$ |
| **SIL 4** | $\geq 10^{-9}$ | $< 10^{-8}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse PFH and PFD bounds. PFH has units of *failures/hour*; PFDavg is *dimensionless* (probability per demand). Mixing them gives nonsense answers and full mark deduction.

---

## 2.7 Architecture Constraints (Part 2, Tables 2/3)

Even if a component achieves the required PFDavg, IEC 61508 imposes **architectural constraints** based on Type (A vs Type B) and SFF. The goal is to **limit residual systematic failures** by enforcing:

- **Minimum Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** based on SIL.
- **Minimum SFF** based on SIL and device Type.

### Type A vs Type B (Part 2, §7.4.4)
- **Type A Device** — Failure modes are fully known, behaviour under fault is deterministic, well-proven in field (e.g., simple resistor, relay).
- **Type B Device** — Complex device with microcontrollers, where failure modes are **not fully known** (e.g., PLC, smart transmitter).

Type B requires **stricter constraints** because unknown failures cannot be fully characterised.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Guarantee:** Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. No shorthand, no "it can be shown that."

---

## 3.1 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo1 Architecture (Single Channel)

**Setup:** A single channel with an undetected dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$. Proof test interval is $T$. We assume the channel fails at a uniformly random time between two proof tests.

The instantaneous **probability of failure at time $t$** is:

$$P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

For very small $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ (typical of safety instruments), the exponential is linearised:

$$P(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

The **average probability of failure on demand over one proof interval** is:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$

Carrying out the integration:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T} \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T} \cdot \dfrac{T^2}{2}$$

Cancelling $T$:

$$\boxed{PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of PFDavg for 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two)

**Setup:** Two identical channels in parallel. The system fails to operate **only if both channels fail** simultaneously. The dangerous undetected failure of each channel is independent and equal to $\lambda_{DU}$.

The probability that **both** channels have failed between two proof tests requires integrating the joint probability:

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2 \, dt$$

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} t^2 \, dt$$

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{T^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2}{3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The PFDavg for 1oo2 is **quadratic** in $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T$ instead of linear. This is why redundancy dramatically improves safety — but only when combined with proof testing.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a 1oo1 architecture has $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The proof test interval is $T = 8760$ hours (one year). Calculate (a) PFDavg, (b) RRF, (c) SIL achieved.

### Step 1: Compute PFDavg

Substitute $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T = 8760$ hr into the 1oo1 formula:

$$PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (8760)}{2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 = 17520 \times 10^{-6} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$

Divide by 2:

$$PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{1.752 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 2: Compute Risk Reduction Factor

$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{8.76 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$RRF \approx 114.16$$

### Step 3: Determine SIL

Compare $8.76 \times 10^{-3}$ with the SIL table for low-demand mode:
- The value lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$.

**Conclusion:** The transmitter achieves **SIL 2**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Architecture vs Reliability

| Architecture | Formula for PFDavg | Order of Magnitude vs 1oo1 | Application |
|--------------|--------------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| 1oo1 | $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T / 2$ | Baseline | Low-criticality loops |
| 1oo2 | $(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2 / 3$ | ~3 to 4 orders better | Common high-integrity loops |
| 2oo2 | $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T$ | Worse than 1oo1 | Tolerates spurious trips |
| 2oo3 | $6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2$ | Slightly worse than 1oo2 but tolerates 1 trip | Fire & Gas systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2oo2 has higher PFD than 1oo1:** In 2oo2, both channels must trip for the safety action to occur. So failure of *either* channel defeats the function — twice as many failure paths. Redundancy improves availability only if you can *tolerate* one channel failing (1oo2 logic).

---

## 3.5 SFF Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A sensor has $\lambda_S = 4 \times 10^{-6}$, $\lambda_{DD} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$, and $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Calculate SFF and DC.

### Step 1: Compute SFF

$$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

Substitute the values:

$$SFF = \dfrac{(4 + 3) \times 10^{-6}}{(4 + 3 + 1) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$SFF = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{SFF = 0.875 \text{ or } 87.5\%}$$

### Step 2: Compute Diagnostic Coverage

$$DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{(3 + 1) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$DC = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEC 61508 Overall Safety Life Cycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Phase 1: Concept and Scope Definition"]
    node2["Phase 2: Hazard and Risk Analysis"]
    node3["Phase 3: Overall Safety Requirements"]
    node4["Phase 4: Overall Operating Procedures"]
    node5["Phase 5: Safety Requirements Allocation"]
    node6["Phase 6: E/E/PE System Planning"]
    node7["Phase 7: E/E/PE System Realisation"]
    node8["Phase 8: Other Technology SRS Realisation"]
    node9["Phase 9: Overall Installation and Commissioning"]
    node10["Phase 10: Overall Validation"]
    node11["Phase 11: Overall Operation Maintenance Modification"]
    node12["Phase 12: Overall Validation Decommissioning"]
    node13["Phase 13: Overall Modification Retrofit"]
    node14["Phase 14: Software Safety Requirements"]
    node15["Phase 15: Software Safety Validation"]
    node16["Phase 16: Overall Safety Modification"]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node11 --> node13
    node13 --> node16
    node14 --> node15 --> node16
    node7 --> node14
```

---

## 4.2 Risk Reduction Concept (Multiple Layers of Protection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Intrinsic Risk Rint"]
    nodeB["Layer 1: Process Design"]
    nodeC["Layer 2: Control System BPCS"]
    nodeD["Layer 3: Alarm Operator Action"]
    nodeE["Layer 4: Safety Instrumented Function SIF"]
    nodeF["Layer 5: Emergency Response Fire Gas"]
    nodeG["Residual Risk Racc"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box subtracts a layer of risk. The SIF (Layer 4) is the **focus of IEC 61508/61511** — its required risk reduction defines the SIL.

---

## 4.3 SIL Determination Flow Using the Risk Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Identify Hazardous Event"]
    nodeC["Determine Consequence Severity C"]
    nodeF["Determine Exposure Frequency F"]
    nodeP["Determine Possibility of Avoidance P"]
    nodeW["Determine Demand Rate W"]
    nodeCalc["Compute Risk Score Risk = C+F+P+W"]
    nodeMap["Map Score to SIL Level"]
    nodeAllocate["Allocate SIL to Safety Function"]

    nodeStart --> nodeC --> nodeF --> nodeP --> nodeW --> nodeCalc --> nodeMap --> nodeAllocate
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Safety Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSensor["Sensor Stage 1oo2"]
    nodeLogic["Logic Solver PLC"]
    nodeFinal["Final Element Valve 1oo2"]
    nodeDemand["Process Demand"]
    nodeSafe["Safe State"]

    nodeDemand --> nodeSensor --> nodeLogic --> nodeFinal --> nodeSafe
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read for KTU:** Each block contributes a PFD. The total $PFD_{SYS} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{Final}$. The IEC 61508-6 budgets typically allocate **35% Sensor, 15% Logic, 50% Final Element** for low-demand SIFs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define IEC 61508 and explain why it is called the "umbrella standard" for functional safety.**

**Model Answer:**
IEC 61508, titled *"Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems,"* is the international standard that provides a generic framework for designing, integrating, validating, operating, and maintaining safety-related systems across industries. It is called an *umbrella standard* because it defines a common methodology from which all sector-specific functional safety standards (such as IEC 61511 for process industry, IEC 62061 for machinery, and IEC 61513 for nuclear) are derived. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Low Demand Mode and High Demand Mode of operation as per IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low Demand Mode | High Demand Mode |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Demand Frequency | $\leq$ 1 demand/year | $>$ 1 demand/year |
| Failure Metric | PFDavg | PFH |
| PFD Units | Probability per demand | Failures per hour |
| Typical Application | Emergency shutdown systems | Continuous burner control |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **IEC 61508 overall safety life cycle** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **Hazard and Risk Analysis** (Phase 2) in determining the Safety Integrity Level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 architecture pressure transmitter system has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ failures/hour and proof test interval $T = 8760$ hr. Calculate the (i) PFDavg, (ii) Risk Reduction Factor, (iii) achievable SIL. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The IEC 61508 safety life cycle (Part 1, Figure 2) consists of **16 phases** divided into three sub-cycles: Overall Safety, E/E/PE Realisation, and Operational phases. The life cycle begins with *Concept and Scope Definition* (Phase 1) and ends with *Decommissioning and Disposal* (Phase 16). It is iterative — discovery of new hazards at any phase forces a loop-back to Phase 1.

In **Phase 2 (Hazard and Risk Analysis)**, the engineering team performs studies such as **HAZOP, FMEA, and LOPA** to identify all hazardous events of the EUC (Equipment Under Control). For each hazard, four parameters are scored:

1. **Consequence Severity (C)** — Harm to people, environment, asset.
2. **Exposure Frequency (F)** — Time spent in hazardous zone.
3. **Possibility of Avoidance (P)** — Can operator escape the harm?
4. **Demand Rate (W)** — How often is the safety function called upon?

The summed risk score is plotted on the **risk graph**, which directly yields a **SIL target (1, 2, 3, or 4)**. This SIL is then allocated to specific safety instrumented functions in Phase 5. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour  
$T = 8760$ hours  
Architecture: 1oo2

**(i) PFDavg Calculation**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T)^2}{3}$$

Compute the term $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T$:

$$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$

Square the result:

$$(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.9184 \times 10^{-5}$$

Divide by 3:

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = 6.395 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**(ii) Risk Reduction Factor**

$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{6.395 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{RRF \approx 1.56 \times 10^{5}}$$

**(iii) SIL Determination**

Comparing $6.395 \times 10^{-6}$ with the SIL table:
- The value lies between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$.

**Conclusion:** The system achieves **SIL 3**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **risk reduction concept** as per IEC 61508. Mention the role of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sensor has $\lambda_S = 6 \times 10^{-6}$, $\lambda_{DD} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$, and $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. Calculate the (i) Total Failure Rate, (ii) Safe Failure Fraction, (iii) Diagnostic Coverage. Comment on the suitability of this sensor for a SIL 2 application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The risk reduction concept states:

$$R_{int} = R_{acc} + R_{red}$$

Where the **risk reduction $R_{red}$** is the sum of contributions from multiple Independent Protection Layers (IPLs). Each IPL is a device, system, or action that **independently** prevents a hazardous scenario from progressing.

The standard identifies the following IPLs in the order of typical priority:

1. **Layer 1 — Process Design** (inherently safer design).
2. **Layer 2 — Basic Process Control System (BPCS)**.
3. **Layer 3 — Safety Instrumented Function (SIF / SIS)**.
4. **Layer 4 — Physical Protection** (relief valves, dikes).
5. **Layer 5 — Emergency Response** (fire-fighting, evacuation).

Each IPL must satisfy four IEC 61511 criteria:
- **Independence** — No shared sensors, logic, or final elements.
- **Dependability** — Quantified reliability.
- **Auditability** — Periodically tested.
- **Risk Reduction** — Known RRF.

The SIF (Layer 3) is the *focus of IEC 61508*, and its required risk reduction defines the SIL target. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$\lambda_S = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour  
$\lambda_{DD} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour  
$\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour

**(i) Total Failure Rate**

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

$$\lambda_{total} = (6 + 4 + 2) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{total} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**(ii) Safe Failure Fraction**

$$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

$$SFF = \dfrac{(6 + 4) \times 10^{-6}}{12 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{SFF = \dfrac{10}{12} = 0.833 \text{ or } 83.33\%}$$

**(iii) Diagnostic Coverage**

$$DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

$$DC = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{(4 + 2) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{DC = \dfrac{4}{6} = 0.667 \text{ or } 66.67\%}$$

**Suitability for SIL 2:**
For a Type B device (smart sensor), IEC 61508 Part 2 requires SFF $\geq 60\%$ at HFT = 0 for SIL 2. Since SFF = 83.33% $> 60\%$, the sensor **passes the architectural constraint** for SIL 2. Additionally, the PFDavg must be between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$, which must be verified separately using proof-test interval. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 — Mixing PFD and PFH:** Students often quote SIL bounds from the PFH table when the question specifies low demand. Always re-read the question for *mode of operation*.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing Architecture:** A common error is writing "1oo2 means both channels must fail" — it actually means *either* channel can perform the safety function. 2oo2 means *both* must agree.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 — Ignoring Architectural Constraint:** Even if PFDavg meets SIL 2, the device may *fail* the SFF/HFT test (Part 2, Tables 2/3). Always cite both.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 — Wrong Units:** $\lambda$ is in *per hour*. Forgetting to convert *T* from years to hours (× 8760) costs 1–2 marks easily.
> 
> **Pitfall 5 — Defining "Tolerable Risk" wrongly:** It is *not* zero risk. It is the residual risk the organization accepts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Snapshot — Read 30 Minutes Before Exam**

- **IEC 61508** is a **7-part**, generic, lifecycle-based functional safety standard covering **E/E/PE** systems.
- It is the **umbrella standard**; IEC 61511 is the process-industry derivative limited to **SIL 1–3**.
- The safety life cycle has **16 phases**, iterative across three sub-cycles.
- **SIL 1–4** are discrete integrity levels; **higher SIL = lower failure probability**.
- Two operation modes:
  - **Low demand** → metric **PFDavg**
  - **High demand / continuous** → metric **PFH**
- Memorize the SIL boundary table (both for PFD and PFH).
- Master formulas: $RRF = 1/PFD_{avg}$; $SFF$ equation; $PFD$ for 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3.
- Risk reduction = Intrinsic risk – (Layer 1 + Layer 2 + … + Layer n).
- SIL determination uses the **risk graph** with parameters C, F, P, W.
- Always check **both** PFDavg *and* architectural constraints (SFF, HFT, Type A/B).
- **Type B devices** (e.g., PLC, smart transmitter) carry stricter SFF requirements than Type A.
- $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$; proof test interval $T$ is the *dominant* tunable parameter.
- 1oo2 improves PFDavg by ~3–4 orders vs 1oo1 (quadratic vs linear).
- 2oo2 *worsens* PFDavg but improves availability against spurious trips.
- Convert all times to **hours** before plugging into $\lambda$ formulas.
- A safety function demands a **safe state** of the EUC, not necessarily shutdown — depends on process.
- Forgot? — IEC 61508 is a *standard*, not a *law*; conformance is voluntary but enforced by IECEE certification bodies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2
