<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: IEC 61511 — FOUNDATIONS OF FUNCTIONAL SAFETY IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61511** is the **International Electrotechnical Commission** standard titled *"Functional safety — Safety instrumented systems for the process industry sector"*. It is the **sector-specific implementation standard** of the umbrella standard **IEC 61508**, specifically tailored for the **process industries** (oil & gas, chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, power, and pulp & paper).

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

IEC 61511 is a **risk-based, performance-based functional safety standard** that provides requirements for the specification, design, installation, operation, and maintenance of a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**. Its purpose is to prevent or mitigate **process hazards** by ensuring that the SIS performs its safety functions with a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

The standard was originally numbered **ANSI/ISA S84.01-1996** in the United States, before being harmonized globally as **IEC 61511** (Parts 1, 2, and 3).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IEC 61511 is **only applicable to Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**. It does NOT cover other types of safety systems such as fire & gas (F&G), emergency shutdown (ESD) control logic that is purely electrical/electronic but not safety-rated, or occupational health & safety management systems covered under ISO 45001.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Code" for Industrial Safety

Think of IEC 61511 as the **"Building Code for Industrial Plants"**:

| Real-World Analogy | IEC 61511 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Building Code (e.g., NBC) | IEC 61511 Standard |
| Architect | Safety Engineer / Process Safety Specialist |
| Building Inspector | TÜV / Certifying Authority |
| Building Risk (earthquake zone) | Process Hazard (overpressure, runaway reaction) |
| Earthquake-resistant design rating | Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1–4) |
| Occupant evacuation plan | Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) |
| Building maintenance schedule | Proof Test Interval (PTI) |

Just as a building in a high-seismic zone must be designed to withstand a certain magnitude earthquake, an oil refinery must design its SIS to handle a certain **probability of dangerous failure** — measured as **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**.

### 1.3 Relationship to IEC 61508

| Feature | IEC 61508 | IEC 61511 |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Generic (cross-industry) | Process industries only |
| **Type** | Umbrella / Basic standard | Sector standard |
| **SIL Range** | SIL 1 to SIL 4 | SIL 1 to SIL 3 *(SIL 4 not recommended in process)* |
| **Risk Approach** | Semi-quantitative | Risk-graph, LOPA, quantitative |
| **Application** | E/E/PE systems broadly | SIS, SIF, sensors, logic solvers, final elements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How is IEC 61511 different from IEC 61508?", the answer is — IEC 61511 is the **process-industry specific interpretation** of IEC 61508; it provides **prescriptive requirements** whereas IEC 61508 is **generic and flexible**.

### 1.4 Scope and Applicability

The standard applies to:
- **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** — a single safety function with a defined SIL
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** — the complete implementation of one or more SIFs
- SIS **subsystems**: sensors (input), logic solver (processing), and final elements (output)
- The **entire safety lifecycle** from concept to decommissioning

The standard does **NOT** apply to:
- Systems already covered by other standards (e.g., nuclear I&C — IEC 61513, medical — IEC 60601)
- Non-SIS protective systems (e.g., relief valves as primary safeguards)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of IEC 61511

The standard exists to:
1. Specify the **minimum requirements** for managing functional safety in process plants.
2. Enable **risk reduction** to a tolerable level by implementing SIS.
3. Provide a **common terminology** (SIF, SIS, SIL, PFD, MTTFS, etc.).
4. Establish a **safety lifecycle** framework ensuring traceability.
5. Specify **performance criteria** for SIS subsystems (PFD, SFF, HFT, etc.).

## 2.2 The IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle — The 11 Phases

The safety lifecycle is the **backbone** of IEC 61511. It defines **what must be done, when, and in what order**, to achieve functional safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lifecycle Rule:** No phase may begin until the preceding phase has been completed and documented. Skipping a phase is the **#1 cause of failed KTU answers** in descriptive questions.

| Phase | Name | KTU-Relevant Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Process & Risk Assessment | HAZOP, FMEA, what-if studies; identify hazards |
| **2** | Allocation of Safety Functions | Decide what is BPCS, alarm, relief, or **SIS** |
| **3** | SIS Design & Engineering | Specify SIL, architecture, sensors, logic solver, valves |
| **4** | Installation, Commissioning & Validation | Verify "as-built" matches design (Site Acceptance Test) |
| **5** | Operation & Maintenance | Operating procedures, training, documentation |
| **6** | Modification Management | Manage changes through a **Management of Change (MOC)** process |
| **7** | Decommissioning | Safe disposal when SIS or plant is retired |
| **8** | Information & Documentation | Maintain **Safety Case** for the entire service life |
| **9** | Verification | Independent review confirming each lifecycle phase |
| **10** | Validation | End-to-end test of installed SIS to confirm it meets requirements |
| **11** | Functional Safety Assessment (FSA) | Independent audit at predefined milestones |

### 2.3 Core Terminology (MUST memorize)

- **SIL — Safety Integrity Level:** Discrete level (1–3) corresponding to a range of PFD values.
- **PFD — Probability of Failure on Demand:** Probability that the SIS fails to respond when demanded.
- **SIF — Safety Instrumented Function:** A single safety function with a specified SIL.
- **SIS — Safety Instrumented System:** The complete hardware/software that implements one or more SIFs.
- **HFT — Hardware Fault Tolerance:** Ability of a system to continue functioning with N faults.
- **SFF — Safe Failure Fraction:** Ratio of safe failures + diagnosed dangerous failures to total failures.
- **MTTFS — Mean Time To Failure Spurious:** Average time between spurious (false) trips.
- **PFDavg — Average Probability of Failure on Demand:** Time-weighted average PFD over the proof test interval.

### 2.4 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The Numerical Backbone

The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is the inverse of PFDavg:

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table:** SIL vs. PFDavg (low-demand mode of operation — most common in process industries)

| SIL | PFDavg Range | RRF Range | Risk Reduction | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | 10×–100× | Burner Management System |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | 100×–1000× | Compressor Anti-Surge |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | 1000×–10000× | Reactor Overpressure Trip |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | Not used in IEC 61511 | (Process industry limit) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often write PFD ranges starting from $10^{-1}$. The correct **lower bound** for SIL 1 is **$10^{-2}$** (i.e., $0.01$), NOT $10^{-1}$. Marks are lost for off-by-one powers of ten.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All the formulas below are **exam-grade** and tested in KTU 2024 scheme assessments.

### Core Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{SIL}: \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \in [10^{-n+1}, 10^{-n}]$ | SIL quantization | Probability |
| 3 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIS}} = \text{PFD}_S + \text{PFD}_L + \text{PFD}_{FE}$ | Total SIS PFD (sensor + logic + final element) | Probability |
| 4 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| 5 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$ | 1-out-of-1 single channel | Probability |
| 6 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{6}$ | 1-out-of-2 dual channel | Probability |
| 7 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,2oo3}} \approx \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2$ | 2-out-of-3 voted | Probability |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF}_S = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | Mean Time To Fail Spurious | Hours |

**Where:**
- $\lambda_D$ = Dangerous failure rate (failures/hour)
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = **Dangerous Undetected** failure rate
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = **Dangerous Detected** (diagnosed) failure rate
- $\lambda_S$ = Safe (spurious) failure rate
- $T_i$ = **Proof Test Interval** (hours)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dominant failure mode** in SIL calculations is $\lambda_{DU}$ (Dangerous Undetected). Proof testing converts $\lambda_{DU}$ failures back to operational state.

## 2.6 Architectural Constraints (Type A vs. Type B)

IEC 61511 requires **two conditions** for SIL compliance:

1. **Architectural constraint** (HFT + SFF requirements) — depends on whether the device is **Type A (simple)** or **Type B (complex/microprocessor)**.
2. **Quantitative PFDavg** — meets the target SIL range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick reference:** For a Type A sensor at SIL 2, HFT = 0 is allowed only if SFF ≥ 60%. For SIL 3, HFT must be ≥ 1 for Type A devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo1 Single-Channel System

We begin with the simplest possible subsystem — a single sensor or single valve with **no redundancy**. This is the foundation for all other architectures.

### Step 1 — Define the Failure Rate Model

Assume the dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ is **constant** over time (standard reliability assumption). The probability of **survival** at time $t$ is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}
$$

The probability of **failure** at time $t$ is:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}
$$

### Step 2 — Average PFD Over One Proof Test Interval

After a proof test, the system is restored to "as-good-as-new" state. We average the unavailability over interval $T_i$:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} F(t)\, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Integrate

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}\right) dt
$$

Split the integral:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_i} 1 \, dt - \int_{0}^{T_i} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \, dt \right]
$$

Evaluate each part:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}}\left( e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i} - 1 \right) \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Small-$\lambda t$ Approximation

Since $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i \ll 1$ (a proof test interval is much shorter than the MTTF), we expand $e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i}$:

$$
e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} T_i + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{2} - \dots
$$

Substituting back:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \left( 1 - \lambda_{DU} T_i + \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2}{2} - 1 \right) \right]
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i - T_i + \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_i^2}{2} \right]
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result (Formula 5):** $\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$

This is one of the most frequently tested formulas in KTU.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — SIL Determination by Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A SIF consists of three subsystems connected in series:
- **Sensor (1oo2 voted)** with $\lambda_{DU,S} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- **Logic Solver (1oo1)** with $\lambda_{DU,L} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- **Final Element (1oo2 voted valve)** with $\lambda_{DU,FE} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour

**Proof Test Interval:** $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year).

Find the total PFDavg of the SIF and the achieved SIL.

### Step 1 — Compute PFD for the Sensor (1oo2 architecture)

For 1oo2 voted system, the formula is:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{6}
$$

Substituting:

$$
PFD_S = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{6} = \frac{10^{-12} \cdot 7.6776 \times 10^7}{6}
$$

$$
PFD_S = \frac{7.6776 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute PFD for the Logic Solver (1oo1 architecture)

$$
PFD_L = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (8760)}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_L = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute PFD for the Final Element (1oo2 valve)

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{6} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.6776 \times 10^7}{6}
$$

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{3.071 \times 10^{-4}}{6} = 5.12 \times 10^{-5}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total SIS PFD (Series Combination)

$$
PFD_{SIS} = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_{FE}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIS} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3} + 5.12 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIS} \approx 2.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 5 — Determine the SIL

Compare $PFD_{SIS} \approx 2.25 \times 10^{-3}$ to the SIL table:

| SIL | PFD Range | Match? |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | ❌ PFD too low |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | ✅ Match |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | ❌ PFD too high |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The SIF achieves **SIL 2** because $10^{-3} \le 2.25 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$.

### Step 6 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{SIS}} = \frac{1}{2.25 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 444
$$

This means the SIS reduces the process risk by a factor of **~444×** — sufficient for SIL 2 but not SIL 3.

### Step 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose IEC 61511

Use this matrix in design-based KTU questions:

| Decision Criterion | IEC 61511 Applicable? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical reactor overpressure protection | ✅ Yes | Process industry hazard |
| Nuclear reactor trip system | ❌ No | Covered by IEC 61513 |
| Medical ventilator alarm | ❌ No | Covered by IEC 60601 |
| Rail signaling system | ❌ No | Covered by IEC 62278/62279 |
| Burner management system | ✅ Yes | Process industry |
| Aircraft autopilot | ❌ No | Covered by DO-178C (avionics) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle Flow

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete 11-phase safety lifecycle** as mandated by IEC 61511-1, Clause 5. The arrows denote the mandatory sequential relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA["Phase 1: Process and Risk Assessment"]
    phaseB["Phase 2: Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers"]
    phaseC["Phase 3: SIS Design and Engineering"]
    phaseD["Phase 4: Installation Commissioning and Validation"]
    phaseE["Phase 5: Operation and Maintenance"]
    phaseF["Phase 6: Modification Management"]
    phaseG["Phase 7: Decommissioning"]
    phaseH["Phase 8: Information and Documentation"]
    phaseI["Phase 9: Verification"]
    phaseJ["Phase 10: Validation"]
    phaseK["Phase 11: Functional Safety Assessment"]

    phaseA --> phaseB
    phaseB --> phaseC
    phaseC --> phaseD
    phaseD --> phaseE
    phaseE --> phaseF
    phaseF --> phaseG
    phaseG -.continues throughout.- phaseH
    phaseC --> phaseI
    phaseD --> phaseJ
    phaseA --> phaseK
    phaseC --> phaseK
    phaseE --> phaseK
```

## 4.2 SIS Block Diagram — Sensor, Logic Solver, Final Element

This block-level architecture shows the three core subsystems of any SIF and the path of the safety signal from process measurement to final mitigation action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    processVar["Process Variable e.g. Pressure Temperature Flow"]
    sensor["Sensor Subsystem 1oo1 or 1oo2 or 2oo3"]
    logic["Logic Solver PES PLC Safety PLC"]
    fe["Final Element Subsystem Valve Pump Breaker"]
    mitigation["Process Returns to Safe State"]

    processVar -- Measurement Signal --> sensor
    sensor -- Input Signal --> logic
    logic -- Shutdown Command --> fe
    fe -- Mechanical Action --> mitigation

    style sensor fill:#ffe5cc
    style logic fill:#cce5ff
    style fe fill:#d5e8d4
    style mitigation fill:#f8cecc
```

## 4.3 SIL Determination Workflow (Risk-Graph Approach)

This flow shows how IEC 61511 recommends determining the **required SIL** of a SIF using the **risk-graph method** (Clause 5.2.6.1.2).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Identify Process Hazard Scenario"]
    step2["Determine Consequence Severity C1 to C4"]
    step3["Determine Occupancy F1 or F2"]
    step4["Determine Frequency of Entry W1 to W3"]
    step5["Determine Possibility of Avoidance P1 or P2"]
    step6["Compute Risk Graph Rating R = sum of risk parameters"]
    step7{"R equals R1 R2 or R3"}
    step8["Required SIL equals SIL 1"]
    step9["Required SIL equals SIL 2"]
    step10["Required SIL equals SIL 3"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 -- R1 --> step8
    step7 -- R2 --> step9
    step7 -- R3 --> step10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Method — LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis):** IEC 61511 explicitly endorses **LOPA** (Clause 5.2.6.1.3) as a **semi-quantitative** method to determine required SIL. LOPA is widely used in industry and is a frequent KTU exam topic.

## 4.4 Independent Protection Layer (IPL) Model — Why SIS is the LAST Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1["Layer 1: Process Design inherently safe"]
    layer2["Layer 2: Basic Process Control System BPCS"]
    layer3["Layer 3: Alarm and Operator Intervention"]
    layer4["Layer 4: Mechanical Relief Devices PSV PRD"]
    layer5["Layer 5: Safety Instrumented System SIS"]
    layer6["Layer 6: Emergency Response Fire Brigade"]

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer6

    style layer5 fill:#f8cecc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** IEC 61511 explicitly requires that the **SIS be the LAST automated layer of protection** before community emergency response. If you can solve the hazard with a relief valve, you should NOT redesign it as a SIF — that would violate the "as low as reasonably practicable" (ALARP) principle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define IEC 61511. List its three parts and state the scope of each part.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

IEC 61511 is the international standard for **functional safety of Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) in the process industry sector**. It is the sector-specific implementation of IEC 61508.

The three parts are:

1. **IEC 61511-1:** Framework, definitions, system, hardware and software requirements — the **main normative** part.
2. **IEC 61511-2:** Guidelines for the application of Part 1 (informative annex).
3. **IEC 61511-3:** Guidance for the determination of the required SIL (informative).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for parts & scope)*

---

### Question A2
**Q: State the difference between a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) and a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) with one industrial example each.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is a single safety function with a specified SIL, for example, *"Close the block valve on the reactor feed line if pressure exceeds 50 barg."*
- A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is the **complete physical implementation** (sensors + logic solver + final elements + power + utilities) that executes one or more SIFs.

| Item | SIF | SIS |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Function (logic) | Implementation (hardware+software) |
| Quantity | Many per plant | Few per plant |
| Example | "Open vent on high level" | The level transmitter + SIS PLC + vent valve assembly |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle**. List all phases and describe the activities performed in **Phases 1, 2, and 3**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A SIF consists of three independent subsystems with the following failure data:

| Subsystem | Architecture | $\lambda_{DU}$ (per hour) | $T_i$ (hours) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | 1oo2 | $2 \times 10^{-6}$ | 4380 |
| Logic Solver | 1oo1 | $5 \times 10^{-7}$ | 8760 |
| Valve | 1oo2 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 |

Calculate the **total PFDavg of the SIS** and determine the **achieved SIL**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Safety Lifecycle (7 marks)

The **IEC 61511 safety lifecycle** is an 11-phase framework that ensures functional safety is achieved and maintained throughout the SIS service life.

**Phase 1 — Process & Risk Assessment (1 mark):**
- Conduct HAZOP, FMEA, What-If studies
- Identify process hazards (e.g., overpressure, runaway reaction)
- Determine severity (consequence) and likelihood of each scenario
- Output: List of identified hazards with severity ratings

**Phase 2 — Allocation of Safety Functions (1 mark):**
- For each hazard, determine the **layers of protection** (BPCS, alarms, relief, SIS)
- Determine the **required SIL** using Risk-Graph, LOPA, or quantitative method
- Specify the **Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)**
- Output: Safety functions allocated to each layer, with required SIL

**Phase 3 — SIS Design & Engineering (1 mark):**
- Choose SIS architecture (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3, etc.)
- Specify sensors, logic solver (PES/PLC), final elements
- Verify **architectural constraints** (HFT + SFF) per Type A/B classification
- Calculate **PFDavg** and confirm SIL is achieved
- Output: Detailed SIS design with verification calculations

**Remaining phases** (briefly list for 3 marks):
- **Phase 4:** Installation, Commissioning & Validation
- **Phase 5:** Operation & Maintenance
- **Phase 6:** Modification Management
- **Phase 7:** Decommissioning
- **Phase 8:** Information & Documentation (Safety Case)
- **Phase 9:** Verification
- **Phase 10:** Validation (end-to-end testing)
- **Phase 11:** Functional Safety Assessment (independent audit)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Sensor PFD (1oo2, 1 mark):**

$$
PFD_S = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{6} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (4380)^2}{6}
$$

$$
PFD_S = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.9184 \times 10^7}{6} = \frac{7.6736 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.279 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Logic Solver PFD (1oo1, 1 mark):**

$$
PFD_L = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (8760)}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Valve PFD (1oo2, 1 mark):**

$$
PFD_V = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{6} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{6}
$$

$$
PFD_V = \frac{1 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.6776 \times 10^7}{6} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFD (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{SIS} = 1.279 \times 10^{-5} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3} + 1.28 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIS} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — SIL Determination (2 marks):**

Since $10^{-3} \le 2.22 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$, the achieved SIL is **SIL 2**.

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{2.22 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 450
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What are **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)?** Explain the relationship between **PFDavg, RRF, and SIL** using a suitable numerical table. Also describe **architectural constraints** in IEC 61511. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Risk-Graph method** and **LOPA method** for SIL determination under IEC 61511. Compare both methods. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — SIL, PFDavg, RRF & Architectural Constraints (7 marks)

A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) that defines the **reliability** of a SIF in terms of the **average probability of failure on demand (PFDavg)**.

**Relationship (3 marks):**

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

| SIL | PFDavg Range | RRF Range |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 (Not used in IEC 61511) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** IEC 61511 only uses SIL 1 to SIL 3 in process industries (SIL 4 is not permitted due to insufficient experience in process sector).

**Architectural Constraints (2 marks):**

In addition to meeting the PFDavg target, IEC 61511 requires that the **hardware architecture** also satisfies constraints based on:
- **Type A devices:** Simple, well-defined, failure modes known (e.g., pressure switch).
- **Type B devices:** Complex, microprocessor-based, failure modes not all known (e.g., smart transmitter).

For Type A at SIL 2: HFT = 0 if SFF ≥ 60%
For Type A at SIL 3: HFT ≥ 1
For Type B at SIL 2: HFT = 1 and SFF ≥ 60%

---

#### Part (b) — Risk-Graph vs LOPA (7 marks)

**Risk-Graph Method (3 marks):**
The risk-graph is a **qualitative-to-semi-quantitative** tool that uses four risk parameters:

| Parameter | Levels | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **C** — Consequence | C1 to C4 | Severity of harm |
| **F** — Occupancy | F1, F2 | Persons exposed |
| **W** — Frequency of entry | W1 to W3 | How often personnel enter the zone |
| **P** — Possibility of avoidance | P1, P2 | Can operator prevent the event? |

The risk graph is **easy to use, fast, and conservative**. It is best for early project phases when data is limited.

**LOPA — Layer of Protection Analysis (3 marks):**
LOPA is a **semi-quantitative** method that:
- Uses an **event tree** of initiating cause + independent protection layers (IPLs)
- Each IPL reduces the **frequency of the scenario**
- The **required PFD of the SIF** is determined by what remains after all IPLs

**Equation:**
$$
f_{tolerable} = f_{initiating} \times PFD_{1} \times PFD_{2} \times \dots \times PFD_{SIF}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF,required} = \frac{f_{tolerable}}{f_{initiating} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_{i}}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Risk-Graph | LOPA |
|---|---|---|
| Quantitative? | No (semi) | Semi-quantitative |
| Data required | Minimal | Moderate |
| Accuracy | Conservative | More precise |
| Time | Fast | Slower |
| Used in | Early design | Detailed design |
| Endorsed by IEC 61511 | Yes (Clause 5.2.6.1.2) | Yes (Clause 5.2.6.1.3) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing **SIL 4** with process industry — IEC 61511 **does not allow SIL 4** in process industries. Use IEC 61508 if SIL 4 is needed.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Writing PFD lower bound as $10^{-1}$ — the correct lower bound of SIL 1 is **$10^{-2}$**.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing the formulas for **1oo1** ($\lambda T_i / 2$) and **1oo2** ($\lambda^2 T_i^2 / 6$) — easy to mix up under exam pressure.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting that **architectural constraints** are an **additional** check — you must meet BOTH the PFDavg AND the HFT/SFF requirements.
> - **Pitfall 5:** In LOPA, students often forget to multiply the **initiating frequency** by ALL IPL PFDs, not just one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IEC 61511**

- **Origin:** IEC 61511 is the process-industry standard for SIS. It is derived from IEC 61508 but is **sector-specific**.
- **Three Parts:** Part 1 (normative), Part 2 (application guidelines), Part 3 (SIL determination).
- **Scope:** Applies only to process industries; only SIL 1 to SIL 3.
- **Safety Lifecycle:** **11 phases**, sequential; no skipping allowed.
- **SIL ↔ PFDavg ↔ RRF:**
  - SIL 1 → PFD in $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$
  - SIL 2 → PFD in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$
  - SIL 3 → PFD in $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
  - $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$
- **Subsystem PFD Combination:** Series — add PFDs of sensor + logic solver + final element.
- **Key PFD Formulas:**
  - 1oo1: $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$
  - 1oo2: $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2 / 6$
  - 2oo3: $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2$
- **Architectural Constraints:** Must satisfy **both** PFDavg target **and** HFT/SFF requirements.
- **Type A vs Type B:** Type A = simple; Type B = complex/microprocessor.
- **Risk Reduction Methods:** Risk-Graph (qualitative), LOPA (semi-quantitative), Quantitative (PFDavg calculation).
- **IPL Layers:** Process Design → BPCS → Alarms → Relief Devices → **SIS** → Emergency Response.
- **Documentation:** Safety Case, SRS, Verification, Validation, FSA.
- **Proof Test Interval ($T_i$):** Affects PFDavg directly; shorter intervals → lower PFDavg.
- **Cross-Cutting Issues:** Management of Change (MOC) is required during operation phase.
- **Independent Layers of Protection:** SIS is one IPL, not the only one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always cite the **Clause Number** (e.g., IEC 61511-1, Clause 5.2.6.1.3) when answering — examiners reward specificity. Memorize at least the structure of Clauses 5, 8, and 11 of Part 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISA 84.01-2003
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISA 84.01-2003

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**ISA 84.01-2003** is a process-industry standard published by the **International Society of Automation (ISA)** titled *"Application of Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industries"*. It is the **United States national adoption of IEC 61511** and establishes the requirements for the design, implementation, operation, and management of **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** in the process sector.

The standard defines a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** as a *system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined conditions are violated*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition Box**
> - **Full Title:** ANSI/ISA 84.01-2003 — *Application of Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industries Sector*
> - **Scope:** Process industries (chemical, oil & gas, petrochemical, refining)
> - **Replaced by:** ISA 84.00.01-2004 (harmonized with IEC 61511-1:2003)
> - **Core Philosophy:** *Risk-based, lifecycle-oriented, performance-based specification*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the safety system of a **domestic pressure cooker**:
- The **sensor** is the pressure-indicating weight on the vent.
- The **logic solver** is your brain — it decides *"too much pressure!"*.
- The **final control element** is the safety valve that releases steam.

Now scale this idea to a **reactor containing hydrocarbon at 50 bar and 300 °C**. The same three-block structure (sensor → logic solver → valve) is used, but now we must **mathematically prove** that the protection works.

**ISA 84.01-2003 is the rulebook that tells us**:
1. *How do we know the protection is needed?* → **Risk analysis**
2. *How good must the protection be?* → **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**
3. *How do we build, test, and maintain it throughout its 20-year life?* → **Safety Lifecycle**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of ISA 84.01-2003 as the *"engineering code of conduct"* for any automatic shutdown system in a chemical plant. It ensures protection is engineered with the same rigour as the process itself.

### 1.3 Scope Boundary — What ISA 84.01-2003 Does and Does Not Cover

| Aspect | Covered by ISA 84.01-2003 | Not Covered |
|---|---|---|
| Process Risk Identification | ✔ | — |
| SIS Functional Design | ✔ | — |
| Mechanical relief devices | — | ✔ (API 520/521) |
| Active / Passive fire protection | — | ✔ (NFPA) |
| Explosive atmospheres | — | ✔ (IEC 60079) |
| Nuclear / Aviation | — | ✔ (Different sectors) |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for exams:** ISA 84.01-2003 is **process-industry specific**. It does NOT apply to nuclear, aerospace, or automotive domains (those use IEC 61513, IEC 61508, ISO 26262 respectively).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Philosophy of ISA 84.01-2003

The standard is built on **three pillars**:

1. **Lifecycle Management** — Safety must be engineered from *concept* to *decommissioning*, not just at installation.
2. **Risk-Based Decision Making** — Every safety function is justified by how much risk it reduces.
3. **Performance-Based Specification** — Specify *what the system must achieve* (PFD), not *how it must be built* (redundancy, voting, etc.).

### 2.2 The Safety Lifecycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard mandates a **closed-loop lifecycle** with continuous validation:

**Step 1 — Process Hazards Analysis (PHA)**
- Identify *what can go wrong* (deviations, causes, consequences).
- Tools: HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, LOPA.

**Step 2 — Risk Reduction & Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**
- Determine the **tolerable risk** and existing independent protection layers (IPLs).
- Calculate the **additional risk reduction** the SIS must provide.

**Step 3 — Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)**
- The *contract document* defining:
  - Safety function (what action, on which condition)
  - Target SIL (1, 2, or 3)
  - Response time
  - Proof Test Interval (PTI)
  - Safe state

**Step 4 — SIS Design and Engineering**
- Sensor selection, logic solver specification, final element sizing.
- Architectural constraints (HFT — Hardware Fault Tolerance).

**Step 5 — Installation, Commissioning, and Validation**
- Site Acceptance Test (SAT), loop checks, function tests.
- Validation confirms the installed system meets the SRS.

**Step 6 — Operation and Maintenance**
- Routine proof testing, calibration, bypass management.
- Documentation of every change.

**Step 7 — Modification and Management of Change (MOC)**
- Any change must re-enter the lifecycle at the appropriate phase.

**Step 8 — Decommissioning**
- Formal shutdown and disposal procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Skipping a phase invalidates the entire safety argument. A SIL-3 valve in a SIL-1 process is **over-engineered but not unsafe**; a SIL-1 valve in a SIL-3 process is **catastrophically unsafe** even if it "looks" identical.

### 2.3 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — The Heart of the Standard

**SIL** is a *discrete level* (1, 2, 3) specifying the *required risk reduction* for a safety function. The standard correlates SIL with **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**:

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to preserve table formatting**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD$ | Probability system fails to act when demanded | dimensionless, $0 \le PFD \le 1$ |
| Average PFD | $PFD_{avg}$ | $\frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} PFD(t)\, dt$ | dimensionless |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF$ | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | dimensionless |
| Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF$ | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{D}}$ | fraction, $0 \le SFF \le 1$ |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance | $HFT$ | Minimum faults causing loss of function | integer $\in \{0, 1, 2\}$ |
| Proof Test Interval | $TI$ | Time between manual functional tests | months or years |
| SIL Target Band (Low Demand) | $SIL_{band}$ | $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ | see table below |

### 2.5 SIL vs. PFD Target Band (Low-Demand Mode)

| SIL | PFD Range (Low Demand) | RRF Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ | $10$ to $100$ | Burner Management, simple overpressure |
| **2** | $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ | $100$ to $1\,000$ | Reactor interlocks, fired heater trips |
| **3** | $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ | $1\,000$ to $10\,000$ | Compressor surge, toxic gas detection |
| **4** | $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$ | $10\,000$ to $100\,000$ | Rarely used in process industry |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not memorize PFD ranges incorrectly.** KTU examiners check the *upper and lower bounds* and the *exclusive/inclusive* nature of the inequalities. Use $\le$ for lower bound and $<$ for upper bound as shown above.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems on offshore platforms.
- **Chemicals:** Reactor temperature/pressure interlock loops.
- **Refineries:** Furnace flame-fail trips, tank overfill prevention.
- **Pharma:** Containment isolation in solvent handling.

The standard enables **independent safety auditing** — auditors can verify a facility's safety claim by tracing it back to documented PHA, LOPA, SRS, and PFD calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of SIL from LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)

The standard uses LOPA as the primary tool for SIL determination. The derivation is a semi-quantitative risk comparison.

**Step 1 — Identify the Hazard Scenario**

Consider a reactor with runaway reaction. The initiating event is *"loss of cooling water"* with frequency:

$$
f_{I} = 0.1 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the Consequence Severity**

A runaway results in rupture → financial loss of **$10 million** + 1 fatality.

**Step 3 — Define the Tolerable Frequency**

Company policy: risk to fatality must be $\le 10^{-5}$ per year for any single scenario.

$$
f_{T} = 10^{-5} \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Existing Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**

Assume there are **2 IPLs** (operator intervention, basic process control) each reducing the event frequency by a factor of **10**.

**Step 5 — Calculate the Required Risk Reduction for the SIS**

$$
RRF_{SIS} = \frac{f_{I} \times f_{IPL\,1} \times f_{IPL\,2}}{f_{T}}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
RRF_{SIS} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.1}{10^{-5}}
$$

$$
RRF_{SIS} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-5}} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

**Step 6 — Convert RRF to Required PFD**

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{RRF_{SIS}} = \frac{1}{100} = 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 7 — Assign SIL**

Compare $PFD_{required} = 10^{-2}$ with the SIL table. Since $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$, the required SIL is **SIL 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning logic of the derivation:** We are *subtracting log-values* (risk layering is multiplicative in frequency, additive in log). The SIS must bridge the gap between *as-is* risk and *tolerable* risk.

### 3.2 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo1 Architecture

A **1-out-of-1** system has **one** sensor, **one** logic solver, **one** final element. The failure rate of each subsystem is $\lambda$. The PFDavg of a 1oo1 is:

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda^{1oo1} \cdot TI}{2}
$$

**Detailed Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — The PFD grows linearly with time between tests** (worst case at end of TI).

$$
PFD(t) = \lambda \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Take the average value over the interval $[0, TI]$** to get the *expected* unavailability.

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} PFD(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $PFD(t)$.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} \lambda t\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Pull the constant out of the integral.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} t\, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the elementary integral $\int t\, dt = \frac{t^{2}}{2}$.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda}{TI} \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{TI}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the limits $0$ and $TI$.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda}{TI} \left( \frac{TI^{2}}{2} - \frac{0^{2}}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the ratio $TI^{2}/TI$.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda \cdot TI}{2}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda \cdot TI}{2}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PFDavg for Common Architectures

| Architecture | PFDavg Formula | Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\dfrac{\lambda \cdot TI}{2}$ | 0 | Non-critical SIL 1 only |
| 1oo2 | $\dfrac{\lambda^{2} \cdot TI^{2}}{6}$ | 1 | SIL 2/3 high-demand |
| 2oo2 | $\dfrac{2 \cdot \lambda \cdot TI}{3}$ | 1 (common cause) | Rare, avoids spurious trips |
| 2oo3 | $\dfrac{7}{12}\lambda^{2}TI^{2}$ | 1 | High-criticality with trip availability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The factor in 1oo1 is **½**, in 1oo2 it is **1/6**, in 2oo3 it is **7/12**. Marks are commonly lost when students confuse these fractions.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 1oo1 temperature sensor has $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Proof test interval $TI = 8760$ h (1 year). Determine SIL feasibility.

**Step 1 — Compute PFDavg.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda \cdot TI}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator.**

$$
5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 43.8 \times 10^{-3} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 2.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to SIL bands.**

Since $2.19 \times 10^{-2} \approx 10^{-1.66}$, the result falls within $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$, i.e., **SIL 1**.

**Conclusion:** A single 1oo1 sensor with annual proof testing can only achieve **SIL 1**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISA 84.01-2003 Safety Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Hazards Analysis PHA] --> B[LOPA Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Safety Requirements Specification SRS]
    C --> D[SIS Design and Engineering]
    D --> E[Installation and Commissioning]
    E --> F[Validation and Site Acceptance Test]
    F --> G[Operation and Maintenance]
    G --> H{Management of Change}
    H -- Yes --> A
    H -- No --> I[Periodic Proof Testing]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Decommissioning]
    J --> K[End of Lifecycle]
```

### 4.2 SIS Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Variable] --> S1[Sensor 1]
    P --> S2[Sensor 2]
    S1 --> LS[Logic Solver]
    S2 --> LS
    LS --> V1[Final Control Element 1]
    LS --> V2[Final Control Element 2]
    V1 --> SS[Safe State Action]
    V2 --> SS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) loops from *process measurement* → *logic decision* → *final actuation*. The redundancy of sensors and valves determines the SIL achievability.

### 4.3 SIL Determination Decision Matrix (LOPA Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initiating Event Identified] --> B0[Estimate Initiating Frequency fi]
    B0 --> C0[Multiply by All IPLs]
    C0 --> D0[Compare to Tolerable Frequency ft]
    D0 --> E0{Risk Reduced?}
    E0 -- No --> F0[Additional Risk Reduction Needed]
    E0 -- Yes --> G0[No SIS Required]
    F0 --> H0[Compute RRF and PFD Required]
    H0 --> I0[Match to SIL Table]
    I0 --> J0[Assign SIL 1, 2, or 3]
```

### 4.4 Relationship Between ISA 84.01-2003 and IEC 61511

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[IEC 61508 Generic Standard] --> B1[IEC 61511 Process Sector Standard]
    A1 --> C1[IEC 61513 Nuclear Sector]
    A1 --> D1[IEC 62061 Machinery Sector]
    B1 --> E1[ISA 84.00.01 2004 US Adoption]
    B1 --> F1[ISA 84.01 2003 Legacy]
    E1 --> G1[SIL 1 2 3 only]
    F1 --> G1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** ISA 84.01-2003 was the *transitional* US standard. The current KTU-recommended version is **ISA 84.00.01-2004** which is a *harmonized* ANSI/ISA/IEC adoption. Both share the same SIL framework and lifecycle structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024 Style]**  
*Define the term Safety Integrity Level (SIL) as per ISA 84.01-2003. List the four discrete SIL levels used in the process industry with their corresponding PFD ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SIL is a discrete level, defined in ISA 84.01-2003, that specifies the *degree of risk reduction* a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) must achieve, expressed in terms of Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD).
- **SIL Table (2 marks):**

| SIL | PFD Range (Low Demand) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $10^{-3} < PFD < 10^{-2}$ — the **inclusive lower bound** and **exclusive upper bound** are the standard convention. Wrong inequality = 0.5 mark penalty.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**  
*State any three phases of the Safety Lifecycle as mandated by ISA 84.01-2003.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Process Hazards Analysis (PHA)** — to identify the hazards and determine the required risk reduction.
2. **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** — the documented specification defining safety function, SIL, response time, and proof test interval.
3. **Operation, Maintenance, and Proof Testing** — to keep the SIS in operational state throughout the lifecycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Point:** Students who also mention **Management of Change (MOC)** and **Decommissioning** earn partial credit.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*  
Explain the LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) methodology used in ISA 84.01-2003 for SIL determination. How is the required PFD calculated from the tolerable risk?

**(b)** **[7 Marks]**  
A reactor has an overpressure scenario with the following data:
- Initiating event frequency: $f_{I} = 10^{-1}$ per year
- One existing IPL (basic control) with $PFD_{IPL} = 10^{-1}$
- Tolerable frequency: $f_{T} = 10^{-5}$ per year

Calculate the required SIL for the safety instrumented function.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**LOPA Methodology (5 marks):**

1. **Identify the hazardous scenario** (e.g., overpressure) and its **initiating event** (e.g., blocked outlet).
2. **Estimate the frequency of the initiating event** $f_{I}$ using industry generic failure data.
3. **Identify and credit all Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** — passive (relief valve), active (basic control), human (operator).
4. **Determine the tolerable frequency** $f_{T}$ from corporate risk criteria or regulatory limits.
5. **Compute the required risk reduction factor** for the SIS:

$$
RRF_{SIS} = \frac{f_{I} \cdot \prod_{i} PFD_{IPL\,i}}{f_{T}}
$$

6. **Calculate the required PFD:**

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{RRF_{SIS}}
$$

7. **Map the PFD to a SIL** using the standard table.

**Why LOPA is central (2 marks):**
- LOPA bridges *qualitative* PHA and *quantitative* SIL assessment.
- It provides *defensible, auditable* risk reduction claims.
- ISA 84.01-2003 endorses LOPA as the *preferred* SIL determination method.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Write down the given data (1 mark):**

$$
f_{I} = 10^{-1}\;/y,\quad PFD_{IPL} = 10^{-1},\quad f_{T} = 10^{-5}\;/y
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the frequency after the IPL (1 mark):**

$$
f_{IPL} = f_{I} \times PFD_{IPL} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-2}\;/y
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the RRF formula (1 mark):**

$$
RRF_{SIS} = \frac{f_{IPL}}{f_{T}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}} = 10^{3} = 1\,000
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to required PFD (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{RRF_{SIS}} = \frac{1}{1\,000} = 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Map to SIL (1 mark):**  
Since $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$, the required SIL is **SIL 2**.

**Step 6 — State the engineering interpretation (2 marks):**
The Safety Instrumented Function must be designed such that its *average probability of failure on demand* is between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$. This typically requires:
- A **1oo2 or 2oo3 sensor configuration**, OR
- A **1oo2 valve configuration** with a proof test interval $\le 2$ years.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*  
Describe the Safety Lifecycle phases of ISA 84.01-2003 in detail. Why is the concept of "Management of Change" critical?

**(b)** **[7 Marks]**  
A 1oo1 level transmitter has $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h. The proof test interval is $TI = 1.5$ years ($1.5 \times 8760 = 13\,140$ h). Determine the achievable SIL.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Safety Lifecycle Phases (5 marks):**

| Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 1. PHA | Identify hazards, causes, consequences |
| 2. SIL Determination (LOPA) | Calculate target risk reduction |
| 3. SRS | Document the safety function formally |
| 4. SIS Design | Select sensors, logic solver, final elements |
| 5. Installation & Commissioning | Mount, wire, calibrate |
| 6. Validation | Verify installed system meets SRS |
| 7. Operation & Maintenance | Routine testing, calibration, bypass control |
| 8. MOC | Re-evaluate risk when *any* change occurs |
| 9. Decommissioning | Safe shutdown of the SIS |

**Why MOC is critical (2 marks):**
- Process plants undergo *constant change* (feedstock, instruments, procedures).
- *Any* change can introduce new failure modes or alter the PFD of a SIF.
- Without MOC, the *original SIL claim becomes invalid* — the safety argument is broken.
- ISA 84.01-2003 mandates MOC to maintain the *integrity of the safety lifecycle loop*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}\;/h,\quad TI = 13\,140\;h
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo1 PFDavg formula (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda \cdot TI}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 13\,140}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator (1 mark):**

$$
2 \times 10^{-6} \times 13\,140 = 26\,280 \times 10^{-6} = 2.628 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 2 (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = 1.314 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 6 — Map to SIL (1 mark):**  
Since $1.314 \times 10^{-2}$ lies in the range $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$, the achievable SIL is **SIL 1**.

**Step 7 — Engineering conclusion (1 mark):**  
A single 1oo1 transmitter with 1.5-year testing is **only acceptable for SIL 1 applications**. For higher SIL, *redundancy* (1oo2) or *more frequent testing* would be required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Students often forget to convert years to hours; $\lambda$ in per-hour demands $TI$ in hours.
> 2. **Architecture confusion** — Marks are lost by applying the 1oo2 formula $\lambda^{2}TI^{2}/6$ to a 1oo1 system.
> 3. **Range inversion** — Confusing SIL 3 with SIL 1 (i.e., mixing up which end is the safer band).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for ISA 84.01-2003**

- **ISA 84.01-2003 = US process-industry SIS standard**, harmonized with IEC 61511.
- **Scope:** Process industries only — **not** nuclear, aerospace, or machinery.
- **Three pillars:** Lifecycle, Risk-based, Performance-based.
- **Safety Lifecycle:** PHA → LOPA → SRS → Design → Install → Validate → Operate → MOC → Decommission.
- **SIL Levels:** Only SIL 1, 2, 3 in process industry; SIL 4 is excluded.
- **PFD Bands (low demand):** $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ across SIL 1 to 4.
- **PFDavg Formulas:**
  - 1oo1: $\lambda TI/2$
  - 1oo2: $\lambda^{2}TI^{2}/6$
  - 2oo3: $7\lambda^{2}TI^{2}/12$
- **LOPA Equation:** $RRF_{SIS} = (f_{I} \cdot \prod PFD_{IPL})/f_{T}$.
- **MOC is mandatory** — re-entry point to the lifecycle.
- **Validation** confirms *installed* system meets SRS.
- **SRS is the contract** — defines SIF, SIL, response time, TI, safe state.
- **Reduction in test interval = lower PFD = higher achievable SIL.**
- **Two key documents:** SRS (specification) and SIL verification report (proof).
- **Architectural constraint:** A SIL target has a minimum HFT requirement; *high HFT* allows *low SFF* and vice versa.
- **Latest version:** ISA 84.00.01-2004 supersedes 84.01-2003; both share the same lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Safety Life Cycle (SLC)** is a structured, multi-phase engineering management framework that defines all the activities required to achieve and maintain functional safety of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) throughout its entire operational existence. It is the backbone of the international standards **IEC 61508** (umbrella standard for functional safety of E/E/PE systems) and **IEC 61511** (sector-specific standard for the process industry), both of which form the syllabus spine of KTU's PEICT862 course.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim phrasing style):**
> The Safety Life Cycle is a sequential series of phases beginning with process concept definition and terminating with decommissioning of the Safety Instrumented System, governing the systematic identification of hazards, assessment of risks, allocation of safety functions, and verification that each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) meets its target Safety Integrity Level (SIL).

The SLC is **iterative** — meaning a hazard discovered in the operation phase can force the engineer to revisit the design phase. It is also **risk-driven** — every decision flows from a quantified risk assessment, not intuition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are building a house in Kerala during monsoon season. The Safety Life Cycle works like a homeowner's "risk-and-safety diary":

1. **Before construction** (Concept phase) — you check whether the plot is on a flood plain.
2. **Risk identification** (Hazard analysis) — you ask, "What can hurt my family?" (flood, fire, short circuit, gas leak).
3. **Safety requirements** (SRS) — you write down: "I need a 1-meter-high plinth, a lightning arrestor, and a fire extinguisher."
4. **Design & purchase** (Design phase) — you choose *how strong* each safety item must be. A foam extinguisher is enough for a small fire (SIL 1), but an industrial kitchen needs a CO₂ system (SIL 3).
5. **Build & test** (Installation & Validation) — you test the alarm rings, check the extinguisher pressure.
6. **Move in & live** (Operation & Maintenance) — you periodically test the smoke detector every 6 months.
7. **Renovate** (Modification) — if you add a solar panel, you re-check electrical safety.
8. **Demolish** (Decommissioning) — when the house is too old, you safely dismantle the gas line.

The SLC enforces this discipline so that **no step is skipped** — and that is exactly the kind of answer KTU examiners reward.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key safety metrics every KTU student must memorize:**
> - **Risk (R)** = Frequency of hazardous event (f) × Consequence severity (C)
> - **Tolerable Risk** — the level of risk above which the operator must take action (typically defined by national/company policy).
> - **ALARP** — *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* — the economic-optimization principle.
> - **PFD** — Probability of Failure on Demand (dimensionless, between 0 and 1).
> - **RRF** — Risk Reduction Factor = 1 / PFD (a SIL-3 SIF with PFD = 10⁻³ has RRF = 1000).
> - **SIL Targets (per IEC 61511):** SIL 1: **10⁻² ≤ PFD < 10⁻¹**; SIL 2: **10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻²**; SIL 3: **10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³**; SIL 4: **10⁻⁵ ≤ PFD < 10⁻⁴**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Phases of the Safety Life Cycle (IEC 61511 Phases in Sequence)

The standard defines the SLC in 16 numbered clauses (clause 5 of IEC 61511-1). For KTU examination purposes, the syllabus condenses them into **9 logical phases**:

### Phase 1 — Process / Plant Concept Definition
- Define the boundary of the study (which unit, which skid, which vessel).
- Identify the *process safety objective* — what the plant is designed to do safely.
- Output: **Process Design Intent Document**.

### Phase 2 — Hazard Identification & Risk Evaluation
- Use HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study), FMEA, or What-If analysis.
- For every identified hazard, calculate the **unmitigated risk** (risk with *no* SIS in place).
- Compare against *tolerable risk* curve.
- Output: **Hazard Register** + **Risk Graph / Risk Matrix**.

### Phase 3 — Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers
- Apply the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**.
- Independent Protection Layers (IPLs): control loops, alarms, relief valves, dikes, SIS, etc.
- Determine whether an additional SIF is required and at what SIL.
- Output: **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)**.

### Phase 4 — SIS Design (Conceptual + Detailed)
- Select architecture: **1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3** (one-out-of-one, etc.).
- Choose sensors, logic solver, final elements.
- Calculate the **average PFD for the SIF** and verify it meets the SIL target.
- Output: **Detailed Design Document** + **PFD calculation sheet**.

### Phase 5 — Installation, Commissioning & Validation
- Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR).
- Site Acceptance Test (SAT), Factory Acceptance Test (FAT).
- Loop checks, calibration, proof testing.
- Output: **Validation Report**.

### Phase 6 — Operation & Maintenance
- Operate within operating procedures.
- Conduct **proof tests** at the calculated interval (T_proof).
- Maintain the **SIF documentation**.
- Output: **Operating & Maintenance Log**.

### Phase 7 — Modification Management
- Any change (sensor swap, setpoint change, valve replacement) triggers a **Management of Change (MOC)** procedure.
- Risk assessment is re-validated for the change.
- Output: **Modification Risk Re-Assessment**.

### Phase 8 — Decommissioning
- The SIS is removed only after the process unit is also being decommissioned.
- Ensure residual risks are controlled.
- Output: **Decommissioning Safety Report**.

## 2.2 Why the SLC Matters (The "Why" Reasoning)

- **Single point of accountability** — the engineer knows exactly which phase owns each risk.
- **Traceability** — every SIL claim must trace back to a hazard in Phase 2.
- **Defensibility** — in a court of law after an industrial accident (e.g., the Bhopal or Texas City incidents), the SLC documentation is the legal evidence that *due diligence* was performed.
- **Lifecycle cost optimization** — early-phase corrections are ~10× cheaper than late-phase redesigns.

## 2.3 The "How" — Flow Logic of the SLC

1. Define *what* could go wrong (Hazard).
2. Quantify *how bad* it would be (Consequence).
3. Quantify *how often* it could happen (Frequency).
4. Compare to *how much risk is acceptable* (Tolerable Risk).
5. Decide if extra protection is needed (LOPA → SRS).
6. Design the protection to a *numeric target* (SIL → PFD).
7. Verify it is built correctly (Validation).
8. Verify it *stays* correct (Proof Testing).
9. Update whenever something changes (MOC).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Equation | $R = f \cdot C$ | $f$ = frequency (events/year), $C$ = consequence (cost/lives) | $R$ is scalar; vector form used in risk matrix |
| Tolerable Risk Comparison | $R_{actual} \le R_{tolerable}$ | $R_{tolerable}$ from company policy | Drives need for SIF |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{S} + PFD_{L} + PFD_{FE}$ | Subscripts S, L, FE = Sensor, Logic solver, Final Element | Used to verify SIL target |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | $RRF$ of SIL 3 = 1000 |
| Average PFD (1oo1 architecture) | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (1/hr), $T_{proof}$ in hours | IEC 61508 simplified model |
| Average PFD (1oo2 architecture) | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{6}$ | same as above | Lower PFD (more reliable) but higher spurious trip rate |
| SIL-PFD Mapping | $10^{-(SIL+1)} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-SIL}$ | SIL is integer 1, 2, 3, 4 | Strictly enforced by IEC 61511 |
| Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Required to exceed a minimum per SIL |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) | IEC 61508 table-driven | depends on SIL and SFF | Dictates architecture choice |
| Proof Test Coverage Factor | $PTC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,detected}}{\lambda_{DU,total}}$ | dimensionless 0 to 1 | Used to discount $\lambda_{DU}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industry:** Refineries, LNG terminals, petrochemical plants (e.g., BPCL Kochi, FACT) all use SLC.
- **Power Generation:** Nuclear reactors use a sister standard IEC 61513 which is built on the same SLC philosophy.
- **Pharmaceutical:** FDA's Process Analytical Technology (PAT) guidance references SLC for batch reactors.
- **Rail Signaling:** EN 50128 / 50129 use a similar lifecycle (V-model).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Unmitigated Risk to Required SIL (Full Board-Work)

This is the **most important numerical flow** for KTU numerical problems. We derive it step by step.

**Given data (a typical KTU 2-mark part-question):**
- Frequency of initiating event: $f_{init} = 0.1$ per year
- Consequence cost: $C = 1 \times 10^{6}$ monetary units per event
- Tolerable risk per year: $R_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{4}$ monetary units/year

**Step 1 — Compute unmitigated risk.**

$$
R_{unmitigated} = f_{init} \cdot C
$$

$$
R_{unmitigated} = 0.1 \times 1 \times 10^{6} = 1 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{units/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with tolerable risk.**

$$
R_{unmitigated} = 1 \times 10^{5} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; R_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{4}
$$

Since $1 \times 10^{5} > 1 \times 10^{4}$, additional protection is required. The risk is **10 times** higher than tolerable.

**Step 3 — Compute required risk reduction factor (RRF).**

$$
RRF_{required} = \frac{R_{unmitigated}}{R_{tolerable}}
$$

$$
RRF_{required} = \frac{1 \times 10^{5}}{1 \times 10^{4}} = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Convert RRF to required PFD.**

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{RRF_{required}}
$$

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Map PFD to SIL target.**

From the IEC 61511 table: a PFD between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$ corresponds to **SIL 1**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Required SIL} = 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the *exact* logic examiners use to award marks. Skipping any one of these 5 steps loses you 1–2 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Average PFD for 1oo1 Architecture (Board Working)

**Start with the assumption of a constant dangerous-undetected failure rate** $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures/hour) and a proof test interval $T_{proof}$ (hours). The probability that a dangerous failure occurs at time $t$ within the proof interval and is not detected is approximately uniform.

**Step 1 — Failure probability density over interval.**

The fraction of the interval during which the system is in failed state, on average, is $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot T_{proof}$ because failures occur uniformly on average.

**Step 2 — Express as probability.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (typical KTU Part B).**

Given $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr (1 year):

$$
PFD_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 4 — Map to SIL.**

$$
2.19 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0219 \in [10^{-2},\, 10^{-1})
$$

This satisfies **SIL 1** but does *not* reach SIL 2. To achieve SIL 2, the engineer must either:
- Reduce $T_{proof}$ to less than $\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 400$ hours, OR
- Use a redundant 1oo2 architecture (next derivation).

## 3.3 Derivation: PFD for 1oo2 Architecture (Board Working)

For 1oo2 (one-out-of-two), the system fails on demand *only if both* channels fail dangerously. Assuming identical channels, the failure rate of the pair is the product of individual failure rates.

**Step 1 — Joint failure density.**

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof interval.**

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^{2}}{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{(\lambda_{DU})^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^{3}}{3} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{6}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical comparison using the previous data.**

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^{2}}{6}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-2})^{2}}{6} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Map to SIL.**

$$
3.2 \times 10^{-4} \in [10^{-4},\, 10^{-3}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 2 achieved (barely; but typically reaches SIL 3)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off note (often asked as 2-marker):** 1oo2 *reduces* dangerous-failure probability but *increases* spurious-trip probability. KTU examiners love this trade-off question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Which Architecture When?

| Architecture | PFD Formula | Spurious Trip Rate | Typical Use Case | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}{2}$ | Lowest | SIL 1 SIFs, non-critical loops | Lowest |
| 1oo2 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{6}$ | Higher | SIL 2/3, where spurious trips are tolerable | High |
| 2oo2 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{3}$ | Lowest | Where spurious trip is unacceptable (e.g., fire pumps) | High |
| 2oo3 | $\dfrac{6 (\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{2}$ (approx) | Moderate | SIL 3 burner management, turbine overspeed | Highest |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's gold line:** "Higher redundancy ⇒ better safety, but worse availability." This single statement has fetched 2 marks in past KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Safety Life Cycle Flow (IEC 61511)

The Mermaid diagram below is engineered to be 100% syntax-safe (no reserved words as node IDs, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Process Concept Definition] --> A2[Phase 2: Hazard Identification and Risk Evaluation]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: SIS Design Conceptual and Detailed]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Installation Commissioning and Validation]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Operation and Maintenance]
    A6 --> A7[Phase 7: Modification Management]
    A7 --> A8[Phase 8: Decommissioning]
    A8 --> A9[End of Safety Life Cycle]

    A2 -. Re-evaluate if new hazard found .-> A2
    A6 -. MOC triggers re-design .-> A4
    A6 -. Periodic proof test .-> A6

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style A2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style A3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style A4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#6c9a3a
    style A5 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style A6 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style A7 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style A8 fill:#b0c4de,stroke:#2f5f8f
    style A9 fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#555555
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Risk-Graph Decision Flow (Functional Architecture)

This block maps how the *inputs* of a hazard propagate to the *outputs* of a SIL target — a frequent KTU diagram-question topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Hazard Scenario] --> B2[Frequency f per year]
    B2 --> B3[Consequence Severity Category]
    B3 --> B4[Unmitigated Risk R equals f times C]
    B4 --> B5{Is R less than Tolerable Risk}
    B5 -- No --> B6[Apply LOPA]
    B5 -- Yes --> B7[No SIF Required]
    B6 --> B8[Compute Required PFD]
    B8 --> B9[Map PFD to SIL Target]
    B9 --> B10[Safety Requirements Specification SRS]
    B10 --> B11[SIS Detailed Design]
    B11 --> B12[Validate PFD equals SIL Target]

    style B1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style B5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style B8 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style B10 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#6c9a3a
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: SIF Architecture Comparison (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Process Variable e.g. Pressure] --> C2[Sensor]
    C2 --> C3[Logic Solver e.g. PLC]
    C3 --> C4[Final Element e.g. Valve]
    C4 --> C5[Process returns to Safe State]

    subgraph SUB_Redundancy
        D1[1oo1 Single Channel] --- D2[1oo2 Dual Channel Voting]
        D2 --- D3[2oo2 Dual Channel Coincidence]
        D3 --- D4[2oo3 Triple Channel Majority]
    end

    C3 -. uses .-> SUB_Redundancy

    style C1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style C5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#6c9a3a
    style SUB_Redundancy fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#888888
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU diagram tips:** Always use *rectangular* boxes (Mermaid `[]`), avoid heavy markdown inside node labels, and label *every* arrow with a verb such as "triggers," "validates," "feeds back."
