<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Safety Life Cycle. List any four phases of the IEC 61511 Safety Life Cycle.**

**Model Answer (board-style):**

The **Safety Life Cycle (SLC)** is a structured, sequential set of engineering activities that begins with the definition of a process and terminates with the decommissioning of the Safety Instrumented System, ensuring that functional safety is achieved and maintained throughout the plant's life.

Four key phases of the IEC 61511 SLC are:
1. **Process / Plant Concept Definition** – establishes the study boundary and process intent.
2. **Hazard Identification & Risk Evaluation** – identifies hazardous events and quantifies their risk.
3. **Safety Function Allocation & SRS** – assigns each SIF a target SIL via LOPA.
4. **Operation & Maintenance** – includes proof testing and record-keeping.

(Any four phases in order; each phase carries 0.5 mark + definition carries 1 mark → **3 marks total**.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the relationship between Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) and the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) of a Safety Instrumented Function?**

**Model Answer:**

The Risk Reduction Factor is the inverse of the average Probability of Failure on Demand of a SIF:

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

For a given SIF, the higher the SIL, the lower the PFD and therefore the higher the RRF. Numerically:
- SIL 1: PFD ∈ [10⁻², 10⁻¹) → RRF ∈ (10, 100]
- SIL 2: PFD ∈ [10⁻³, 10⁻²) → RRF ∈ (100, 1000]
- SIL 3: PFD ∈ [10⁻⁴, 10⁻³) → RRF ∈ (1000, 10000]
- SIL 4: PFD ∈ [10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁴) → RRF ∈ (10000, 100000]

Thus, an increase of one SIL corresponds to a ten-fold improvement (increase) in RRF. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a block diagram, explain the different phases of the Safety Life Cycle as per IEC 61511.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A hazardous event in a refinery has an initiating-event frequency of **0.05 per year** and a consequence cost of **₹50 lakhs per event**. The company's tolerable risk is **₹25,000 per year**.
   (i) Compute the unmitigated risk. (2 marks)
   (ii) Determine whether an additional SIF is required. (1 mark)
   (iii) If yes, find the required SIL. (4 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
Draw the Mermaid-style block diagram (or hand-draw) showing 8 phases in a left-to-right flow with a feedback arrow from Operation & Maintenance back to Design (Modification). Each phase should be captioned in one line.

- *Concept & Scope* → *Hazard & Risk Analysis* → *Allocation of Safety Functions (LOPA)* → *SIS Design* → *Installation & Validation* → *Operation & Maintenance* → *Modification* → *Decommissioning*. **(5 marks)**
- Mention that the SLC is **iterative**, with feedback from Operation to Design whenever a hazard is discovered or a change is made. **(1 mark)**
- Brief one-line purpose of each phase. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Unmitigated risk**

$$
R_{unmitigated} = f_{init} \cdot C = 0.05 \times 50 \times 10^{5} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{₹/year}
$$

**(ii) Compare with tolerable risk**

$$
R_{unmitigated} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{vs.}\;\; R_{tolerable} = 2.5 \times 10^{4}
$$

Since unmitigated risk > tolerable risk, **a SIF is required**. (1 mark)

**(iii) Required SIL**

Step 1 — Required RRF:

$$
RRF = \frac{R_{unmitigated}}{R_{tolerable}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{5}}{2.5 \times 10^{4}} = 10
$$

Step 2 — Required PFD:

$$
PFD_{required} = \frac{1}{RRF} = \frac{1}{10} = 10^{-1}
$$

Step 3 — Map to SIL:

$$
PFD = 10^{-1} \in [10^{-1},\, 10^{-2}) \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{SIL 1}}
$$

(4 marks — 1 for each sub-step + 1 for conclusion)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of *Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)* and show how it is used to determine the required SIL of a Safety Instrumented Function.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 1oo1 sensor channel has a dangerous-undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof-test interval is **6 months** (≈ 4380 hours).
   (i) Calculate the average PFD of the sensor. (3 marks)
   (ii) Identify the SIL achieved by this sensor alone. (1 mark)
   (iii) If the entire SIF consists of three such 1oo1 channels (sensor, logic solver, valve), and each has identical PFD, find the total SIF PFD. (2 marks)
   (iv) Does the SIF meet SIL 2? Justify. (1 mark)

**Model Solution for (a):**
- Define LOPA as a **semi-quantitative** method that sits between qualitative HAZOP and full Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). **(2 marks)**
- List typical IPLs: process design, basic process control, alarms, relief devices, dikes, SIS. **(2 marks)**
- Show that LOPA multiplies the *initiating-event frequency* by the *PFD of each independent protection layer* to get the *mitigated event frequency*. If the mitigated frequency is still above the company target, the difference is the SIF's required PFD, which maps to a SIL. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Average PFD of 1oo1 sensor**

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(ii) SIL of the sensor alone**

$$
4.38 \times 10^{-3} \in [10^{-3},\, 10^{-2}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 2}
$$

**(iii) Total SIF PFD (three identical channels in series)**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{valve} = 3 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-3} = 1.314 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**(iv) SIL assessment**

$$
1.314 \times 10^{-2} \in [10^{-2},\, 10^{-1}) \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{SIL 1 only}}
$$

The SIF does **not** meet SIL 2 because the total PFD exceeds the SIL-2 ceiling of $10^{-2}$. The engineer must either (a) reduce $T_{proof}$ to less than ~ 3,300 hours, (b) add redundancy, or (c) replace the sensor with a higher-quality device having lower $\lambda_{DU}$. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the series summation:** Many students compute the PFD of *one* channel and stop. The KTU model answer explicitly awards marks for $PFD_{SIF} = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_{FE}$.
> 2. **Confusing SIL 1 and SIL 2 boundaries:** A PFD of exactly $10^{-2}$ belongs to SIL 1 (the *upper bound* belongs to the lower SIL). Get the inequality direction wrong and you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring units of $\lambda_{DU}$:** It is per hour; convert $T_{proof}$ from years to hours, not the other way around.
> 4. **Skipping the conclusion line:** Always end with a boxed $\boxed{\text{SIL} = n}$ statement. Examiners scan for it.
> 5. **Mermaid on the answer sheet:** KTU does *not* allow Mermaid. Practice drawing neat rectangles with arrows in pen before the exam.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- **SLC = 8 (or 16) sequential phases**, starting with concept, ending with decommissioning. *(KTU frequently asks for the phase list.)*
- **Risk equation:** $R = f \times C$. Always compare with $R_{tolerable}$ to decide if a SIF is needed.
- **PFD ranges** for SIL 1/2/3/4: $[10^{-1},10^{-2})$, $[10^{-2},10^{-3})$, $[10^{-3},10^{-4})$, $[10^{-4},10^{-5})$ respectively. *Note the strict open interval on the low side.*
- **RRF = 1 / PFD.** An order-of-magnitude change in PFD = one SIL level.
- **1oo1 PFD** $= \lambda_{DU} T_{proof} / 2$. **1oo2 PFD** $= (\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2} / 6$.
- **Trade-off mantra:** "Higher redundancy ⇒ better safety, worse availability (more spurious trips)." Use it whenever a question asks for design justification.
- **PFD_SIF = PFD_sensor + PFD_logic + PFD_final element** (series addition of probabilities).
- **ALARP** must be invoked *before* settling on a SIL — the cheapest option that meets ALARP is preferred.
- **LOPA** is the standard semi-quantitative method for SIL allocation; one IPL ≈ one order-of-magnitude risk reduction (PFD ≈ 10⁻¹).
- **Proof test interval $T_{proof}$** is the *single most powerful* design knob: halving it halves the PFD.
- **SFF and HFT** are hardware architectural requirements defined in IEC 61508 tables — used to decide if a device can be used at a given SIL.
- **Management of Change (MOC):** Any change re-triggers Phases 2–4 of the SLC.
- **Decommissioning** is rarely examined, but examiners love it for 2-markers: "SIF is removed only after the process is also being decommissioned."
- **Industrial example for viva:** A Furnace Burner Management System (BMS) in a refinery heater is a classic SIL-3 application using 2oo3 voting on flame scanners.
- **Mnemonic to recall SLC order:** **"C**oncept **H**azard **A**llocation **D**esign **I**nstall **O**perate **M**odify **D**ecommission" → **CHAI-OM-D**.
- **Last-line catch phrase for answers:** Always close with "*This satisfies IEC 61511 requirements for functional safety management.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Integrity Parameters, Safety Integrity Level Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Integrity Parameters, Safety Integrity Level Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Safety Integrity?

**Safety Integrity** is defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** as the probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) performs its required safety function successfully under all stated conditions within a stated period of time. In the KTU 2024 framework, safety integrity is a *quantitative* measure (a number) and a *qualitative* measure (architectural constraints) combined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> "Safety Integrity is the likelihood of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) satisfactorily performing the required safety functions under all stated conditions within a stated period of time." — *IEC 61508, Part 4, Clause 3.5.8*

The concept of safety integrity is **two-dimensional**:

1. **Systematic Safety Integrity** — determined by design, specification, installation, commissioning, operation, maintenance, and modification practices.
2. **Random Hardware Safety Integrity** — determined by the reliability of the random hardware failures.

---

## 1.2 What is Safety Integrity Level (SIL)?

A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety instrumented functions to be allocated to the safety instrumented systems. The higher the SIL number, the higher the risk reduction required, and consequently the lower the probability of dangerous failure.

| SIL | Risk Reduction Demand |
| :-- | :-- |
| 4   | Extremely High        |
| 3   | High                  |
| 2   | Medium                |
| 1   | Low                   |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Convention:** In the **Process Industry** (IEC 61511), only **SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3** are commonly used. **SIL 4** is rarely required and is generally confined to nuclear, aerospace, and certain rail signalling applications. For the KTU PEICT862 syllabus, the process-industry convention (SIL 1–3) is the primary focus.

---

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Staircase of Defenses"

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a staircase that leads to a 10-metre-deep industrial pit. The pit is the *hazard*; the staircase is the *protection*.

- If you have a single weak handrail and an old ladder, the chance of slipping is high → **Low Safety Integrity (SIL 1)**.
- Add a strong industrial handrail, a non-slip surface, and a safety net → **Medium Safety Integrity (SIL 2)**.
- Add enclosed glass barriers, motion sensors, automatic stops, and a harness → **High Safety Integrity (SIL 3)**.
- Build a fully enclosed, AI-monitored, redundant, fault-tolerant elevator with mechanical locks → **Very High Safety Integrity (SIL 4)**.

Each "extra layer" is essentially a **reduction in the probability that the protective function fails** when demanded. **SIL is the engineering language that quantifies this protection.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of SIL as a "safety dial." Turn it up (higher number) → the system must be **more reliable** → the **probability of failure must be smaller** → the **risk reduction must be greater**.

---

## 1.4 The Two Operating Modes — A Critical Distinction

A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) can be demanded in two fundamentally different ways, and the mathematical parameter for measuring safety integrity differs:

| Mode of Operation | Trigger Frequency | KTU 2024 Parameter |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| **Low Demand Mode** | ≤ 1 demand per year | **PFDavg** (Average Probability of Failure on Demand) |
| **High Demand / Continuous Mode** | > 1 demand per year (or continuous) | **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> If a question describes a process that is "checked annually" or "tested once a year," expect a **PFDavg** answer. If the question describes a continuous burner management system or a continuous overspeed trip, expect a **PFH** answer. Mixing the two units is one of the most common mark-losing mistakes in KTU valuation.

---

## 1.5 Why Do We Need SIL Selection?

**SIL selection** is the engineering methodology used to determine *how much* safety integrity is required for a given hazard. It is essentially the **bridge** between the *risk assessment* (how dangerous is it?) and the *engineering design* (how reliable must the protection be?).

The flow of logic is:

$$\text{Hazard Identification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Risk Estimation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Risk Tolerance Criteria} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SIL Determination} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SIF Design}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The syllabus specifically requires knowledge of: (i) the quantitative parameters PFD, PFH, RRF, SFF, HFT, and β-factor; (ii) the SIL selection methods — Risk Graph, Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA), and the calibrated risk matrix / IEC 61511 risk graph. Memorise the numerical boundaries of the SIL table — this is a guaranteed Part-A question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Safety Integrity Parameters

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of six core parameters. Each is explained below in operational bullet logic.

---

### 2.1.1 PFD — Probability of Failure on Demand

**Definition:** The probability that a SIF fails to perform its safety function when a demand occurs. It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

**Operational Logic (Why and How):**
- **Why:** It is the *natural metric* for low-demand protection systems. A fire & gas system on a storage tank may be demanded only once in 10 years; we cannot measure "failures per hour" meaningfully. PFD is, therefore, a probability.
- **How:** For a redundant 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) architecture, the PFD is dominated by the *common cause* and *failure to danger* of both channels simultaneously.

For a **single channel (1oo1)** system:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{sys}} = \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$$

where:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (failures per hour)
- $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (proof test interval / 2, ideally)

---

### 2.1.2 PFDavg — Average Probability of Failure on Demand

**Definition:** The *time-averaged* PFD over the entire proof test interval. It accounts for the fact that PFD rises linearly with time after a proof test and drops back to near zero after the test.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why:** A SIF is periodically proof-tested. Right after a test, PFD is at its minimum; right before the next test, PFD is at its maximum. The "average" is what the certification bodies use.
- **How:** Integrated over one proof test interval $T_i$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} \text{PFD}(t)\,dt$$

For a 1oo1 system, the standard simplification is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D \cdot T_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **½** comes from the average of a linear ramp from 0 to $\lambda_D T_i$ over the period $T_i$. This is the most-tested derivation in KTU examination papers.

---

### 2.1.3 PFH — Probability of Failure per Hour

**Definition:** The average frequency of a dangerous failure of a SIF performing its safety function, expressed per hour. It is the reciprocal-like metric for **high-demand or continuous-mode** operation.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why:** A continuous-mode system (e.g., burner management) is *always* in operation. The "demand" is effectively continuous; a "failure to function" is measured as a *rate* (failures per hour), not a probability.
- **How:** The KTU board tests the relationship between the sum of dangerous failure rates of the channels and the diagnostic coverage.

For a 1oo1 system:

$$\text{PFH}_{1oo1} = \lambda_D$$

For a 1oo2 (1-out-of-2 voted) system — assuming the danger is *failure of both*:

$$\text{PFH}_{1oo2} \approx 2\,(1 - \beta)\,\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta\,\lambda_{DU}$$

where $\beta$ is the **beta-factor** (fraction of failures that are common cause).

---

### 2.1.4 RRF — Risk Reduction Factor

**Definition:** The ratio of the frequency of the hazardous event *without* the SIF to the frequency *with* the SIF. RRF is the inverse of PFDavg.

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$$

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why:** Risk analysts prefer talking in terms of "how much did we reduce the risk?" rather than "what is the failure probability?" RRF is intuitive — "we made the system 1000 times safer."
- **How:** A SIF with $\text{PFDavg} = 10^{-3}$ provides an RRF of **1000** (often written as "three 9s" of risk reduction in industry jargon).

---

### 2.1.5 SFF — Safe Failure Fraction

**Definition:** The fraction of all failures (safe + dangerous) that result in a *safe* state.

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

where:
- $\lambda_S$ = safe (spurious) failure rate
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = detected dangerous failure rate
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = undetected dangerous failure rate

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why:** The SFF determines whether a given architecture *can* achieve a particular SIL. A high SFF means most failures are *safe* — the system "fails in a safe direction" — which is good for safety, even if it is bad for production.
- **How:** SFF is paired with **HFT** (Hardware Fault Tolerance) to enforce architectural constraints per IEC 61508 Tables 2 and 3.

---

### 2.1.6 HFT — Hardware Fault Tolerance

**Definition:** The number of faults a hardware subsystem can tolerate *while still* performing its safety function. It is a non-negative integer (0, 1, 2 …).

**Examples:**
- **HFT = 0:** A single sensor. One fault → system fails.
- **HFT = 1:** A dual-redundant (1oo2 or 2oo2) system. One fault → system *still works*.
- **HFT = 2:** A triple or quad-redundant system. Two faults → system still works.

> [!NOTE]
> **1oo2 vs 2oo2 — A Common Confusion:**
> - **1oo2** (1-out-of-2) trips on the *first* trip signal from either channel — used for **high-integrity safety** (e.g., emergency shutdown).
> - **2oo2** (2-out-of-2) requires *both* channels to agree — used to **prevent spurious trips** in critical process control (e.g., aviation fly-by-wire).

---

## 2.2 The IEC 61508 / 61511 SIL Table — Memorise This

### Table 2.A: SIL vs PFDavg (Low-Demand Mode)

| SIL | PFDavg Target Range | RRF Range |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 4   | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 |
| 3   | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 |
| 2   | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 |
| 1   | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |

### Table 2.B: SIL vs PFH (High-Demand / Continuous Mode)

| SIL | PFH Target Range (per hour) |
| :-- | :-- |
| 4   | $\geq 10^{-9}$ to $< 10^{-8}$ |
| 3   | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ |
| 2   | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ |
| 1   | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students confuse **PFDavg ranges** with **PFH ranges**. The numerical bounds are *different* because the units are different. Memorise both tables. A common valuation error: writing "SIL 2 = PFDavg $10^{-6}$" — the correct lower bound is $10^{-3}$.

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield equations you must be able to reproduce from memory in a KTU exam.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | PFDavg (1oo1) | $\tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | PFDavg (1oo2) | $\tfrac{1}{3}\lambda_D^2 T_i^2 + \beta \lambda_D \tfrac{T_i}{2}$ | dominant term is $\beta \lambda_D T_i / 2$ for typical $\beta$ |
| 3 | PFDavg (2oo3) | $\lambda_D^2 T_i^2$ | average of three pair combinations |
| 4 | PFH (1oo1) | $\lambda_D$ | per hour |
| 5 | PFH (1oo2) | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta \lambda_{DU}$ | per hour |
| 6 | RRF | $1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | dimensionless, integer-like |
| 7 | SFF | $(\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD})/(\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$ | fraction (0 to 1) or % |
| 8 | MTTF (sensor) | $1/\lambda$ | hours; assume $\lambda \approx \lambda_D + \lambda_S$ for total |
| 9 | Architectural Constraint (Type A, HFT=0) | Requires SFF 60–90% for SIL 1, 90–99% for SIL 2 | See IEC 61508 Table 2 |
| 10 | Architectural Constraint (Type B, HFT=1) | SFF 60–90% for SIL 1, 60–90% for SIL 2, 90–99% for SIL 3 | See IEC 61508 Table 3 |

> [!TIP]
> **Notation Conventions for KTU 2024:**
> - $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ (total dangerous failure rate = detected + undetected).
> - $T_i$ = proof test interval.
> - $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime ≈ $T_i/2$ for undetected failures.
> - $DC$ = diagnostic coverage = $\lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These parameters are not academic — they are the **legal and contractual basis** for safety system certification worldwide:

1. **Process Industry (Oil & Gas, Petrochemical):** SIL 2 and SIL 3 are the daily working levels. A SIF with PFDavg > $10^{-2}$ is generally *unacceptable* for a high-consequence scenario.
2. **Pharmaceutical & Fine Chemicals:** Batch reactors, exothermic reactions, and dust-explosion hazards are protected with SIL 1 to SIL 2 SIFs.
3. **Power Generation:** Boiler overspeed trips and turbine protection are continuous-mode SIL 3 systems.
4. **Functional Safety Audits:** TÜV, exida, and Bureau Veritas use the PFDavg/PFH/SFF values *literally* to certify the SIF before commissioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industry Connect:** Most Indian process plants (Reliance Jamnagar, BPCL Kochi, ISRO, ONGC) require all ESD (Emergency Shutdown) and F&G (Fire & Gas) systems to be **TÜV-certified** at the stated SIL. The numerical boundary of SIL 2 ($10^{-3} \le \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$) is a working reality on every plant.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFDavg of a 1oo1 System

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a single-channel Safety Instrumented Function (1oo1) with:
- Constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ (failures per hour).
- Proof test interval $T_i$ (hours).
- After each proof test, the system is restored to "as good as new."
- Failure is detected only by proof test (no online diagnostics).

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Reasoning

Let $P(t)$ be the (unreliability) probability that the system has *failed dangerously* by time $t$ after a proof test. Since failures follow a Poisson process with rate $\lambda_D$, for small $\lambda_D t$:

$$P(t) \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t$$

(We use the first-order Taylor expansion because $\lambda_D t \ll 1$ in practice — a SIF is designed to be highly reliable.)

### 3.1.3 The Instantaneous PFD

At time $t$ after a test, the PFD is:

$$\text{PFD}(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t$$

### 3.1.4 Time-Averaging Over One Test Interval

The average PFD over $[0, T_i]$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \text{PFD}(t)\, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \lambda_D t\, dt \\
&= \frac{\lambda_D}{T_i} \cdot \left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{T_i} \\
&= \frac{\lambda_D}{T_i} \cdot \frac{T_i^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i}$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the proof test interval $T_i$ *doubles* the PFDavg. Halving $\lambda_D$ (e.g., by using a more reliable sensor) *halves* the PFDavg. This linear relationship is what makes 1oo1 the *baseline* architecture in functional safety textbooks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFDavg of a 1oo2 System (Simplified)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A 1oo2 (1-out-of-2 voted) system has two parallel channels. The safety function trips if *either* channel demands a trip. The system *fails dangerously* only if **both** channels fail dangerously.

Include the **β-factor** to account for common-cause failures (CCF): a fraction $\beta$ of the dangerous failures affect both channels simultaneously.

### 3.2.2 Reasoning — Independent Component

For an *independent* failure of both channels (probability $(1-\beta)\lambda_D t$ for each):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}}(t) &\approx (1-\beta)^2 \lambda_D^2 t^2 \approx (1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 t^2 \quad \text{(leading order)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(We drop the $(1-\beta)^2$ factor difference and use the dominant term.)

### 3.2.3 Reasoning — Common-Cause Component

A fraction $\beta$ of the dangerous failures is common to both channels. For this component, the system behaves like a 1oo1:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}}(t) \approx \beta \lambda_D t$$

### 3.2.4 Total Instantaneous PFD

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2}(t) \approx (1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta \lambda_D t$$

### 3.2.5 Time-Averaged PFDavg

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} &= \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^{T_i}\!\left[(1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta \lambda_D t\right] dt \\
&= \frac{1}{T_i}\left[(1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 \frac{T_i^3}{3} + \beta \lambda_D \frac{T_i^2}{2}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 T_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\beta \lambda_D T_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.6 Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \frac{1}{3}(1-\beta)\lambda_D^2 T_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\beta \lambda_D T_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For typical $\beta \approx 0.05$–$0.1$ and $T_i = 1$ year, the second term (CCF, $\beta$ term) **dominates** by an order of magnitude. The CCF is the *real* limitation of redundancy — the *independent* improvement from the second channel is nearly free in theory but only marginally useful in practice because both channels share the same design, environment, and maintenance regime. This is the **"common cause failure trap"** and it is a favourite KTU question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between RRF and PFDavg

### 3.3.1 Starting Point

The Risk Reduction Factor is, by definition, the reciprocal of the average probability of failure on demand:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example

A SIF is to be designed to reduce the frequency of a hazardous event from $F_{\text{unprotected}} = 10^{-2}$ events/year down to a tolerable frequency $F_{\text{tolerable}} = 10^{-5}$ events/year.

**Step 1 — Compute Required RRF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF}_{\text{required}} &= \frac{F_{\text{unprotected}}}{F_{\text{tolerable}}} \\
&= \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}} \\
&= 10^{3} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to PFDavg:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, required}} &= \frac{1}{\text{RRF}_{\text{required}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify SIL:**

From Table 2.A, $10^{-3} \le \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$ corresponds to **SIL 2**.

**Conclusion:** The SIF must be designed and certified to SIL 2.

---

## 3.4 The Architectural Constraint (Route 2H or Route 1H)

IEC 61508 provides *two* routes to demonstrate that a subsystem meets a given SIL:

### Route 1H (Hardware Fault Tolerance Route)

The SFF and HFT together determine the maximum achievable SIL. The KTU 2024 reference values for **Type A** (simple, well-understood) subsystems are:

| SFF | HFT = 0 | HFT = 1 | HFT = 2 |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| < 60% | Not allowed | SIL 1 | SIL 2 |
| 60% to < 90% | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| 90% to < 99% | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |
| ≥ 99% | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | SIL 4 |

For **Type B** (complex, less well-understood) subsystems, the maximum SIL at HFT = 0 is reduced by one level.

### Route 2H (Quantified Probabilistic Route)

The PFDavg (or PFH) is calculated using the formulas in §3.1–3.2, and the result is checked against the SIL target table (Table 2.A or 2.B).

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Approach in Industry:** Most real-world designs use **both routes** — the probabilistic calculation shows the numerical SIL achieved, while the architectural constraint ensures that the *design itself* is robust enough (e.g., you cannot claim SIL 3 on a single transmitter with no diagnostics, even if the proof test is so frequent that the PFDavg looks low).

---

## 3.5 SIL Selection Methods — The Three Industry-Standard Techniques

### 3.5.1 Method 1 — Risk Graph (IEC 61511 / IEC 61508)

The risk graph is a *qualitative-to-semi-quantitative* technique. Four parameters are evaluated:

| Parameter | KTU Abbreviation | Possible Values |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Consequence of the hazardous event | C | C1 (minor injury), C2 (serious injury or death), C3 (multiple fatalities), C4 (catastrophic) |
| Frequency of exposure to the hazard | F | F1 (rare), F2 (frequent) |
| Possibility of avoidance | P | P1 (possible under certain conditions), P2 (almost impossible) |
| Demand rate (probability of the dangerous event occurring without the SIF) | W | W1 (very low, < $10^{-2}$/yr), W2 (low, $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$/yr), W3 (medium to high, > $10^{-1}$/yr) |

The risk graph is traversed as:

$$C + F + P + W \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SIL recommendation}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** A typical exam question gives a 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 risk graph and asks "What SIL is recommended for a C2, F2, P1, W2 scenario?" The student is expected to follow the path through the graph. Memorise the **typical recommended starting point** of the graph: C2-F2-P1-W2 → **SIL 2**.

### 3.5.2 Method 2 — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)

LOPA is a *semi-quantitative* method. Each **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** is assigned a typical PFD:

| Layer of Protection | Typical PFD |
| :-- | :-- |
| Control loop (BPCS) | $10^{-1}$ |
| Operator intervention (trained, with clear indication) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Alarm (BPCS-based) | $10^{-1}$ |
| Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) — depends on SIL | $10^{-1}$ (SIL 1) to $10^{-5}$ (SIL 4) |
| Relief valve | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| Dike / Bund | $< 10^{-2}$ |
| Fire & Gas detection (with mitigation) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |

The LOPA equation is:

$$f_{\text{event, mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating event}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{N}\text{PFD}_i$$

The required SIL of the SIF is then determined by:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF, required}} = \frac{f_{\text{tolerable}}}{f_{\text{initiating event}} \times \prod_{i \neq \text{SIF}} \text{PFD}_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPL Requirements (LOPA Discipline):** Each layer must be (i) **independent** (no shared components, sensors, or logic), (ii) **auditable**, (iii) **effective** for the specific scenario, and (iv) **separate from initiating-event mitigation**. These are the four "LOPA tests" KTU 2024 expects you to apply.

### 3.5.3 Method 3 — Calibrated Risk Matrix (As Low As Reasonably Practicable — ALARP)

A pre-existing risk matrix is *calibrated* against a set of reference incident scenarios, so that the resulting SIL recommendations are consistent across the plant. This is the simplest method, but it requires corporate pre-approval and is hard to standardise across industries.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Method

| Method | Data Required | Time to Apply | Best Suited For |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Risk Graph | Qualitative judgement on 4 parameters | Fast (minutes) | Screening many scenarios, green-field design |
| LOPA | PFD of each layer, initiating-event frequency | Medium (hours) | Detailed analysis, retrofit projects, hazardous scenarios |
| Calibrated Risk Matrix | Pre-built matrix, reference scenarios | Fastest (seconds) | High-volume screening, ongoing operations |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Heuristic:** If the question gives a *single hazard scenario with numbers*, the answer is **LOPA**. If the question gives *four categorical parameters*, the answer is **the risk graph**. If the question asks "what method should a company adopt to standardise SIL selection across 200+ SIFs?", the answer is the **calibrated risk matrix**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Overall SIL Determination Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hazard Identified] --> A1[Consequence Analysis: Severity, Cost, Injury]
    A1 --> A2[Initiating Event Frequency Estimation]
    A2 --> A3[Existing Protection Layer Inventory]
    A3 --> A4{Selection Method?}
    A4 -->|Risk Graph| A5[Evaluate C, F, P, W]
    A4 -->|LOPA| A6[Multiply PFDs of Independent Layers]
    A4 -->|Calibrated Matrix| A7[Look Up Corporate Pre-Calibrated SIL]
    A5 --> A8[Determine Required SIL Level]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Compute Required PFDavg or PFH]
    A9 --> A10[Assign SIF Architecture: 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3, 2oo4]
    A10 --> A11[Verify Architectural Constraint: SFF vs HFT]
    A11 --> A12[Verify Probabilistic PFDavg vs SIL Table]
    A12 --> A13[SIL Certified SIF]
    A13 --> A14[Independent Safety Assessment by TUV or exida]
```

## 4.2 Risk Graph Decision Logic (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Consequence C?]
    R1 -->|C1 Minor| R2a[SIL 0 or No SIF]
    R1 -->|C2 Serious Injury| R3a
    R1 -->|C3 Multiple Fatalities| R4a
    R1 -->|C4 Catastrophic| R5a[SIL 3 or SIL 4]
    R3a{Frequency F?}
    R3a -->|F1 Rare| R3b{Possibility P?}
    R3a -->|F2 Frequent| R3c{W Demand Rate W?}
    R3b -->|P1 Possible| R3d[SIL 1]
    R3b -->|P2 Impossible| R3e[SIL 2]
    R3c -->|W1 Very Low| R3f[SIL 1]
    R3c -->|W2 Low| R3g[SIL 2]
    R3c -->|W3 Medium High| R3h[SIL 3]
    R4a{Frequency F?}
    R4a -->|F1 Rare| R4b[SIL 2]
    R4a -->|F2 Frequent| R4c[SIL 3 or higher]
```

## 4.3 SIF Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Process Hazard Source] -->|Process Variable| A1[Sensor Channel 1]
    S -->|Process Variable| A2[Sensor Channel 2]
    A1 -->|Signal| L1[SIS Logic Solver Channel A]
    A2 -->|Signal| L2[SIS Logic Solver Channel B]
    L1 -->|Trip Signal| F1[Final Element 1: Shutdown Valve]
    L2 -->|Trip Signal| F2[Final Element 2: Shutdown Valve]
    F1 --> P[Mitigation of Hazard]
    F2 --> P
```

## 4.4 PFDavg vs Time — Proof Test Cycle (Mermaid Plot Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Time 0: After Proof Test, PFD near zero] -->|Linear ramp| T1[Time Ti: Before Next Proof Test, PFD at maximum lambdaD x Ti]
    T1 -->|Proof test resets| T2[Time Ti+: PFD back to near zero]
    T2 -->|Linear ramp again| T3[Time 2Ti: Max PFD reached again]
    T3 -->|Average over cycle| AVG[Mean PFD = half x lambdaD x Ti]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The saw-tooth shape represents the PFD rising linearly from a low value (post-test) to a peak (pre-test) and then dropping back after the next proof test. The horizontal line marked "PFDavg" represents the time-averaged value, which is exactly half the peak value. This is the visual basis of the $\frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i$ formula.

## 4.5 SIL Selection Method Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: Need to Determine SIL] --> S1{Question Complexity}
    S1 -->|Low: many SIFs to screen| S2[Use Calibrated Risk Matrix]
    S1 -->|Medium: 4 categorical params given| S3[Use Risk Graph]
    S1 -->|High: numerical PFDs available| S4[Use LOPA]
    S2 --> S5[Look up pre-calibrated table]
    S3 --> S6[Trace C-F-P-W path through graph]
    S4 --> S7[Multiply PFDs, derive PFD SIF required, look up SIL]
    S5 --> S8[Result: SIL Level]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
```

## 4.6 Common-Cause Failure Concept (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Common Cause Failure Source e.g., shared power supply, shared calibration error] -->|Affects both| CH1[Channel 1 of SIF]
    C -->|Affects both| CH2[Channel 2 of SIF]
    CH1 -->|Channel 1 fails dangerous| M1
    CH2 -->|Channel 2 fails dangerous| M2
    M1 --> AND[AND Logic: Both Must Fail]
    M2 --> AND
    AND --> SYS[System Fails Dangerously]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`

**Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). With the help of a table, show the relationship between SIL and PFDavg for low-demand mode of operation.**

#### Model Answer (Expected for 3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) used to specify the safety integrity requirements of safety instrumented functions, as defined in IEC 61508 / IEC 61511.

**Table (2 marks):**

| SIL | PFDavg Range (Low-Demand Mode) |
| :-- | :-- |
| 1   | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ |
| 2   | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ |
| 3   | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ |
| 4   | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]`

**Differentiate between PFDavg and PFH. Under what operating conditions is each parameter used?**

#### Model Answer (Expected for 3 Marks)

| Aspect | PFDavg | PFH |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Full form | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | Probability of Failure per Hour |
| Unit | Dimensionless probability | Failures per hour |
| Operating mode | Low-demand mode (≤ 1 demand/year) | High-demand / continuous mode (> 1 demand/year) |
| Standard reference | IEC 61508 Table 2 | IEC 61508 Table 3 |
| Typical application | Emergency shutdown tested yearly | Burner management, overspeed trip |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (1 mark reserved for correct unit/usage matching):** Many students write "PFDavg for high demand" and lose a mark. Always check the *mode of operation* in the question first.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 - Choice 1]`

#### (a) (7 Marks) — PFDavg Derivation and Numerical

**Derive the expression for the average probability of failure on demand (PFDavg) for a 1oo1 architecture. A single-channel SIF has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and is proof-tested every year. Compute its PFDavg and identify the achieved SIL.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — State the assumption (1 mark):** Assume the system is restored to "as good as new" after each proof test. The dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ is constant and small such that $\lambda_D t \ll 1$.

**Step 2 — Instantaneous PFD (1 mark):**

$$\text{PFD}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t$$

**Step 3 — Time-averaging (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} \lambda_D t\, dt \\
&= \frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark):**

$T_i = 1$ year $= 8760$ hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 \\
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify SIL (1 mark):** Since $10^{-2} \le 2.19 \times 10^{-2} < 10^{-1}$, the achieved SIL is **SIL 1**.

---

#### (b) (7 Marks) — LOPA-Based SIL Selection

**A chemical reactor has an overpressure hazard with the following layers of protection: BPCS control loop ($\text{PFD} = 10^{-1}$), operator response to a high-pressure alarm ($\text{PFD} = 10^{-1}$), and a relief valve ($\text{PFD} = 10^{-2}$). The initiating event frequency is $10^{-1}$ per year. The tolerable frequency of the hazardous event is $10^{-6}$ per year. Determine the SIL of the SIF to be added.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — State the LOPA equation (1 mark):**

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the product of non-SIF layers (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i \neq \text{SIF}} \text{PFD}_i &= (10^{-1}) \times (10^{-1}) \times (10^{-2}) \\
&= 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for required SIF PFD (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF, req}} &= \frac{f_{\text{tolerable}}}{f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i} \\
&= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-1} \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-5}} = 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify SIL (2 marks):** A required PFDavg of $10^{-1}$ is in the range $10^{-2} \le \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-1}$, corresponding to **SIL 1**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 - Choice 2]`

#### (a) (7 Marks) — SFF and Architectural Constraint

**For a complex (Type B) sensor subsystem used in a Safety Instrumented Function, the failure rates are: $\lambda_S = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /h, $\lambda_{DD} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /h, $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /h. Calculate the SFF and the maximum achievable SIL for an architecture with HFT = 1.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Total dangerous failure rate (1 mark):**

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

**Step 2 — Total failure rate (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\text{total}} &= \lambda_S + \lambda_D \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 10^{-6} = 14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute SFF (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SFF} &= \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{-6} + 4 \times 10^{-6}}{14 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{14 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.857 = 85.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify SIL from the Type B / HFT = 1 constraint table (3 marks):**
For a Type B subsystem with HFT = 1, an SFF in the range **60% to < 90%** allows up to **SIL 2**. Therefore, the maximum achievable SIL is **SIL 2**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Many students will compute SFF correctly but then jump to "SIL 3" by ignoring the **Type A vs Type B** distinction. Per IEC 61508 Table 3, a Type B subsystem with HFT = 1 cannot achieve SIL 3 unless SFF ≥ 90%. **Always state the table you are using.**

---

#### (b) (7 Marks) — Risk Reduction Factor and SIL Selection

**A SIF must reduce the frequency of a tank overflow event from $5 \times 10^{-2}$ per year down to a tolerable level of $5 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. (i) Compute the required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). (ii) Convert RRF to PFDavg. (iii) State the SIL to which the SIF must be designed.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute RRF (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF}_{\text{required}} &= \frac{f_{\text{unprotected}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^{3} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert RRF to PFDavg (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, req}} &= \frac{1}{\text{RRF}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify SIL (3 marks):**

Comparing with Table 2.A, the value $10^{-3}$ is the *lower boundary* of SIL 2 and the *upper boundary* of SIL 3. Per IEC convention, the inequality is $10^{-3} \le \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$ for SIL 2. Since $10^{-3}$ is *included* in this range, the required SIL is **SIL 2** (the lower bound of SIL 2 inclusive).

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary Convention:** This is a high-frequency KTU mark-loser. The convention is $10^{-3} \le \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$ for SIL 2. If the calculation gives exactly $10^{-3}$, it is **SIL 2, not SIL 3**. Always state the convention explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Module:**
> 1. **Mixing PFD and PFH** — using PFD in hours or PFH in probability units.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor** in the 1oo1 PFDavg derivation.
> 3. **Ignoring the β-factor** when designing a redundant SIF — the CCF term usually dominates.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the SIL boundary** — boundary at $10^{-3}$ belongs to SIL 2, not SIL 3.
> 5. **Confusing SFF calculation** — dividing by total including $\lambda_{DU}$ vs total excluding it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **SIL** is a discrete risk-reduction target on a 1-to-4 scale, with **4** being the most stringent.
- [ ] **PFDavg** applies to **low-demand mode** (≤ 1 demand/year) and is dimensionless.
- [ ] **PFH** applies to **high-demand / continuous mode** and is measured in **per hour**.
- [ ] The **SIL table (low demand):** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$, SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- [ ] The **SIL table (high demand):** SIL 1 = $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ /h, SIL 2 = $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ /h, SIL 3 = $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ /h, SIL 4 = $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ /h.
- [ ] **PFDavg for 1oo1:** $\frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i$ — the canonical derivation.
- [ ] **PFDavg for 1oo2:** dominated by the **β-factor (CCF)** term, not by the $\lambda_D^2$ term.
- [ ] **RRF = 1 / PFDavg** — the conceptual link between reliability and risk reduction.
- [ ] **SFF** = (safe + detected dangerous) / (total failures); required together with **HFT** for architectural constraints.
- [ ] **HFT** = number of faults tolerated; for Type A / HFT = 0 / SFF ≥ 60%, **SIL 1** is achievable; for Type A / HFT = 0 / SFF ≥ 99%, **SIL 3** is achievable.
- [ ] **Three SIL selection methods:** **Risk Graph** (qualitative), **LOPA** (semi-quantitative, IPL-based), **Calibrated Risk Matrix** (corporate-wide screening).
- [ ] **LOPA equation:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$.
- [ ] **Industry standard:** IEC 61508 (generic) and IEC 61511 (process industry); TÜV / exida certification is the practical proof of SIL compliance.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for SIL parameter boundaries:** "**S**afe **P**arameters **F**rom **H**igh **D**emand" — SPFH-D, where S=PFD, P=PFH, F=factor, H=hour, D=demand.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining SIL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defining SIL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 / IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (one out of a possible four) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety instrumented functions (SIFs) to be allocated to the safety instrumented systems (SIS). SIL is defined in the international standard **IEC 61508** (Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems) and is operationalized in the process industry via **IEC 61511**.

Where **Safety Integrity** is defined as the *probability of a safety instrumented system (SIS) satisfactorily performing the required safety functions under all stated conditions, within a stated period of time*.

There are **four discrete SIL levels — SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, and SIL 4** — with **SIL 4** being the most stringent (highest level of risk reduction) and **SIL 1** being the least.

| Mode of Operation | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Demand Mode** (PFD_avg) | ≥ 10⁻² to < 10⁻¹ | ≥ 10⁻³ to < 10⁻² | ≥ 10⁻⁴ to < 10⁻³ | ≥ 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁴ |
| **High Demand / Continuous Mode** (PFH per hour) | ≥ 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ | ≥ 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ | ≥ 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ | ≥ 10⁻⁹ to < 10⁻⁸ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two operating regimes defined by IEC 61508:**
> - **Low Demand Mode:** The safety function is demanded no more than **once per year**, and a **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** metric is used.
> - **High Demand / Continuous Mode:** The safety function is demanded more than once per year, and a **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)** metric is used.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Airbag"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a car's airbag system.** The airbag is a *protective layer* that is normally dormant. It must reliably deploy *the instant* a crash happens. We don't use airbags every day — most days, the car drives fine. When a crash occurs, we need the airbag to inflate with a very high probability of success.

The **SIL** of the airbag system is like a **grade of reliability label** stamped on it:
- A car requiring a basic airbag (to meet minimum legal safety) might be **SIL 1** (decent protection, ~1 in 10 chance it might fail to deploy when needed).
- A high-end luxury or autonomous vehicle might require **SIL 3**-grade redundancy (extremely reliable — fails in only ~1 in 10,000 demands).
- Spacecraft or nuclear-reactor-grade safety would demand **SIL 4**.

The **higher the SIL**, the more rigorously engineered, redundantly designed, and thoroughly tested the safety system must be.

---

## 1.3 Why Defining SIL Matters in Process Industries

In a refinery, chemical plant, or LNG terminal, uncontrolled releases of hydrocarbons, toxic gases, or high-pressure steam can lead to catastrophic loss of life, asset damage, and environmental disasters. **Defining a SIL** for each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is the *quantitative backbone* of the entire functional safety lifecycle. Without it:

- You cannot justify the cost of redundant transmitters, voting logic (1oo2, 2oo3), or high-grade final elements.
- You cannot demonstrate regulatory compliance (e.g., OSHA PSM, COMAH, HSE UK).
- You cannot communicate the *required* risk reduction to designers, operators, and auditors in a single, internationally agreed number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions on "Defining SIL" typically test three ideas: (1) the discrete-level concept, (2) the relationship to PFD/PFH, and (3) the difference between demand-mode and continuous-mode SIL targets. Master all three to score full marks.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

The following metrics are the **bedrock numerical language** used in defining SIL per **IEC 61508:2010**:

- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** — dimensionless probability in [0, 1], typically expressed in orders of magnitude (e.g., 10⁻³).
- **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)** — failures per hour, e.g., 10⁻⁷ /hr.
- **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** — dimensionless integer ratio, RRF = 1 / PFD.
- **SIL** — discrete ordinal level (1, 2, 3, 4) per **IEC 61508 Tables 2 and 3**.
- **λ (Failure Rate)** — failures per hour, often split into **λ_DU (dangerous undetected)** and **λ_DD (dangerous detected)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard:** The numerical SIL targets cited in Section 1.1 are taken directly from **Table 2 (low demand) and Table 3 (high demand / continuous mode)** of **IEC 61508-1:2010**. These boundaries are *fixed by the standard* and must be memorized verbatim for KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Hazard to SIL

To **"define"** a SIL is to execute a structured, defensible, *quantitative* reasoning chain. Below is the KTU-recommended logical flow that every Board examiner expects a student to articulate:

1. **Identify the Hazard:** Pinpoint the process deviation (e.g., high pressure in a reactor) that could lead to harm.
2. **Identify the Consequence:** What happens if the hazard is unmitigated? (e.g., vessel rupture, fire, fatality).
3. **Determine the Risk without Protection:** Estimate the unmitigated frequency × severity (this is the *inherent risk*).
4. **Determine the Tolerable Risk:** Consult corporate / regulatory risk-tolerance criteria.
5. **Calculate the Required Risk Reduction:** The difference between inherent and tolerable risk gives the **Required Risk Reduction Factor (RRR)**.
6. **Allocate the Risk Reduction:** Decide how much is achieved by **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)**, **Alarm + Operator Intervention**, **Mechanical Safeguards (relief valves, dikes)**, and the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**.
7. **Assign the SIL:** The SIF's portion of risk reduction maps to a SIL per IEC 61508 Tables 2/3.

> [!TIP]
> The "Why" behind the SIL: It is the *bridge* between qualitative hazard analysis (HAZOP, LOPA) and quantitative hardware design. Without SIL, you cannot specify a transmitter, a logic solver, or a valve with engineering rigor.

---

## 2.2 PFD vs. PFH — Why Two Different Metrics?

- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** is used in **low demand** because we care about: *the next time the safety system is asked to act, will it succeed?* It is **a probability (dimensionless)**, not a rate.
- **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)** is used in **high demand / continuous** because the system is essentially *always* exposed to the hazard. A probability per unit time (e.g., per hour) is the only meaningful way to express its dangerous failure rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual distinction:** PFD is integrated over time (probability over the *next demand*). PFH is instantaneous (rate of failure *per hour of operation*). Mixing them in a calculation is a **common KTU student error** that costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-testable.** Master the units, the range of PFD for each SIL, and the RRF relationship.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Boundary | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RRF = 1 / PFD_avg | Risk Reduction Factor | Dimensionless integer | RRF ≥ 1 (always) |
| 2 | PFD_avg = 1 / RRF | Avg Probability of Failure on Demand | Dimensionless [0, 1] | Used in low demand |
| 3 | PFH = 1 / (mean time to spurious failure equivalence) | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | per hour (hr⁻¹) | Used in high/continuous demand |
| 4 | PFD_SIS = PFD_Sensor + PFD_Logic + PFD_FinalElement | Sum of sub-system PFDs | Dimensionless | Add contributions from each SIF sub-system |
| 5 | λ_DU_total = λ_DU_sensor + λ_DU_logic + λ_DU_valve | Dangerous undetected failure rate sum | per hour | For 1oo1 architecture |
| 6 | PFD_1oo1 ≈ λ_DU × T_proof / 2 | For 1oo1 (single channel) | Dimensionless | T_proof = proof test interval |
| 7 | PFD_1oo2 ≈ (λ_DU × T_proof)² / 6 | For 1oo2 (one-out-of-two voting) | Dimensionless | Lower PFD than 1oo1 |
| 8 | PFD_2oo3 ≈ (λ_DU × T_proof)² / 4 | For 2oo3 voting | Dimensionless | More complex — uses Beta factor model |
| 9 | SIL 1 → PFD ∈ [10⁻², 10⁻¹) | Lowest discrete level | — | Boundary at 10⁻¹ and 10⁻² |
| 10 | SIL 2 → PFD ∈ [10⁻³, 10⁻²) | Moderate level | — | Most common in process industries |
| 11 | SIL 3 → PFD ∈ [10⁻⁴, 10⁻³) | High level | — | Demands redundancy & high T_proof |
| 12 | SIL 4 → PFD ∈ [10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁴) | Highest (rare in process industries) | — | Mainly transport / nuclear |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation alert:** All inequalities are *half-open intervals*. PFD = 10⁻² exactly falls into **SIL 1** (since 10⁻² ≥ 10⁻²). The *upper* bound of a SIL is **not included** in that SIL — it is included in the next lower SIL.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical **ethylene cracker plant**, an emergency shutdown (ESD) of a fired heater might be designated a **SIL 3** SIF. This means:
- The **pressure transmitter** must be **redundant** (e.g., 2oo3 voting).
- The **logic solver** must be **SIL 3-certified** (e.g., a TÜV-certified safety PLC like Siemens S7-400F/H).
- The **final control element** (e.g., a shutdown valve) must achieve a PFD of ~10⁻⁴ or better over the proof-test interval (typically 1–5 years).
- **Proof test intervals** must be defined and tracked in a **Safety Lifecycle Management System**.

This is not theory — it is the literal specification engineers use to purchase equipment, design cabinets, and pass audits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From PFD to RRF (Foundational)

We are given that the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is *defined* as the inverse of its **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg)**. Let us derive and verify this relationship formally.

### Step 1: Define PFD_avg
> **Conversion logic:** By definition, PFD_avg is a *dimensionless probability* (a real number between 0 and 1) representing the likelihood that the SIF *fails* to perform its safety function when a demand occurs.

$$
PFD_{avg} \in [0,\ 1]
$$

### Step 2: Define the Inverse — RRF
> **Conversion logic:** RRF is the *multiplicative factor* by which the SIF *reduces* the inherent (unprotected) risk. If a hazard occurs once every 10 years without protection, and the SIF cuts this to once every 10,000 years, then the RRF is 1000.

$$
RRF \equiv \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

### Step 3: Verify with a Numerical Check
> **Conversion logic:** Insert a representative SIL 2 PFD_avg value (mid-range, 5 × 10⁻³) into the equation and confirm the result is the standard SIL 2 RRF range.

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200
$$

This is consistent with the SIL 2 RRF range of **100 ≤ RRF < 1000** (since 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻²).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFD of a 1oo1 Architecture (Single Channel)

For a *single-channel* (1oo1) SIF with **dangerous undetected failure rate λ_DU** and **proof test interval T_proof**, the average probability of failure on demand is approximated by the standard IEC 61508 formula:

### Step 1: State the assumption
> **Conversion logic:** IEC 61508 assumes that undetected failures accumulate linearly between two proof tests, and are revealed (and repaired) at the proof test. The average PFD is therefore the average of this linear ramp.

$$
PFD_{1oo1}(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_{proof}
$$

### Step 2: Average over the proof-test interval
> **Conversion logic:** The *average* of a linear function f(t) = λ·t over [0, T] is its integral divided by T.

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\ dt
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral
> **Conversion logic:** The antiderivative of t is t²/2. Apply the limits.

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[ \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify
> **Conversion logic:** Substitute upper limit T_proof, lower limit 0 vanishes.

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}^{2}}{2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

### Step 5: Final form
$$
\boxed{\ PFD_{1oo1} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}\ }
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Mapping PFD to SIL

> **Problem Statement:** A SIF consists of a pressure transmitter, a logic solver, and a shutdown valve. The dangerous-undetected failure rates are:
> - λ_DU (transmitter) = 5 × 10⁻⁷ /hr
> - λ_DU (logic solver) = 1 × 10⁻⁷ /hr
> - λ_DU (valve) = 2 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
>
> The proof-test interval is **2 years** (T_proof = 2 × 8760 = **17,520 hours**). The architecture is **1oo1** for all three. Determine the **overall PFD** and the **corresponding SIL**.

### Step 1: Compute PFD of each sub-system (1oo1 formula)
> **Conversion logic:** Use PFD = (λ_DU × T_proof) / 2 for each subsystem independently.

$$
PFD_{TX} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 17520}{2} = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
PFD_{LOGIC} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-7} \times 17520}{2} = \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
PFD_{VALVE} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520}{2} = \frac{3.504 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}
$$

### Step 2: Sum the contributions
> **Conversion logic:** In a 1oo1 SIF, all three subsystems are in *series* — any one failing causes SIF failure. So we add PFDs.

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{TX} + PFD_{LOGIC} + PFD_{VALVE}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 8.76 \times 10^{-4} + 1.752 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 0.876 \times 10^{-3} + 17.52 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ PFD_{SIF} = 22.776 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.28 \times 10^{-2}\ }
$$

### Step 3: Compute RRF
$$
RRF = \frac{1}{2.28 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 43.9
$$

### Step 4: Map to SIL
> **Conversion logic:** 2.28 × 10⁻² lies in the interval [10⁻², 10⁻¹). Per the IEC 61508 Table 2, this is **SIL 1**.

| Quantity | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| PFD_SIF | 2.28 × 10⁻² | Falls in [10⁻², 10⁻¹) → **SIL 1** |
| RRF | ≈ 44 | Risk reduced by a factor of ~44 |
| Dominant contributor | Valve (≈ 77% of total PFD) | Focus quality efforts on the final element |

> [!TIP]
> **Design insight:** Notice that the **valve** dominates the PFD budget (1.752 × 10⁻² out of 2.28 × 10⁻², i.e., ~77%). To achieve a higher SIL (e.g., SIL 2), the engineer should *not* spend money improving the transmitter or logic solver — the only meaningful action is to **upgrade the valve to a redundant 1oo2 or 2oo2 architecture** or **shorten the proof-test interval**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SIL Targets at a Glance

| SIL | PFD_avg (Low Demand) | PFH (High/Continuous Demand) | RRF (Low Demand) | Typical Process Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10⁻² ≤ PFD < 10⁻¹ | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁵ | 10 to 100 | Simple overpressure protection in low-hazard services |
| 2 | 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻² | 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶ | 100 to 1000 | Most common in oil & gas; furnace ESD; compressor anti-surge |
| 3 | 10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³ | 10⁻⁸ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁷ | 1000 to 10,000 | Critical reactors, LNG ESD, offshore blowout preventers |
| 4 | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFD < 10⁻⁴ | 10⁻⁹ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁸ | 10,000 to 100,000 | Rare in process; used in nuclear / some rail signalling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The SIL Definition Logic Chain (Hazard → SIL)

Below is a Mermaid block-flow diagram showing how a SIL is *defined* for a Safety Instrumented Function, starting from the hazard and ending at the discrete SIL label.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identification HAZOP LOPA] --> B[Consequence Analysis Severity Estimation]
    B --> C[Inherent Risk Frequency x Severity]
    C --> D{Compare with Tolerable Risk Criterion}
    D -- Exceeds --> E[Calculate Required Risk Reduction RRR]
    D -- Within --> F[No SIF Required]
    E --> G[Allocate Risk Reduction BPCS Alarms Mechanical Safeguards SIF]
    G --> H[SIF PFD target required]
    H --> I[Map PFD target to SIL via IEC 61508 Table 2]
    I --> J[Assign SIL 1 2 3 4 Label to SIF]
    J --> K[Hardware Design Voting Redundancy Proof Test Interval]
    K --> L[Verify Achieved PFD within SIL target]
    L -- Pass --> M[Operational SIS Documented and Audited]
    L -- Fail --> N[Re-engineer Adjust Architecture or T_proof]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top-left (HAZOP) and follow the arrows downward. The diamond shape (D) is the decision point: *is the inherent risk tolerable without additional protection?* If "Exceeds", the chain continues to a SIF. If "Within", the chain terminates at F (no safety instrumented system required).

---

## 4.2 Architecture Block Diagram — Subsystem PFD Contributions

The following diagram shows how a SIF is decomposed into three sub-systems, each contributing its own PFD, with the total summed to give the overall SIF PFD — and hence the SIL.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Sub-system 1oo1 or 1oo2 or 2oo3] --> S2[Logic Solver Sub-system Safety PLC]
    S2 --> S3[Final Element Sub-system Shutdown Valve]
    S1 -- PFD_Sensor --> SUM((Sum Sigma PFDs))
    S2 -- PFD_Logic --> SUM
    S3 -- PFD_FinalElement --> SUM
    SUM --> S4[Total SIF PFD]
    S4 --> S5{IEC 61508 Table 2 Boundary Check}
    S5 -- 10 to 1 --> S6[SIL 1]
    S5 -- 100 to 10 --> S7[SIL 2]
    S5 -- 1000 to 100 --> S8[SIL 3]
    S5 -- 10000 to 1000 --> S9[SIL 4]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block interpretation:** The `SUM` node is a logical aggregation — not a physical device. In the exam, label it as *"Total PFD = Σ PFD_sub"*. The decision diamond at S5 maps a numerical PFD to its discrete SIL level. Memorize the four boundary checks.

---

## 4.3 Concept-Block Diagram — PFD vs. PFH Decision

This diagram helps students *choose* the right metric when "defining" a SIL — a common exam trap.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[How often is the safety function demanded?] --> Q2{Demand frequency}
    Q2 -- Less than or equal to 1 per year --> R1[Use PFD Probability of Failure on Demand]
    Q2 -- Greater than 1 per year --> R2[Use PFH Probability of Failure per Hour]
    R1 --> S1[Apply IEC 61508 Table 2 Low Demand Mode]
    R2 --> S2[Apply IEC 61508 Table 3 High or Continuous Demand Mode]
    S1 --> T1[SIL assigned based on PFD range]
    S2 --> T2[SIL assigned based on PFH range]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1.1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**"Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). List the four SIL levels with their corresponding PFD_avg ranges in low demand mode as per IEC 61508."** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) used to specify the safety integrity requirements of safety instrumented functions (SIFs) within a safety instrumented system (SIS), as defined by **IEC 61508** / **IEC 61511**.
- **Low Demand Mode PFD Table (2 Marks):**

| SIL | PFD_avg Range |
|---|---|
| 1 | 10⁻² ≤ PFD < 10⁻¹ |
| 2 | 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻² |
| 3 | 10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³ |
| 4 | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFD < 10⁻⁴ |

---

### Question A1.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"Differentiate between PFD and PFH. In which mode of operation is each used?"** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **PFD — Probability of Failure on Demand (1.5 Marks):** Dimensionless probability that a SIF fails to operate on *demand*. Used in **low demand mode** (demand ≤ 1 per year). Bounded by IEC 61508 **Table 2**.
- **PFH — Probability of Failure per Hour (1.5 Marks):** Failure rate per hour (units: hr⁻¹). Used in **high demand or continuous mode** (demand > 1 per year). Bounded by IEC 61508 **Table 3**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** Examiners award 1.5 marks for *what the metric means*, and 1.5 marks for *the mode in which it is used*. Stating only one half is a common cause of 1.5 mark deductions.

---

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question B — Choice A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Part (a)** — Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). Explain with a neat block diagram the procedure for *defining* the SIL of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) in a process plant. Discuss the role of LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) in this process. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):
**1. Definition of SIL (1.5 Marks):**
SIL is a discrete ordinal level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity of SIFs, defined in **IEC 61508** for E/E/PE systems and **IEC 61511** for the process industry. SIL quantifies the *probability* that a SIF will perform its required safety function under stated conditions.

**2. Procedure for Defining SIL (Block Diagram) (3.5 Marks):**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Hazard Analysis (HAZOP) | List of scenarios & consequences |
| 2 | Consequence severity classification | Severity category (e.g., Cat 1–5) |
| 3 | Determine inherent (unmitigated) event frequency | Frequency in events/year |
| 4 | Apply independent protection layers (IPL) credit | Frequency reduction per layer |
| 5 | Compute mitigated event frequency | Final residual frequency |
| 6 | Compare with tolerable frequency target | Gap = *Required Risk Reduction* |
| 7 | Allocate to SIF as PFD target | Numeric PFD range |
| 8 | Map PFD to SIL | SIL 1 / 2 / 3 / 4 label |

**3. Role of LOPA (2 Marks):**
LOPA is a *semi-quantitative* risk-assessment methodology used between HAZOP (qualitative) and SIL assignment (quantitative). It provides a *structured* way to evaluate the *probability* of occurrence of a hazardous event after each *Independent Protection Layer (IPL)* — such as BPCS, alarms, relief valves, dikes, and the SIF itself. By assigning numerical credits to each IPL, LOPA determines the residual event frequency and hence the **PFD target** for the SIF. LOPA is the **bridge** from HAZOP to SIL.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love the phrase *"LOPA is the bridge from HAZOP to SIL."* Memorize and use it.

**Part (b)** — A SIF in a refinery consists of a transmitter (λ_DU = 4 × 10⁻⁷/hr), a logic solver (λ_DU = 1 × 10⁻⁷/hr), and a shutdown valve (λ_DU = 3 × 10⁻⁶/hr). All sub-systems use **1oo1 architecture**. The proof-test interval is **T_proof = 1 year (8760 hr)**. Compute the overall PFD, the RRF, and the SIL achieved. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):
**Step 1 — PFD of each sub-system (1oo1 formula):** (3 Marks)

$$
PFD_{TX} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{3.504 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
PFD_{LOGIC} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.876 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
PFD_{VALVE} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{2.628 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 1.314 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum (series logic):** (1.5 Marks)

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 1.752 \times 10^{-3} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} + 1.314 \times 10^{-2} \approx 1.533 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 — RRF:** (1 Mark)

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{1.533 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 65.2
$$

**Step 4 — SIL mapping:** (1.5 Marks)
Since 1.533 × 10⁻² lies in the interval **[10⁻², 10⁻¹)**, the SIF achieves **SIL 1** (PFD range 10⁻² to 10⁻¹).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the product** of λ_DU × T_proof in the 1oo1 formula (you lose 1 mark per sub-system affected).
> 2. **Mixing up units:** T_proof must be in **hours**, not days or years. If T_proof is given as "1 year", the student must convert to **8760 hr** explicitly.
> 3. **Incorrect SIL boundary:** A PFD of exactly 1.0 × 10⁻² falls into **SIL 1**, not SIL 2 (the upper bound of SIL 1 is inclusive).
> 4. **Adding architectures as if 1oo2:** When the problem says *1oo1*, do **not** divide by 2 or use the (λT)²/6 formula. That is a different architecture.
> 5. **Forgetting the LOPA bridge phrase** in part (a). Examiners expect to see "LOPA bridges HAZOP to SIL" for full marks.

---

### Question B — Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Part (a)** — Explain the concept of *Safety Integrity* as defined in IEC 61508. Differentiate between **hardware safety integrity** and **systematic safety integrity**. List the SIL targets for low demand and high/continuous demand modes. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):
**1. Safety Integrity Concept (1.5 Marks):**
Safety Integrity is the *probability* that a safety-related system will satisfactorily perform the required safety functions under all stated conditions, within a stated period of time (per **IEC 61508-4:2010, Clause 3.5.8**).

**2. Hardware vs. Systematic Safety Integrity (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hardware Safety Integrity | Systematic Safety Integrity |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin of failure** | Random hardware failures (component aging, wear-out) | Systematic failures (design errors, software bugs) |
| **Quantification** | Quantified via PFD / PFH and failure rates (λ) | Quantified via SIL *capability* of the equipment (after quality & lifecycle controls) |
| **Reduction method** | Redundancy, voting, proof testing, diverse hardware | Quality management, validation, diversity, proven-in-use |
| **Time dependence** | Random, increases with operating time | Stable (depends on design) — can be revealed during operation |
| **Examples** | Burnt-out transmitter, stuck valve, dead relay | Incorrect trip setpoint in code, wrong I/O wiring |

**3. SIL Targets Tables (2.5 Marks):** As shown in Section 1.1 above — PFD ranges for low demand (10⁻² to 10⁻⁵) and PFH ranges for high/continuous (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁹).

**Part (b)** — A 1oo2 redundant pressure transmitter system is to achieve SIL 2. Given the following data, compute the maximum allowable **T_proof**.
- λ_DU per transmitter = 2 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
- β-factor (common cause failure) = 5%
- Use the standard 1oo2 PFD formula: PFD_1oo2 = (λ_DU × T_proof)² / 3 *(assuming negligible β contribution for simplicity)*. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):
**Step 1 — Identify the target PFD (1 Mark):**
For SIL 2: 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻². We target the *mid-range* (or the design target — we use the SIL 2 upper boundary, **PFD_target = 10⁻²**, to be conservative).

**Step 2 — State the 1oo2 formula (1 Mark):**

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for T_proof (3 Marks):**

$$
T_{proof}^{2} = \frac{3 \cdot PFD_{target}}{\lambda_{DU}^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{4 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
T_{proof}^{2} = 7.5 \times 10^{9}\ hr^{2}
$$

$$
T_{proof} = \sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{9}} \approx 8.66 \times 10^{4}\ hr
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{proof} = \frac{8.66 \times 10^{4}}{8760} \approx 9.9\ \text{years} \approx 10\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical interpretation (1 Mark):**
A **10-year** proof-test interval is feasible but operationally challenging. Most plants adopt T_proof ≈ **5 years** with online diagnostics, and rely on the redundancy to keep the achieved PFD within SIL 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Choice B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not mix 1oo1 and 1oo2 formulas.** A 1oo1 problem demands PFD = λ·T/2; a 1oo2 problem demands (λ·T)²/3 or (λ·T)²/6 depending on the exact IEC 61508-6 model. Always *restate* which architecture you are using.
> 2. **SIL boundary — conservative vs. midpoint:** Examiners may credit either 10⁻² (upper bound) or 5 × 10⁻³ (midpoint) as the design target, *provided* the student explicitly states the choice. Always write: *"We target PFD = 10⁻² (the SIL 2 upper boundary) for a conservative design."*
> 3. **Units of T_proof:** State the final answer in **years** as well as hours. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if conversion is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Defining SIL**

- ✅ **SIL = Safety Integrity Level** — a *discrete* level (1, 2, 3, 4) defined by **IEC 61508** and applied in process industries via **IEC 61511**.
- ✅ **Four SIL levels**, with **SIL 4 = most reliable**, **SIL 1 = least reliable**.
- ✅ **Two operating modes:** Low demand (use **PFD_avg**) and high/continuous demand (use **PFH**).
- ✅ **PFD ranges (low demand):** SIL 1: [10⁻², 10⁻¹), SIL 2: [10⁻³, 10⁻²), SIL 3: [10⁻⁴, 10⁻³), SIL 4: [10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁴).
- ✅ **PFH ranges (high/continuous demand):** SIL 1: [10⁻⁶, 10⁻⁵), SIL 2: [10⁻⁷, 10⁻⁶), SIL 3: [10⁻⁸, 10⁻⁷), SIL 4: [10⁻⁹, 10⁻⁸) — *all in per-hour units*.
- ✅ **RRF = 1 / PFD_avg** — a dimensionless integer ratio (10, 100, 1000, 10000 for SIL 1, 2, 3, 4 respectively, lower bound).
- ✅ **SIF subsystems** are in **series** for PFD summation: PFD_SIF = PFD_Sensor + PFD_Logic + PFD_FinalElement.
- ✅ **1oo1 PFD formula:** PFD ≈ (λ_DU × T_proof) / 2 — *must halve the product*.
- ✅ **1oo2 PFD formula:** PFD ≈ (λ_DU × T_proof)² / 3 — *do not use 1oo1 formula for redundant systems*.
- ✅ **T_proof** (proof-test interval) is in **hours** — convert from years explicitly (1 year = **8760 hr**).
- ✅ **LOPA is the bridge from HAZOP to SIL** — use this phrase in long answers.
- ✅ **Hardware safety integrity** is about *random* failures; **systematic safety integrity** is about *design and software* errors.
- ✅ The **final element (valve) is typically the dominant PFD contributor** — focus engineering effort there.
- ✅ When in doubt about a SIL boundary, use the **upper bound of the next lower SIL** for *conservative* design.
- ✅ Reference standard: **IEC 61508:2010**, **Tables 2 and 3** — memorize these verbatim.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second recall:** "*SIL is a 1-to-4 discrete level that quantifies the *probability* of a SIF doing its job. Low demand uses PFD (10⁻² → 10⁻⁵); high demand uses PFH (10⁻⁶ → 10⁻⁹). RRF = 1/PFD. LOPA bridges HAZOP to SIL. The valve is usually the weak link.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIL Selection Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SIL Selection Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Selection** is the systematic engineering methodology used to determine the required safety performance (probability of failure on demand, PFD) of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) within a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). The selection quantifies the necessary **risk reduction** by mapping the process hazard level to the discrete integrity tiers defined under **IEC 61511 (Functional safety – SIS for the process industry sector)**.

The SIL Selection Process is fundamentally a **risk-based decision activity** that integrates process safety information (PSI), hazard identification outputs, and consequence analysis to establish the target SIL for each independent protection layer (IPL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student must understand that SIL is **not a property of a device**; it is a **performance target assigned to a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** as a whole. The achieved SIL is verified only after the complete SIF loop (sensor + logic solver + final element) is designed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Braking System Analogy**
>
> Imagine driving a fully loaded truck down a steep mountain road. The truck has:
> - **Engine brakes** (BPCS — basic process control)
> - **Retarder / exhaust brake** (alarm + operator action)
> - **Service brakes** (one safety system)
> - **Emergency brakes / fail-safe spring-applied brake** (the SIF)
>
> Now, before the truck rolls down the hill, an engineer must decide: *How powerful must the emergency brake be?* If the road is dry, light traffic, gentle slope → a *weak* brake suffices (SIL 1). If icy, fully loaded, hairpin bends → a *very powerful* brake is required (SIL 3).
>
> **SIL Selection is exactly this "brake-power calibration"** — except instead of physics, we calibrate using **probability, frequency, and consequence severity**.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Anchors (KTU 2024 Board Favourite)

The four discrete SIL bands, with target PFD ranges per IEC 61511, are:

| SIL | PFDavg (Low Demand) | RRF (Risk Reduction Factor) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SIL 1 | **10⁻² to 10⁻¹** | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | **10⁻³ to 10⁻²** | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³** | 1000 to 10000 |
| SIL 4 | **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴** | 10000 to 100000 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Reduction Logic Chain
