Two safeguards sharing a sensor, a logic solver, a power supply, or a human operator are **NOT independent** — only credit one of them. - **Typical PFD values to memorize for the exam:** BPCS = 10⁻¹, Operator Action = 10⁻¹, PSV = 10⁻², Bund = 10⁻², SIL-2 SIF = 10⁻³, SIL-3 SIF = 10⁻⁴. - **Dual initiating events = AND logic = multiply, never add.** Adding frequencies is the #1 KTU trap. - **The Swiss Cheese / Onion Model:** Each layer has "holes" (failures); a hazard reaches the consequence only if all holes align. - **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) — after meeting TMEL, a cost-benefit check may still be required. - **LOPA is not a substitute for HAZOP** — HAZOP identifies scenarios; LOPA evaluates *how much risk reduction* is needed. - **BPCS vs SIS** — BPCS provides *control* (PFD ≈ 10⁻¹); SIS provides *protection* (PFD per SIL). Never use BPCS as a credited IPL when the IE is a BPCS failure. - **Procedural safeguards** ("operator to check daily") are **not valid IPLs** in LOPA — they are not auditable in real-time. - **Documentation discipline:** Every LOPA node must be captured in a worksheet with assumption, frequency, PFD, calculation, and conclusion — this is a KTU valuation expectation.

topics.title: Conceptual Design / SIL Verification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design / SIL Verification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conceptual Design in Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Conceptual Design** of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is the engineering phase that translates the findings of the **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** and the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** into a logical, sensor-logic-final-element architecture. It defines *what* functions are required, *how* they will be achieved (SIF loops), and the target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** for each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), **before** any hardware is selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Anchor Point:** Conceptual Design answers three fundamental questions:
> 1. **WHAT** hazards must be mitigated? (List of SIFs)
> 2. **HOW** will each hazard be mitigated? (Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element architecture)
> 3. **WITH WHAT CERTAINTY** must it operate? (Target SIL, typically SIL 1, 2, or 3)

## 1.2 SIL Verification

**Formal Definition:**
> **SIL Verification** is the rigorous mathematical and documentary proof that the **as-designed** (or **as-installed**, **as-operated**) Safety Instrumented Function achieves a **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** or **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** that falls within the quantitative range prescribed by IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 for the targeted SIL.

> [!NOTE]
> **SIL Determination** answers: *"What SIL do I need?"*
> **SIL Verification** answers: *"Does my chosen hardware/architecture actually ACHIEVE that SIL?"*
> Verification is performed **after** component selection, **after** architecture choice, and must be repeated after any field modification.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aircraft Pre-Flight" Analogy

Imagine you are an airline engineer responsible for the **autopilot safety system** of a new aircraft:

| Engineering Phase | Aircraft Analogy | SIS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Identifying Risk** | Knowing that engine flame-out is catastrophic | Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) |
| **Setting Target Safety** | "Engine must auto-restart with 99.999% certainty" | Setting Target SIL (e.g., SIL 3) |
| **Conceptual Design** | Choosing 2 sensors + 2 computers + 2 fuel valves (redundancy) | Choosing 2oo3 architecture |
| **SIL Verification** | Mathematically proving the system's failure rate is below the FAA limit | PFD calculation showing 10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³ |
| **Operation** | Aircraft flies passengers safely | Plant operates safely |

> [!TIP]
> **The Crux of Verification:** Just because you *want* SIL 3 does not mean your *design* delivers SIL 3. SIL Verification is the mathematical audit that bridges intent and reality.

## 1.4 Standards Governing Conceptual Design & SIL Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standards (must be memorised for KTU exams):**
> - **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of E/E/PE systems (generic master standard)
> - **IEC 61511** — Functional safety: SIS for the process industry sector
> - **ISA 84.01** — North American equivalent of IEC 61511
> - **ISO 13849** — Safety of machinery (alternative, PL rating system)

## 1.5 Key Quantitative Boundaries (Highlighted for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **SIL ↔ PFDavg ↔ RRF Mapping Table (Low Demand Mode) — Learn by Heart**

| SIL | PFDavg Range | RRF Range | Availability Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **10⁻² to 10⁻¹** | **10 to 100** | 90% – 99% |
| **2** | **10⁻³ to 10⁻²** | **100 to 1000** | 99% – 99.9% |
| **3** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³** | **1000 to 10,000** | 99.9% – 99.99% |
| **4** | **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴** | **10,000 to 100,000** | 99.99% – 99.999% |

> Industry practice (and IEC 61511) limits SIS to **SIL 3 maximum**; SIL 4 is reserved for very high-consequence systems (e.g., nuclear).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Design Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The conceptual design is **not a single activity** but a structured sequence. Below is the canonical KTU/IEC 61511 workflow:

### Step 1: Review the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)
- Identify all **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** required.
- Each SIF must have a **tag number**, a **cause**, a **consequence**, and a **target SIL**.

### Step 2: Decompose Each SIF into Subsystems
A SIF is decomposed into three distinct sub-systems:
1. **Sensor Subsystem (Input)** — detects the abnormal condition (e.g., pressure transmitter, level switch).
2. **Logic Solver Subsystem (Brain)** — the controller/relay that decides to act (e.g., Safety PLC, trip amplifier).
3. **Final Element Subsystem (Output)** — the actuator that brings the process to a safe state (e.g., shutdown valve, motor contactor).

### Step 3: Choose the Architecture (Voting Arrangement)
For each subsystem, decide the redundancy. Voting is denoted as **MooN** (M out of N — M channels voted out of N installed):
- **1oo1** — Single channel, no redundancy (cheapest, lowest availability)
- **1oo2** — Either of two trips the system (high safety, low availability — failsafe)
- **2oo2** — Both of two must trip (low spurious trip risk)
- **2oo3** — Two out of three trips (balanced, common in turbine control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MooN?** Because redundancy *changes* the failure probability. Adding a parallel sensor with a 1oo2 voting logic does **not** double the reliability; the math is governed by the **beta factor** (common cause failure).

### Step 4: Assign Component Failure Rates
Pull from certified databases (e.g., **OREDA**, **exida**, **SINTEF**):
- **λDU** — Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour)
- **λDD** — Dangerous Detected failure rate (per hour)
- **λSU** — Safe Undetected failure rate (per hour)
- **λSD** — Safe Detected failure rate (per hour)
- **T₁** — Proof Test Interval (typically 1 year = 8760 hr)
- **β** — Beta factor for common cause failure (typically 5%–10%)

### Step 5: Compute the PFDavg for Each Subsystem
Use the **simplified PFDavg formulas** for each MooN architecture. (See Formula Sheet below.)

### Step 6: Compute the Total SIF PFDavg
The SIF PFDavg is the **sum** of all three subsystems' PFDavg values:
$$PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{FinalElement}$$

### Step 7: Compare Against Target SIL
If the computed PFDavg falls within the target SIL band → **Verification PASSED**.
If not → **modify** (add redundancy, increase proof test frequency, replace components).

## 2.2 Why Is SIL Verification Necessary?

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual "Why"** — Three engineering realities drive the need for verification:
> 1. **Uncertainty in Failure Data:** Manufacturers' failure rates are statistical averages, not guarantees.
> 2. **Architectural Amplification/Attenuation:** A SIL 2 sensor combined with a SIL 2 logic solver in a **2oo3** architecture may yield SIL 3 (or fail at SIL 1 if poorly chosen).
> 3. **Proof Test Effectiveness:** Real-world proof tests are rarely 100% effective. A 90% effective proof test (PTC = 0.9) can degrade SIL by **one full level**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-arsenal** for solving any SIL verification problem in KTU. Master the formulas, units, and conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PFDavg (1oo1)** | $PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2}$ | λDU, T₁ | Simplified, assumes perfect proof test |
| 2 | **PFDavg (1oo2)** | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3}$ | λDU, T₁ | High safety, low availability |
| 3 | **PFDavg (2oo2)** | $PFD_{2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1$ | λDU, T₁ | Both channels must fail |
| 4 | **PFDavg (2oo3)** | $PFD_{2oo3} = (\lambda_{DU})^2 \cdot T_1^{\,2}$ | λDU, T₁ | Approx. form (exact uses 6λ²T²) |
| 5 | **With Common Cause (β)** | $PFD_{MooN, \beta} = (1-\beta) \cdot PFD_{MooN} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1 / 2$ | β = 5–10% | The "β-factor" model |
| 6 | **With Proof Test Coverage (PTC)** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3} \cdot (1 - PTC)^2 + \dots$ | PTC ∈ [0,1] | Imperfect proof test penalty |
| 7 | **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless | 1oo1 SIL 2 → RRF ≈ 100 |
| 8 | **SIF Total PFD** | $PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensor} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{FE}$ | Subsystem sum | Standard series addition |
| 9 | **PFH (High Demand / Continuous)** | $PFH = \lambda_{DU}$ | λDU only | For high-demand mode (SIL 3 < 10⁻⁷) |
| 10 | **Proof Test Interval Limit (SIL n)** | $T_1 \leq \dfrac{-\ln(1 - 2 \cdot PFD_{target})}{\lambda_{DU}}$ | Target PFD | Maximum T₁ for given SIL |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Do **not** confuse **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand, dimensionless) with **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour, hr⁻¹). They apply to **different operating modes** — PFD is for low-demand, PFH is for high-demand/continuous.

## 2.4 The Beta Factor Concept — Why "Redundancy" Is Not Magic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Intuition:** Common Cause Failures (CCF) defeat redundancy. If two pressure transmitters share the same power supply, calibration procedure, manufacturer batch, and environmental exposure, they may fail **simultaneously** even though they are "redundant". The **β factor** quantifies this:
> - Typically **β = 5%** to **10%** of failures are common-cause.
> - IEC 61508 mandates that β be considered, or verification is invalid.
> - The diagnostic of CCF is performed using the **"beta-factor checklist"** (independent mounting, diverse technology, etc.).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

- **Oil & Gas (Offshore Platforms):** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves are verified as **SIL 3** using 2oo3 voting on pressure sensors and **1oo2** voting on shutdown valves, with proof tests every 6 months.
- **Chemical Reactor Cooling:** High-integrity pressure transmitters in 1oo2D (1oo2 with diagnostics) verified at SIL 2.
- **Power Plant Turbine Overspeed:** Tachometers in **2oo3** voting verified at SIL 3 with annual proof test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFDavg for 1oo1 Architecture

> **Question Setup:** A single channel trips on dangerous failure with rate λDU. Proof test interval is T₁. Find the average probability of failure on demand.

### Step 1: Define the instantaneous unavailability (failure probability at time t)
A channel that has not been proof-tested since t=0 has an increasing failure probability. For a dangerous undetected failure, the unavailability is linear in t:

$$U(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Apply a perfect proof test that restores the channel
Immediately after the proof test (duration negligible), unavailability drops to 0, and it ramps linearly again.

### Step 3: Average the unavailability over the interval [0, T₁]
The average of a linear function from 0 to T₁ is the area under the triangle divided by T₁:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_1} \int_{0}^{T_1} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integral
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_1} \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{T_1^{\,2}}{2}$$

### Step 5: Final simplification
$$\boxed{PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2}}$$

> **Units Check:** λDU is in hr⁻¹, T₁ is in hr → product is dimensionless (as required for probability). ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFDavg for 1oo2 Architecture

### Step 1: System fails only if BOTH channels fail dangerously (AND gate logic)
For independent failures, the system unavailability is the product of individual unavailabilities:

$$U_{system}(t) = U_1(t) \cdot U_2(t) = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2 = \lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot t^2$$

### Step 2: Average over the proof test interval
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_1} \int_{0}^{T_1} \lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot t^2 \, dt$$

### Step 3: Evaluate
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}}{T_1} \left[ \dfrac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{T_1^{\,3}}{3}$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$\boxed{PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3}}$$

> **Sanity Check:** 1oo2 PFD is much smaller than 1oo1 PFD (squared vs linear) → high safety. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFDavg for 2oo3 Architecture (Approximate Form)

### Step 1: System fails if any 2 of 3 channels fail
The system fails if the **third** channel also fails while two are already down. Since the system can tolerate one failure, the relevant failure probability is the probability that **two** dangerous failures occur before proof test.

### Step 2: Use the binomial sum
For 3 channels with the same λDU, the average PFD approximation is:

$$PFD_{2oo3} \approx 6 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1)^2}{6} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1)^2$$

### Step 3: Simplified engineering form
$$\boxed{PFD_{2oo3} \approx \lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot T_1^{\,2}}$$

> **Note:** The exact IEC 61508 form is $PFD_{2oo3} = 6(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^2 - 8(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^3 + 3(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^4$ but the leading term dominates for small λ·T products.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full SIL Verification (KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A chemical reactor has a **high-pressure trip** SIF with the following design data:
> - **Sensor:** Two pressure transmitters in **1oo2** voting, λDU_sensor = 5 × 10⁻⁶ /hr, β = 5%
> - **Logic Solver:** Single Safety PLC, λDU_logic = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
> - **Final Element:** Two solenoid-operated shut-down valves in **1oo2** voting, λDU_FE = 3 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
> - **Proof Test Interval T₁ = 8760 hr (1 year)**
> - **Target SIL: 2** (PFDavg must be between 10⁻³ and 10⁻²)

### Step 1: Compute PFD for Sensor (1oo2 with β-factor)

First, ideal PFD for 1oo2:
$$PFD_{1oo2, ideal} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU, sens})^2 \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3}$$

Numerically:
$$PFD_{1oo2, ideal} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.677 \times 10^{7}}{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.919 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.398 \times 10^{-4}$$

Now apply β-factor correction (β = 0.05):
$$PFD_{sensor, \beta} = (1 - \beta) \cdot PFD_{ideal} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU, sens} \cdot T_1 / 2$$

$$= 0.95 \cdot 6.398 \times 10^{-4} + 0.05 \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2}$$

$$= 6.078 \times 10^{-4} + 0.05 \cdot 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$= 6.078 \times 10^{-4} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{sensor} = 1.703 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 2: Compute PFD for Logic Solver (1oo1)
$$PFD_{logic} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU, logic} \cdot T_1}{2} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} = 4.380 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 3: Compute PFD for Final Element (1oo2)
$$PFD_{FE, ideal} = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.677 \times 10^{7}}{3} = 2.303 \times 10^{-4}$$

Assume β = 0.05 for FE also:
$$PFD_{FE, \beta} = 0.95 \cdot 2.303 \times 10^{-4} + 0.05 \cdot \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2}$$

$$= 2.188 \times 10^{-4} + 6.57 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{FE} = 8.758 \times 10^{-4}}$$

### Step 4: Compute Total SIF PFD
$$PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{FE}$$

$$= 1.703 \times 10^{-3} + 4.380 \times 10^{-3} + 8.758 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 6.959 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 5: Compute RRF
$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{SIF}} = \dfrac{1}{6.959 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 143.7$$

### Step 6: Verification Decision

| Quantity | Value | SIL 2 Band | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| PFDavg | 6.96 × 10⁻³ | 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻² | ✔ Within band |
| RRF | 143.7 | 100 ≤ RRF < 1000 | ✔ Within band |

> **Conclusion:** The SIF **achieves SIL 2** as designed. **VERIFICATION PASSED** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Notice that the **logic solver** (1oo1) contributes the **largest single share** of the PFD (4.38 × 10⁻³ of 6.96 × 10⁻³ ≈ 63%). If the exam asks "where would you improve the design?", the answer is to **redundantly add a second logic solver** (1oo2 voting) to dramatically reduce this dominant term.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Architecture on PFD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a quick reference for design trade-offs. Numerical values assume λDU = 10⁻⁵ /hr and T₁ = 8760 hr (so λDU·T₁ = 0.0876).

| Architecture | PFDavg Formula | Numerical PFD | SIL Achieved | Spurious Trip Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | λ·T₁/2 | 4.38 × 10⁻² | SIL 1 | Lowest |
| 1oo2 | (λ·T₁)²/3 | 2.56 × 10⁻³ | SIL 2 | Highest (failsafe prone) |
| 2oo2 | λ·T₁ | 8.76 × 10⁻² | Below SIL 1 | Very low |
| 2oo3 | (λ·T₁)² | 7.68 × 10⁻³ | SIL 2 | Moderate |
| 1oo2D | 1oo2 + diagnostics | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ | SIL 3 possible | Moderate |

> **Design Insight:** 1oo2 has the **best SIL for the cost** when false trips are tolerable. 2oo3 is preferred in **high-consequence, low-spurious-trip** applications (e.g., turbine overspeed).

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Conceptual Design Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Hazard Analysis PHA] --> B[List of Identified Hazards]
    B --> C[Safety Requirements Specification SRS]
    C --> D[Identify Safety Instrumented Functions SIFs]
    D --> E[Decompose each SIF into Subsystems]
    E --> F1[Sensor Subsystem]
    E --> F2[Logic Solver Subsystem]
    E --> F3[Final Element Subsystem]
    F1 --> G[Choose MooN Architecture per Subsystem]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Assign Component Failure Rates lambda DU]
    H --> I[Compute PFDavg per Subsystem]
    I --> J[Sum Subsystem PFDs to get SIF PFD]
    J --> K{Compare PFD to Target SIL Band}
    K -- Within band --> L[SIL Verification PASSED]
    K -- Outside band --> M[Redesign: More Redundancy / Shorter T1 / Better PTC]
    M --> G
    L --> N[Document Verification Report]
    N --> O[Periodic Re-verification on Changes]
```

> **Interpretation:** This flow captures the iterative nature of conceptual design. Verification is not a one-shot activity — every hardware or operational change triggers re-verification.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The SIF Loop and Its PFD Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable e.g. Pressure] --> B[Sensor 1]
    A --> C[Sensor 2]
    B --> D[Safety Logic Solver]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Final Element 1 Shutdown Valve]
    D --> F[Final Element 2 Shutdown Valve]
    E --> G[Safe State Achieved]
    F --> G

    subgraph PFD1[Subsystem PFDs]
        P1[PFD Sensor]
        P2[PFD Logic]
        P3[PFD Final Element]
    end

    P1 --> P4[PFD SIF = P1 + P2 + P3]
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5{Within SIL n Band?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[SIL n Verified]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Modify Design]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — SIL Verification Decision Tree (Architecture Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin: Target SIL and PFD Target Set]) --> Q1{Is Spurious Trip Cost High?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use 2oo3 or 2oo2 Architecture]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use 1oo2 or 1oo2D Architecture]
    Q2 --> Q4{Calculate PFD with beta factor}
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5{PFD within Target SIL Band?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Done([Verification PASSED])
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Is T1 the Bottleneck?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Reduce T1 Proof Test Interval]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Add More Redundancy]
    Q7 --> Q4
    Q8 --> Q4
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Conceptual Design vs Detailed Design vs Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 PHA] --> P2[Phase 2 Conceptual Design]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Detailed Design]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Installation Commissioning]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Operation and Maintenance]
    P5 -- Modification --> P2

    P2 -. SIL Verification .-> P2
    P3 -. PFD Recalculation .-> P2
    P5 -. Periodic Re-verification .-> P2
```

> **Key Insight:** The dotted "feedback loops" illustrate that SIL Verification is **continuously required** throughout the SIS lifecycle — it is not a one-time Phase 2 activity.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle Mapping (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Phase 1 Process and Hazard Analysis] --> L2[Phase 2 Allocation of Safety Functions]
    L2 --> L3[Phase 3 SIS Design and Engineering]
    L3 --> L4[Phase 4 Installation and Commissioning]
    L4 --> L5[Phase 5 Operation and Maintenance]
    L5 --> L6[Phase 6 Modification and Recommissioning]
    L6 --> L7[Phase 7 Decommissioning]
    L1 -. Verification .-> L1
    L3 -. SIL Verification Conceptual .-> L3
    L5 -. SIL Re-verification .-> L3
```

> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks "in which phase of the IEC 61511 lifecycle is SIL Verification performed?" — The correct answer is that **Conceptual SIL Verification is in Phase 3** (SIS Design and Engineering), with **re-verification** occurring in Phases 4, 5, and 6.
