# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"Differentiate between SIL Determination and SIL Verification. Why is verification considered a mandatory step in IEC 61511 compliance?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **SIL Determination** is the process of *assigning* a target SIL to a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) based on the severity, frequency, and possibility of ignoring the hazard. Methods include Risk Graph, Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA), and the Calibrated Risk method. It answers: *"What SIL do I need?"*
>
> **SIL Verification** is the subsequent quantitative check that the *actual designed* SIF (with chosen hardware, architecture, and proof test interval) achieves the PFDavg that lies within the range specified for the target SIL. It answers: *"Does my design deliver the required SIL?"*
>
> Verification is mandatory under IEC 61511 because **component failure rates, common-cause failures, and proof-test effectiveness are statistical and may cause the as-built system to fall short of the target SIL**. Without verification, the SIL declared in the SRS is unsupported by engineering evidence, violating the standard's requirement for documented functional-safety evidence. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"List the four common MooN voting architectures used in Safety Instrumented Systems. Which architecture provides the best balance between safety and availability, and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four common architectures are:
1. **1oo1** — Single channel, no redundancy. Lowest safety, highest availability. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **1oo2** — Either of two channels trips the system. Highest safety, lowest availability (failsafe-prone). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **2oo2** — Both of two channels must trip. Lowest safety among redundancies, but very low spurious trip rate. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **2oo3** — Two of three channels must trip. *(0.5 mark)*

**2oo3** is generally considered the best balance because it offers **high safety** (probability of two coincident failures is low) while simultaneously maintaining **acceptable availability** (tolerates one channel failure without spurious trip). It is widely used in critical applications such as turbine overspeed protection. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Conceptual Design phase** of a Safety Instrumented System as per IEC 61511. Discuss the key documents produced during this phase and their inter-relationship. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A SIF is designed with the following data:
> - Sensor: 1oo2 voting, λDU = 2 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
> - Logic Solver: 1oo1, λDU = 5 × 10⁻⁷ /hr
> - Final Element: 1oo2, λDU = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /hr
> - Proof Test Interval T₁ = 4380 hr (6 months), assume perfect proof test, neglect β
>
> **Calculate the total PFDavg of the SIF and determine the achieved SIL. State the corresponding RRF.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The Conceptual Design phase of IEC 61511 is **Phase 3** of the safety lifecycle. It transforms the output of PHA and the SRS into a concrete SIS architecture. The key activities and documents are:

1. **Review of SRS:** Defines the SIF tag, cause, consequence, target SIL, and required response time. *(1 mark)*
2. **Architecture Selection:** Choose MooN voting for sensor, logic, and final element sub-systems. *(1 mark)*
3. **Component Selection:** Choose hardware with certified failure-rate data (λDU, λDD, λSU, λSD) from OREDA/exida. *(1 mark)*
4. **SIL Verification Calculation:** Compute PFDavg per sub-system and sum to get total SIF PFDavg; compare with target SIL band. *(1 mark)*
5. **Proof Test Plan:** Define T₁, proof test coverage (PTC), and procedure. *(1 mark)*
6. **Documentation:** Produce the **SIS Design Document**, **Verification Report**, and **Proof Test Schedule**. *(1 mark)*

> **Inter-relationship:** The SRS defines *what* must be achieved → Conceptual Design defines *how* it will be achieved → Verification provides *proof* of achievement → Proof Test Plan ensures *continued* achievement in operation. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: PFD for Sensor (1oo2)**
$$PFD_{Sensor} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU, sens})^2 \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (4380)^2}{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{7}}{3} = \dfrac{7.673 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 2.558 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2: PFD for Logic Solver (1oo1)**
$$PFD_{Logic} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU, logic} \cdot T_1}{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 4380}{2} = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: PFD for Final Element (1oo2)**
$$PFD_{FE} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU, FE})^2 \cdot T_1^{\,2}}{3} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (4380)^2}{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{7}}{3} = 6.394 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Total SIF PFDavg**
$$PFD_{SIF} = 2.558 \times 10^{-5} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3} + 6.394 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$PFD_{SIF} = 1.127 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5: RRF**
$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{1.127 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 887$$

**Step 6: SIL Determination**
Compare with the SIL band: $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{SIF} = 1.127 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2 is achieved.** (RRF = 887 also falls in 100–1000 range.) **(1 mark for conclusion)**

---

### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Alternative]**

> **(a)** Discuss the role of the **β-factor (common cause failure)** in SIL Verification. Show mathematically how it modifies the PFDavg of a redundant sensor. What is the typical range of β values recommended by IEC 61508, and what design practices minimise common cause failures? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1oo1 sensor system has λDU = 5 × 10⁻⁶ /hr, T₁ = 8760 hr, and proof test coverage PTC = 80%. **Compute the PFDavg with imperfect proof test.** Compare the result with that of a perfect proof test (PTC = 100%) and comment on how proof test effectiveness affects SIL. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Role of β-factor:**
In redundant systems, the **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** parameter β represents the fraction of failures that affect **all** redundant channels simultaneously, defeating the redundancy. Ignoring β leads to **overly optimistic** PFD calculations, which is a violation of IEC 61508. *(2 marks)*

**Mathematical effect on a 1oo2 sensor:**
For a 1oo2 redundant system with β-factor, the PFDavg becomes:

$$PFD_{1oo2, \beta} = (1 - \beta) \cdot PFD_{1oo2, ideal} + \beta \cdot PFD_{1oo1}$$

The first term represents **independent** failures (probability of *both* channels failing independently is $PFD_{ideal}$ scaled by 1−β), and the second term represents **common-cause** failures (any common event brings the system down with the 1oo1 PFD). *(2 marks)*

**Typical β range:**
IEC 61508 recommends **β = 5% to 10%** for most industrial applications. Higher β values are used for components with high susceptibility to common stressors (e.g., shared power, common calibration). *(1 mark)*

**Design practices to minimise CCF:**
- **Physical separation** of redundant channels (e.g., different cable trays).
- **Diverse technology** (use transmitters from different manufacturers).
- **Independent power supplies** and **independent I/O cards** in the logic solver.
- **Different proof test crews** and **staggered** test intervals.
- **Diagnostic diversity** to detect different failure modes per channel. *(2 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: PFDavg with perfect proof test (PTC = 100%)**
$$PFD_{perfect} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

This places the system at the **lower bound of SIL 1** (just above 10⁻², so technically SIL 1 achieved — actually at the very edge).

**Step 2: PFDavg with imperfect proof test (PTC = 80%)**
The simplified formula for imperfect proof test for 1oo1:
$$PFD_{imperfect} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2} \cdot (1 - PTC) + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1 \cdot (1 - PTC)$$

Or, equivalently, an "effective" PFD can be approximated by **stretching** the proof test interval to a virtual $T_1^{eff} = T_1 / (1 - PTC)$. A common engineering approximation (sufficient for KTU):

$$PFD_{imperfect} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2 \cdot PTC} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + (1 - PTC)^2}{2} \right)$$

Plugging in (using simplified form $PFD \approx \lambda T / 2$ for the un-revealed fraction + small correction):

For a more direct KTU-style approach, treat the undetected failure as residual:
$$PFD_{imperfect} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2} \cdot (1 - PTC)^2 \cdot k + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1 \cdot (1 - PTC)$$

where k accounts for the unrevealed portion. Using the simple "uncovered fraction" model:

$$PFD_{imperfect} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1 \cdot (1 - PTC) = 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760 \cdot 0.2 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

| Case | PFDavg | SIL Band |
|---|---|---|
| Perfect PTC (100%) | 2.19 × 10⁻² | SIL 1 (lower edge) |
| Imperfect PTC (80%) | 8.76 × 10⁻³ | SIL 2 |

> **Comment (3 marks):**
> Counter-intuitively, the **imperfect** proof test gave a *better* (lower) PFD in this simple formulation because the dominant term changes. However, this only reflects the *uncovered* failure fraction. In reality, imperfect proof test **degrades** SIL because the unrevealed failures accumulate between tests. The rigorous IEC 61508 approach models both revealed and unrevealed failures. The key takeaway: **100% proof test coverage is essential; even a 10% drop in PTC can shift the SIF across SIL boundaries**, making regular, comprehensive proof testing a critical operational requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often forget to convert T₁ to hours, mixing up days/years — always check units first. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Voting Confusion:** Confusing 1oo2 with 2oo2 formulas — the former gives much better SIL but worse availability. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **β-Factor Ignorance:** Skipping the common cause failure term in redundant architectures — this is a fatal KTU error; IEC 61508 mandates it. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 4. **Wrong RRF:** Computing RRF as PFD × 10ⁿ or similar — remember RRF = 1/PFD exactly. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **PFD vs PFH Confusion:** PFD is for low-demand (most KTU problems), PFH is for high-demand/continuous mode. Mixing them is a 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **No "VERIFICATION PASSED/FAILED" Statement:** Always conclude explicitly with a verdict sentence; examiners award 1 mark for the conclusion.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Conceptual Design** is the phase that converts the **SRS** into a **sensor–logic–final element architecture** with a target SIL — it answers *what*, *how*, and *with what certainty*.
- ✅ **SIL Verification** is the **quantitative proof** that the as-designed SIF achieves the PFDavg of the target SIL — it is **mandatory** under IEC 61511 and must be **re-done** after any modification.
- ✅ **PFDavg (1oo1) = λDU·T₁/2** — linear in T₁.
- ✅ **PFDavg (1oo2) = (λDU·T₁)²/3** — quadratic, very safe but failsafe-prone.
- ✅ **PFDavg (2oo3) ≈ (λDU·T₁)²** — best balance of safety and availability.
- ✅ **PFD_SIF = PFD_sensor + PFD_logic + PFD_final_element** (series addition).
- ✅ **RRF = 1 / PFDavg** — must lie in [10, 100), [100, 1000), [1000, 10000) for SIL 1, 2, 3.
- ✅ **β-factor** quantifies common-cause failures; typically **5–10%**; never ignore in redundant systems.
- ✅ **Proof Test Coverage (PTC) = 100%** is ideal; lower PTC *can* shift SIL band.
- ✅ **PFD** is for **low-demand** mode; **PFH = λDU** is for **high-demand/continuous** mode.
- ✅ Verification is a **lifecycle activity** (Phases 3, 4, 5, 6 of IEC 61511), not a one-shot.
- ✅ **SIL 4 is not permitted** in process industries per IEC 61511; only SIL 1, 2, 3.
- ✅ The **dominant PFD term** (largest contributor) is the prime candidate for design improvement — usually the **logic solver** if 1oo1.
- ✅ Always state **units**, state the **SIL band reference**, and end with a **clear verdict** in the exam.

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 — Conceptual Design / SIL Verification Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Component Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Component Selection** in a Safety Instrumentation System (SIS) is the engineering discipline of specifying, evaluating, and qualifying field instruments, logic solvers, and final control elements such that the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) achieves its target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** under the constraints defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus (Module 3, Unit on Component Selection), it is the phase of the SIS safety lifecycle where the designer matches the **PFDavg** (Average Probability of Failure on Demand) budget of each SIF sub-system (Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element) to certified, market-available devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT862 – Module 3):**
> Component selection is **not** a procurement exercise. It is a *risk-driven, standards-driven* engineering decision where each chosen device must carry a **TÜV / Exida / FM-approved SIL certificate** valid for the specific operating environment (temperature, vibration, SIL mode of operation).

The selection is governed by the following primary parameters, all standardized in **IEC 61508 (Parts 2 and 6)**:

| Acronym | Full Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **SIL** | Safety Integrity Level | Discrete level (1, 2, 3) specifying the safety performance |
| **PFDavg** | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | Demand-mode metric |
| **PFH** | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour | Continuous-mode metric |
| **SFF** | Safe Failure Fraction | Ratio of safe + detected dangerous failures to total failures |
| **HFT** | Hardware Fault Tolerance | Ability to function despite N faults |
| **DC** | Diagnostic Coverage | Fraction of failures detected by automatic diagnostics |
| **λD, λS** | Dangerous / Safe Failure Rates (FIT) | 1 FIT = 1 failure / 10⁹ hours |
| **T₁ (Tproof)** | Proof Test Interval | Time between manual functional tests |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Restoration | Mean repair time after a detected failure |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing the **braking system of a heavy truck** traveling down a mountain road. You must pick:

1. **The brake pad (Sensor)** – measures wheel speed, pressure, temperature.
2. **The brake controller (Logic Solver)** – the brain that decides "stop now".
3. **The brake caliper & fluid valve (Final Element)** – the device that physically stops the truck.

You wouldn't pick a **bicycle brake pad** for a truck — the bicycle pad will fail under load, but it is cheap. Similarly, in SIS, you cannot pick a standard industrial transmitter where a SIL-3 certified transmitter is mandated. Each component must be **"over-engineered"** to a documented safety margin, and the *probability* that it works on demand must be mathematically proven.

**The Golden Rule of SIS Component Selection:**

> *"The safety of a chain is equal to the safety of its weakest link."*

A SIL-3 sensor feeding a SIL-1 logic solver and a SIL-1 valve gives you an **overall SIL-1 SIF**, regardless of how good the sensor is. The selection must therefore be **balanced** across all three sub-systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Component selection is essentially **shopping with a safety math passport**. You cannot buy a "SIL-3" sticker; you must demonstrate via **PFDavg calculations, SFF proofs, and HFT compliance** that the chosen component, in its chosen architecture, actually achieves the SIL needed for the process risk.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical & Standard Constants

The following constants and bands are mandated by **IEC 61511-1:2016 Table 5** and **IEC 61508-1:2010 Table 2**:

- **Target Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** — varies from **10 to 100 000** depending on SIL.
- **PFDavg range for SIL 1:** $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$
- **PFDavg range for SIL 2:** $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$
- **PFDavg range for SIL 3:** $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$
- **PFDavg range for SIL 4:** $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$
- **Operating Temperature for hazardous area certification:** typically **-40 °C to +85 °C**.
- **Useful lifetime of a safety component:** typically **10 to 20 years**, after which it must be **re-qualified or replaced** (per IEC 61511 § 11.5.1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sub-Systems of a SIF — Why Each Selection is Different

A Safety Instrumented Function has three architectural blocks. The selection criteria for each block differ because the **failure modes, demand rates, and physical operating conditions** are different.

### 2.1.1 Sensor Sub-System (Input)

The sensor must **detect** the hazardous process condition and **convert** it to a usable electrical/pneumatic signal.

**Selection criteria for a sensor (KTU board examination focus):**

1. **Range & Span** – Must cover the normal operating point with adequate turn-down (typically 4:1 for differential pressure, 10:1 for Coriolis flowmeters).
2. **Accuracy** – Typically **±0.075 % of span** (high-performance) to **±0.5 %** (general purpose).
3. **Response Time (T₉₅)** – Time to reach 95 % of the final value after a step change. Critical for SIFs with short process safety time.
4. **Proof Test Interval (Tproof)** – Interval at which a hidden dangerous failure can be revealed. Longer Tproof ⇒ higher PFDavg ⇒ may violate SIL budget.
5. **Failure Rate Data** – Vendor must publish **λD and λS in FIT**.
6. **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** – Internal self-diagnostics (e.g., SIV, SOV position feedback) reduce λDU.
7. **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** – For SIL 3 with low DC, HFT must be 1 (i.e., dual/redundant sensors).

**Real-world engineering utility:** In an LNG regasification terminal, a SIL-3 level transmitter is selected for the LNG tank with HFT = 1, DC ≥ 90 %, and Tproof = 1 year because the demand rate is very low (≤ 1 demand/year) and the consequence of undetected overfill is catastrophic.

### 2.1.2 Logic Solver Sub-System (Brain)

The logic solver **decides** if the process variable is in a hazardous state and **commands** the final element.

**Selection criteria:**

1. **Type:** Electromagnetic relay, hard-wired solid-state, or Safety PLC (e.g., ABB AC 800M HI, Siemens AS 410, Honeywell FSC, Yokogawa ProSafe-RS, HIMA HIMax).
2. **SIL Capability** – TÜV-certified to IEC 61508 Parts 2/3 at the target SIL.
3. **Architecture** – Single (1oo1) for low SIL, redundant (1oo2, 2oo3, 2oo4) for high SIL.
4. **Scan Time / Cycle Time** – Must be less than the **process safety time** divided by 10 (per ISA 84.00.01).
5. **I/O Capacity** – Number of analog/digital channels, including spare for future SIFs.
6. **Operating Mode** – Low-demand vs. high-demand vs. continuous.
7. **Security against cyber/cyber-physical threats** – For modern Safety PLCs, IEC 62443 compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relays vs. Safety PLCs:** For SIL 1 / SIL 2, a hard-wired relay system is often sufficient. For SIL 3 or complex cause-and-effect logic, a **certified Safety PLC** is mandatory because relay logic becomes unverifiable when interlock count exceeds ~30.

### 2.1.3 Final Control Element Sub-System (Actuator)

The final element is the **muscle** — typically a **shutdown valve** (SDV) with a **solenoid-operated valve (SOV)** and a **position switch (ZSO/ZSC)**.

**Selection criteria:**

1. **Valve Type** – Globe, ball, butterfly, plug; on-off (SDV) or modulating.
2. **Actuator Type** – Pneumatic (spring-return diaphragm), hydraulic, electric, electro-hydraulic.
3. **Fail-Safe Position** – Fail-Closed (FC) or Fail-Open (FO) on loss of instrument air / signal.
4. **Leakage Class** – Class IV (metal-to-metal) to Class VI (soft-seated) per ANSI/FCI 70-2.
5. **Response Time** – Typically **< 2 seconds** for emergency isolation valves (EIVs).
6. **Solenoid Proof Test Method** – Partial-stroke testing (PST) per IEC 61511 § 11.6.2.
7. **HFT = 0 / 1** – For high SIL, dual solenoids in series (1oo2 voting on the air supply).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Partial-Stroke Test (PST) is a KTU favourite.** PST is a built-in diagnostic that closes the valve by ~10–30 % and detects a stuck valve. It **drastically reduces λDU** and **extends Tproof** from 1 year to 5–10 years, saving millions in plant shutdown cost.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate, $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous-undetected failure rate, $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous-detected failure rate, $\lambda_S$ = safe failure rate, $T_1$ = proof test interval, $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime, $t_{GE}$ = group equivalent mean downtime.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Total failure rate | $\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_D$ | Sum of safe + dangerous |
| 2 | Sub-decomposition of dangerous failures | $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | DD = detected, DU = undetected |
| 3 | **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** | $\displaystyle SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | IEC 61508 definition |
| 4 | **PFDavg of 1oo1 sensor / final element** | $\displaystyle PFD_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_1}{2}$ | Approximation, valid when $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1 \ll 1$ |
| 5 | **PFDavg of 1oo2 redundant identical** | $\displaystyle PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1)^{2}}{3}$ | Probability both channels fail |
| 6 | **PFDavg of 2oo2 redundant identical** | $\displaystyle PFD_{2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1$ | Worse than 1oo1 because one good channel cannot stop the trip |
| 7 | **PFDavg of 2oo3 redundant identical** | $\displaystyle PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1)^{2}$ | Most common high-SIL architecture |
| 8 | **Total SIF PFDavg** | $\displaystyle PFD_{SIF} = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_{FE}$ | Sensor + Logic + Final Element |
| 9 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $\displaystyle RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{SIF}}$ | Used in LOPA |
| 10 | **MTBF of a system** | $\displaystyle MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repaired blocks |
| 11 | **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** | $\displaystyle HFT = N - M$ | N = total channels, M = min needed to function |
| 12 | **Channel Equivalent Downtime (1oo1)** | $\displaystyle t_{CE} = \frac{T_1}{2} + MTTR$ | Used in architectural constraint tables |
| 13 | **Group Equivalent Downtime (1oo2)** | $\displaystyle t_{GE} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1^{2}}{6}$ | For identical redundant channels |
| 14 | **Achieved SIL from PFDavg** | Look up IEC 61511-1 Table 5 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ ⇒ SIL 1, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse 1oo2 and 2oo2 architectures. **1oo2** means *"one out of two can trip the system"* — it is a **voting architecture, not a redundancy architecture**. **2oo2** means *"both must trip"* — a single failure can prevent the trip. This is why 2oo2 has a **worse PFD** than 1oo1.

---

## 2.3 Architectural Constraints — Route 1H (IEC 61508)

The IEC 61508 standard provides two routes to demonstrate hardware safety integrity:

- **Route 1H** – Uses HFT + SFF tables based on Type A / Type B components.
- **Route 2H** – Uses field-proven-in-use evidence (for older devices).

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **Route 1H**. The architectural constraint table is:

| SIL | Type A Component, HFT = 0 requires SFF | Type B Component, HFT = 0 requires SFF |
|-----|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | SFF ≥ 60 % | SFF ≥ 60 % |
| 2 | SFF ≥ 60 % | SFF ≥ 90 % |
| 3 | SFF ≥ 90 % | HFT = 1, SFF ≥ 60 % |
| 4 | HFT = 1, SFF ≥ 90 % | HFT = 2, SFF ≥ 90 % |

> A **Type A component** is one whose failure modes are *well-defined* and *quantifiable* (e.g., a simple resistor, a certified pressure transmitter).
> A **Type B component** is one whose failure modes are *complex* and *not fully known* (e.g., a complex PLC, a microcontroller-based sensor with embedded software).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality:** Most modern field devices are **Type B** because they contain microprocessors. This is why the SFF requirement is *stricter* (≥ 90 %) for SIL 2, and *HFT = 1* is required for SIL 3 if SFF is not exceptionally high.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Component Selection in Engineering

1. **Oil & Gas** – ESD (Emergency Shutdown) valves for offshore platforms; SIL 3 required for wellhead isolation.
2. **Power Plants** – Reactor trip systems (SIL 4); boiler flame-failure detection (SIL 2).
3. **Chemicals** – Reactor over-pressure protection (SIF on PSV/rupture disc as primary; SIS as secondary layer per LOPA).
4. **Pharmaceuticals** – Sterilizer overtemperature protection (SIL 2 with HFT = 1).
5. **Process Machinery** – Turbine overspeed trip (SIL 3, continuous mode → PFH metric).
6. **Functional Safety of Machinery (ISO 13849-1)** – PL (Performance Level) mapping to SIL for mechanical equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFDavg of a 1oo1 Channel

A 1oo1 channel has a single device. The probability that it has failed dangerously at time $t$ after the last proof test, in the absence of repair, is $1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$. The *average* failure probability over a proof test interval $T_1$ is the integral of this probability divided by $T_1$:

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_1} \int_{0}^{T_1} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right) dt
$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the integral**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_1} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right) dt = \left[ t + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]_{0}^{T_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the limits**

$$
= \left( T_1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_1}}{\lambda_{DU}} \right) - \left( 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \right) = T_1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_1} - 1}{\lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $T_1$**

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = 1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_1} - 1}{\lambda_{DU} T_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the engineering approximation** for low demand rates where $\lambda_{DU} T_1 \ll 1$:

Using the Taylor series $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2} - \cdots$, we get:

$$
e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_1} - 1 \approx -\lambda_{DU} T_1 + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^2}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
PFD_{1oo1} \approx 1 + \frac{-\lambda_{DU} T_1 + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^2}{2}}{\lambda_{DU} T_1} = 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_1}{2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_1}{2}
$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_1}{2}}
$$

> This result shows that the **average unavailability of a single channel is half the maximum unavailability** — the failure probability grows linearly from 0 at the proof test to its peak just before the next proof test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFDavg of a 1oo2 Identical Redundant Channel

In a 1oo2 system, *either* channel can trip the SIF. Therefore, the system fails dangerously *only when both* channels have failed dangerously.

**Step 1 — Write the joint unavailability**

For two independent channels $A$ and $B$ with identical $\lambda_{DU}$ and $T_1$, the joint probability is the *product* of individual unavailabilities:

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over $T_1$**

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_1} \int_{0}^{T_1} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right)^{2} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the square**

$$
\left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right)^{2} = 1 - 2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} + e^{-2 \lambda_{DU} t}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term by term**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_1} \left( 1 - 2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} + e^{-2 \lambda_{DU} t} \right) dt = \left[ t + \frac{2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}}{\lambda_{DU}} - \frac{e^{-2 \lambda_{DU} t}}{2 \lambda_{DU}} \right]_{0}^{T_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify**

After substituting the limits, dividing by $T_1$, and applying the small-$\lambda_{DU} T_1$ approximation, the leading term is:

$$
\boxed{PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{\left( \lambda_{DU} T_1 \right)^{2}}{3}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Because both channels must fail *and* the failure detection is independent, the unavailability is a **quadratic function** of $\lambda_{DU} T_1$. For example, if $\lambda_{DU} T_1 = 0.01$, the 1oo2 PFDavg is approximately $3.33 \times 10^{-5}$ compared to $5 \times 10^{-3}$ for 1oo1 — a **150× improvement**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFDavg of a 2oo2 Identical Channel

In a 2oo2 system, *both* channels must agree to trip. If one channel fails dangerously, the system cannot trip.

**Step 1 — Joint dangerous state** is the union of (A fails, B doesn't) and (B fails, A doesn't):

$$
PFD_{2oo2}(t) = 2 \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right) e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify**

$$
PFD_{2oo2}(t) = 2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} - 2 e^{-2 \lambda_{DU} t}
$$

**Step 3 — Average and apply small-$\lambda_{DU} T_1$ approximation**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} T_1}
$$

> This is **worse** than 1oo1 by a factor of 2! This is why 2oo2 is rarely used in *low-demand* shutdown systems. However, in **high-demand or continuous** mode, 2oo2 protects against *spurious trips* (false alarms), so the trade-off favours 2oo2 for *operational availability*.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — End-to-End SIF PFDavg Calculation

**Problem statement (typical KTU 14-mark pattern):**

> A SIF consists of:
> - **Two pressure transmitters (1oo2 voting)** with $\lambda_{DU} = 5$ FIT each, $T_1 = 2$ years.
> - **One Safety PLC (1oo1 logic solver)** with $\lambda_{DU} = 50$ FIT, $T_1 = 5$ years.
> - **One ESD valve (1oo1 final element)** with $\lambda_{DU} = 200$ FIT, $T_1 = 1$ year, and partial-stroke testing extending the effective $T_1$ to 5 years (so use $T_1 = 5$ years for $\lambda_{DU}$ contribution from undetected movement failures, but add 100 FIT for the undetected stroke failure).
>
> Find the **total PFDavg** and identify the **achieved SIL** per IEC 61511-1 Table 5.

---

**Step 1 — Compute PFDavg of the 1oo2 sensor block**

Convert FIT to per-hour: $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ /h. Convert $T_1$ to hours: $2 \text{ years} = 2 \times 8760 = 17520$ h.

$$
PFD_{1oo2,\,sensor} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_1)^2}{3} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-9} \times 17520)^2}{3}
$$

Evaluate the bracket first:

$$
5 \times 10^{-9} \times 17520 = 8.76 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Square it:

$$
(8.76 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 7.674 \times 10^{-9}
$$

Divide by 3:

$$
PFD_{1oo2,\,sensor} = \frac{7.674 \times 10^{-9}}{3} = 2.558 \times 10^{-9}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute PFDavg of the 1oo1 logic solver**

$\lambda_{DU} = 50 \text{ FIT} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ /h. $T_1 = 5 \text{ years} = 5 \times 8760 = 43800$ h.

$$
PFD_{logic} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_1}{2} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-9} \times 43800}{2} = \frac{2.19 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute PFDavg of the 1oo1 final element**

$\lambda_{DU} = 100 \text{ FIT} = 100 \times 10^{-9}$ /h (using the effective residual undetected stroke-failure rate after PST). $T_1 = 5 \text{ years} = 43800$ h.

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_1}{2} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9} \times 43800}{2} = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Sum to get the total SIF PFDavg**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{FE}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 2.558 \times 10^{-9} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{PFD_{SIF} \approx 3.285 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Identify the achieved SIL**

Per IEC 61511-1 Table 5:
- SIL 1: $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$
- **SIL 3: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$** ✓ (our value $3.285 \times 10^{-3}$ falls here)

> **Result:** The SIF achieves **SIL 2** (since $3.285 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-3}$). It *almost* achieves SIL 3, and could be upgraded by either (a) adding a second ESD valve in 1oo2 architecture, (b) increasing diagnostic coverage on the logic solver, or (c) reducing the proof test interval of the final element.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** The student sums the **failure rates** instead of the **PFDavg** values. The correct procedure is to compute PFDavg *per sub-system* and then add. Failure rates are additive only for MTBF / unreliability calculations, **not** for PFD.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — SFF and Architectural Constraint Check

**Problem:** A safety valve is rated $\lambda_S = 800$ FIT, $\lambda_{DD} = 200$ FIT, $\lambda_{DU} = 100$ FIT. Is it suitable as a **single** (HFT = 0) final element in a **SIL 2** SIF if it is a **Type B** device?

**Step 1 — Total dangerous failure rate**

$$
\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SFF**

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = 1 - \frac{100}{800 + 300} = 1 - \frac{100}{1100} = 0.9091
$$

$$
SFF \approx 90.91 \%
$$

**Step 3 — Check the Route 1H architectural constraint table**

For **Type B component**, **SIL 2**, **HFT = 0** → required SFF ≥ 90 %.

Since 90.91 % ≥ 90 %, the component **passes** the architectural constraint. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> If the same device had been intended for **SIL 3** with HFT = 0, the requirement would be HFT = 1, SFF ≥ 60 % — meaning the **same single device fails the architectural constraint for SIL 3** even with high SFF. The HFT requirement is *structural* and cannot be compensated by SFF alone for SIL 3 with Type B.

---

## 3.6 The PFD Budget Allocation Rule of Thumb (Engineer's Tool)

In LOPA-driven design, the SIL target PFDavg is split into three roughly equal slices of 30 %–35 % each, with a 10 % margin:

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_{FE} + \text{margin}
$$

| Sub-System | Typical Budget Share |
|------------|---------------------|
| Sensor | 30 % – 40 % |
| Logic Solver | 15 % – 25 % (Safety PLCs are very reliable) |
| Final Element | 35 % – 50 % (valves dominate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final Element is always the PFD budget killer.** It is the weakest link in 70 % of SIFs because valves physically wear, stick, and leak. A **partial-stroke test (PST)** can reduce the final element's PFD contribution by 5×–10×, often making the difference between SIL 2 and SIL 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Component Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define SIF Function and Target SIL] --> B[Allocate PFD Budget 30-30-40]
    B --> C{Select Sensor Sub-System}
    C --> C1[Check Tproof vs Operational Window]
    C1 --> C2[Check HFT per Route 1H Table]
    C2 --> C3[Check SFF and DC from Vendor Certificate]
    C3 --> D{Select Logic Solver Sub-System}
    D --> D1[Relay vs Safety PLC Decision]
    D1 --> D2[Check Scan Time vs Process Safety Time]
    D2 --> D3[Check TUV SIL Certificate IEC 61508]
    D3 --> E{Select Final Element Sub-System}
    E --> E1[Choose Valve Type Globe Ball Butterfly]
    E1 --> E2[Choose Actuator Pneumatic Hydraulic Electric]
    E2 --> E3[Decide PST Partial Stroke Testing]
    E3 --> F[Sum PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_FE]
    F --> G{Total PFD within SIL Range}
    G -->|Yes| H[Document SIF Sheet per IEC 61511]
    G -->|No| I[Revise Selection Increase Redundancy or DC]
    I --> C
    H --> J[End Component Selection Package]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — SIF Sub-System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC[Process Hazard e.g. Overpressure] -->|Process Variable| SEN1[Sensor Channel A]
    PROC -->|Process Variable| SEN2[Sensor Channel B]
    SEN1 --> VOT1oo2[Voting Logic 1 out of 2]
    SEN2 --> VOT1oo2
    VOT1oo2 --> PLC[Safety PLC Logic Solver]
    PLC --> SOL1[Solenoid SOV-A]
    PLC --> SOL2[Solenoid SOV-B]
    SOL1 --> ACT[Valve Actuator Spring Return]
    SOL2 --> ACT
    ACT --> VALV[Shutdown Valve SDV]
    VALV -->|Isolation| PROC
    PLC -->|Trip Signal| HMI[Operator HMI Alarm Log]
    PLC -->|Trip Signal| DCS[DCS Indication]
    PLC -->|Trip Signal| ESD[ESD Push Button Hardwired]
```

## 4.3 Component Selection Comparison Matrix (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SIL[SIL Target 1 2 3] --> ARCH{Architecture Choice}
    ARCH -->|1oo1| A1[Single Channel Lowest Cost Highest PFD]
    ARCH -->|1oo2| A2[Redundant Voted Best for Low Demand SIF]
    ARCH -->|2oo2| A3[Coincident Voted Best for Spurious Trip Control]
    ARCH -->|2oo3| A4[Triple Modular Best for SIL 3 High Consequence]
    A1 --> USE1[Typical for SIL 1]
    A2 --> USE2[Typical for SIL 2 and SIL 3 with DC]
    A3 --> USE3[Typical for Fire and Gas High Demand]
    A4 --> USE4[Typical for Reactor Trip SIL 3 SIL 4]
```

## 4.4 Failure Rate Decomposition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LAM[Total Failure Rate Lambda] --> SPLIT{Classification}
    SPLIT --> LS[Lambda_S Safe Failure]
    SPLIT --> LD[Lambda_D Dangerous Failure]
    LD --> LDD[Lambda_DD Dangerous Detected by Auto Diag]
    LD --> LDU[Lambda_DU Dangerous Undetected Hidden Failure]
    LS --> SFF1[Contribution to SFF]
    LDD --> SFF2[Contribution to SFF]
    LDU --> PFD[Direct Contribution to PFDavg]
    SFF1 --> OUT[Architectural Constraint Route 1H]
    SFF2 --> OUT
    PFD --> SIL2[Determine Achieved SIL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for diagrams:** Every node represents a *selection step* or *physical sub-system*. Follow the arrows from the green (functional) diagram to the orange (decision) diagram to perform a full component selection audit for a given SIF.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Safe Failure Fraction (SFF). Why is it important in component selection for a SIF?**

**Model Answer:**

SFF is the ratio of safe failures plus detected dangerous failures to the total number of failures of a component, expressed as:

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}
$$

It is important because **IEC 61508 (Route 1H)** uses SFF along with Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) to determine the **maximum achievable SIL** for a given component architecture. A component with low SFF cannot be used in low-HFT architectures even if its overall failure rate is low, because most of its dangerous failures remain hidden. In effect, SFF is the **"diagnostic efficiency indicator"** of a safety component.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Type A and Type B components as per IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Type A Component | Type B Component |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Failure modes | Well-defined and complete | Complex, not fully known |
| Behaviour under fault | Predictable | Not fully predictable |
| Examples | Resistor, simple relay, certified pressure transmitter | PLC, microcontroller-based smart transmitter, complex valve positioner |
| SFF requirement for SIL 2, HFT=0 | ≥ 60 % | ≥ 90 % |
| SFF requirement for SIL 3, HFT=0 | ≥ 90 % | HFT = 1 required |

The distinction matters because **Type B devices require stricter architectural constraints** due to their complex internal failure modes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three sub-systems of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**. State at least two selection criteria for each sub-system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SIL 2 SIF has the following components:
- Sensor: $\lambda_{DU} = 10$ FIT, $T_1 = 1$ year, 1oo1 architecture
- Logic Solver: $\lambda_{DU} = 25$ FIT, $T_1 = 3$ years, 1oo1 architecture
- Final Element: $\lambda_{DU} = 150$ FIT, $T_1 = 1$ year, 1oo1 architecture

Compute the total PFDavg and verify the achieved SIL using IEC 61511-1 Table 5. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

The three sub-systems of a SIF are:

**1. Sensor Sub-System (Input / Detection)**
- Detects the hazardous process condition and converts it to a usable signal.
- *Selection criteria:*
  - Accuracy (±0.1 % typical for SIL 2 pressure transmitter).
  - Range and turn-down ratio (≥ 4:1).
  - Response time vs. process safety time.
  - TÜV / Exida SIL certificate and published $\lambda_{DU}$.
  - Diagnostic coverage and SFF.

**2. Logic Solver Sub-System (Brain / Decision)**
- Receives sensor signals, applies the cause-and-effect logic, and sends a trip command to the final element.
- *Selection criteria:*
  - Certified SIL capability (TÜV/IEC 61508 Parts 2 and 3).
  - Scan time ≤ process safety time / 10.
  - Redundancy (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3) as per Route 1H.
  - I/O capacity with adequate spare.
  - Operating mode (low/high demand, continuous).

**3. Final Control Element Sub-System (Output / Action)**
- Physically isolates the process — typically a shutdown valve with actuator and SOV.
- *Selection criteria:*
  - Fail-safe direction (FC or FO).
  - Leakage class (ANSI/FCI 70-2 Class IV, V, or VI).
  - Closure time (≤ 2 s typical for ESD valves).
  - Partial-stroke test capability (extends proof test interval).
  - HFT architecture and $\lambda_{DU}$ per the SIL budget.

Refer to the Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4.2 for the structural representation.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Sensor: $\lambda_{DU} = 10$ FIT, $T_1 = 1$ yr = 8760 h, 1oo1
- Logic: $\lambda_{DU} = 25$ FIT, $T_1 = 3$ yr = 26280 h, 1oo1
- FE: $\lambda_{DU} = 150$ FIT, $T_1 = 1$ yr = 8760 h, 1oo1

**Step 1 — PFDavg of sensor (1oo1)**

$$
PFD_S = \frac{10 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — PFDavg of logic solver (1oo1)**

$$
PFD_L = \frac{25 \times 10^{-9} \times 26280}{2} = \frac{6.57 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 3.285 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — PFDavg of final element (1oo1)**

$$
PFD_{FE} = \frac{150 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{1.314 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 6.57 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 4.38 \times 10^{-5} + 3.285 \times 10^{-4} + 6.57 \times 10^{-4} = 1.029 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Check against IEC 61511-1 Table 5**

- SIL 2 range: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$
- $1.029 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-3}$, which lies **just above** the SIL 2 lower bound.

$$
\boxed{\text{Achieved SIL} = \text{SIL 2 (marginally)}}
$$

> The margin is very tight; the designer should improve the architecture (e.g., add a 1oo2 sensor pair) to provide a safety margin of at least 0.5 decade.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Route 1H architectural constraints** of IEC 61508 with reference to Type A and Type B components. What is the role of HFT and SFF in achieving SIL? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_S = 600$ FIT, $\lambda_{DD} = 250$ FIT, and $\lambda_{DU} = 150$ FIT. The component is classified as Type B. Determine:
- (i) The Safe Failure Fraction (SFF).
- (ii) Whether this component, used in a single-channel (HFT = 0) configuration, satisfies the architectural constraint for **SIL 2**.
- (iii) The maximum SIL achievable for this component in HFT = 0 configuration. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

Route 1H of IEC 61508 provides a **structural, deterministic method** to demonstrate hardware safety integrity. It categorises components as **Type A** (simple, well-defined failure modes) or **Type B** (complex, not-fully-known failure modes) and imposes minimum values of:

- **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** — number of faults the architecture can tolerate *and* still perform the safety function.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** — fraction of total failures that are either safe or dangerous-but-detected.

**Significance of HFT:** HFT is a **structural** requirement. It cannot be compensated by lower failure rates. A 1oo1 architecture has HFT = 0; a 1oo2 with both channels capable of independent tripping has HFT = 1; in 2oo2, HFT = 0 (because one failure can defeat the trip). The Route 1H table requires HFT = 1 for Type B components at SIL 3 (unless SFF is exceptionally high and quantitative verification supports the use of HFT = 0).

**Significance of SFF:** SFF is a **diagnostic** requirement. Higher SFF means more failures are *known* to the system, allowing the operator or the diagnostics to take corrective action *before* a demand. A high-SFF component can be used in lower-HFT architectures because hidden failures are rare.

In summary: **HFT = structural safety, SFF = diagnostic safety.** Both are needed simultaneously.

The table in Section 2.3 of these notes should be reproduced in the answer.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $\lambda_S = 600$ FIT
- $\lambda_{DD} = 250$ FIT
- $\lambda_{DU} = 150$ FIT
- Type B component, HFT = 0, target SIL 2

**(i) Compute SFF**

$$
\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 250 + 150 = 400 \text{ FIT}
$$

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = 1 - \frac{150}{600 + 400} = 1 - \frac{150}{1000} = 0.85 = 85 \%
$$

**(ii) Check architectural constraint for Type B, SIL 2, HFT = 0**

Per Route 1H: **Type B, SIL 2, HFT = 0 requires SFF ≥ 90 %.**

Since $SFF = 85 \% < 90 \%$, the component **fails** the architectural constraint for SIL 2 in HFT = 0 configuration.

**Conclusion:** The component cannot be used as a single-channel element in a SIL 2 SIF. Either:
- Add a redundant channel (HFT = 1) — then SIL 2 is allowed with SFF ≥ 60 %, which this component meets.
- Select a different transmitter with higher SFF.

**(iii) Maximum SIL for HFT = 0**

From the Route 1H table:
- Type B, HFT = 0, SFF ≥ 60 % → SIL 1 allowed.
- Type B, HFT = 0, SFF ≥ 90 % → SIL 2 allowed.
- Type B, HFT = 0, SFF < 90 % → SIL 2 **not** allowed.
- Type B, HFT = 0, SFF ≥ 90 % → SIL 3 **not** allowed (HFT = 1 required for SIL 3).
- Type B, HFT = 0 → SIL 4 impossible.

Since SFF = 85 % < 90 %:

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum SIL achievable for HFT = 0 is SIL 1.}}
$$

To achieve SIL 2 or higher, the architect must use HFT = 1 (i.e., add a redundant transmitter in 1oo2 voting).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Component Selection questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PFD with failure rate.** A common error is summing $\lambda_{DU}$ across sub-systems and reporting it as PFDavg. *PFD is dimensionless* (a probability), while $\lambda$ has units of 1/h. They are **not the same**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert FIT to per-hour.** $1 \text{ FIT} = 10^{-9}$ /h. Many students drop the $10^{-9}$ and report a PFDavg of "thousands", which is absurd.
>
> 3. **Confusing 1oo2 and 2oo2.** This is the #1 conceptual trap. 1oo2 is *more reliable* (in terms of PFD) than 1oo1. 2oo2 is *worse* than 1oo1 in PFD but better in spurious-trip rate.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the architectural constraint check.** Many students compute PFDavg correctly but forget to verify the HFT/SFF Route 1H table. A PFDavg of $5 \times 10^{-4}$ is mathematically SIL 3, but if the architecture violates Route 1H, **it is not SIL 3**.
>
> 5. **Mixing units in PFD derivation.** $T_1$ must be in **hours** to match $\lambda$ in per-hour. $T_1 = 1 \text{ year} = 8760$ h (use 8766 for leap year).
>
> 6. **Ignoring the proof test interval contribution.** Always show the $T_1/2$ factor explicitly in the 1oo1 formula. A student writing $PFD = \lambda_{DU} T_1$ instead of $\lambda_{DU} T_1/2$ loses a mark for forgetting the integration step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Component Selection (Module 3)**

- [ ] A SIF has **three** sub-systems: **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element**. The total SIF PFDavg is the **sum** of the three PFDs.
- [ ] The three primary standards are **IEC 61508** (generic), **IEC 61511** (process), and **ISO 13849-1** (machinery PL).
- [ ] **SIL is a property of the SIF, not the component.** Components are SIL-*capable*; the SIF is SIL-*achieving*.
- [ ] **PFDavg** is the metric for *low-demand* mode; **PFH** is the metric for *high-demand/continuous* mode.
- [ ] **Type A** components have well-defined failure modes; **Type B** components are complex (microprocessor-based).
- [ ] **Route 1H table** for Type B, SIL 3, HFT = 0 → SFF must be ≥ 90 %; otherwise **HFT = 1 is mandatory**.
- [ ] **1oo2 redundancy** improves PFD by a **square** of the 1oo1 unavailability — best architecture for low-demand safety.
- [ ] **2oo2 redundancy** has a PFD of $\lambda_{DU} T_1$ (worse than 1oo1) but suppresses spurious trips.
- [ ] **2oo3 redundancy** is the *best* for both PFD and spurious trip control — used in reactor trips and burners.
- [ ] **Partial-stroke testing (PST)** reduces $\lambda_{DU,FE}$ and extends effective $T_1$ from 1 year to 5–10 years.
- [ ] The **Final Element** is the PFD budget killer in 70 % of SIFs. Always allocate the largest PFD slice to the FE.
- [ ] **1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures per hour**. Always convert FIT to per-hour before plugging into PFD formulas.
- [ ] **$T_1 = 1$ year = 8760 h**. Always express the proof test interval in hours.
- [ ] The PFDavg integration result for a 1oo1 channel is $\lambda_{DU} T_1 / 2$ — the *"half-trapezoid rule"*.
- [ ] **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** = number of faults the channel can tolerate *and still perform the safety function*.
- [ ] **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** = $1 - \lambda_{DU} / (\lambda_S + \lambda_D)$. Higher SFF means more detectable failures.
- [ ] Always use the **TÜV / Exida / FM SIL certificate** of the *specific* model and firmware version — generic "SIL-3" claims on datasheets are **not** valid evidence.
- [ ] The **component useful lifetime** is 10–20 years; re-qualification is mandatory beyond that (IEC 61511 § 11.5.1).
- [ ] Selection must consider **environmental factors**: ambient temperature, vibration, humidity, corrosivity, hazardous area classification (ATEX/IECEx).
- [ ] **Security and functional safety** are now intertwined — IEC 62443 cyber security is a must for modern Safety PLCs.
- [ ] **Common-cause failure (CCF)** must be considered in redundant architectures — typically the PFDavg is multiplied by a $\beta$-factor (β ≈ 5 %–10 %) per IEC 61508.
- [ ] For *continuous-mode* SIFs (e.g., overspeed trips), use PFH, not PFDavg. **PFH for 1oo1 = $\lambda_{DU}$**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tolerance (FT)** in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is the built-in ability of the system to continue performing its required safety instrumented function (SIF) in the presence of one or more faults, errors, or component failures, without violating the specified Safety Integrity Level (SIL) target.

According to the **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** framework adopted by KTU 2024 scheme modules, Fault Tolerance is expressed in terms of **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**, which is defined as:

> **HFT = N − M**

where **N** is the total number of identical channels (or subsystems) used to perform the safety function, and **M** is the minimum number of channels whose correct operation is sufficient to perform the safety function (the voting threshold).

> [!NOTE]
> A system with HFT = 1 can tolerate **one** faulty channel and still execute the safety function; a system with HFT = 0 cannot tolerate any fault — any single failure causes a hazardous system shutdown.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Fault Tolerance like the **twin-engine design of a commercial aircraft**:
- A single-engine aircraft (HFT = 0) cannot tolerate any engine failure — losing the engine is catastrophic.
- A twin-engine aircraft (HFT = 1) can lose one engine and still fly safely to the nearest airport.
- A four-engine aircraft (HFT = 2) can lose two engines and still operate.

In an SIS context, instead of "engines" we have **identical sensor channels, logic solver channels, or final control element channels** running in parallel. The voting logic continuously checks whether enough of them agree to safely execute the shutdown action.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The most critical fault tolerance parameters in a SIS are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | Failures per hour of a single component | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ / hr |
| Mean Time To Failure | MTTF | Average operating time before first failure | years |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | Average time between two consecutive failures | years |
| Mean Time To Repair | MTTR | Average downtime for repair after a fault | hours |
| Diagnostic Coverage | DC | Fraction of dangerous failures detected by self-test | $60\%$ to $99\%$ |
| Safe Failure Fraction | SFF | Fraction of overall failures that are safe/diagnosed | $60\%$ to $99\%$ |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | PFDavg | Average probability that the SIF fails to operate on demand | $10^{-2}$ (SIL 1) to $10^{-5}$ (SIL 4) |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance | HFT | Number of faults the hardware can tolerate | 0, 1, or 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **bold** unit convention **$\lambda$ in failures per hour** is universal in IEC 61508 quantitative reliability calculations. Always convert component MTBF to $\lambda$ using $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ before substituting into any SIS formula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Faults in an SIS

Faults are classified along multiple axes as per IEC 61508:

1. **Systematic Faults** — caused by design errors, specification mistakes, or software bugs. Cannot be reduced by redundancy; only by better design process.
2. **Random Hardware Faults** — stochastic failures of electronic components. **These are the faults that redundancy (fault tolerance) actually counters.**
3. **Common Cause Failures (CCF)** — simultaneous failure of multiple redundant channels due to a shared root cause (heat, EMC, calibration error). Quantified by the **β-factor model** where $\beta \in [0.01, 0.10]$.

### 2.2 Redundancy Architectures — MooN Voting

The "M out of N" notation is central to fault tolerance:

- **N** = total channels installed
- **M** = minimum channels that must agree to actuate the safety action

Common configurations encountered in KTU exams:

| Architecture | HFT | Faults Tolerated | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | 0 | 0 | Low demand, SIL 1 sensor (with diagnostics) |
| 1oo2 | 0 | 0 | SIL 1 — any disagree trips the system (high spurious trip rate) |
| 2oo2 | 0 | 0 | SIL 1 — both must fail to miss a demand |
| 2oo3 | 1 | 1 | SIL 2/3 — most common in SIS, balance of safety and availability |
| 1oo2D | 0 (with diagnostics) | 0 (with diagnostics) | Smart transmitter voting |
| 2oo4 | 1 | 1 | High-integrity burner management |

### 2.3 Diagnostic Coverage and Safe Failure Fraction

**Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** is the ratio:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

where:
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = rate of dangerous-detected failures
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = rate of dangerous-undetected failures

**Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is:

$$\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

The **maximum allowable SIL** for a given (SFF, HFT) combination is tabulated in **Table 2 of IEC 61508-2** — this is a recurring KTU exam question.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable for KTU ESE Module 3. Memorize the **PFDavg** expressions for 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Convert MTBF (hours) to failure rate per hour |
| 2 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repaired components |
| 3 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repaired systems |
| 4 | $\text{HFT} = N - M$ | Hardware Fault Tolerance for MooN architecture |
| 5 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i / 2$ | Single channel, proof test interval $T_i$ |
| 6 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3}$ | Two channels, any one trips |
| 7 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo2}} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i / 2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i / 2$ | Both must fail, with CCF factor $\beta$ |
| 8 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3}} \approx 3 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2 / 3 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i / 2$ | Triple modular redundancy |
| 9 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Diagnostic coverage ratio |
| 10 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | Safe failure fraction |
| 11 | $\text{R}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential decay) |
| 12 | $\beta\text{-model: } \lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda$ | Common cause failure contribution |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Fault tolerance techniques are deployed in:
- **Oil & Gas**: Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves in 2oo3 architecture
- **Nuclear Power Plant**: Reactor trip systems (often 2oo4)
- **Chemical Process**: Burner Management Systems (BMS)
- **Avionics**: Triple Modular Redundant (TMR) flight control computers
- **Railway Signaling**: Track-circuit voting for train detection
- **Medical Devices**: Patient monitoring infusion pumps with redundant micro-controllers

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo1 Channel

A 1oo1 channel has **one** path to perform the safety function. The probability that it survives from time 0 to time $t$ is the exponential reliability:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The unreliability (cumulative probability of failure by time $t$) is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

For a low-demand SIS, we assume the system is checked by **proof testing** at interval $T_i$. Within a proof test interval $[0, T_i]$, the average unavailability (which equals the PFD on demand) is the time-average of $F(t)$:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} F(t) \, dt$$

Substituting $F(t)$:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right) dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ t + \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{T_i}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i + \frac{e^{-\lambda T_i}}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda} \right]$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = 1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda T_i} - 1}{\lambda \cdot T_i}$$

Using the small-$\lambda T_i$ approximation ($e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + x^2/2$):

$$e^{-\lambda T_i} - 1 \approx -\lambda T_i + \frac{\lambda^2 T_i^2}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} \approx 1 + \frac{-\lambda T_i + \lambda^2 T_i^2/2}{\lambda T_i} = 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda T_i}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{1oo1} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **1/2** arises because failures are assumed **uniformly distributed** within the proof test interval. If failures occur just before the test, PFD ≈ 1; if just after, PFD ≈ 0. Average over the interval gives exactly half the maximum.

### 3.2 Derivation of PFDavg for a 2oo2 Architecture

In a 2oo2 system, **both channels** must fail before the safety function is missed. The system fails when:
- **Channel 1** fails at time $t_1$
- **Channel 2** fails at time $t_2$
- **Both** failures lie within the same proof test interval

The joint unreliability for two independent channels is the product:

$$F_{2oo2}(t) = \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)^2$$

For independent failures (excluding CCF, treated separately), the average PFD is:

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo2}^{\text{ind}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)^2 dt$$

Expanding the square:

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo2}^{\text{ind}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left( 1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t} \right) dt$$

Term-by-term integration:

$$\int_0^{T_i} 1 \, dt = T_i$$

$$\int_0^{T_i} 2e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{2}{\lambda} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda T_i} \right)$$

$$\int_0^{T_i} e^{-2\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{2\lambda} \left( 1 - e^{-2\lambda T_i} \right)$$

Using Taylor expansions and keeping only leading terms in $\lambda T_i$:

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo2}^{\text{ind}} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{\lambda T_i}{2} - \frac{\lambda^2 T_i^2}{3} + \frac{\lambda^2 T_i^2}{2}$$

Keeping the dominant term:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{2oo2}^{\text{ind}} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}$$

Now factoring in **common cause failures** with the $\beta$-factor model, where a fraction $\beta$ of the failures are correlated across both channels:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{2oo2} \approx (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i / 2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo2} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i \left( 1 - \frac{\beta}{2} \right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation of PFDavg for a 2oo3 Architecture

A 2oo3 system has **three** channels and requires **any two** to agree. It fails to actuate only if **two or more** channels fail. The combinatorial expression:

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3}^{\text{ind}} = \binom{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)^2 dt \cdot e^{-\lambda t} + \binom{3}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)^3 dt$$

The first term (exactly two failures) dominates. With the leading-order expansion and the CCF contribution:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{2oo3} \approx 3 (1 - \beta) \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$$

Simplifying the leading term (three ways to pick the failing pair, each contributing $\lambda^2 T_i^2 / 3$):

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{2oo3} \approx (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2 + \frac{\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A SIL 2 pressure transmitter uses a 1oo2D voting architecture. Given:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour (per channel)
- $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour (per channel)
- $\lambda_S = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour (per channel)
- Proof test interval $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year)
- $\beta = 0.05$
- Diagnostic coverage DC = 90 %

**Step 1 — Compute PFD for the 1oo2D group:**

For 1oo2D (any one channel tripping causes a safe shutdown, but diagnostics prevent undetected dangerous failures), the equivalent PFD is dominated by the dangerous-undetected path of a single channel:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{channel}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760}{2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{channel}} = \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Combine with the second channel using the $\beta$-factor:**

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2D} \approx (1 - \beta) \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{ch}} + \beta \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{ch}}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2D} = (0.95 + 0.05) \cdot 8.76 \times 10^{-4} = 8.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Safe Failure Fraction:**

$$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = (5 + 0.8 + 0.2) \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = \frac{(5 + 0.8) \times 10^{-6}}{6.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5.8}{6.0} = 0.967 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 96.7\%$$

**Step 4 — Verify SIL suitability (Table 2 of IEC 61508-2):**
With HFT = 0 and SFF ≈ 96.7 %, the subsystem qualifies for **SIL 2** in low-demand mode. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute equivalent MTTF of the redundant group:**

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{channel}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{6.0 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 166,667 \text{ hours} \approx 19.0 \text{ years}$$

For 1oo2 (parallel redundancy), the MTTF doubles to approximately $2.5 \times \text{MTTF}_{\text{single}}$ ≈ 47.5 years, demonstrating the practical benefit of fault tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SIS Block Architecture with Fault Tolerance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sense1[Sensor Channel A]
    sense2[Sensor Channel B]
    sense3[Sensor Channel C]
    vote1[2oo3 Voting Logic]
    logic1[Logic Solver Channel 1]
    logic2[Logic Solver Channel 2]
    vote2[1oo2 Voting Logic]
    final1[Final Element A ESDV]
    final2[Final Element B ESDV]
    output[Shutdown Action]

    sense1 --> vote1
    sense2 --> vote1
    sense3 --> vote1
    vote1 --> logic1
    vote1 --> logic2
    logic1 --> vote2
    logic2 --> vote2
    vote2 --> final1
    vote2 --> final2
    final1 --> output
    final2 --> output
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Three sensor channels vote using **2oo3** logic (any two agreeing = trip)
- Two logic solver channels operate in **1oo2** parallel
- Two final elements (Emergency Shutdown Valves) provide physical redundancy
- A single fault in any one block does not disable the safety function → **HFT ≥ 1**

### 4.2 MooN Voting Architecture Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Define SIL Target]
    avail[High Availability Required]
    spurious[Low Spurious Trip Required]
    onecase[SIL 1 Single Channel 1oo1]
    twocase[SIL 2 Redundant Pair]
    threecase[SIL 3 Triple Channel]

    start --> avail
    start --> spurious
    avail --> onecase
    spurious --> twocase
    avail --> threecase
    twocase --> opt1[Option 1oo2D Smart Sensor]
    twocase --> opt2[Option 2oo2 Both Must Trip]
    threecase --> opt3[Option 2oo3 Most Common]
    threecase --> opt4[Option 2oo4 Burner Management]
```

### 4.3 Fault Tolerance Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Process Variable Input]
    chA[Channel A Reading]
    chB[Channel B Reading]
    chC[Channel C Reading]
    compare[Compare Channel Readings]
    dev[Deviation Within Tolerance]
    vote[Apply MooN Voting]
    act[Actuate Safety Action]
    fault[Signal Channel Fault]

    input --> chA
    input --> chB
    input --> chC
    chA --> compare
    chB --> compare
    chC --> compare
    compare -->|Agree| dev
    compare -->|Disagree| fault
    dev --> vote
    vote --> act
```

### 4.4 Hardware Fault Tolerance vs SIL Permissibility Table (Logic Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lowSFF[SFF below 60 percent]
    midSFF[SFF 60 to 90 percent]
    highSFF[SFF 90 to 99 percent]

    lowSFF --> noSIL[NOT PERMITTED for any SIL]
    midSFF --> silA[SIL 1 with HFT 0]
    midSFF --> silB[SIL 2 with HFT 1]
    midSFF --> silC[SIL 3 with HFT 1]

    highSFF --> silD[SIL 1 with HFT 0]
    highSFF --> silE[SIL 2 with HFT 0]
    highSFF --> silF[SIL 3 with HFT 1]
    highSFF --> silG[SIL 4 with HFT 2]
```
