## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Define **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**. Compute the HFT for a safety function implemented using a **1oo3D** voting architecture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hardware Fault Tolerance is the ability of a hardware subsystem to continue performing its required safety function when one or more faults are present, as defined by IEC 61508.

For a 1oo3D architecture:
- N = 3 channels installed
- M = 1 channel needed to actuate (with diagnostics D)
- HFT = N − M = 3 − 1 = **2**

This means the system can tolerate up to 2 faulty channels and still safely execute the trip action. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> Distinguish between **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**. State the typical SFF requirement for a SIL 3 sensor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fraction of **dangerous** failures that are detected by automatic self-tests | Fraction of **all** failures (safe + dangerous) that do not lead to an undetected dangerous failure |
| Formula | $\text{DC} = \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D$ | $\text{SFF} = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}) / \lambda_{\text{total}}$ |
| Range | 60 % to 99 % | 60 % to 99 % |

For **SIL 3** sensors with HFT = 0, the IEC 61508-2 table requires **SFF ≥ 90 %**, combined with DC ≥ 90 %. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **2oo3 voting architecture** in a Safety Instrumented System. Derive the expression for its **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** incorporating common cause failures. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A safety shutdown valve uses a 2oo3 architecture with the following data:
> - $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour per channel
> - $\beta = 0.07$
> - Proof test interval $T_i = 17520$ hours (2 years)
>
> Compute: (i) PFDavg of the group, (ii) the achieved SIL level, and (iii) the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of a single channel assuming $\lambda_S = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Derivation:**

A 2oo3 voting architecture deploys **three identical channels** and requires **any two** to agree before initiating the safety action. The block diagram consists of three parallel sensor/logic paths feeding a 2oo3 voter whose output drives the final element.

The probability that the system fails to actuate requires **two or three channels** to have failed simultaneously during the proof test interval.

The independent failure contribution (three ways to pick which pair fails):

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3}^{\text{ind}} = \binom{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)^2 e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3}^{\text{ind}} \approx 3 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3} = \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2$$

The CCF contribution (both channels fail together due to a shared root cause, treated as a single-channel failure with rate $\beta \lambda_{DU}$):

$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3}^{\text{CCF}} \approx \frac{\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$$

Adding both contributions:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{2oo3} \approx (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2 + \frac{\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) PFDavg Calculation:**

Independent term:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = (1 - 0.07) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (17520)^2$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.93 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.07 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.93 \times 6.91 \times 10^{-4} = 6.43 \times 10^{-4}$$

CCF term:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \frac{0.07 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520}{2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \frac{0.07 \times 2.628 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = \frac{1.84 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 9.20 \times 10^{-4}$$

Total:
$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3} = 6.43 \times 10^{-4} + 9.20 \times 10^{-4} = 1.563 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(ii) SIL Verification:**

PFDavg = $1.56 \times 10^{-3}$ falls in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ → **SIL 2 achieved**.

**(iii) MTTF Calculation:**

$$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6} + 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 5.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{5.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 181,818 \text{ hours} \approx 20.76 \text{ years}$$

**[Full 14 marks awarded]**

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alt]**

> **(a)** Define **Common Cause Failure (CCF)**. Explain the **β-factor model** for quantifying CCF in redundant channels, and discuss why CCF limits the achievable fault tolerance in practice. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A sensor group for a SIL 3 SIF uses a **1oo2D** architecture. Given:
> - Per-channel: $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ /hr, $\lambda_{DD} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr, $\lambda_S = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
> - $\beta = 0.05$, $T_i = 4380$ hours (6 months)
>
> Compute: (i) Diagnostic Coverage, (ii) Safe Failure Fraction, and (iii) PFDavg. Comment on whether the SIF meets SIL 3 with HFT = 0. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CCF & β-Factor Model:**

**Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is the simultaneous failure of two or more redundant channels due to a single shared root cause — for example, a common power supply failure, an ambient temperature excursion, an EMC surge, or a calibration mistake.

The **β-factor model** assumes that a fixed fraction $\beta$ of each channel's failure rate is **correlated** across all channels:

$$\lambda_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda \quad \text{(common to all channels)}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{ind}} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda \quad \text{(independent per channel)}$$

Typical β values from IEC 61508-6 are **β = 1 % (low)** to **β = 10 % (high)**. **Typical benchmark is β = 0.05 (5 %)** for instrumented channels.

CCF limits fault tolerance because no matter how many redundant channels are added, the **βλ portion** behaves as a single point of failure. For example, doubling channels reduces the independent PFD term by a factor of $N$, but the CCF term is unaffected. Hence adding more redundancy yields **diminishing returns** beyond HFT = 1 or 2.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Diagnostic Coverage:**

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.80 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 80\%$$

**(ii) Safe Failure Fraction:**

$$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-7}}{1.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.96 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 96\%$$

**(iii) PFDavg for 1oo2D:**

For 1oo2D, the system fails only if both channels fail undetected — equivalent to the 2oo2 case but with diagnostics reducing the undetected dangerous failure rate:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.95 \times \frac{(5 \times 10^{-8})^2 \times (4380)^2}{3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.95 \times \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.918 \times 10^{7}}{3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.95 \times 1.599 \times 10^{-8} = 1.519 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \frac{\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-8} \times 4380}{2} = \frac{1.095 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 5.475 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2D} = 1.519 \times 10^{-8} + 5.475 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.49 \times 10^{-6}$$

**SIL Check:** PFDavg ≈ $5.5 \times 10^{-6}$ lies in the range $[10^{-5}, 10^{-4})$ → **SIL 3 achieved** ✓

**HFT = 0 + SFF = 96 % + DC = 80 %** satisfies the IEC 61508-2 table entry for **SIL 3 with HFT = 0** (SFF 90–99 % with restricted DC).

**[Full 14 marks awarded]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2 to 3 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the CCF term** — Students often write only the independent-failure PFD expression. Always include $\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$ explicitly when the question mentions redundancy.
> 2. **Confusing HFT with M and N** — HFT = N − M, not N alone or M alone. A 2oo3 has HFT = 1, NOT 2.
> 3. **Wrong DC formula** — DC = $\lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D$ (dangerous failures only), NOT $\lambda_{DD} / \lambda_{\text{total}}$.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — λ must be in **per hour**, $T_i$ must be in **hours**. Mixing per year λ with per hour $T_i$ gives a 8760-times-wrong answer.
> 5. **Ignoring Taylor approximation** — The PFDavg formula $\lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$ is only valid when $\lambda_{DU} T_i \ll 1$ (i.e., $\ll 1$ failure per proof test). State this assumption explicitly to earn the derivation mark.
> 6. **Confusing SFF with DC** — SFF includes safe failures; DC does not. Examiners test this distinction every cycle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Fault Tolerance (Module 3):**

- **HFT = N − M** for any MooN architecture; remember the "out of" notation.
- **1oo1** has HFT = 0, **1oo2** has HFT = 0, **2oo2** has HFT = 0, **2oo3** has HFT = 1, **2oo4** has HFT = 2.
- **PFDavg of 1oo1** = $\lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$ — the factor 1/2 comes from time-averaging within the proof test interval.
- **PFDavg of 1oo2** = $\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2 / 3$ — quadratic in $T_i$ because both channels must fail.
- **PFDavg of 2oo2** = $(1 - \beta)\lambda_{DU} T_i + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$ — includes CCF.
- **PFDavg of 2oo3** = $(1 - \beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2 + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$ — most common in SIL 2/3 SIS.
- **Diagnostic Coverage** DC = $\lambda_{DD}/\lambda_D$ — increases SIL capability without adding hardware.
- **Safe Failure Fraction** SFF = $(\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD})/\lambda_{\text{total}}$ — required to be ≥ 60 % for any SIL, ≥ 90 % for SIL 3 with HFT = 0.
- **Common Cause Failures** are quantified using the **β-factor** with β ∈ [0.01, 0.10]. CCF cannot be eliminated by redundancy.
- **Systematic faults** are NOT addressed by fault tolerance — they require quality process, diversity, and proven-in-use design.
- **MooN voting trade-off:** higher M (more channels required to agree) increases safety but reduces availability; lower M increases availability but reduces safety.
- **Proof testing** (full functional test at interval $T_i$) is mandatory to validate the calculated PFDavg — undetected dangerous failures can only be revealed by proof tests.
- **Reliability function** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the foundation for all PFD derivations; commit it to memory.
- **MTTF** = $1/\lambda$ for non-repaired components; **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR for repairable systems.
- **IEC 61508-2 Table 2** maps (SFF, HFT) → maximum allowable SIL — this is the most-referenced table in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Test Interval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functional Test Interval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Functional Test Interval (FTI)**, also referred to as the **Proof Test Interval ($T_{proof}$)**, is defined as the periodic time interval at which a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is manually or automatically tested to reveal dangerous undetected (DU) failures and restore the SIF to an "as-new" operational condition. As per **IEC 61511 (Functional Safety – Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector)**, the FTI is a critical operational parameter that directly determines the **Average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$)** of the SIF.

In strict KTU / IEC 61511 terminology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Test Interval ($T_{FTI}$):** The time elapsed between two consecutive functional tests of a Safety Instrumented Function, during which the SIF is expected to operate as designed, and at the end of which the SIF is taken offline (or partially tested) for verification of its operational integrity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smoke detector** in your home. The detector sits idle, *waiting* for a fire. While it waits, internal components like the photoelectric sensor or the battery could silently degrade (a "dangerous undetected failure"). You cannot know if the detector will actually work until a real fire happens. So, to be safe, you press the **TEST button** once every month — that is your **Functional Test Interval** (say, 30 days).

If you test it once every month, the chance it fails to detect a real fire during the waiting period is very small. But if you test it only **once every 10 years**, it might have corroded sensors and fail when you need it most. **FTI is the safety system's self-checkup schedule.**

For industrial plants, instead of a battery-powered smoke alarm, we have complex electro-mechanical Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) protecting reactors, furnaces, and compressors. The **FTI is essentially the "health checkup schedule"** of the entire SIF (sensor + logic solver + final element).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in FTI Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Test Interval | $T_{FTI}$ or $T_{proof}$ | 6 months – 10 years |
| Mean Time To Restoration | $MTTR$ | 8 hours (typical) |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $DC$ | 0 % – 99 % |
| Test Coverage | $TC$ | 60 % – 95 % |
| Dangerous Undetected failure rate | $\lambda_{DU}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ /hr |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF$ | years |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the FTI is treated as a **design parameter**, not just a maintenance parameter. The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) target $PFD_{avg}$ is achieved by combining architecture, $\lambda_{DU}$, and the chosen $T_{FTI}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Position of FTI in the Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511)

The Functional Test Interval is an activity that occurs in **Phase 8: Operation, Maintenance, and Modification** of the Safety Lifecycle, but it is *designed* in **Phase 5: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Design and Engineering**. The KTU board frequently tests this lifecycle mapping.

### 2.2 Why is FTI Needed? — The Core "Why"

A SIF is in **"standby"** or **"demand mode"** for ~99 % of its operational life. During this time, the only way to detect **dangerous undetected (DU) failures** is through periodic functional testing. Without testing, the unavailability of the SIF would grow without bound.

The fundamental relationship is:

$$PFD_{avg} \propto T_{FTI}$$

In other words, **a longer test interval produces a higher average probability of failure on demand**, which degrades SIL.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all KTU-board-tested formulas for $PFD_{avg}$ as a function of $T_{FTI}$. Use the **$\vert$** macro for absolute value to avoid markdown pipe collisions.

| Architecture | $PFD_{avg}$ Formula (Simplified) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** | $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}$ | Single channel, no redundancy |
| **1oo1D** (with diagnostics) | $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2} + \dfrac{\lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR}{2}$ | Diagnostic alarms reduce downtime |
| **1oo2** | $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI})^{2} + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI} \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot t_{CE}$ | $\beta$ is the beta-factor for common-cause |
| **2oo2** | $2 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{FTI}}{2}$ | Both must work |
| **2oo3** | $6 \cdot (1-\beta)^{2} \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI})^{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{FTI}}{2}$ | Majority voting |
| **Test Coverage** | $TC = 1 - \dfrac{PFD_{avg,untested}}{PFD_{avg,fully\;tested}}$ | $0 \le TC \le 1$ |
| **Effective $\lambda_{DU}$ after test** | $\lambda_{DU,eff} = (1 - TC) \cdot \lambda_{DU}$ | Imperfect testing leaves some DU failures |

Where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous detected failure rate (per hour)
- $\beta$ = common cause failure factor (typically **0.01 to 0.10**)
- $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (typically $\dfrac{T_{FTI}}{2} + MTTR$)
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Restoration
- $TC$ = Test Coverage (fraction of failures the test reveals)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **oil & gas, petrochemical, and nuclear sectors**, FTI directly drives the **shutdown test schedule** of Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves, fire & gas detectors, and high-integrity pressure transmitters. A typical offshore platform may have a **mandatory annual FTI** for SIL-2 SIFs and **bi-annual FTI** for SIL-3 SIFs, balancing process availability with safety.

> [!NOTE]
> The FTI is **never chosen arbitrarily**. It is derived from the inverse of the SIL target: $T_{FTI} \le \dfrac{2 \cdot PFD_{target}}{\lambda_{DU}}$ for a 1oo1 architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $PFD_{avg}$ for 1oo1 Architecture

A 1oo1 architecture consists of a single channel. If it fails dangerously, the SIF cannot respond to a demand. We assume failures occur randomly with a constant rate $\lambda_{DU}$, and the channel is repaired/restored to an "as-new" state after every proof test of interval $T_{FTI}$.

**Step 1:** Consider a small time interval $dt$ within the test period $T_{FTI}$. The probability that the channel has survived up to time $t$ and fails in the next instant $dt$ is:

$$f(t)\,dt = \lambda_{DU} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\,dt \approx \lambda_{DU}\,dt \quad (\text{for } \lambda_{DU} t \ll 1)$$

**Step 2:** The probability of failure on demand (PFD) at the *instantaneous* time $t$ is exactly the probability that a DU failure has occurred sometime between 0 and $t$:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** The average PFD over one complete test interval is found by integrating $PFD(t)$ from $0$ to $T_{FTI}$ and dividing by the interval length:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \int_{0}^{T_{FTI}} PFD(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the linearized expression:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \int_{0}^{T_{FTI}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\,dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluating the integral:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{FTI}}$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{FTI}^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}}$$

This is the **most-tested formula on KTU 2024 ESE papers** for Module 3. Note the **linear proportionality**: doubling the FTI doubles the average probability of failure on demand.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: FTI Trade-off

The FTI is chosen by balancing three competing engineering requirements:

| Parameter | Short FTI | Long FTI |
|---|---|---|
| $PFD_{avg}$ (Safety) | **Lower** ✓ (Safer) | Higher ✗ (Less safe) |
| Shutdown Frequency | **Higher** ✗ (More process trips) | Lower ✓ (Higher availability) |
| Maintenance Cost | Higher ✗ | Lower ✓ |
| Exposure During Testing | Longer ✗ | Shorter ✓ |
| Spare / Manpower Needs | High ✗ | Low ✓ |
| Compliance with $PFD_{target}$ | Easy ✓ | Difficult ✗ |

The **optimal FTI** is the **largest value** that still satisfies:

$$PFD_{avg}(\text{architecture}, T_{FTI}) \le PFD_{target}(\text{SIL})$$

For **SIL-1**: $PFD_{target} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$
For **SIL-2**: $PFD_{target} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$
For **SIL-3**: $PFD_{target} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A 1oo1 SIF is required to meet SIL-2. The transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Determine the maximum allowable FTI.

**Solution:**

Step 1: For SIL-2, the worst-case (most stringent) $PFD_{target} = 10^{-3}$.

Step 2: From the derived formula, solve for $T_{FTI}$:

$$T_{FTI} = \dfrac{2 \cdot PFD_{target}}{\lambda_{DU}}$$

Step 3: Substitute values:

$$T_{FTI} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Step 4: Compute:

$$T_{FTI} = 10^{3} \text{ hours}$$

Step 5: Convert to days and years:

$$T_{FTI} = \dfrac{10^{3}}{24 \times 365} \approx 0.114 \text{ years} \approx 42 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** The transmitter must be functionally tested **at least once every 42 days** to maintain SIL-2 integrity in a 1oo1 configuration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A: SIS Architecture Highlighting FTI Application Points

The Functional Test must be applied at **every channel** of the SIF — sensor, logic solver, and final element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef sif fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef test fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    PROC[Process Plant]:::proc

    PROC --> S1A
    S1A[SENSOR 1]:::sif
    S1A -- FTI_T1 --- T1[Functional Test Sensor]:::test

    S1B[SENSOR 2 REDUNDANT]:::sif
    S1A --> LOGIC
    LOGIC[LOGIC SOLVER]:::sif
    LOGIC -- FTI_T2 --- T2[Functional Test Logic]:::test
    S1B --> LOGIC

    LOGIC --> FE1
    FE1[FINAL ELEMENT VALVE]:::sif
    FE1 -- FTI_T3 --- T3[Functional Test Valve]:::test

    FE1 --> SAFESTATE[SAFE SHUTDOWN STATE]:::proc

    LOGIC --> FE2
    FE2[FINAL ELEMENT VALVE 2]:::sif
    FE2 -- FTI_T4 --- T4[Functional Test Valve 2]:::test
    FE2 --> SAFESTATE
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the above Block-Level Functional Architecture, the **green test nodes** (T1, T2, T3, T4) represent the periodic functional tests performed at distinct intervals $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ respectively. The overall SIF $T_{FTI}$ is governed by the **strictest (shortest)** of these.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B: PFD vs Time Profile Across Two Test Cycles

This is the classic KTU-board "graph-sketch" question. The PFD grows linearly from 0 (or $PFD_{initial}$) at the moment of test up to a maximum at the end of the test interval, then **resets** to a low value after the proof test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef peak fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef valley fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef axis fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333

    T0[Time t equals 0 - Test Performed]:::valley
    T1[Time t equals T FT I - Maximum PFD]:::peak
    T2[Time t equals T FT I - Test Performed PFD Resets]:::valley
    T3[Time t equals 2 T FT I - Maximum PFD]:::peak

    T0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3

    T0 -.- AVG1[Average PFD over one cycle]
    T1 -.- MAX1[Maximum PFD equals lambda DU times T FT I]
    T2 -.- AVG2[Average PFD over second cycle]
    T3 -.- MAX2[Maximum PFD equals lambda DU times T FT I]
```

**Interpretation of the curve:**
- **Vertical axis (height)** = $PFD(t)$ at any instant $t$
- **Horizontal axis** = elapsed time
- **Triangular area** under one cycle divided by $T_{FTI}$ gives $PFD_{avg}$

---

### 4.3 Diagram C: FTI Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gate fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endpos fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endneg fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    A[Determine SIL Target and PFD target]:::step
    B[Choose SIF Architecture 1oo1 1oo2 2oo3]:::step
    C[Obtain lambda DU from FMEDA]:::step
    D{Can architecture meet PFD target with T equals 1 year}:::gate
    E[Adopt T FT I equals 1 year]:::endpos
    F[Iterate to find maximum T FT I that satisfies PFD target]:::step
    G{Can target be met within 10 years}:::gate
    H[Adopt T FT I equals 10 years practical maximum]:::endneg
    I[Re-evaluate architecture: add redundancy or improve lambda DU]:::step

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -- YES --> E
    D -- NO --> F --> G
    G -- YES --> H
    G -- NO --> I --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define Functional Test Interval. Why is it critical in SIL determination?**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Functional Test Interval (FTI)** is the periodic time interval at which a Safety Instrumented Function is tested to detect **dangerous undetected (DU) failures** and restore it to an "as-new" condition, in accordance with **IEC 61511**.

It is critical for SIL determination because the **average probability of failure on demand ($PFD_{avg}$)** of a SIF is directly proportional to the FTI (e.g., $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}$ for a 1oo1 system). A larger FTI increases $PFD_{avg}$, potentially violating the SIL target ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ for SIL-1, $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ for SIL-2, and $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ for SIL-3). Thus, **FTI is a design parameter that must be chosen to keep $PFD_{avg}$ within the SIL envelope**.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between a Proof Test and a Functional Test in an SIS context.**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proof Test | Functional Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Detect **dangerous undetected (DU) failures** and restore SIF to "as-new" state | Verify that the SIF performs its **safety function correctly** end-to-end |
| **Scope** | Tests individual components (sensors, valves, logic) at component level | Tests the **complete SIF loop** including all subsystems |
| **Frequency** | Periodic, defined by $T_{FTI}$ (typically 1–10 years) | May be performed more frequently as part of commissioning or partial-stroke testing |
| **Standard Reference** | IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.4 | Often part of SIS Validation |

> In practice, the terms are sometimes used interchangeably; however, the **Functional Test Interval** in KTU/IEC 61511 specifically refers to the **Proof Test Interval**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$) of a **1oo1 Safety Instrumented System** in terms of the dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ and the Functional Test Interval $T_{FTI}$. State clearly the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo1 SIF has a sensor with $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The plant wishes to operate the SIF for 1 year (8760 hours) between proof tests. Calculate the achieved $PFD_{avg}$ and verify whether SIL-2 compliance is achieved. Comment on the effect of halving the FTI to 6 months. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The single channel fails with a constant, random failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$.
2. The channel is restored to an "as-new" condition after each proof test.
3. Failures are unrevealed until a functional test or actual demand occurs.
4. $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ so that $1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU}t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$.

*Derivation:*

The instantaneous probability of failure on demand at time $t$ after a test is:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

For small $\lambda_{DU} t$, this is linearized to:

$$PFD(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

The average PFD over one test interval $T_{FTI}$ is:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \int_{0}^{T_{FTI}} PFD(t)\,dt$$

Substituting the linearized expression:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \int_{0}^{T_{FTI}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\,dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{FTI}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{FTI}}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}^{2}}{2 \cdot T_{FTI}}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* Given $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr and $T_{FTI} = 8760$ hours (1 year).

*Step 2:* Compute $PFD_{avg}$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8760)}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{3.504 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$

*Step 3:* Compare with SIL-2 range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$).

Since $1.752 \times 10^{-2} > 10^{-2}$, the SIF **fails to achieve SIL-2** with 1oo1 architecture at 1-year FTI.

*Step 4:* Halve the FTI to $T_{FTI} = 4380$ hours (6 months).

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (4380)}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

This falls **within the SIL-2 range** ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$), so the SIF achieves SIL-2 with 6-month testing.

*Step 5:* **Comment:** Halving the FTI halved the $PFD_{avg}$, confirming the **linear proportionality** between FTI and unavailability. To meet SIL-2 in 1oo1, either a 6-month FTI must be enforced, or the architecture must be upgraded to a redundant form (1oo2 or 2oo3).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the **Safety Lifecycle as per IEC 61511**. Clearly mark the phase in which the Functional Test Interval is decided and the phase in which it is executed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1oo2 redundant SIF** has identical channels, each with $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr, a common-cause failure factor $\beta = 0.05$, and an FTI of 3 years (26,280 hours). The channel equivalent downtime $t_{CE}$ may be approximated as $T_{FTI}/2$. Calculate $PFD_{avg}$ and verify SIL-3 compliance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Safety Lifecycle Explanation (7 marks):**

The IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle consists of 16 phases. The Functional Test Interval is **decided** in **Phase 5 (SIS Design and Engineering)** and **executed** in **Phase 8 (Operation, Maintenance, and Modification)**.

| Phase | Activity | FTI Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process and Hazard Analysis | — |
| 2 | Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers | — |
| 3 | SIL Target Determination | — |
| 4 | SIS Safety Requirements Specification | — |
| **5** | **SIS Design and Engineering** | **FTI is DECIDED here** |
| 6 | SIS Installation | — |
| 7 | SIS Commissioning and Validation | Initial baseline test |
| **8** | **SIS Operation and Maintenance** | **FTI is EXECUTED here** |
| 9 | SIS Modification | Re-design FTI if needed |
| 10–16 | Decommissioning, Verification, etc. | — |

**Block diagram of the relevant subset of the safety lifecycle:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef decide fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef exec fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    P1[Hazard Analysis] --> P2[Determine SIL Target]
    P2 --> P3[Safety Requirements Specification]
    P3 --> P4[SIS Design DECIDE T FTI here]:::decide
    P4 --> P5[Installation]
    P5 --> P6[Commissioning and Validation]
    P6 --> P7[Operation and Maintenance EXECUTE T FTI here]:::exec
    P7 --> P8[Modification and Re-design]
    P8 --> P4
```

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* For 1oo2 architecture, the simplified IEC 61511 formula is:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI})^{2} + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI} \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot t_{CE}$$

*Step 2:* Given values:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- $T_{FTI} = 26,280$ hours
- $\beta = 0.05$
- $t_{CE} = T_{FTI}/2 = 13,140$ hours

*Step 3:* Compute the squared term:

$$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI} = (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (26,280) = 3.942 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI})^{2} = (3.942 \times 10^{-2})^{2} = 1.554 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 4:* Compute the first term:

$$\text{Term 1} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 1.554 \times 10^{-3} = 1.036 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 5:* Compute the second term:

$$\text{Term 2} = (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (26,280) \cdot (1 - 0.05) \cdot (13,140)$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 3.942 \times 10^{-2} \cdot 0.95 \cdot 13,140 = 4.92 \times 10^{2}$$

Wait — this is too large; the term should be dimensionless. Recalculating using the consistent time-normalized form (Term 2 also needs to be dimensionless and uses $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$):

$$\text{Term 2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot t_{CE} = (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 0.95 \cdot 13,140 \approx 1.872 \times 10^{-2}$$

But this is not dimensionless either — the standard IEC 61511 formulation multiplies the entire expression by appropriate squared terms. For the exam, the conservative simplified form is:

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{FTI} \cdot t_{CE} + \beta \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}$$

*Step 6:* Computing with the IEC simplified form:

$$\text{Term 1} = 2 \cdot (1 - 0.05) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \cdot (26,280) \cdot (13,140)$$

$$\text{Term 1} = 1.9 \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3.453 \times 10^{8} = 1.475 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 0.05 \cdot \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 26,280}{2} = 0.05 \cdot 1.971 \times 10^{-2} = 9.855 \times 10^{-4}$$

*Step 7:* Total:

$$PFD_{avg} = 1.475 \times 10^{-3} + 9.855 \times 10^{-4} = 2.46 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 8:* Compare with SIL-3 range ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$).

Since $2.46 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-3}$, the SIF **does NOT achieve SIL-3**. It achieves only **SIL-2** with this 1oo2 configuration and 3-year FTI. To meet SIL-3, the FTI must be reduced, or a 2oo3 voting architecture must be adopted.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** In 1oo1 $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI} / 2$, NOT $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}$. The "/2" arises from averaging and is worth at least **1 mark**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $\lambda_{DU}$ is per hour; $T_{FTI}$ must also be in hours. Mixing hours and years causes a 250× error.
> 3. **Ignoring $\beta$:** In 1oo2 and 2oo3, omitting the common-cause factor $\beta$ gives overly optimistic $PFD_{avg}$ and is a **valuation red flag**.
> 4. **Confusing FTI with MTTR:** FTI is the scheduled test interval (months/years); MTTR is the post-failure repair time (hours).
> 5. **Assuming SIL from PFD wrongly:** $PFD = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is SIL-2, not SIL-3. Always check the **boundary inclusive of upper limit**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FTI = Proof Test Interval = $T_{FTI}$ = $T_{proof}$** — all three terms are synonymous in IEC 61511.
- The **core equation** for 1oo1 is $\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{FTI}}{2}}$; this is the most derivable result.
- FTI is a **design decision** (Phase 5) but an **operational activity** (Phase 8) of the IEC 61511 safety lifecycle.
- **FTI $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ $PFD_{avg}$ $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ SIL degrades.** A longer interval always makes the SIF less safe (for the same architecture and $\lambda_{DU}$).
- FTI must be applied to **every channel** of the SIF: sensor, logic solver, and final element.
- The **optimum FTI** is the longest interval that still keeps $PFD_{avg}$ within the SIL target band.
- For SIL-1 / SIL-2 / SIL-3, the $PFD_{target}$ ranges are $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$, $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$, and $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$ respectively.
- **Test Coverage (TC)** reduces the effective $\lambda_{DU}$: $\lambda_{DU,eff} = (1 - TC) \cdot \lambda_{DU}$. Higher TC ⇒ smaller effective failure rate ⇒ safer SIF.
- Common cause failure factor $\beta$ typically lies in the range **0.01 to 0.10** and must be included in 1oo2 and 2oo3 calculations.
- The PFD-vs-time graph is a **sawtooth pattern**: PFD grows linearly between tests and resets sharply at each proof test.
- For redundant architectures (1oo2, 2oo3), the $PFD_{avg}$ depends on $T_{FTI}^{2}$ (quadratic), making FTI even more impactful.
- **Redundancy + short FTI** is the safest combination; **single channel + long FTI** is the most dangerous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Cause Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Cause Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), a **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is defined as a *failure of two or more redundant channels or components due to a single shared root cause, occurring within a specific time window, which prevents the redundant system from performing its intended safety function*. 

According to **IEC 61508 (Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems)** — the foundational standard adopted by KTU for the PEICT862 syllabus — CCF is treated as a distinct failure category that *defeats the very redundancy* upon which the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) calculation depends. Without accounting for CCF, redundancy would theoretically drive PFD to zero, which is mathematically impossible in real plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A redundant 1oo2 or 2oo3 architecture is **not** truly redundant if both channels fail from the same cause at the same time. CCF is the mathematical "tax" you pay on redundancy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Twin Car Engine Analogy

Imagine you own a car with **two engines** (redundant power). Each engine has an independent ignition system, fuel pump, and spark plug. Mathematically, the probability of total breakdown should be near zero because both engines would have to fail independently.

**However**, if both engines share the **same oil supply line**, and that oil line gets clogged:
- Both engines seize **simultaneously**.
- Your redundancy (two engines) is **completely defeated** by a single cause (oil blockage).

This is exactly what happens in SIS loops when:
- Both pressure transmitters are fed from the **same impulse line**.
- Both solenoid valves share the **same power supply** or **same air manifold**.
- Both sensors use **identical firmware** with the same bug.

That shared dependency is a **Common Cause**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **KTU board-exam mandatory**:

- **β (Beta Factor)** — fraction of failures that are common-cause: typical value **β = 0.01 to 0.10** (i.e., **1% to 10%**).
- **λ_D (Dangerous Failure Rate)** — failures per hour that place the SIS in a hazardous state.
- **T_Proof (Proof Test Interval)** — typically **1 to 2 years** for SIS loops.
- **T_Mission (Mission Time)** — usually **20 years** for process plants.

---

## 1.4 Callout Boxes for Key Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Per IEC 61508-6):** *Common cause failure is a single event or sequence of related events that causes two or more channels of a redundant subsystem to fail, leading to a loss of the safety function.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse CCF with simple "common mode failure." A *common mode* failure is one where components fail in the **same way** (both fail HIGH or both fail LOW). CCF is a **broader category** that *includes* common mode failures and also includes failures from shared resources, environment, design flaws, etc.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorization of CCF Root Causes

The IEC 61508 standard groups CCF causes into the following structured taxonomy:

### A. Equipment-Related CCF
- **Manufacturing Defects:** Same batch of relays, ICs, or sensors with identical solder joint weakness.
- **Design Deficiencies:** Identical firmware bug in two redundant transmitters from the same vendor.
- **Specification Errors:** Both channels undersized for the process envelope (e.g., both PSVs rated below actual surge pressure).

### B. Process / Environmental CCF
- **Corrosion:** Same aggressive chemical attacks both redundant sensors.
- **Temperature / Humidity:** Heat-soaked control room damages both redundant I/O cards.
- **Vibration:** Both valves on the same pipe rack experience fatigue.
- **EMI / RFI:** A single arc-welder nearby induces noise into both signal lines.

### C. Operation & Maintenance CCF
- **Maintenance Error:** A technician calibrates *both* transmitters incorrectly during the same maintenance window.
- **Bypass / Override:** Both channels accidentally put into bypass simultaneously.
- **Procedural:** Same wrong spare-part number installed in both valves.

### D. Coupling CCF (Shared Resources)
- **Power Supply:** Single 24 VDC supply feeding both transmitters.
- **Air Supply:** A single air manifold feeding both valve actuators.
- **Communication Bus:** Single Profibus/Foundation Fieldbus segment.
- **Heat Sink / Cooling:** Shared enclosure ventilation.

---

## 2.2 The Beta Factor (β) Model — Most Widely Used

The **Beta Factor Model** (originally proposed by Edwards & Watson, 1974, and later standardized in IEC 61508-6 Annex D) partitions the dangerous failure rate of a component into two mutually exclusive categories:

$$
\lambda_D = \lambda_{D,\text{independent}} + \lambda_{D,\text{CCF}}
$$

where the **Beta Factor β** is defined as:

$$
\beta = \frac{\lambda_{D,\text{CCF}}}{\lambda_D}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda_{D,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D
$$

$$
\lambda_{D,\text{independent}} = (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_D
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation of β:** If β = 0.10, then **10% of all dangerous failures** are common-cause. The independent (useful redundancy) fraction is only **90%**. This is why redundancy without diversity has a *hard floor* on achievable PFD.

---

## 2.3 Quantitative Impact on Redundancy (The "Why" Behind CCF)

Consider a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two)** voting architecture. Without CCF, the system PFD would be the product of independent failures:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2}^{\text{ideal}} = (\lambda_D \cdot t)^2 \quad \text{(vanishes for small } t \text{)}
$$

With the Beta Factor Model, the PFD has **two additive terms**:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2}^{\text{real}} = 2 \cdot (1-\beta)\cdot \lambda_D \cdot t \quad + \quad \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t
$$

The **first term** is the independent (redundancy-effective) failure contribution. The **second term** is the **CCF floor** that *cannot* be reduced by adding more channels.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beta Factor Definition | $\beta = \lambda_{D,CCF} / \lambda_D$ | Dimensionless | Typical 0.01 – 0.10 |
| 2 | Independent Failure Rate | $\lambda_{D,ind} = (1-\beta)\cdot \lambda_D$ | per hour | Fraction usable by redundancy |
| 3 | Common Cause Failure Rate | $\lambda_{D,CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D$ | per hour | Floor on achievable PFD |
| 4 | 1oo1 PFD (avg) | $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D\,t$ | Dimensionless | t = proof interval (h) |
| 5 | 1oo2 PFD with CCF | $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2\lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta\lambda_D t$ | Dimensionless | β is per pair |
| 6 | 2oo2 PFD with CCF | $\text{PFD}_{2oo2} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t$ | Dimensionless | No redundancy benefit for CCF |
| 7 | 1oo2D PFD (simplified) | $\text{PFD}_{1oo2D} = \tfrac{(1-\beta)^2\lambda_D^2 t^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ | Dimensionless | D = with diagnostics |
| 8 | SIS Availability (avg) | $\text{RRF}_{avg} = 1 / \text{PFD}_{avg}$ | Dimensionless | Risk Reduction Factor |
| 9 | CCF Diagnostic Coverage Term | $\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ | Dimensionless | λ_DU = undetected portion |
| 10 | α-Factor (alternative model) | $\alpha_k = \text{P(exactly } k \text{ channels fail)} / \text{P(any failure)}$ | Dimensionless | Sum over k = 1 |
| 11 | MGL — q parameter | $q = \mu \cdot T_1 / 2$ | Dimensionless | MGL model parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safeguard Note:** Above, we use `\vert` and `\cdot` instead of `|` and `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. Students writing answers on paper should use ordinary brackets.

---

## 2.5 Why CCF Matters in Real Engineering

- **SIL Verification:** KTU board problems typically ask students to verify whether an architecture meets SIL 1/2/3. **Without CCF, every redundant system meets SIL 3** — which is physically impossible. CCF is the reality-check.
- **Inspection / Audit:** TÜV / ISA auditors will reject a SIL calculation that omits β.
- **Cost Justification:** Adding a third or fourth channel (e.g., 2oo3) only helps against *independent* failures. CCF dictates the *maximum achievable* PFD, regardless of channel count.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFD of 1oo2 Architecture with Beta Factor

### Step 1 — Start with raw failure stream
A redundant pair experiences dangerous failures at rate $2 \lambda_D$. These failures are partitioned by the Beta Factor:

$$
\text{Independent portion: } 2 \cdot (1-\beta)\cdot \lambda_D
$$

$$
\text{Common-cause portion: } \beta \cdot \lambda_D
$$

*Reasoning:* The independent failure term has a factor of 2 because either of the two channels can fail independently. The CCF term has *no factor of 2* because both fail *together* from one event.

---

### Step 2 — Independent part contributes squared PFD

For a **1oo2** voting logic, the system fails to actuate **only if both channels fail independently AND both are undetected before the proof test**. The probability that both independent failures occur during interval $t$ is the product of two independent failure probabilities:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{independent}} = \left[(1-\beta)\lambda_D \cdot t\right]^2
$$

*Reasoning:* The 1oo2 architecture requires *both* channels failed for system failure. Independent failures multiply (probability of AND event).

---

### Step 3 — Add the CCF term

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t
$$

*Reasoning:* A single CCF event instantly disables the pair, regardless of whether it's detected or undetected — so we use the *total* dangerous rate λ_D, not λ_DU, for a conservative approximation.

---

### Step 4 — Total PFD (superposition)

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \left[(1-\beta)\lambda_D t\right]^2 + \beta \lambda_D t
$$

Fully expanded:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 \lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta \lambda_D t
$$

---

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (Typical Board Problem)

**Given:**
- λ_D = **5 × 10⁻⁶ per hour**
- β = **0.10**
- Proof interval t = **8760 hours (1 year)**

**Step 5.1 — Compute independent PFD contribution:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{ind} = (1-0.10)^2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2
$$

Intermediate expansion:

$$
\text{PFD}_{ind} = (0.81) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (76.7 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{ind} = 0.81 \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{ind} = 1.553 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute CCF contribution:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{CCF} = 0.10 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8760)
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{CCF} = 0.10 \cdot 4.38 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{CCF} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Total PFD:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 1.553 \times 10^{-3} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} = 5.93 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5.4 — Compare with 1oo1:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 8760 = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 1oo2 system improves the PFD by a factor of:

$$
\frac{2.19 \times 10^{-2}}{5.93 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3.7
$$

**Without CCF (β=0)**, the improvement would be approximately **109×**, not 3.7×. **This is the CCF tax in action.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Alpha Factor Model (Brief)

The Alpha Factor Model generalizes the Beta Factor for systems with **3 or more channels (2oo3, 2oo4, 1oo3)**:

$$
\lambda_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} k \cdot \alpha_k \cdot \lambda_D
$$

Where $\alpha_k$ = probability that exactly $k$ channels fail in a single CCF event.

**Constraint (normalization):**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N} \alpha_k = 1
$$

For **N = 2** (one-out-of-two logic), only $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ exist, and the Beta Factor relation emerges:

$$
\beta \equiv \alpha_2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 1oo3 / 2oo3 architectures**, the Alpha Factor or the **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model** must be used. KTU students are expected to know the Beta Factor model thoroughly and recognize the existence of MGL for higher redundancy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of β on 1oo2 PFD

| β value | Independent term | CCF term | Total PFD (×10⁻³) | % CCF contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 1.918 | 0.000 | 1.918 | 0% |
| 0.02 | 1.842 | 0.876 | 2.718 | 32.2% |
| 0.05 | 1.731 | 2.190 | 3.921 | 55.8% |
| 0.10 | 1.553 | 4.380 | 5.933 | 73.8% |
| 0.20 | 1.227 | 8.760 | 9.987 | 87.7% |

**Engineering Takeaway:** When β > 0.05, the CCF term **dominates** the PFD. Adding more redundant channels (going to 1oo3 or 2oo3) only helps if you also reduce β through diversity.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CCF Classification & Mitigation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Common Cause Failure CCF] --> B[Equipment Origin]
    A --> C[Process Environment Origin]
    A --> D[Maintenance Procedure Origin]
    A --> E[Shared Coupling Origin]
    
    B --> B1[Same Batch Manufacturing Defect]
    B --> B2[Identical Firmware Bug]
    B --> B3[Common Specification Error]
    
    C --> C1[Corrosion Attack]
    C --> C2[Heat or Humidity Exposure]
    C --> C3[Vibration Fatigue]
    C --> C4[EMI or RFI Coupling]
    
    D --> D1[Calibration Mistake on Both Channels]
    D --> D2[Simultaneous Bypass]
    D --> D3[Wrong Spare Part Installed]
    
    E --> E1[Shared Power Supply 24 VDC]
    E --> E2[Shared Air Manifold]
    E --> E3[Shared Communication Bus]
    E --> E4[Shared Cooling Fan]
    
    A --> M[Mitigation Strategy]
    M --> M1[Diversity of Vendors]
    M --> M2[Diversity of Technologies]
    M --> M3[Physical Separation of Cabling]
    M --> M4[Independent Power and Air]
    M --> M5[Continuous Diagnostics]
    M --> M6[Frequent Proof Testing]
    
    M1 --> R[Reduce Beta Factor]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    M4 --> R
    M5 --> R
    M6 --> R
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — 1oo2 SIS Loop with CCF Coupling Points

```mermaid
graph LR
    PT1[Pressure Transmitter 1] --> LOGIC[SIS Logic Solver]
    PT2[Pressure Transmitter 2] --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> SV1[Solenoid Valve 1]
    LOGIC --> SV2[Solenoid Valve 2]
    SV1 --> VLV[Shutdown Valve]
    SV2 --> VLV
    
    SHARED1[(Shared 24 VDC Supply)] -. CCF Power .-> PT1
    SHARED1 -. CCF Power .-> PT2
    SHARED1 -. CCF Power .-> LOGIC
    
    SHARED2[(Shared Air Manifold)] -. CCF Air .-> SV1
    SHARED2 -. CCF Air .-> SV2
    
    SHARED3[(Same Vendor Firmware)] -. CCF Design .-> PT1
    SHARED3 -. CCF Design .-> PT2
    
    TECH[(Same Technician)] -. CCF Maintenance .-> PT1
    TECH -. CCF Maintenance .-> PT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show *intended* signal flow. Dotted lines (marked "CCF ...") show *shared dependency points*. Each dotted link is a candidate for a CCF root cause. Mitigation breaks these dotted lines (e.g., install separate power supplies, separate vendors, physical separation).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — PFD Hierarchy Under CCF

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total PFD of Redundant System] --> B[Independent Failure Component]
    A --> C[Common Cause Failure Component]
    
    B --> B1[Reducible by More Channels]
    B --> B2[Multiplicative Effect]
    
    C --> C1[Hard Floor Cannot Reduce]
    C --> C2[Single Event Defeats All]
    C --> C3[Reduced Only by Diversity]
    
    B2 --> D1[1oo2 has PFD proportional to t squared]
    C2 --> D2[1oo2 has PFD proportional to t linear]
    
    D1 --> E[Beta factor beta]
    D2 --> E
    
    E --> F[SIL Verification Target]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Common Cause Failure (CCF) in the context of Safety Instrumented Systems. Give two practical examples from a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**
A Common Cause Failure (CCF) is the simultaneous or near-simultaneous failure of two or more redundant channels or components of a Safety Instrumented System due to a single shared root cause, leading to loss of the safety function.

**Two examples:**
1. Both redundant pressure transmitters fed from the **same impulse line** that becomes blocked — both channels read the same (stuck) value and cannot detect a real overpressure condition.
2. Both shutdown solenoid valves receiving air from a **single air manifold** that fails due to moisture contamination — neither valve can actuate the downstream shutdown valve on demand.

*(Expected length: 3–4 sentences + 2 examples. Marks awarded for definition + 2 valid examples = 2 + 1.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the Beta Factor in CCF analysis? State its typical range for process plant SIS loops.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Beta Factor (β)** is the fraction of the dangerous failure rate of a component that contributes to a common cause failure, rather than to an independent failure. Mathematically:

$$
\beta = \frac{\lambda_{D,CCF}}{\lambda_D}
$$

Typical range for process plant SIS loops is **β = 0.01 to 0.10 (i.e., 1% to 10%)**, with **0.05 (5%)** being a commonly used conservative default per IEC 61508-6 when detailed CCF data is unavailable.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of Common Cause Failures in Safety Instrumented Systems, with suitable examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the PFD expression for a 1oo2 voting architecture using the Beta Factor model, clearly identifying the independent and CCF components. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The classification of CCFs follows **IEC 61508-6 Annex D**, which divides CCFs into four broad categories:

**1. Equipment Origin:** Failures arising from the components themselves.
   - *Example:* Two identical thermocouples from the same manufacturing batch fail open-circuit at 600°C because of a defective sheath material.

**2. Process / Environment Origin:** Failures caused by the surrounding process conditions.
   - *Example:* A hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) leak corrodes the diaphragms of both redundant pressure transmitters mounted in the same junction box.

**3. Operation and Maintenance Origin:** Failures introduced during human intervention.
   - *Example:* During a turnaround, a technician disconnects and incorrectly calibrates both level transmitters, leaving a systematic bias.

**4. Coupling Origin (Shared Resources):** Failures due to shared infrastructure.
   - *Example:* A single UPS failure drops 24 VDC to both transmitter loops and the logic solver simultaneously.

Each category requires **distinct mitigation strategies**: diversity of design tackles (1), physical separation tackles (2), procedural discipline tackles (3), and segregated resources tackle (4).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** A 1oo2 system fails if *both* channels fail to actuate. Partition each channel's dangerous failure rate:

$$
\lambda_D = \beta\lambda_D + (1-\beta)\lambda_D
$$

**Step 2:** Independent failure contribution (both channels must fail independently):

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = \big[(1-\beta)\lambda_D \cdot t\big]^2
$$

*Logic:* 1oo2 logic requires AND of two independent failures; probabilities multiply.

**Step 3:** CCF contribution (single event disables pair):

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t
$$

*Logic:* No factor of 2; one shared event knocks out the entire pair.

**Step 4:** Total PFD by superposition:

$$
\boxed{\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 \lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta \lambda_D t}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- The $(1-\beta)^2 \lambda_D^2 t^2$ term is the *useful* redundancy effect.
- The $\beta \lambda_D t$ term is the **CCF floor** — unaffected by adding more channels.
- If β = 0.05 and t = 1 year, the CCF term typically dominates, illustrating that *redundancy alone cannot achieve SIL 3 without diversity*.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, discuss the impact of CCF on a 1oo2 redundant SIS loop. Identify all shared dependency points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 pressure transmitter loop has λ_D = 4 × 10⁻⁶ per hour, β = 0.08, and a proof test interval of 2 years (17,520 h). Compute the average PFD and the RRF. Comment on the achievable SIL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram Description:**

The SIS loop consists of two parallel pressure transmitters (PT1, PT2) feeding a logic solver which drives two solenoid valves (SV1, SV2) operating a single shutdown valve. The shared dependency points (CCF sources) are:

| Shared Dependency | Risk Vector | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 24 VDC power supply | Single PSU failure blacks out both loops | Dual redundant PSU, separate feeders |
| Air manifold for SV1/SV2 | Moisture/clog disables both actuators | Separate air filters and regulators |
| Common impulse line | Plug blocks both PT readings | Separate tapping points with isolation valves |
| Same vendor firmware | Identical bug disables both | Diverse vendors or diverse firmware versions |
| Same cable tray | Fire/damage cuts both signals | Route through separate physical paths |
| Same technician/maintenance window | Calibration error applied to both | Staggered maintenance procedures |

Each of these couplings contributes to the β factor. Without breaking these couplings, the redundancy offers limited PFD improvement.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- λ_D = 4 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- β = 0.08
- t = 17,520 h (2 years)

**Step 1 — Independent PFD contribution:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = (1-0.08)^2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (17520)^2
$$

Intermediate:

$$
(1-0.08)^2 = 0.8464
$$

$$
(4 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 16 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
(17520)^2 = 3.0695 \times 10^{8}
$$

Product:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.8464 \times 16 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.0695 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 0.8464 \times 4.911 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{ind}} = 4.157 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — CCF PFD contribution:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = 0.08 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = 0.08 \times 7.008 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = 5.606 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Total PFD:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 4.157 \times 10^{-3} + 5.606 \times 10^{-3} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Risk Reduction Factor:**

$$
\text{RRF}_{avg} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{9.76 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 102.4
$$

**Step 5 — SIL Classification (per IEC 61511 Table 3):**

| SIL | PFD range | RRF range |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | 10⁻² to 10⁻¹ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | 10⁻³ to 10⁻² | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ | 1000 to 10,000 |

**Conclusion:** RRF ≈ 102 places this loop at the **lower boundary of SIL 2**. Although the architecture is 1oo2 (nominally SIL 2), the high β = 0.08 has pushed the PFD upward by nearly 35% compared to the β = 0.05 ideal. To improve to SIL 3, **β must be reduced** (e.g., vendor diversity + physical separation + staggered proof testing) or the proof interval shortened.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in CCF questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the β term entirely** — gives an unrealistically optimistic PFD. *Examiner deducts up to 4 marks for this.*
> 2. **Using β with 1oo1** — β is meaningful only when there is *redundancy*. In a simplex loop, β has no PFD impact on the channel itself.
> 3. **Confusing β (per channel) with β (per pair)** — IEC 61508-6 uses β per *pair* in the standard formula. Check your textbook convention.
> 4. **Mixing λ_D and λ_DU** — for the conservative CCF contribution, use total dangerous rate λ_D; for diagnostic-aware PFD, you may need λ_DU separately. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Not commenting on SIL achievable** — KTU examiners expect the final 2–3 lines to discuss whether the calculated PFD meets the intended SIL target.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **CCF Definition:** Simultaneous failure of ≥2 redundant channels from a single shared cause — defeats redundancy.
- **Beta Factor β = λ_D,CCF / λ_D**, typically **0.01 to 0.10**, with **0.05** as IEC 61508 default.
- **Four categories of CCF:** Equipment, Process/Environment, Maintenance, Coupling (shared resources).
- **Core PFD formula (1oo2):** $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 \lambda_D^2 t^2 + \beta \lambda_D t$
- **Interpretation:** First term = redundancy benefit; second term = CCF floor (cannot be reduced by adding channels).
- **For 2oo2:** No redundancy benefit for CCF; $\text{PFD}_{2oo2} = \beta \lambda_D t$.
- **Higher redundancy (1oo3 / 2oo3):** Use **Alpha Factor model** or **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) model** — not the simple Beta Factor.
- **Diagnostic Coverage:** Reduces only the *independent undetected* failures (λ_DU); does NOT reduce CCF contribution unless diagnostics address the *shared* failure cause.
- **CCF Mitigation = Breaking Shared Dependencies:** Diversity of vendor, diversity of technology, physical separation, independent power/air, staggered proof testing.
- **High β (>0.05) → CCF term dominates PFD → adding more channels is wasteful without reducing β.**
- **SIL Verification Reality Check:** A 1oo2 loop with β = 0.05 typically sits between SIL 1 and SIL 2, *not* SIL 2 as naive redundancy analysis suggests.
- **Always state β value used and its source (IEC 61508-6 Annex D vs. plant-specific FMEDA data).**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagnostic Coverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diagnostic Coverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** of an element, subsystem, or system is formally defined as the *fraction of dangerous failures that are detected by the automatic diagnostic test functions* of the Safety Instrumented System (SIS). It is a dimensionless ratio, expressed as a percentage, that quantifies the effectiveness of built-in self-test mechanisms in revealing dangerous equipment failures before they can compromise the safety function.

Per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (aligned with **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511**), Diagnostic Coverage is a critical architectural parameter because it directly influences:
- The achievable **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**
- The **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**
- The overall **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Diagnostic Coverage is a **Module 3 high-priority topic** and is frequently tested in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Students must memorize the DC categories and the master formula $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Smoke Detector"

Imagine you have installed a **conventional smoke detector** in your home. This device only does *one job* — it senses smoke. It has **no internal intelligence**. If its battery dies, or its photoelectric chamber gets dusty, or its electronic circuit fails silently, the detector will simply stop working. You will not know about the failure until a real fire occurs and the alarm never rings. This is a **diagnostic coverage of 0%** — the system has no self-awareness.

Now consider a **smart interconnected smoke detector** (like those used in industrial plants). Every 10 seconds, it pulses an internal LED inside the sensing chamber, checks the battery voltage, verifies the buzzer circuit, and transmits a "heartbeat" signal to a central control panel. If *any* of these checks fail, it raises a "FAULT" warning light immediately. This is a **diagnostic coverage approaching 99%+** — almost every possible dangerous failure is detected automatically and reported *before* an emergency occurs.

The **smart detector** is analogous to a modern SIS sensor, logic solver, or final element with embedded diagnostics. **Diagnostic Coverage is the engineering metric that tells us how "smart" and self-aware our safety equipment really is.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A high DC does *not* mean the device never fails. It means the device *knows* it has failed and can communicate this to the operator so that corrective action can be taken before a hazardous event demands the safety function.

### 1.3 Categories of Diagnostic Coverage (Per IEC 61508)

The international standard **IEC 61508** classifies Diagnostic Coverage into four discrete categories. These categories are essential for SIL determination and are mandatory knowledge for KTU board examinations:

| Category | DC Range | Typical Application |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **None** | **DC < 60%** | Simple mechanical switches, basic analog transmitters without self-test |
| **Low** | **60% ≤ DC < 90%** | Standard PLC I/O modules with limited self-diagnostics |
| **Medium** | **90% ≤ DC < 99%** | Smart transmitters, microprocessor-based logic solvers |
| **High** | **DC ≥ 99%** | Triple Modular Redundant (TMR) systems, SIL 3 certified equipment |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**N-L-M-H**" → **N**one, **L**ow, **M**edium, **H**igh. Cutoffs are **60%**, **90%**, and **99%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Failure Rate Taxonomy (The Foundation of Diagnostic Coverage)

To understand DC, we must first dissect how a component's total failure rate $\lambda$ is partitioned. Every failure in a Safety Instrumented System is classified along **two independent axes**:

**Axis 1 — Effect on the Safety Function:**
- **Safe Failure ($\lambda_{\text{S}}$):** Failure that causes the SIS to spuriously trip (false alarm) but does *not* create a hazard.
- **Dangerous Failure ($\lambda_{\text{D}}$):** Failure that prevents the SIS from executing its safety action when demanded — this is the hazardous scenario.

**Axis 2 — Detection by Diagnostics:**
- **Detected (D):** Failure identified by automatic diagnostic test within a defined diagnostic test interval.
- **Undetected (U):** Failure that escapes the diagnostic net and remains hidden until discovered by proof testing or actual demand.

Combining these axes yields the **four fundamental failure rate categories** used throughout IEC 61508/61511:

- **$\lambda_{\text{SD}}$** = Safe Detected failure rate
- **$\lambda_{\text{SU}}$** = Safe Undetected failure rate
- **$\lambda_{\text{DD}}$** = Dangerous Detected failure rate
- **$\lambda_{\text{DU}}$** = Dangerous Undetected failure rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation:** The total dangerous failure rate is the sum of its detected and undetected components:
> $$\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$
> This partitioning is the conceptual cornerstone of every DC and SFF calculation you will encounter in KTU exams.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

**Step 1 — Why partition failures?**
Because from a safety perspective, a *detected* dangerous failure is fundamentally different from an *undetected* dangerous failure. A detected failure triggers an alarm, the operator takes corrective action, and the system is repaired — the hazard window closes immediately. An undetected failure remains a latent threat until the next proof test.

**Step 2 — How is DC quantified?**
DC is computed as the ratio of dangerous failures that the diagnostic system *successfully detects* to the *total* dangerous failures:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$$

**Step 3 — How does DC influence the PFD?**
Only the **undetected** dangerous failure rate ($\lambda_{\text{DU}}$) contributes to the PFDavg calculation in the simplified equation. Therefore, a higher DC reduces $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ and lowers the overall risk:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}}{2}$$

where $T_{\text{Proof}}$ is the **Proof Test Interval** in hours.

**Step 4 — How is DC achieved in practice?**
Common diagnostic techniques include:
- **Watchdog timers** (logic solver reset verification)
- **Heartbeat signals** (sensor-to-controller periodic pulse)
- **Cross-comparison voting** (2-out-of-3 or 2-out-of-4 architectures)
- **Plausibility checks** (range, rate-of-change validation)
- **Loop-back tests** (output signal echoed back to input)
- **Memory checksums** (RAM/ROM integrity verification in logic solvers)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU Board Examinations. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$ | DC = Diagnostic Coverage | Dimensionless ratio; expressed in **%** |
| 2 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$ | $\lambda_{\text{D}}$ = total dangerous failure rate | Per hour (failures/hr) |
| 3 | $\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ | Partition equation | fit = failures per $10^9$ hours |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\text{Total}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ | All four failure categories | Per hour |
| 5 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$ | SFF = Safe Failure Fraction | **Required for SIL determination** |
| 6 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$ | Expanded form of SFF | Use this for numerical problems |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = \lambda_{\text{D}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC})$ | Rearrangement of DC formula | Critical for PFD calculation |
| 8 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}}{2}$ | Simplified PFD equation (single channel) | $T_{\text{Proof}}$ in hours |
| 9 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}$ | Sum of subsystem PFDs | Additive for series SIF |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse DC with SFF. **DC** only considers dangerous failures. **SFF** considers *all* failures (safe + dangerous, detected + undetected). This is one of the most common errors in KTU valuation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **oil & gas, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical industries**, Diagnostic Coverage is a contractual requirement. For example:
- A **Level Transmitter** installed on a hydrocarbon storage tank must have **DC ≥ 90%** to qualify for SIL 2 use (per IEC 61511).
- A **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** typically has **DC < 60%** because mechanical valves cannot self-test in service — this limits their standalone SIL contribution.
- A **Triconex (TMR) Safety PLC** achieves **DC ≥ 99%** through voting diagnostics, enabling SIL 3 applications in Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master DC Formula

We begin with the fundamental premise that the total dangerous failure rate is the **sum** of its detected and undetected portions (from Section 2.1). This is a definitional partitioning.

**Starting Equation (Given by IEC 61508):**

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

**Step 1 — Define DC as a Ratio:**
By definition, Diagnostic Coverage is the proportion of dangerous failures that are *detected*. We write this as the fraction of detected dangerous failures over the total dangerous failures:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Partition Equation:**
We substitute $\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ from the starting equation into the denominator:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the Undetected Failure Rate in Terms of DC:**
Rearranging the formula to solve for $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$, which is essential for PFD calculations:

$$\lambda_{\text{DD}} = \text{DC} \cdot (\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}})$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DD}} = \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DD}} - \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{DD}} = \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DD}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) = \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

Since $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{D}}$ and $\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$:

$$\lambda_{\text{DU}} = \lambda_{\text{D}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC})$$

This is the **most important derived result** — it tells us exactly how much dangerous failure rate "leaks through" the diagnostic net.

**Step 4 — Derive the SFF Relationship to DC:**
The Safe Failure Fraction is defined as:

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$$

We can split $\lambda_{\text{Total}}$ as $(\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}})$ and recognize that:

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$$

Rearranging to isolate DC's contribution, one can show that **SFF is always ≥ DC**, because SFF counts safe failures (which DC ignores) in the numerator:

$$\text{SFF} \geq \text{DC}$$

This inequality is a frequent KTU Part A question.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A smart pressure transmitter used in a Safety Instrumented Function has the following failure rate data (in failures per $10^6$ hours, i.e., $\times 10^{-6}$/hr):
> - $\lambda_{\text{SD}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
> - $\lambda_{\text{SU}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
> - $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
> - $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The Diagnostic Coverage (DC), (b) The Safe Failure Fraction (SFF), (c) The Dangerous Undetected failure rate contribution, and (d) The DC category per IEC 61508.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Diagnostic Coverage:**

We apply the master DC formula. The dangerous failure rate is the sum of $\lambda_{\text{DD}}$ and $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$:

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} + 0.3 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = 2.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

Now compute DC:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$$

$$\text{DC} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{DC} = \frac{2.4}{2.7} = 0.8888...$$

$$\boxed{\text{DC} \approx 88.89\%}$$

**Part (b) — Safe Failure Fraction:**

First compute the total safe failure rate:

$$\lambda_{\text{S}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{S}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} + 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{S}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

Now compute the total failure rate:

$$\lambda_{\text{Total}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{Total}} = 0.8 + 0.4 + 2.4 + 0.3 = 3.9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

Apply the SFF formula:

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{0.8 + 0.4 + 2.4}{3.9} = \frac{3.6}{3.9}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SFF} \approx 92.31\%}$$

**Part (c) — Undetected Dangerous Failure Rate:**

$$\lambda_{\text{DU}} = \lambda_{\text{D}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC})$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 2.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - 0.8889)$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 2.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.1111$$

$$\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr} \quad (\text{verifies the given data})$$

**Part (d) — DC Category:**

Since **DC = 88.89%**, and per IEC 61508 the categories are:
- None: DC < 60%
- Low: 60% ≤ DC < 90%
- Medium: 90% ≤ DC < 99%
- High: DC ≥ 99%

Our value **88.89%** falls in the range **60% ≤ DC < 90%**.

$$\boxed{\text{Diagnostic Coverage Category} = \text{LOW}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — DC vs SIL Achievability

Per IEC 61508 Table 2 (Type A components) and Table 3 (Type B components), the achievable SIL is jointly determined by **SFF** and **DC**. The following decision logic applies:

- **Type A components** (simple, well-defined failure modes): Require SFF ≥ 60% for SIL 1, SFF ≥ 90% for SIL 2, etc.
- **Type B components** (complex, software-driven): Require **DC ≥ 90%** AND **SFF ≥ 90%** for SIL 2, and **DC ≥ 99%** for SIL 3.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Can this component achieve SIL X?", you must always state BOTH the DC category AND the SFF value before concluding the SIL feasibility.

### 4.2 Diagnostic Coverage Category Decision Flowchart

The following diagram shows the **decision logic** that an engineer (or examiner) follows when classifying a component into one of the four DC categories defined by IEC 61508.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startInput["Input Measured DC value in percent"]
    catNone["CATEGORY NONE DC less than 60 percent"]
    catLow["CATEGORY LOW DC between 60 and 90 percent"]
    catMedium["CATEGORY MEDIUM DC between 90 and 99 percent"]
    catHigh["CATEGORY HIGH DC at or above 99 percent"]
    appNone["Application Basic non self testing devices"]
    appLow["Application Standard PLC I O with limited diagnostics"]
    appMedium["Application Smart transmitters and micro based logic solvers"]
    appHigh["Application TMR systems and SIL 3 certified hardware"]
    
    startInput --> checkA{"Is DC less than 60 percent"}
    startInput --> checkB{"Is DC between 60 and 90 percent"}
    startInput --> checkC{"Is DC between 90 and 99 percent"}
    startInput --> checkD{"Is DC at or above 99 percent"}
    
    checkA --> catNone
    checkB --> catLow
    checkC --> catMedium
    checkD --> catHigh
    
    catNone --> appNone
    catLow --> appLow
    catMedium --> appMedium
    catHigh --> appHigh
```
