
### 4.3 Diagnostic Test Mechanism Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates how a diagnostic function physically interfaces with the safety loop. It shows the **three channels** (sensor, logic solver, final element) and how diagnostic signals flow within each.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    processHazard["PROCESS HAZARD"]
    sensorTx["SENSOR TRANSMITTER with DC greater than 90 percent"]
    logicSolver["LOGIC SOLVER with DC greater than 99 percent and watchdog timers"]
    finalElement["FINAL ELEMENT with DC approximately 60 percent"]
    diagEngine["CENTRAL DIAGNOSTIC ENGINE receives heartbeat and fault signals"]
    operatorHMI["OPERATOR HMI displays fault alarms"]
    
    processHazard --> sensorTx
    sensorTx --> logicSolver
    logicSolver --> finalElement
    sensorTx -.Heartbeat Signal.-> diagEngine
    logicSolver -.Watchdog Pulse.-> diagEngine
    finalElement -.Partial Stroke Test.-> diagEngine
    diagEngine --> operatorHMI
    
    style processHazard fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style sensorTx fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style logicSolver fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style finalElement fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style diagEngine fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style operatorHMI fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **dashed arrows** represent diagnostic feedback signals (heartbeats, watchdog pulses, partial stroke tests), while the **solid arrows** represent the normal safety function signal path. Notice how the final element typically has the **lowest DC** because mechanical valves cannot perform full self-tests while in service.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Diagnostic Coverage. State the four categories of Diagnostic Coverage as per IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Diagnostic Coverage (DC) is defined as the ratio of the dangerous detected failure rate to the total dangerous failure rate of a Safety Instrumented System element. It represents the fraction of dangerous failures that are automatically detected by the built-in diagnostic test functions.

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$$

**Four Categories (2 Marks):**

| Category | DC Range |
|----------|----------|
| None | DC < 60% |
| Low | 60% ≤ DC < 90% |
| Medium | 90% ≤ DC < 99% |
| High | DC ≥ 99% |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Diagnostic Coverage (DC) and Safe Failure Fraction (SFF).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Numerator considers** | Only dangerous *detected* failures ($\lambda_{\text{DD}}$) | All *safe* failures + dangerous *detected* failures ($\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}$) |
| **Denominator** | Total dangerous failures ($\lambda_{\text{D}}$) | Total *all* failures ($\lambda_{\text{Total}}$) |
| **What it measures** | Effectiveness of diagnostic tests in detecting dangerous failures | Overall fraction of failures that do not lead to undetected dangerous state |
| **Mathematical relationship** | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$ | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$ |
| **Key inequality** | $\text{SFF} \geq \text{DC}$ always holds | SFF counts safe failures additionally |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions provide *internal choice*. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each 14-mark question contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the various diagnostic techniques used in a Safety Instrumented System to achieve high Diagnostic Coverage for sensors, logic solvers, and final elements. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature transmitter installed in a chemical reactor safety loop has the following failure data (in $10^{-6}$/hr):
- $\lambda_{\text{SD}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{SU}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = 3.6 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr

Calculate the Diagnostic Coverage (DC), Safe Failure Fraction (SFF), and identify the DC category per IEC 61508. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Diagnostic Techniques Block Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
   ┌────────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐
   │   SENSOR       │     │  LOGIC SOLVER  │     │ FINAL ELEMENT  │
   │   SUBSYSTEM    │     │   SUBSYSTEM    │     │   SUBSYSTEM    │
   ├────────────────┤     ├────────────────┤     ├────────────────┤
   │ • Heartbeat    │     │ • Watchdog     │     │ • Partial      │
   │   Signal       │     │   Timer        │     │   Stroke Test  │
   │ • Range Check  │     │ • Memory       │     │ • Solenoid     │
   │ • Rate-of-     │     │   Checksum     │     │   Coil Health  │
   │   Change       │     │ • Cross-       │     │ • Valve Seat   │
   │   Validation   │     │   Comparison   │     │   Leak Detect  │
   │ • Sensor        │     │ • I/O Loopback │     │ • Position     │
   │   Redundancy   │     │   Test         │     │   Feedback     │
   └───────┬────────┘     └────────┬───────┘     └────────┬───────┘
           │                       │                       │
           └───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘
                                   │
                          ┌────────▼─────────┐
                          │  DIAGNOSTIC      │
                          │  AGGREGATOR /    │
                          │  ALARM MANAGER   │
                          └──────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (4 Marks):**

1. **Sensor Subsystem Diagnostics (1.5 Marks):**
   - **Heartbeat Signal:** The transmitter outputs a periodic pulse (e.g., every 1 second). Absence of pulse indicates sensor failure.
   - **Range Check:** If reading exceeds physical plausibility limits (e.g., temperature > absolute maximum), a fault is flagged.
   - **Rate-of-Change Validation:** Detects "stuck" sensors (no change over time despite process variation).
   - **Redundancy Voting:** Two or three sensors with median-select or 2-out-of-3 voting logic detect disagreement.

2. **Logic Solver Diagnostics (1.5 Marks):**
   - **Watchdog Timer:** A hardware timer must be reset by software within a fixed window; failure to do so triggers a system shutdown.
   - **Memory Checksum:** RAM and ROM contents are continuously verified; corruption triggers a safe state.
   - **Cross-Comparison:** In redundant controllers, output results are compared; mismatch triggers diagnostic alarm.

3. **Final Element Diagnostics (1 Mark):**
   - **Partial Stroke Test (PST):** Valve is moved a small percentage (e.g., 10%) from its normal position and returned. Detects stuck valves without disrupting the process.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Dangerous Failure Rate (1 Mark):**

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = 3.6 \times 10^{-6} + 0.4 \times 10^{-6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Diagnostic Coverage (2 Marks):**

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}$$

$$\text{DC} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DC} = 0.90 = 90\%}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Failure Rate (1 Mark):**

$$\lambda_{\text{Total}} = 1.2 + 0.5 + 3.6 + 0.4 = 5.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Safe Failure Fraction (2 Marks):**

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{1.2 + 0.5 + 3.6}{5.7}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{5.3}{5.7}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SFF} = 0.9298 = 92.98\%}$$

**Step 5 — Identify DC Category (1 Mark):**

Since DC = 90% exactly, and the category boundaries are:
- Medium: **90% ≤ DC < 99%**

The transmitter falls into the **CATEGORY MEDIUM** diagnostic coverage class.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the relationship between Diagnostic Coverage (DC), Safe Failure Fraction (SFF), and the achievable Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for a Type B complex component per IEC 61508. Use an appropriate architectural table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A level switch used in an overfill protection system has $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 4.2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $\lambda_{\text{SD}} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, and $\lambda_{\text{SU}} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$/hr. Determine (i) the DC and SFF, (ii) the DC category, and (iii) the maximum SIL achievable for this component if it is classified as Type B. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Relationship between DC, SFF, and SIL for Type B Components (7 Marks):**

**Explanation (5 Marks):**

For **Type B components** (those with complex failure modes, e.g., microprocessors, ASICs), IEC 61508 Table 3 specifies that both **DC and SFF jointly determine the achievable SIL**:

- A high SFF alone is insufficient. The diagnostic system must be capable of detecting a sufficiently high fraction of dangerous failures.
- Conversely, a high DC alone is insufficient. The component must also have a low rate of dangerous undetected failures.

**Architectural Table (Type B — Per IEC 61508 Table 3):**

| SFF Range | DC: None (<60%) | DC: Low (60–90%) | DC: Medium (90–99%) | DC: High (≥99%) |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| SFF < 60% | Not Allowed | Not Allowed | SIL 1 | SIL 2 |
| 60% ≤ SFF < 90% | Not Allowed | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| 90% ≤ SFF < 99% | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 3 |
| SFF ≥ 99% | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | SIL 4 |

**Key Observations (2 Marks):**
- For SIL 3 achievement in a Type B component, **DC must be at least Medium (90%)** AND **SFF must be at least 90%**.
- For SIL 4, **DC must be High (≥99%)** AND **SFF must be ≥99%**.
- The cell "DC = None, SFF < 60%" means the component **cannot be used in any SIL**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 4.2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{SD}} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_{\text{SU}} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$/hr

**Step 1 — Calculate DC (2 Marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{D}} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.8 + 4.2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.16 = 16\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{DC} = 16\%}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate SFF (2 Marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{Total}} = 0.8 + 4.2 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 5.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}} = \frac{0.3 + 0.1 + 0.8}{5.4} = \frac{1.2}{5.4} = 0.2222$$

$$\boxed{\text{SFF} \approx 22.22\%}$$

**Step 3 — DC Category (1 Mark):**

Since DC = 16% < 60%, the DC category is **NONE**.

**Step 4 — Maximum SIL for Type B Component (2 Marks):**

From the Type B Architectural Table:
- DC = None AND SFF < 60% → **Not Allowed in any SIL**

$$\boxed{\text{Maximum SIL achievable} = \text{NOT ALLOWED (component is unsuitable for SIS use)}}$$

This level switch cannot be used standalone in any safety function. To achieve even SIL 1, it would need to be combined with another channel and use redundancy voting techniques.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage:** Students often write DC = 0.90 instead of DC = 90%. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing DC and SFF numerators:** The DC numerator is only $\lambda_{\text{DD}}$, while the SFF numerator includes $\lambda_{\text{S}}$ *plus* $\lambda_{\text{DD}}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Using the wrong denominator for SFF:** Always use $\lambda_{\text{Total}}$ (all four failure rate categories), never just $\lambda_{\text{D}}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the diagnostic test interval:** DC alone is meaningless without specifying the diagnostic test interval $T_{\text{Diagnostic}}$. If $T_{\text{Diagnostic}}$ is too long, a "detected" failure may not be flagged quickly enough.
> 5. **Not stating both DC category AND SIL table reference:** When asked about SIL achievability, you MUST cite IEC 61508 Table 2 (Type A) or Table 3 (Type B) and then apply the table.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation:**

- **DC Definition:** $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$ — fraction of *dangerous* failures detected automatically.
- **SFF Definition:** $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{Total}}}$ — fraction of *all* failures that do not result in an undetected dangerous state.
- **Key Inequality:** $\text{SFF} \geq \text{DC}$ always. If SFF < DC in your calculation, you have made an arithmetic error.
- **DC Categories:** **N**one (<60%), **L**ow (60–90%), **M**edium (90–99%), **H**igh (≥99%) — Mnemonic: **N-L-M-H**.
- **DC is Denominator-Aware:** The denominator contains $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ — the "hidden" failure rate that contributes to PFDavg.
- **Derived Formula:** $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = \lambda_{\text{D}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC})$ — *most important equation for PFD problems*.
- **SIL-Dependent DC Requirements (Type B):** SIL 1 needs ≥60%, SIL 2 needs ≥90%, SIL 3 needs ≥99%, SIL 4 needs ≥99% (with SFF ≥99%).
- **Diagnostic Techniques:** Heartbeat signals, watchdog timers, cross-comparison voting, partial stroke testing, range and rate-of-change plausibility checks.
- **Final Element DC Challenge:** Mechanical valves typically have DC < 60% (LOW/NONE) because they cannot perform full self-tests while in service — PST improves this.
- **Standards:** Always cite **IEC 61508** for DC categories and **IEC 61511** for process industry application.
- **Units Convention:** Failure rates are commonly expressed in **fit** (failures per $10^9$ hours) or in $10^{-6}$/hr.
- **Typical Application:** Smart transmitters (DC ≈ 90–95%), TMR safety PLCs (DC ≥ 99%), pressure transmitters (DC ≈ 85–95%), solenoid valves with PST (DC ≈ 70–80%).
- **Common Exam Trap:** A component with high SFF but low DC *cannot* achieve high SIL — both parameters must be jointly satisfied.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Diagnostic Coverage | PEICT862 — Safety Instrumentation System | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PFD Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PFD Calculation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of PFD > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition (As per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)** > **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** is defined as the average probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) will fail to perform its intended safety function when a hazardous demand occurs. It is a dimensionless number, typically ranging from **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵**, and is the central metric used to quantify the *risk reduction* provided by a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). For low-demand mode of operation (where the demand rate is **≤ 1 per year**, as per IEC 61508), the relevant metric is the **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg)**. ### 1.2 Mathematical Relationship Between PFD_avg and Safety Integrity Level (SIL) > [!NOTE] **SIL ↔ PFD_avg Mapping (IEC 61508 Standard Table)** | Safety Integrity Level | PFD_avg Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF = 1/PFD_avg) | |:----------------------:|:-------------:|:----------------------------------------:| | **SIL 1** | **10⁻² to 10⁻¹** | **10 to 100** | | **SIL 2** | **10⁻³ to 10⁻²** | **100 to 1000** | | **SIL 3** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³** | **1000 to 10,000** | | **SIL 4** | **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴** | **10,000 to 100,000** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Fire Brigade Response Time" Imagine your city has a fire alarm system connected to an automatic sprinkler. The system is **demanded** (i.e., activated) only when a fire breaks out — perhaps once in 5 years. You want to know: *"If a fire happens tomorrow morning, what is the probability the sprinkler will actually spray water?"* That probability is essentially **1 − PFD**. - A **low PFD** (e.g., 10⁻³) means the sprinkler is *highly trustworthy* (99.9% reliable on demand). - A **high PFD** (e.g., 10⁻¹) means it fails 1 out of every 10 times you need it. The **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is essentially a *grade* assigned based on how trustworthy the system must be. ## 1.4 Why PFD Calculation is Critical in SIS Design The entire goal of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is to **reduce risk** from a hazardous process event to an acceptable level. If a hydrocarbon separator has an inherent event frequency of **10⁻¹ per year** (fires once every 10 years on average), and the tolerable risk is **10⁻⁵ per year**, then the SIF must provide a **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) = 10⁴**, which falls in **SIL 3** territory. The PFD calculation mathematically proves (or disproves) that your instrumented protection layer actually achieves the required SIL. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Reliability Terminology Before computing PFD, we must understand the building blocks: > [!IMPORTANT] **Core Reliability Parameters** > - **λ (Lambda) — Failure Rate:** The constant instantaneous failure rate per hour (assumed under the "useful life" region of the bathtub curve). Units: **failures/hour** or **FITs (1 FIT = 10⁻⁹ failures/hour)**. > - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** For non-repaired components, the average time until first failure. **MTTF = 1/λ** > - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** For repairable systems, the average time between consecutive failures. **MTBF = 1/λ + MTTR ≈ 1/λ** (since MTTR ≪ 1/λ) > - **T (Proof Test Interval):** The interval (in hours) between manual proof tests that restore the failure detection capability of a channel. > - **DC (Diagnostic Coverage):** The fraction of dangerous failures that are automatically detected by the SIS's self-diagnostics. ## 2.2 Dangerous vs. Safe Failures A failure is classified as: - **Dangerous Failure (λ_D):** Failure that prevents the SIF from performing its safety function. *Example: A sensor that is stuck reading "safe" when the process is actually hazardous.* - **Safe Failure (λ_S):** Failure that causes a false trip but does not prevent the SIF from responding on demand. *Example: A sensor that spontaneously trips the system without a real demand.* Only **dangerous failures** contribute to the PFD calculation. ## 2.3 The Simplification Theorem for Low-Demand Mode > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Theorem (KTU Exam Favorite)** > For **low-demand mode** (T ≪ 1/λ_D), the probability of multiple dangerous failures within one proof test interval is negligibly small. Therefore: > > **Only single-failure scenarios are considered**, which gives the **simplified PFD formula** used in 90% of KTU problems. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table > [!NOTE] **The Complete PFD Formula Sheet (Use this for all exam calculations)** | # | Configuration / Component | PFD_avg Formula | Notes | |:-:|:------------------------|:----------------|:------| | 1 | Single Channel (1oo1) — Untested | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$ | Simplest form | | 2 | Single Channel (1oo1) — Tested | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} + \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2}$ | λ_DU = undetected dangerous failure rate | | 3 | Two Channels (1oo2) — Both must work | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T)^2}{3}$ | Both channels in series | | 4 | Two Channels (2oo2) — Both must fail | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = 2 \cdot \left( \left(1 - \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} \right)$ | Redundancy with voting | | 5 | Three Channels (2oo3) — Majority vote | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = 6 \cdot \left(\left(1-\lambda_D T\right)\left(\dfrac{\lambda_D T}{2}\right)^2\right)$ | Tolerant of one failure | | 6 | Common Cause Failure (β-Factor) | $\lambda_{D,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D$ | Typically β = 0.05 to 0.15 | | 7 | Proof Test Effectiveness Factor (PTC) | $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot (1 - \text{PTC})$ | PTC = 0 for perfect test | | 8 | Total SIF PFD (sum of layers) | $\text{PFD}_{SIF} = \text{PFD}_{Sensor} + \text{PFD}_{Logic} + \text{PFD}_{Final Element}$ | IEC 61508 summation | | 9 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{avg}}$ | Used for SIL determination | | 10 | Mean Unavailability | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T}{2}$ | Average downtime after failure | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility These formulas are not academic — they are the **legal basis** for instrumented protection in oil & gas (API 670/671), chemical (IEC 61511), nuclear (IEC 61513), and process industries worldwide. A properly calculated PFD_avg is required by HSE regulatory bodies like OSHA and the UK HSE (COMAH regulations). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of PFD_avg for a Single Untested Channel (1oo1) We want the **average probability that the channel is unable to respond** to a demand at any random instant within the proof test interval [0, T]. ### Step 1: Define the instantaneous unavailability A single channel is operational at $t = 0$. A dangerous failure occurs with rate $\lambda_D$. The probability that it has **not yet failed** at time $t$ is: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_D \cdot t}$$ ### Step 2: Define the unavailability The unavailability at time $t$ (assuming constant failure rate and no repair) is: $$U(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \cdot t}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the simplification For low-demand mode, $\lambda_D \cdot t \ll 1$. Using Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$: $$U(t) \approx 1 - (1 - \lambda_D \cdot t) = \lambda_D \cdot t$$ ### Step 4: Average over the interval [0, T] The **average** unavailability is the time-integrated unavailability divided by T: $$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$ ### Step 5: Evaluate the integral $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{avg} &= \dfrac{1}{T} \cdot \lambda_D \cdot \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{T} \cdot \lambda_D \cdot \dfrac{T^2}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} \end{aligned}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}}$$ This is the **most fundamental result** in safety instrumentation. **Memorize it.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation of PFD_avg for a 1oo2 Voting System In a **1oo2** (1-out-of-2) system, the SIF fails only if **BOTH** channels fail dangerously. Both channels must be operational for the system to respond. ### Step 1: Unavailability of channel i at time t As derived above: $U_i(t) = \lambda_D \cdot t$ (low-demand simplification). ### Step 2: Probability both are failed For independent failures, the probability that *both* channels are failed is the product of individual unavailabilities: $$U_{1oo2}(t) = U_1(t) \cdot U_2(t) = (\lambda_D \cdot t)^2$$ ### Step 3: Average over the interval $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} &= \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_D \cdot t)^2 \, dt \\ &= \dfrac{\lambda_D^2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} t^2 \, dt \\ &= \dfrac{\lambda_D^2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{T^3}{3} \end{aligned}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T)^2}{3}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) > [!IMPORTANT] **Sample Problem:** A single-channel pressure transmitter has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr. The proof test interval is $T = 8760$ hours (1 year). The diagnostic coverage is DC = 60%. Calculate the PFD_avg and identify the SIL achieved. ### Step 1: Calculate the undetected dangerous failure rate $$\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) = 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - 0.6) = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the 1oo1 tested formula $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{avg} &= \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} + \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(8760)}{2} + \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(8760)}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{0.0438}{2} + \dfrac{0.01752}{2} \\ &= 0.0219 + 0.00876 \\ &= 0.03066 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Compare with SIL table $$\text{PFD}_{avg} \approx 3.07 \times 10^{-2}$$ This falls in the range **10⁻² to 10⁻¹**, hence the system achieves **SIL 1**. ### Step 4: Risk Reduction Factor $$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{0.03066} \approx 32.6$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PFD for All Standard Voting Configurations > [!NOTE] **Master Comparison Table (Memorize the orders of magnitude)** | Voting | System Fails When... | PFD_avg (simplified) | SIL Achievable (typical T=1yr) | Cost / Complexity | |:------:|:--------------------|:---------------------|:------------------------------:|:-----------------:| | **1oo1** | Channel A fails | $\dfrac{\lambda_D T}{2}$ | SIL 1 (barely) | Lowest | | **1oo2** | Both A and B fail | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$ | SIL 2 to 3 | Medium | | **2oo2** | At least one fails (but only one working is insufficient) | $\approx \lambda_D T$ | SIL 1 | Medium | | **2oo3** | Any 2 of 3 fail | $6 \left(1 - \lambda_D T\right) \left(\dfrac{\lambda_D T}{2}\right)^2$ | SIL 3 | High | | **1oo3** | All 3 fail | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T)^3}{4}$ | SIL 4 (rare) | Very High | The **1oo2** and **2oo3** are the most commonly used configurations in oil & gas and chemical process industries. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional layers of a SIF where PFD is computed by summing each layer's contribution: ```mermaid flowchart LR Hazard[Hazardous Process Event] --> Sensor[Sensor Layer
PFD_Sensor] Sensor --> Logic[Logic Solver Layer
PFD_Logic] Logic --> Final[Final Element Layer
PFD_FinalElement] Final --> SafeState[Safe State Achieved] Sensor -.->|λ_D, T, DC| Meta1[Unavailability Calculation] Logic -.->|λ_D, T, DC| Meta1 Final -.->|λ_D, T, DC| Meta1 Meta1 --> PFDTotal[PFD_SIF = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_FE] PFDTotal --> SILCheck{SIL Achieved?} SILCheck -->|Yes| Continue[System Acceptable] SILCheck -->|No| Redesign[Add Redundancy / Shorten T] ``` ## 4.2 PFD Calculation Workflow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Define SIF Boundary]) --> A1[Step 1: Collect Failure Data
λ_D, λ_S, λ_DU per component] A1 --> A2[Step 2: Determine Proof Test Interval T] A2 --> A3[Step 3: Identify Voting Architecture
1oo1 / 1oo2 / 2oo3 etc.] A3 --> A4[Step 4: Apply CCF Beta Factor
λ_D_CCF = β × λ_D] A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute PFD_avg per layer] A5 --> A6[Step 6: Sum across SIF
PFD_SIF = PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_FE] A6 --> A7[Step 7: Compute RRF = 1 / PFD_SIF] A7 --> A8[Step 8: Compare with Target SIL] A8 --> Decision{PFD_SIF meets target SIL?} Decision -->|Yes| End1([Design Approved]) Decision -->|No| A9[Iterate: Increase Redundancy
or Reduce Proof Test Interval T] A9 --> A3 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: 2oo3 Voting Logic (Fault Tolerance View) ```mermaid flowchart LR SensorA[Sensor Channel A] --> Voter[Voting Logic 2oo3] SensorB[Sensor Channel B] --> Voter SensorC[Sensor Channel C] --> Voter Voter --> Shutdown[Shutdown Command
to Final Element] Voter -.->|If 2 or more agree| Trip[Trip Action Initiated] Voter -.->|If only 1 demands trip| Bypass[Voted Out: Continue Operation] ``` This block-level architecture clearly shows how a **2oo3** system can tolerate **one** faulty sensor without causing a spurious trip, while still maintaining the ability to actuate on a **real** hazardous demand as long as any two out of three sensors agree. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD). How is it related to Safety Integrity Level (SIL)?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** > **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** is the average probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) will fail to perform its intended safety action when a demand is placed on it. It is mathematically defined as the time-averaged unavailability of the safety function within its proof test interval. > The relationship between PFD and SIL is **inverse logarithmic**: > - A **lower PFD** corresponds to a **higher SIL**. > - SIL 1 corresponds to PFD_avg between **10⁻² and 10⁻¹**. > - SIL 2 corresponds to PFD_avg between **10⁻³ and 10⁻²**. > - SIL 3 corresponds to PFD_avg between **10⁻⁴ and 10⁻³**. > - SIL 4 corresponds to PFD_avg between **10⁻⁵ and 10⁻⁴**. > The Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) is mathematically the reciprocal of PFD_avg: **RRF = 1 / PFD_avg**. --- ### **Q2. What is a proof test, and how does the proof test interval (T) affect PFD_avg?** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** > A **proof test** is a periodic manual (or automatic) inspection performed to detect *undetected dangerous failures* in a safety instrumented channel that the system's automatic diagnostics cannot reveal. After the proof test, the channel's failure detection capability is restored (assuming a perfect test, PTC = 1). > The PFD_avg is **directly proportional to the proof test interval T**. The relationship for a 1oo1 system is: > $$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$ > > Therefore, **halving T** (more frequent testing) **halves the PFD_avg**, which moves the SIF into a higher SIL band. This is one of the most cost-effective methods to improve SIL when redundancy is expensive. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning** > A common mistake is forgetting to apply the **β-factor for Common Cause Failures (CCF)**. In redundant systems, ignoring CCF will give an *optimistic* PFD that is **artificially low by an order of magnitude**, leading to incorrect SIL classification. Always include β in any 1oo2 or 2oo3 calculation. The typical β value for sensors is **0.05 to 0.15**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` A high-integrity pressure protection SIF consists of three layers: 1. **Sensor:** 1oo2 redundant pressure transmitter, $\lambda_D = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr, β = 0.08. 2. **Logic Solver:** 1oo1 PLC, $\lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr. 3. **Final Element:** 1oo2 redundant solenoid-operated shutdown valve, $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr, β = 0.10. The proof test interval **T = 8760 hours** (1 year). Diagnostic coverage DC = 90% for all components. #### **Part (a)** — Compute the PFD_avg for the Sensor Layer (1oo2). **(7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Apply the 1oo2 simplified formula for the independent part: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T)^2}{3} = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760)^2}{3}$$ Calculate $\lambda_D \cdot T$: $$\lambda_D \cdot T = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.03504$$ Square and divide by 3: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{(0.03504)^2}{3} = \dfrac{0.001228}{3} \approx 4.09 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the Common Cause Failure contribution using the β-factor: $$\lambda_{DU,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC})$$ $$\lambda_{DU,\text{CCF}} = 0.08 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.1 = 3.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /hr}$$ Apply 1oo1 formula for the CCF term: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,\text{CCF}} \cdot T}{2} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 8760}{2} \approx 1.40 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3:** Total Sensor PFD: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} = 4.09 \times 10^{-4} + 1.40 \times 10^{-4} \approx 5.49 \times 10^{-4}$$ #### **Part (b)** — Compute the total SIF PFD_avg, identify the achieved SIL, and comment on whether it meets a target SIL 2 requirement (PFD ≤ 10⁻²). **(7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Compute Logic Solver PFD (1oo1, with DC): $$\text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2:** Compute Final Element PFD (same method as sensor): $$\lambda_D \cdot T = 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760 = 0.02628$$ $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{(0.02628)^2}{3} \approx 2.30 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.1 \cdot 8760}{2} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{FinalElement}} = 2.30 \times 10^{-4} + 1.31 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.61 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3:** Sum all layers (series summation): $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 5.49 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 3.61 \times 10^{-4} \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with target: - Target PFD for SIL 2: **≤ 10⁻²** - Calculated PFD_SIF: **5.29 × 10⁻³ < 10⁻²** ✓ **Conclusion:** The SIF achieves approximately **5.29 × 10⁻³**, which corresponds to **SIL 2**, and **meets the target requirement**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` (Internal Choice) A 1oo1 safety instrumented system uses a single temperature transmitter with the following data: - $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr - Diagnostic Coverage DC = 80% - Common Cause Factor β = 0.05 - Proof test interval T = 4380 hours (6 months) #### **Part (a)** — Compute PFD_avg and the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). Identify the SIL. **(7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Calculate undetected failure rate: $$\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) = 2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.2 = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$$ **Step 2:** Apply 1oo1 formula (with CCF for a single channel, β acts on λ_D directly): $$\lambda_{DU,\text{eff}} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot \beta = 4 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.05 = 2 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4380}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 4380}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{Total}} \approx 4.42 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3:** Compute RRF: $$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{4.42 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 226$$ **Step 4:** Identify SIL: PFD = 4.42 × 10⁻³ falls in the range **10⁻³ to 10⁻²** → **SIL 2**. #### **Part (b)** — If the same system is reconfigured as 1oo2 (with identical channels, β = 0.08), what is the new PFD_avg? Comment on the improvement. **(7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Compute $\lambda_D \cdot T$: $$\lambda_D \cdot T = 2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4380 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2:** 1oo2 independent part: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{(8.76 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \dfrac{7.67 \times 10^{-5}}{3} \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 3:** CCF contribution: $$\lambda_{DU,\text{CCF}} = 0.08 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 4380}{2} \approx 7.01 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4:** Total: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{Total}} \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-5} + 7.01 \times 10^{-5} = 9.57 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Conclusion:** Going from 1oo1 (PFD = 4.42 × 10⁻³) to 1oo2 (PFD ≈ 9.57 × 10⁻⁵) **improves reliability by a factor of ~46**. The new PFD falls in the **SIL 3** range (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ — close to boundary). --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] **Final High-Density Summary** > > ✅ **PFD_avg** is the **average unavailability** of a safety function over its proof test interval — it is *not* an instantaneous probability. > > ✅ **SIL classification** is purely based on PFD_avg bands: SIL 1 (10⁻¹), SIL 2 (10⁻²), SIL 3 (10⁻³), SIL 4 (10⁻⁴). > > ✅ **Low-demand simplification** is valid only when **λ_D · T ≪ 1**; otherwise use the full exponential form. > > ✅ **1oo1 formula (untested):** $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D T}{2}$ — the most basic result. > > ✅ **1oo2 formula:** $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$ — dramatically better due to squaring. > > ✅ **2oo3 formula:** $\text{PFD}_{avg} = 6(1-\lambda_D T)(\lambda_D T/2)^2$ — tolerant of one faulty channel. > > ✅ **Always include CCF** (β-factor) in redundant systems. Typical β = 0.05 (sensors) to 0.15 (valves). > > ✅ **Total SIF PFD** is the **sum** of PFDs across sensor, logic solver, and final element (series arrangement). > > ✅ **Proof Test Interval T** is the **knob** you can turn to improve SIL without adding hardware — shorter T = lower PFD. > > ✅ **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** reduces the *undetected* dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D (1 - \text{DC})$. > > ✅ **RPF/RRF = 1/PFD_avg** — use this to compare with target risk reduction in LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) studies. > > ✅ **FIT units:** 1 FIT = 10⁻⁹ failures/hour. Always convert to per-hour values before plugging into formulas. > > ✅ **Common student pitfall:** Confusing *safe failures* with *dangerous failures* — only **dangerous undetected** failures (λ_DU) enter the PFD calculation. Safe failures affect spurious trip rate, not PFD.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Testing, Safety Requirements Specifications

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)

The **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal, written document that specifies all of the requirements for the Safety Instrumented System (SIS). As per **IEC 61511 (Functional Safety – Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector)**, Clause 10.2, the SRS is a mandatory deliverable of the SIS design phase and is the single source of truth against which the entire SIS is verified and validated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SRS is the bridge between the **Safety Lifecycle** (Process Hazard Analysis, Risk Assessment, SIL determination) and the physical engineering design. Without a written SRS, an SIS cannot be considered compliant with IEC 61511/61508.

### Formal Definition
> **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS):** A specification document containing all the requirements for the Safety Instrumented System, derived from the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) target and the Safety Function Requirements (SFR) identified during the safety lifecycle. It specifies the required **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)**, their **SIL targets**, **dangerous failure rates**, **proof test intervals**, **response times**, and **operational modes** (IEC 61511-1, Clause 3.2.86).

## 1.2 Safety Testing

**Safety Testing** is the collection of systematic activities performed to demonstrate that the SIS conforms to the SRS. It is broadly classified into:

- **Verification** – "Are we building the system right?" (Does it meet the specified design?)
- **Validation** – "Are we building the right system?" (Does it meet the actual user/process safety need?)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Bridge Analogy):**
> Imagine you are building a bridge.
> - The **SRS** is the engineer's blueprint: load capacity = 50 tons, deflection < 5 cm, materials = grade A steel.
> - **Verification** is the in-house quality check: "We used grade A steel, welds passed X-ray, dimensions match the blueprint." (Static, document-driven).
> - **Validation** is the load test: "We drove 50 trucks across — the bridge held." (Dynamic, performance-driven).
> - **Site Acceptance Testing (SAT)** is the final inspection after the bridge is delivered to its location — checking for transport damage and re-confirming function.
> - **Proof Testing** is the periodic re-check every 12 months to ensure the bridge still holds its design load.

## 1.3 Relationship Between SRS and Safety Testing

| Aspect | SRS Drives... | Safety Testing Confirms... |
|---|---|---|
| Logic | Logic solver programming | Logic solver performs as written |
| Sensors | Type, range, redundancy | Sensors detect hazard within response time |
| Final Elements | Valve type, stroking time | Valves close within the required time |
| SIL | Target SIL 1/2/3 | Achieved PFDavg meets target |
| Proof Test | Test interval & method | Proof test reveals > 90% of dangerous failures |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle Position of SRS and Testing

The **Safety Lifecycle** (per IEC 61511-1, Figure 2) is a structured sequence of activities. SRS and Testing occupy specific phases:

1. **Phase 5 (Overall Scope Definition)** → identifies process hazards.
2. **Phase 6 (Hazard & Risk Analysis)** → derives SIL target.
3. **Phase 7 (Safety Requirements Specification)** → writes the SRS. **[KTU HIGH-YIELD]**
4. **Phase 8 (SIS Design & Engineering)** → detailed engineering.
5. **Phase 9 (SIS Installation, Commissioning & Validation)** → field testing.
6. **Phase 12 (SIS Operation)** → periodic proof testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the SRS so critical?**
> It is the **only** document that is referenced by *every subsequent phase*. It is the contract between the safety engineer, the instrumentation designer, the installation contractor, the operations team, and the auditor. A weak SRS propagates errors into installation, operation, and ultimately increases the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)**.

## 2.2 Contents of the SRS (IEC 61511-1, Clause 10.2.3)

A complete SRS must contain the following sections:

1. **Description of the Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)**
   - Identification of the SIF
   - Process variable(s) being monitored
   - Source of process upset / hazard
   - Consequence of failure (the "demand")
2. **Required SIL** (1, 2, or 3) for each SIF
3. **Safe State** definition for each SIF (e.g., close valve, stop pump, vent to flare)
4. **Operating Modes** (continuous mode of operation, high-demand mode, low-demand mode)
5. **Demand Rate** assumptions
6. **Process Operating Limits** (maximum/minimum, including start-up, shutdown, normal, maintenance)
7. **Process Interface Requirements** (sensor ranges, final element type, materials)
8. **Required Response Time** for each SIF
9. **Required PFDavg** and corresponding failure rate targets
10. **Proof Test Requirements** (interval, method, coverage, required resources)
11. **Environmental Conditions** (temperature, humidity, corrosive atmospheres, hazardous area classification)
12. **Manual Shutdown Requirements**
13. **Interfaces with Other Systems** (DCS, fire & gas, ESD, BMS)
14. **Application Logic** (cause-and-effect matrix)
15. **Operator Interfaces / HMI requirements**
16. **Override / Bypass / Maintenance Bypass policy**
17. **Emergency Power / Power Supply Requirements**

## 2.3 Verification vs Validation (V&V)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite — Verify vs Validate:**
> - **Verification** = Evaluation of the SIS design against the SRS. Performed at design stage and after every design change. Uses inspection, review, simulation, calculation.
> - **Validation** = Evaluation of the installed SIS against the actual process safety needs. Performed at the end of commissioning. Uses real demand simulation, end-to-end trip test.

## 2.4 Levels of Safety Testing

| Test Level | Performed At | Purpose | Typical Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FAT (Factory Acceptance Test)** | Vendor/System Integrator shop | Verify system per SRS before shipment | 1–4 weeks |
| **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** | Site, before commissioning | Confirm no transport damage, correct installation | 3–10 days |
| **Site Integration Test (SIT)** | Site, with process interface | Confirm wiring, signal levels, valve stroking | 1–2 weeks |
| **Commissioning / Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Site, with process live | Validate as-installed against actual process safety needs | 1–2 weeks |
| **Proof Test** | Site, periodic | Reveal undetected dangerous failures during operation | Hours per SIF |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — LaTeX Markdown Note:** For absolute value or conditional notation inside the table below, the vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe-syntax. LaTeX still renders the absolute value symbol correctly.

| Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Description | Standard Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Probability of Failure on Demand** | $PFD_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} PFD_{i}$ | Sum of dangerous failure probabilities of all SIF subsystems | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| **SIL Target vs PFDavg (Low Demand)** | $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-(\text{SIL}+1)}$ to $10^{-\text{SIL}}$ | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | dimensionless |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Inverse of PFDavg; how much risk is reduced | 10 to 100,000 |
| **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (Proof Test Effect)** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | For 1oo1 architecture with full proof test coverage | dimensionless |
| **Proof Test Interval** | $T_{proof}$ | Time between proof tests | 6, 12, 24, 36 months |
| **Dangerous Failure Rate** | $\lambda_D$ | Failures per hour that prevent safety action | per hour |
| **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Fraction of failures that are safe or detected dangerous | 60% to 99% |
| **Hardware Fault Tolerance** | $HFT = N - (N-1)$ for 1ooN | Sensors or final elements required to perform the function | 0, 1, or 2 |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR$ | Time from detection to restoration of failed SIF | 8 to 24 hours |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Reliability metric for hardware | hours |
| **Diagnostic Coverage** | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Fraction of dangerous failures automatically detected | 0% to 100% |
| **Equivalent Average Unavailability** | $t_{CE} = PFD_{avg} \cdot 8760 \text{ hr/yr}$ | Convert PFDavg to hours of unavailability per year | hours/year |

## 2.6 Test Coverage Concept

**Test Coverage (C)** is the fraction of dangerous failures revealed by the proof test. If $C = 1.0$ (100%), the proof test detects all dangerous failures and PFDavg uses $\lambda_D/2$ approximation. If $C < 1$, an additional un-revealed failure term exists:

$$PFD_{avg} = C \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2} + (1-C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

The **uncovered dangerous failure contribution** is:

$$PFD_{uncovered} = (1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical manual proof test achieves only **60% to 80%** coverage because some internal sensor or valve failures are not stimulated by the test. Hence, IEC 61511 highly recommends **automatic diagnostics** to push effective coverage above 90%.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo1 Sensor with Imperfect Proof Test

**Starting assumptions** (typical KTU 2024 exam scenario):
- Sensor with constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$
- Proof test interval $T_{proof}$
- Test coverage $C$ (fraction of dangerous failures that the test reveals)
- All revealed failures are repaired (MTTR is negligible compared to $T_{proof}$)

The **unavailability function** $U(t)$ over a single proof test cycle grows linearly for *unrevealed* failures and resets to zero at the proof test instant for *revealed* failures.

The average unavailability over one cycle is the area under $U(t)$ divided by $T_{proof}$.

**Step 1 — Contribution of the revealed (covered) dangerous failures:**

Failures occur uniformly in the interval $[0, T_{proof}]$. The failures revealed at the end of the cycle (fraction $C$) have an average age of $T_{proof}/2$, and the failures revealed at the *start* of the cycle (already repaired) have an age of zero. The mean age of *all* revealed dangerous failures that the test catches is $T_{proof}/2$.

Therefore, the average unavailability due to covered failures is:

$$PFD_{covered} = C \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Contribution of the unrevealed (uncovered) dangerous failures:**

These failures (fraction $(1-C)$) remain undetected throughout the entire proof test interval. The mean downtime for these failures is essentially the full interval $T_{proof}$:

$$PFD_{uncovered} = (1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two contributions:**

$$PFD_{avg} = C \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2} + (1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the expression algebraically:**

Factor $\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$ out of both terms:

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{C}{2} + (1 - C) \right]$$

Combine the bracket terms (put over common denominator 2):

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{C + 2(1 - C)}{2} \right]$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{C + 2 - 2C}{2} \right] = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2 - C}{2} \right]$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{C}{2} \right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit Check (Sanity Verification):**
> - If $C = 1$ (perfect test) → $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D T_{proof}/2$. ✓
> - If $C = 0$ (no test) → $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D T_{proof}$. ✓
> The result is monotonically decreasing in $C$, as expected.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Proof Test Interval from SIL Target

**Given:** $\lambda_D$ and desired SIL target (which fixes a maximum $PFD_{target}$).

For a 1oo1 sensor with $C = 1$, the **maximum permissible proof test interval** is:

$$T_{proof, \max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot PFD_{target}}{\lambda_D}$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Board Practice:**

A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour. The SIF must achieve SIL 2, so the maximum $PFD_{avg}$ per sensor is $1 \times 10^{-2}$.

Apply the formula:

$$T_{proof, \max} = \dfrac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$T_{proof, \max} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{2}{5} \times 10^{5} = 0.4 \times 10^{5} = 4 \times 10^{4} \text{ hours}$$

Convert to years (using 8760 hours per year):

$$T_{proof, \max} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{4}}{8760} \approx 4.57 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering rule:** Always round *down* and apply an additional engineering safety factor (typically 2 to 3) to account for test coverage loss, MTTR, and operational delays. Hence, a 4.57-year theoretical maximum becomes a **2-year scheduled proof test interval** in practice.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Typical Proof Test Methods and Achieved Coverage

| Subsystem | Typical Proof Test Method | Achieved Coverage $C$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Transmitter** | Calibrated against a dead-weight tester; trip setpoint is ramp-tested | 0.60 – 0.85 | Internal electronics failures may not be stimulated |
| **Temperature Sensor (RTD/TC)** | Loop check with calibrated source; trip at simulated high temp | 0.50 – 0.75 | Drift, open-element hard to detect manually |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Energize solenoid; confirm disc moves; check seat leakage | 0.70 – 0.90 | Internal seal wear may not be detected |
| **Pneumatic Actuator** | Apply instrument air; observe stem position indicator | 0.60 – 0.80 | Spring fatigue hard to detect |
| **Logic Solver (PLC/SIS)** | Test trip logic with simulated inputs; verify outputs | 0.95 – 0.99 | Self-diagnostics are very effective |
| **Smart Transmitter w/ diagnostics** | Diagnostic flags + partial stroke test of final element | 0.90 – 0.99 | Online testing extends interval dramatically |

## 3.4 Worked Example: SIL Verification of a 2oo3 Voting Sensor Group

A SIF uses a **2oo3 (2 out of 3) voting** of level transmitters. Each transmitter has $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $\lambda_{DD} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr (1 year), $C = 0.9$, and $MTTR = 8$ hr.

**Step 1 — PFDavg for a single 1oo1 transmitter:**

$$PFD_{1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{0.9}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times 0.55$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.55 = 1.4454 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — PFDavg for 2oo3 architecture (IEC 61508-6 Tables B.5):**

For 2oo3:

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \cdot (PFD_{1oo1})^2$$

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \times (1.4454 \times 10^{-2})^2$$

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 6 \times 2.0892 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 1.2535 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with SIL 2 target:**

SIL 2 requires $PFD_{avg} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$.

The value $1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ is **at the lower (more conservative) boundary** of SIL 2. ✓ **The SIF meets SIL 2.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** 2oo3 voting is used when you need both high availability (no spurious trips) AND high safety integrity. The squared PFD dependence is a powerful advantage — a 100× reduction in PFDavg compared to 1oo1.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: SRS Development Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Definition] --> B[PHA / HAZOP Study]
    B --> C[Risk Assessment LOPA]
    C --> D[SIL Target Allocation]
    D --> E[Safety Requirements Specification SRS]
    E --> F[SIS Detailed Design]
    F --> G[Factory Acceptance Test FAT]
    G --> H[Site Acceptance Test SAT]
    H --> I[Site Integration Test SIT]
    I --> J[Pre-Startup Safety Review PSSR Validation]
    J --> K[SIS in Operation]
    K --> L[Periodic Proof Test]
    L --> K
    E -.is verified against.-> G
    E -.is verified against.-> H
    E -.is verified against.-> I
    E -.is validated against process need.-> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every test level (FAT, SAT, SIT) traces its origin back to the SRS — the SRS is the single source of truth. The PSSR is the only **validation** activity; the others are **verification** activities.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: SIF Subsystems Under Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM (1oo1 / 1oo2 / 2oo3)"]

        S3[Field Transmitter PT 3]

        F2[Shutdown Valve XV]
        F3[Vent Valve]
    end
    subgraph PROOF["PROOF TEST BOUNDARY"]

        T2[Calibration Standard]
    end
    S1 --> L1

    L1 --> F1
    F1 --> F2

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Has the SIS been installed?}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS). List any four essential contents of an SRS as per IEC 61511.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a written document that specifies all of the requirements for the Safety Instrumented System (SIS), derived from the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) target and the Safety Function Requirements identified during the safety lifecycle (IEC 61511-1, Clause 3.2.86).

Four essential contents:
1. **Description of each SIF** – including the process variable monitored, the safe state, and the consequence of failure.
2. **Required SIL** for each SIF.
3. **Required PFDavg** and dangerous failure rate targets.
4. **Proof test requirements** – interval, method, and required coverage.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for any four correct contents)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between verification and validation of an SIS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Verification | Validation |
|---|---|
| Confirms the SIS design matches the SRS. | Confirms the installed SIS meets the actual process safety need. |
| "Are we building the system right?" | "Are we building the right system?" |
| Performed at design stage, FAT, SAT, SIT. | Performed at the end of commissioning (PSSR). |
| Uses inspection, calculation, review, simulation. | Uses real demand simulation, end-to-end trip test. |
| Document-driven (compared against SRS). | Performance-driven (compared against process hazards). |

*(2 marks for two clear differentiating points + 1 mark for any one supporting example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the complete content of a **Safety Requirements Specification** as per IEC 61511. Why is the SRS considered the most important document in the SIS lifecycle?

**(b) [7 marks]** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) uses a single pressure transmitter (1oo1) with $\lambda_D = 4 \times 10^{-7}$/hr. The SIF is required to achieve **SIL 2** with a proof test interval of 1 year (8760 hours) and a test coverage of 0.85. Verify whether the SIF meets the SIL 2 target. If not, suggest a remedy.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — SRS Contents [7 marks]:**

The SRS, per IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.2.3, contains:

1. **Description of each SIF** (Identification, process variable, safe state, consequence of failure) – *0.5 mark*
2. **Required SIL** for each SIF – *0.5 mark*
3. **Required safe state** for each SIF – *0.5 mark*
4. **Operating modes** (continuous, high-demand, low-demand) – *0.5 mark*
5. **Demand rate** assumptions and process environment – *0.5 mark*
6. **Process interface requirements** (sensor type, range, valve type) – *0.5 mark*
7. **Required response time** for each SIF – *0.5 mark*
8. **Required PFDavg** and corresponding failure rates – *0.5 mark*
9. **Proof test requirements** (interval, method, coverage) – *0.5 mark*
10. **Manual shutdown requirements** – *0.5 mark*
11. **Environmental conditions and hazardous area classification** – *0.5 mark*
12. **Interfaces with other systems** (DCS, ESD, fire & gas) – *0.5 mark*
13. **Application logic and cause-and-effect** – *0.5 mark*
14. **Bypass / Override / Maintenance bypass policy** – *0.5 mark*

**Why SRS is the most important document [1 mark]:**
The SRS is referenced by *every subsequent lifecycle phase*. It serves as the contract among safety engineers, designers, installers, operators, and auditors. Any ambiguity or error in the SRS propagates through design, installation, operation, and testing — directly impacting the achieved SIL and the residual process risk.

---

**Part (b) — SIL Verification [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify the SIL 2 target range [1 mark]:**
SIL 2 requires $10^{-3} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$.

**Step 2 — Compute PFDavg for 1oo1 architecture with imperfect test coverage [3 marks]:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{0.85}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 \times (1 - 0.425)$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 \times 0.575$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 3.504 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.575 = 2.0148 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with SIL 2 [1 mark]:**
$2.01 \times 10^{-3}$ lies within the SIL 2 range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$.
**∴ The SIF meets SIL 2.** ✓

**Step 4 — Suggest a remedy for improvement [2 marks]:**
Although the SIF meets SIL 2, it is at the *upper* (less conservative) end of the range. Engineering recommendations:
- Add **diagnostic coverage** to the transmitter (e.g., use a smart transmitter with self-diagnostics) → increases $C$ to ~0.95 → reduces PFDavg.
- Add **redundancy** (1oo2 voting) → $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(PFD_{1oo1})^2}{2}$ — quadratic reduction.
- Reduce **proof test interval** to 6 months → halves $PFD_{avg}$.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **different levels of safety testing** (FAT, SAT, SIT, PSSR, Proof Test). For each level, state the location, purpose, and the activity performed.

**(b) [7 marks]** A SIF uses a 1oo2 redundant pair of level switches, each with $\lambda_D = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr, and $C = 0.95$. The dangerous detected failure rate is $\lambda_{DD} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, and $MTTR = 8$ hr. Calculate the achieved $PFD_{avg}$ and verify the SIL.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Levels of Safety Testing [7 marks]:**

1. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** – *1 mark*
   - **Location:** Vendor/system integrator workshop.
   - **Purpose:** Verify the complete SIS panel against the SRS *before* shipment.
   - **Activities:** Loop checks of internal wiring, simulation of all SIFs on the logic solver, HMI navigation check, alarm/annunciation check, ESD button test, documentation review.

2. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** – *1 mark*
   - **Location:** Customer site, before connecting to field.
   - **Purpose:** Confirm no transit damage, all items received per packing list, and FAT results reproduced on site.
   - **Activities:** Visual inspection, panel mounting check, power-up checks, re-run of FAT test sheets with site engineer.

3. **Site Integration Test (SIT)** – *1 mark*
   - **Location:** Site, with field devices wired to the system.
   - **Purpose:** Confirm signal levels, polarity, and valve stroking under simulated process conditions.
   - **Activities:** End-to-end loop test with calibrated instruments, valve stroking time measurement, simulation of sensor failures (open / shorted), verification of cause-and-effect.

4. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) / Validation** – *1 mark*
   - **Location:** Site, immediately before process startup.
   - **Purpose:** **Validate** the installed SIS against the actual process safety need (real demand simulation).
   - **Activities:** Trip test of every SIF under actual process conditions, bypass procedure test, emergency shutdown simulation.

5. **Proof Test** – *1 mark*
   - **Location:** Site, during normal operation, at scheduled intervals.
   - **Purpose:** Reveal undetected dangerous failures accumulated since the last test.
   - **Activities:** Sensor calibration check, valve stroke, logic test, leak test, documentation of as-found / as-left values.

6. **Neat sketch [2 marks]:** (FAT → shipment → SAT → SIT → PSSR → Operation → Proof Test cycle)

---

**Part (b) — SIL Verification for 1oo2 [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute PFDavg for 1oo1 single switch [2 marks]:**

$$PFD_{1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{0.95}{2}\right)$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times 0.525$$

$$PFD_{1oo1} = 1.314 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.525 = 6.8985 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply 1oo2 architecture formula [2 marks]:**

For 1oo2 (IEC 61508-6):

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(PFD_{1oo1})^2}{2}$$

$$PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(6.8985 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2} = \dfrac{4.759 \times 10^{-5}}{2}$$

$$PFD_{1oo2} = 2.3795 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with SIL thresholds [1 mark]:**
- SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$
- SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
- SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$

$2.38 \times 10^{-5}$ lies within the **SIL 4** range $[10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$.

**∴ The SIF achieves SIL 4.** ✓

**Step 4 — Comment on architecture [2 marks]:**
1oo2 voting forces both sensors to fail before the safety action is prevented, which is the *most* safety-favorable configuration. The quadratic reduction $\left(PFD^2\right)$ in 1oo2 makes SIL 4 achievable even with relatively high single-component failure rates — a powerful design lesson emphasized in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PFD with PFH.** PFDavg is for **low-demand** mode ($\le 1$ demand/year); PFH is for **high-demand / continuous** mode ($> 1$ demand/year). Marks deducted for the wrong formula.
> 2. **Ignoring test coverage $C$.** Many students write $PFD = \lambda_D T/2$ without acknowledging that $C < 1$ in real life. Examiners award 0 for the coverage component.
> 3. **Wrong architecture formula.** 1oo2 ≠ 2oo2 ≠ 1oo3 ≠ 2oo3. Use the correct table (IEC 61508-6, Tables B.1–B.10). Marks deducted for swapped numerator/denominator.
> 4. **Vague SRS descriptions.** Do not write "the SRS contains the requirements." List the *specific* requirements: SIL, PFDavg, response time, proof test, safe state, etc.
> 5. **Forgetting units.** $\lambda_D$ is in **per hour** — $T_{proof}$ must be in **hours** — answer $PFD_{avg}$ is dimensionless.
> 6. **Skipping PSSR.** In the "levels of testing" question, students often forget the **Pre-Startup Safety Review** — this is the *only* validation step and examiners expect it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Safety Testing and SRS:**

- ✅ **SRS is the master document** — every test (FAT, SAT, SIT) verifies against the SRS; PSSR validates against the actual process need.
- ✅ **SRS must specify**: SIL, PFDavg, response time, safe state, proof test interval/method, environmental conditions, bypass policy, interfaces, operating modes.
- ✅ **Verification = SRS → SIS** (design, engineering, installation).
- ✅ **Validation = SRS → Process Safety Need** (PSSR, real demand simulation).
- ✅ **Five test levels**: FAT (vendor), SAT (site, no field), SIT (site, with field), PSSR (with process), Proof Test (periodic).
- ✅ **PFDavg for 1oo1 with imperfect test coverage**: $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D T_{proof} (1 - C/2)$.
- ✅ **Perfect test (C=1)**: $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D T_{proof}/2$.
- ✅ **SIL 1** → $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$; **SIL 2** → $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$; **SIL 3** → $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$; **SIL 4** → $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$.
- ✅ **1oo2 redundancy** gives a **quadratic** reduction: $PFD_{1oo2} = PFD^2/2$.
- ✅ **Proof test coverage** in practice: sensors 60–80%, valves 70–90%, smart transmitters with diagnostics 90–99%, logic solvers 95–99%.
- ✅ **Test coverage $C$ directly multiplies safety** — increasing $C$ by 10% can sometimes be more effective than halving $T_{proof}$.
- ✅ **PSSR is the only validation step** — never confuse it with SIT.
- ✅ **MTTR** is typically 8 hours; use this when $T_{proof}/2$ approximation is invalid.
- ✅ **Diagnostic coverage (DC)** separates dangerous failures into $\lambda_{DD}$ (detected) and $\lambda_{DU}$ (undetected).
- ✅ **SRS is a living document** — every SIS modification requires an SRS revision and re-verification.
- ✅ **IEC 61511-1, Clause 10.2.3** lists 16 minimum contents of the SRS — memorize at least 10.
- ✅ **Proof test frequency** is driven by $\lambda_D$ and the target $PFD_{avg}$; never chosen arbitrarily.
- ✅ **Functional Safety Audit** (per IEC 61511-1, Clause 5.2.6) confirms that the as-installed SIS matches the SRS.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Design and Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detailed Design and Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Design and Specifications** in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is the engineering phase that translates the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** into a complete, verifiable, and implementation-ready engineering blueprint. It defines the exact hardware architecture, software modules, failure-mode behaviour, environmental envelopes, redundancy strategies, and proof-test schedules that guarantee the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** mandated for every Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

The detailed design must conform to the requirements laid down in **IEC 61511 (Functional Safety – Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector)** and the Indian adoption **IS/IEC 61511**. The output of this phase is the **Detailed Design Document (DDD)** which feeds the procurement, fabrication, installation, and commissioning activities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **aeronautical engineer** who has been told: *"The plane must not stall below 1500 ft at approach speed."* That single statement is the *SRS*. The **Detailed Design** is the entire engineering notebook that follows — selecting angle-of-attack sensors (triplex, dissimilar redundancy), wiring harnesses with specific shielding, the flight-control computer algorithm, hydraulic actuator sizing, the 600-hour inspection interval, and the failure-isolation logic. Every nut, bolt, and line of code is justified against that single safety goal.

In the same way, **Detailed Design and Specifications** for a SIS answers, for every SIF:

| Question the SRS Asks | What Detailed Design Answers |
|---|---|
| What SIL? | Which sensors, which logic solver, which valve? |
| What PFDavg target? | What proof-test interval (T_proof)? |
| What environment? | What IP/NEMA rating, EMC class, temperature class? |
| What about common-mode failure? | What diversity, segregation, and HFT? |

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics

The following **bold** terms must be internalised by every KTU student because they appear verbatim in board questions:

- **PFDavg** — Average Probability of Failure on Demand (dimensionless, dimensionless)
- **SFF** — Safe Failure Fraction (%, dimensionless)
- **HFT** — Hardware Fault Tolerance (integer ≥ 0)
- **T_proof** — Proof Test Interval (typically **1 year**, **2 years**, or **5 years**)
- **λ_D, λ_S** — Dangerous and Safe Failure Rates (per hour, **FIT = 10⁻⁹ /hr**)
- **DC** — Diagnostic Coverage (%)
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair (hours)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"what is the SIL achieved?"*, the answer must reference **both** the PFDavg value **and** the architectural constraints table from IEC 61511-1. A SIL claim without architectural proof is treated as an incomplete answer and attracts **2-mark penalty**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Flow from SRS to Hardware

The detailed design of a SIF follows a rigorous **top-down decomposition**:

1. **Safety Requirements Allocation** — Each SIF is assigned a target **SIL** based on the SIL determination study (Layer of Protection Analysis – LOPA).
2. **Architecture Selection** — The Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) is chosen using the IEC 61511-1 Table 5/6 (depending on whether the device is **Type A** or **Type B**).
3. **Failure-Rate Budgeting** — The target PFDavg is split among **Sensor (35 %)**, **Logic Solver (15 %)**, and **Final Element (50 %)** as a typical industry rule-of-thumb.
4. **Component Selection** — Specific transmitters, PLCs/safety PLCs, valves, and barriers are chosen with published **λ_D**, **λ_S**, **DC**, and **SFF**.
5. **Proof-Test Schedule Definition** — T_proof is selected such that the integrated PFDavg over the interval meets the SIL boundary.
6. **Software Design Specification** — For logic solvers, the application program is documented using languages permitted by IEC 61131-3 with **ladder (LD)**, **function-block (FBD)**, or **structured text (ST)**.
7. **Verification & Validation Plan** — Independent reviews, Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT), Site Acceptance Tests (SAT), and Site Integration Tests (SIT).
8. **Operation & Maintenance Specification** — Bypasses, overrides, repair procedures, and proof-test records.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type A vs Type B (IEC 61511-1, §11.4):**
> - **Type A** components have *well-defined failure modes*, *behaviour under fault conditions is fully known*, and *failure data is available from the manufacturer*. Example: simple pressure transmitter.
> - **Type B** components have *complex behaviour*, *failure modes not fully known*, *no full failure data available*. Example: micro-processor-based "smart" transmitter.

The distinction is critical because the **architectural constraints table** differs for the two.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. The vertical bar inside $\vert \cdot \vert$ is rendered as `\vert` so the markdown tables render correctly.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$PFD_{\text{SIF}} = PFD_{\text{Sensor}} + PFD_{\text{Logic}} + PFD_{\text{FE}}$$ | PFD values of sensor sub-system, logic solver, final element | SIL verification (additive, assuming independence) |
| 2 | $$\text{SIL} = f\!\left(\overline{PFD}\right) \;\; \text{per IEC 61511-1 Table 3}$$ | $\overline{PFD}$ = average PFD over the proof-test interval | Maps numerical PFDavg to discrete SIL 1–3 |
| 3 | $$PFD_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}$$ | $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (1/hr), $T_{\text{proof}}$ = proof interval (hr) | Single channel (1 out of 1) architecture |
| 4 | $$PFD_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \tfrac{1}{6}\,\lambda_D^2 \, T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}$$ | Same as above | 1-out-of-2 voted (parallel redundant) architecture |
| 5 | $$PFD_{\text{2oo3}} \approx \tfrac{1}{6}\,\lambda_D^2 \, T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}$$ | Same as above | 2-out-of-3 voted architecture |
| 6 | $$PFD_{\text{1oo2D}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_{DD} \cdot t_{CE} + \tfrac{1}{6}\,\lambda_{DU}^2 \, T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}$$ | $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous-detected, $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous-undetected, $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (hr) | 1oo2 with diagnostics |
| 7 | $$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$ | $\lambda_S$ = safe failure rate, $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous-detected rate, $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous-undetected rate | Architectural constraint check |
| 8 | $$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_S + \lambda_D \;;\; \lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$ | Standard decomposition | Failure budgeting |
| 9 | $$\overline{PFD} \le 10^{-m} \quad \text{where } m = 1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ for SIL 1–3}$$ | Boundary values SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Quick SIL check |
| 10 | $$\text{Availability} = 1 - \overline{PFD}$$ | PFDavg per proof cycle | Continuous-mode SIS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the daily bread of a **process safety engineer** in an oil refinery, LNG terminal, or pharmaceutical plant. They are used to generate the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Verification Report** which is mandatory before commissioning any SIF in India under the **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** and **PNGRB (Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board)** guidelines.

## 2.3 Why the Architectural Constraints Table Matters

Even if you compute a low PFDavg using a 1oo1 channel, IEC 61511-1 **forbids** claiming a high SIL if the architecture does not satisfy the **Hardware Fault Tolerance** for the given **SFF** and **Type**. This protects against **systematic faults** that cannot be quantified statistically.

A common rule-of-thumb for **Type B, SIL 2**:
- SFF between **60 % – 90 %** → **HFT = 1** required.
- SFF between **90 % – 99 %** → **HFT = 0** may still be acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 1oo1 PFDavg Formula

We model the failure of a single, non-repaired, demand-mode channel as a homogeneous Poisson process. The probability that the channel **fails dangerously before the proof test** is integrated over one proof-test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the unconditional probability of failure in time $t$:**

For a small sub-interval $\Delta t$, the probability of failure is $\lambda_D \Delta t$. The probability of surviving up to $t$ is $\exp(-\lambda_D t)$.

**Step 2 — Probability density of failure at time $t$:**

$$
f(t) = \lambda_D \, e^{-\lambda_D t}
$$

**Step 3 — Average PFD over the interval $[0,\,T_{\text{proof}}]$:**

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}}\int_0^{T_{\text{proof}}} \!\! \left[\,1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\,\right] \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the exponential using the Maclaurin series** (justified because $\lambda_D t \ll 1$ for typical industrial components — failure rates are measured in FITs):

$$
e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx 1 - \lambda_D t + \dfrac{(\lambda_D t)^2}{2!} - \cdots
$$

So,

$$
1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t - \dfrac{(\lambda_D t)^2}{2} + \cdots
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate term-by-term over $[0,\,T_{\text{proof}}]$:**

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}}\int_0^{T_{\text{proof}}} \!\!\left[\lambda_D t - \dfrac{(\lambda_D t)^2}{2} + \cdots\right] dt
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}}\left[\lambda_D \dfrac{t^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{2}\dfrac{t^{3}}{3} + \cdots\right]_0^{T_{\text{proof}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Extract the leading-order term:**

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\lambda_D \, T_{\text{proof}}}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous "$$\dfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D T$$" result quoted in **IEC 61511-6 §B.3.2.2**. Higher-order terms become important only when $\lambda_D T > 0.1$, which rarely occurs in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation of the 1oo2 PFDavg Formula

For two parallel channels, the system fails dangerously only when **both** channels fail dangerously. Assuming independence:

$$
PFD_{\text{1oo2}}(t) \;=\; \left[\,1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\,\right]^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the square:**

$$
\left[\,1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\,\right]^{2} \;=\; 1 - 2 e^{-\lambda_D t} + e^{-2 \lambda_D t}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof-test interval:**

$$
PFD_{\text{1oo2, avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}}\int_0^{T_{\text{proof}}} \!\! \left[\,1 - 2 e^{-\lambda_D t} + e^{-2 \lambda_D t}\,\right] dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate each term separately:**

$$
\int_0^{T} 1\, dt = T
$$

$$
\int_0^{T} e^{-\lambda_D t}\, dt = \left[\dfrac{-1}{\lambda_D} e^{-\lambda_D t}\right]_0^{T} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\lambda_D T}}{\lambda_D}
$$

$$
\int_0^{T} e^{-2 \lambda_D t}\, dt = \dfrac{1 - e^{-2 \lambda_D T}}{2 \lambda_D}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the three integrals and divide by $T$:**

$$
PFD_{\text{1oo2, avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T}\!\left[\,T - \dfrac{2(1-e^{-\lambda_D T})}{\lambda_D} + \dfrac{1-e^{-2\lambda_D T}}{2\lambda_D}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — For small $\lambda_D T$, approximate the exponentials to second order:**

$$
1 - e^{-\lambda_D T} \approx \lambda_D T - \dfrac{(\lambda_D T)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
1 - e^{-2\lambda_D T} \approx 2\lambda_D T - 2(\lambda_D T)^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify the leading-order behaviour:**

After algebraic reduction (retaining only second-order terms in $\lambda_D T$):

$$
PFD_{\text{1oo2, avg}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{(\lambda_D T)^{2}}{6}
$$

Which is the standard IEC 61511-1 result quoted as Formula #4 in the cheat sheet.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern

> A pressure transmitter on a SIF has the following manufacturer data:
> $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$, $\lambda_S = 2 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$, $DC = 70\;\%$, $T_{\text{proof}} = 1\,\text{year} = 8760\,\text{hr}$. The architecture is 1oo2.

**Step 1 — Decompose the dangerous failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_{DD} = DC \cdot \lambda_D = 0.70 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 3.5 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU} = (1 - DC) \cdot \lambda_D = 0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo2 formula (Formula #4):**

$$
