PFD_{\text{Sensor}} = \dfrac{1}{6}\, \lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\, T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2} = \dfrac{1}{6}\,(1.5 \times 10^{-7})^{2}\,(8760)^{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{Sensor}} = \dfrac{1}{6}\,(2.25 \times 10^{-14})\,(7.673 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{Sensor}} = \dfrac{1}{6} \times 1.7265 \times 10^{-6} = 2.88 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 3 — SIL Interpretation (IEC 61511-1 Table 3):**

$$
PFD \approx 2.88 \times 10^{-7} \;\in\; [10^{-4},\,10^{-3}] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 3 contribution.}
$$

**Step 4 — Budget verification (assuming Logic Solver = $5 \times 10^{-4}$, Final Element = $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$):**

$$
PFD_{\text{SIF}} = 2.88 \times 10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\approx\; 6.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Final SIL mapping:**

$$
6.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\in\; [10^{-3},\,10^{-2}] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 2 achieved.}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **sensor's 1oo2 PFD is calculated using $\lambda_{DU}$ (undetected) only**, not $\lambda_D$. Using $\lambda_D$ inflates the contribution and gives an incorrect SIL claim.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — SIS Detailed Design Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SRS Layer: SIL Target per SIF] --> B[SIL Determination from LOPA]
    B --> C[Architecture Selection: HFT, Voting 1oo1 or 1oo2 or 2oo3]
    C --> D[Failure Rate Budget: Sensor 35%, Logic 15%, FE 50%]
    D --> E[Component Selection: Transmitters, Logic Solver, Valves]
    E --> F[SFF and DC Evaluation]
    F --> G{Architectural Constraints Met?}
    G -- No --> H[Re-design: Add Redundancy or Diversify]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[PFDavg Calculation per Sub-System]
    I --> J{SIL Verification: SIF meets target?}
    J -- No --> K[Reduce T_proof or Change Vendor]
    K --> E
    J -- Yes --> L[Software Design Specification: IEC 61131-3]
    L --> M[Environmental and EMC Specifications]
    M --> N[Verification and Validation Plan FAT, SAT, SIT]
    N --> O[Operation and Maintenance Specification]
    O --> P[Detailed Design Document Released]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Sensor Sub-System with Diagnostics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Channel A] --> DC[Diagnostic Logic in Logic Solver]
    S2[Sensor Channel B] --> DC
    DC --> CO[Comparator: Out-of-Range or Stuck-Output Check]
    CO --> AL[Alarm and Trip Voter]
    AL --> LE[Logic Solver Processing]
    LE --> FE1[Final Element A]
    LE --> FE2[Final Element B]
    S1 -.-> SFF[SFF and DC Reporting]
    S2 -.-> SFF
    DC -.-> SFF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Specification Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Equipment Specification: Sensor] --> A1[Range, Accuracy, Turndown Ratio]
    S1 --> A2[Material of Construction, Process Connection]
    S1 --> A3[Failure Rates, SFF, DC, SIL Certificate]
    S1 --> A4[Environmental: IP Rating, Temperature Class]

    S2[Equipment Specification: Logic Solver] --> B1[SIL Certification IEC 61508]
    S2 --> B2[Programming Languages: LD, FBD, ST]
    S2 --> B3[Scan Time and Response Time]
    S2 --> B4[Redundancy: CPU, Power, Communication]

    S3[Equipment Specification: Final Element] --> C1[Valve Type: Globe, Ball, Plug]
    S3 --> C2[Failure Position: FC or FO or FL]
    S3 --> C3[Actuator: Pneumatic, Hydraulic, Electric]
    S3 --> C4[Proof Test Method and Interval]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Proof Test Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Operational
    Operational --> Operational: Demand occurs, SIF responds correctly
    Operational --> ProofTestDue: T_proof timer elapsed
    ProofTestDue --> UnderTest: Maintenance team performs proof test
    UnderTest --> Operational: Test passed, PFD reset to zero
    UnderTest --> FaultFound: Dangerous fault detected
    FaultFound --> Maintenance: Repair and replacement
    Maintenance --> Operational: System restored, log updated
    Operational --> DangerousFailure: Undetected failure during demand
    DangerousFailure --> [*]: Process hazard realised
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each transition in the state diagram above corresponds to a line item in the **Detailed Design Document (DDD)**. The probability of transitioning from *Operational* directly to *Dangerous Failure* is what $\overline{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$ mathematically represents. The transition to *Proof Test Due* quantifies the operator's compliance with the scheduled proof-test interval.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1a]**
**Define "Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)" and explain its role in the architectural-constraint check of IEC 61511.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Safe Failure Fraction is the ratio of the average rate of safe failures plus dangerous-detected failures to the total failure rate of a component:
>
> $$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

> **Role in architectural constraints (1 mark):** SFF is used together with the **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and the **device type (A or B)** to determine the **maximum permissible SIL** that may be claimed. Higher SFF allows a lower HFT for the same SIL target.

> **KTU Examiner's Insight (1 mark):** SFF must be ≥ 60 % for SIL 1 in Type B devices with HFT = 0, and must be ≥ 90 % to claim SIL 3 with HFT = 0 in Type B. This protects against systematic faults.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1, Q1b]**
**List any three deliverables of the "Detailed Design and Specifications" phase of a Safety Instrumented System project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hardware Design Specification** — sensor, logic solver, and final-element selection with published failure rates, SFF, DC, and SIL certificate.
2. **Software Design Specification** — application program in IEC 61131-3 languages, I/O list, scan-time budget, and force/disable policies.
3. **Verification & Validation Plan** — independent reviews, FAT, SAT, and SIT procedures aligned to the SIL targets of each SIF.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ▶ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module-4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** document. Discuss its role as the input to the detailed design phase and list its key elements with respect to IEC 61511. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** for a **1oo1 architecture** in demand-mode operation. State clearly all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

> **Definition (2 marks):** The SRS is the complete specification of the safety requirements for each SIF, expressed in quantitative (SIL targets) and qualitative (operating modes, response time) terms.

> **Role as design input (2 marks):** The SRS is the single source-of-truth that the detailed design must satisfy. Every hardware choice, software block, proof-test plan, and bypass procedure must be traceable back to a clause in the SRS.

> **Key elements of an IEC 61511 SRS (3 marks):**
> 1. Description of each SIF and its safety goals.
> 2. Required SIL and PFDavg target per SIF.
> 3. Process operating modes (start-up, normal, shutdown, maintenance).
> 4. Response-time requirements and spurious-trip rate limits.
> 5. Demand rate assumptions and demand source (process, operator, environmental).
> 6. Proof-test interval and method.
> 7. Required operating environment (temperature, humidity, corrosive atmosphere, EMC class).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

> **Step 1 — State the assumptions (2 marks):**
> 1. Component failures are exponentially distributed.
> 2. $\lambda_D T_{\text{proof}} \ll 1$ so higher-order terms are negligible.
> 3. Repairs are performed only at scheduled proof tests.

> **Step 2 — Set up the unconditional probability (2 marks):**
> $$P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

> **Step 3 — Average over the proof-test interval (2 marks):**
> $$\overline{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}}\int_0^{T_{\text{proof}}} \!\! \left[\,1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\,\right] dt$$

> **Step 4 — Simplify using series expansion (1 mark):**
> $$\overline{PFD} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D \, T_{\text{proof}}$$

---

### ▶ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Type A** and **Type B** components in IEC 61511. Explain how this classification affects the architectural constraints table. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1oo2D sensor sub-system has the following data per channel: $\lambda_D = 8 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$, $\lambda_S = 3 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$, $DC = 60\;\%$, $T_{\text{proof}} = 2 \;\text{years}$. Compute (i) $\lambda_{DU}$, (ii) the **sensor sub-system PFDavg**, and (iii) the **Safe Failure Fraction**. Classify the device as Type A or Type B if its SFF is 88 % and the project requires SIL 2. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

> **Definition of Type A (2 marks):** Simple devices with well-defined failure modes, deterministic behaviour under fault, and complete failure data. Examples: simple orifice plate, mechanical pressure switch.

> **Definition of Type B (2 marks):** Complex devices whose failure modes cannot be completely characterised, often micro-processor-based. Examples: smart HART transmitter, PLC-based logic solver.

> **Effect on architectural constraints (3 marks):**
> - **Type A:** Less restrictive table. For SIL 2, HFT = 0 may be acceptable even with moderate SFF.
> - **Type B:** Stricter table. For SIL 2 with HFT = 0, SFF must be ≥ 90 %; otherwise HFT = 1 is required.
> - This dichotomy prevents claiming high SIL for complex devices without proven diagnostic coverage.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

> **(i) Compute $\lambda_{DU}$ (2 marks):**
> $$\lambda_{DU} = (1-DC)\,\lambda_D = (0.40)(8 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.2 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$$

> **(ii) Sensor PFDavg using 1oo2 formula (3 marks):**
> With $T_{\text{proof}} = 2 \times 8760 = 17520\;\text{hr}$,
>
> $$PFD_{\text{Sensor}} = \tfrac{1}{6}\,\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2} = \tfrac{1}{6}\,(3.2 \times 10^{-7})^{2}\,(17520)^{2}$$
>
> $$PFD_{\text{Sensor}} = \tfrac{1}{6}\,(1.024 \times 10^{-13})\,(3.0695 \times 10^{8}) \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-6}$$

> **(iii) Safe Failure Fraction and Type classification (2 marks):**
> $$\lambda_{DD} = 0.60 \times 8 \times 10^{-7} = 4.8 \times 10^{-7}\;/\text{hr}$$
>
> $$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-7} + 4.8 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-7} + 4.8 \times 10^{-7} + 3.2 \times 10^{-7}}$$
>
> $$SFF = \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{-7}}{11.0 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 70.9\;\%$$
>
> Since $SFF \in [60\;\%,\;90\;\%)$ and project needs SIL 2 with **Type B**, IEC 61511-1 Table 6 requires **HFT ≥ 1**, which is already satisfied by the **1oo2D** architecture. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to multiply the 1-year failure rate by 8760 when $T_{\text{proof}}$ is given in years. Always convert to hours **before** plugging into the formula.
> 2. **Wrong use of $\lambda_D$ vs $\lambda_{DU}$ (–2 marks):** Use $\lambda_{DU}$ in the 1oo2 formula; using $\lambda_D$ inflates the result and mis-classifies the SIL.
> 3. **Forgetting architectural constraints (–2 marks):** A numerical SIL claim without verifying the HFT–SFF–Type constraint table is treated as incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- ⚡ The **Detailed Design phase** translates the SRS into implementation-ready hardware, software, and procedural artefacts.
- ⚡ **Three sub-systems** of every SIF: **Sensor (input)**, **Logic Solver (brain)**, **Final Element (actuator)**.
- ⚡ **Failure-rate budgeting** is conventionally **35 / 15 / 50** % (Sensor / Logic / Final Element).
- ⚡ **PFDavg for 1oo1:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{\text{proof}}$. **For 1oo2:** $\tfrac{1}{6}\lambda_{DU}^{2} T_{\text{proof}}^{2}$.
- ⚡ **SIL 1:** $10^{-2} \le \overline{PFD} \le 10^{-1}$ ; **SIL 2:** $10^{-3} \le \overline{PFD} \le 10^{-2}$ ; **SIL 3:** $10^{-4} \le \overline{PFD} \le 10^{-3}$.
- ⚡ **SFF formula:** $SFF = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD})/(\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$.
- ⚡ **Type B devices** require **stricter** architectural constraints than **Type A** devices for the same SIL.
- ⚡ **Software design** must use IEC 61131-3 languages and document scan-time, I/O, and force/disable policy.
- ⚡ **Verification & Validation** must include FAT, SAT, and SIT — KTU frequently asks for the difference.
- ⚡ **Proof-test interval** is a powerful design lever: halving $T_{\text{proof}}$ roughly halves the 1oo1 PFDavg.
- ⚡ A **SIL claim** is incomplete without both a **PFDavg** calculation **and** an **architectural-constraint** verification.
- ⚡ Cite the governing standard: **IEC 61511-1 (2016)** — short form **IEC 61511**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Procedure Development

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procedure Development** in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) refers to the systematic creation, documentation, validation, and maintenance of structured sets of instructions that govern the **safe operation, maintenance, testing, and modification** of safety instrumented functions (SIFs) throughout the operational phase of the safety lifecycle, in accordance with **IEC 61511 (Functional Safety – Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector)** clauses 11.5, 13, 14, 16, and 17.

In plain engineering language, **Procedure Development** is the discipline of writing, organizing, and continuously updating the "rule-books" that tell operators what to do, tell maintenance technicians how to test safety equipment, and tell engineers exactly how a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) must be bypassed, repaired, or modified — *without ever compromising the integrity of the safety protection layer*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Aircraft Pilot's Checklist"

Imagine a commercial aircraft pilot preparing for takeoff. The pilot does not rely on memory alone. Instead, there is a **standardized checklist** for:
- Pre-flight inspection
- Engine startup
- Takeoff
- In-flight emergencies (e.g., engine failure, depressurization)
- Landing

This checklist is a **procedure**. It is:
- Written down (documented)
- Reviewed by multiple experts before use (validated)
- Updated whenever aircraft systems change (management of change)
- Strictly followed every single time (mandatory compliance)

In a process plant with an SIS, **Procedure Development** is exactly the same discipline. The "aircraft" is a reactor, a distillation column, or a furnace; the "pilot" is the operator; the "checklist" is the operating procedure; the "emergency drills" are the trip and alarm response procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why procedures are non-negotiable in SIS:**
> Human error contributes to roughly **70–80 %** of all process industry incidents (per industry incident databases such as the **HSE UK** and **CCPS** reports). A well-developed procedure is the single most effective **administrative safeguard** against human error.

### 1.3 The Role of Procedure Development in the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle

Procedure Development belongs primarily to the **Operation, Maintenance, and Modification phases** of the IEC 61511 safety lifecycle, but its roots are in the earlier design phases. Without a well-developed procedure, even a perfectly designed SIS can fail under the pressure of a real emergency.

| Safety Lifecycle Phase | Procedure Development Activity |
|---|---|
| Analysis (Phase 3) | Identify what tasks humans will perform |
| Design (Phase 4) | Define SIS design constraints affecting procedures |
| Operation (Phase 5) | Develop operating, alarm-response, and emergency procedures |
| Maintenance (Phase 6) | Develop proof test, calibration, repair procedures |
| Modification (Phase 7) | Develop management-of-change (MOC) procedures |
| Decommissioning (Phase 8) | Develop shutdown and disposal procedures |

### 1.4 Core Categories of Procedures Required in an SIS

There are **seven major categories** of procedures that must be developed for any functional SIS:

1. **Operating Procedures** – how to run the plant normally and safely.
2. **Alarm and Trip Response Procedures** – how to react to specific safety alarms and trips.
3. **Maintenance Procedures** – how to inspect, test, and repair SIS components.
4. **Proof Test Procedures** – how to periodically validate that SIFs still meet their target SIL.
5. **Bypass / Override Procedures** – how to temporarily disable a SIF (with strict controls).
6. **Commissioning Procedures** – how to install and initially validate the SIS.
7. **Management of Change (MOC) Procedures** – how to authorize any modification that affects the SIS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Examiners frequently ask students to *list the types of procedures* or to *explain any two procedures in detail*. Memorize the seven categories above; they appear in **Part A (3-mark)** questions almost every semester.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEC 61511 Clause 11.5 Framework

The most authoritative reference for SIS procedure development is **IEC 61511-1:2016, clause 11.5 – "Operation"**. This clause mandates that the operator shall develop and maintain procedures for:

- **Safe authorization to operate** the EUC (Equipment Under Control).
- **Startup, normal operation, shutdown, and emergency shutdown** sequences.
- **Responding to alarms and trips**, including first-out indication handling.
- **Documenting operational activities** and deviations.
- **Management of overrides and bypasses** during operation.
- **Coordination between the basic process control system (BPCS) and the SIS** to avoid conflicting actions.
- **Returning the process to a safe state** after a trip.

In parallel, **clause 16 – "Operation, Maintenance and Modification of the SIS"** requires that procedures be written, reviewed, approved, and distributed to all personnel who need them. They must be **readily accessible** at the location where the activity is performed (e.g., the control room, the field junction box).

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Well-Developed Procedure

A high-quality SIS procedure has the following structured anatomy. KTU examiners award marks for **structured, systematic answers**, so always organize your answer using this template.

1. **Title and unique document number** – uniquely identifies the procedure.
2. **Purpose and scope** – why does this procedure exist and what does it cover?
3. **References** – links to P&ID, SRS, cause & effect matrix, vendor manuals.
4. **Definitions and acronyms** – SIS, SIF, SIL, BPCS, ESD, PSD, TUV, etc.
5. **Roles and responsibilities** – who is authorized to do what.
6. **Pre-conditions and prerequisites** – LOTO, permits, isolation, etc.
7. **Step-by-step actions** – the main body, written in imperative tense.
8. **Safety precautions** – PPE, hazard warnings, environmental notes.
9. **Hold points** – mandatory pause-and-confirm points.
10. **Validation and acceptance criteria** – how do we know the step succeeded?
11. **Records and documentation** – what must be signed and filed.
12. **Annexures** – checklist, calibration sheet, valve position list.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "describe a procedure," always structure your answer in this order. It signals to the examiner that you understand professional procedure-writing standards.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure Development Methodology

The development of any SIS procedure follows a **five-step methodology** that aligns with the KTU syllabus:

### Step 1 – Identify the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)
- Refer to the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)**.
- Identify each SIF (SIF-1, SIF-2, …) and its target **SIL** (1, 2, or 3).
- Identify the **initiating cause** (e.g., high pressure, high temperature) and the **final element** (e.g., shutdown valve, motor stop).

### Step 2 – Identify the Required Human Tasks
- Determine what an operator or technician must do *before, during, and after* a SIF activation.
- Examples: acknowledge alarm, close manual valve, reset trip, perform visual inspection.

### Step 3 – Perform a Task-Based Human Error Analysis
- Apply techniques such as **HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique)** or **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)** to estimate the **probability of human error** for each task.
- If the human error probability is too high, modify the procedure (e.g., add a check step, add an interlock, add a hold point).

### Step 4 – Write the Procedure in a Standardized Format
- Use the structured anatomy described in Section 2.2.
- Use **active, imperative language** (e.g., "Open valve V-101" not "Valve V-101 should be opened").
- Number every step. Limit each step to a single action.

### Step 5 – Review, Approve, Validate, and Distribute
- **Review** by operations, maintenance, process safety, and management.
- **Approve** by a designated authority.
- **Validate** by a walk-through or simulation.
- **Distribute** as controlled document; train all relevant personnel.
- **Update** whenever the plant, process, or SIF changes (this is the MOC trigger).

## 2.4 Detailed Description of the Seven Procedure Categories

### 2.4.1 Operating Procedures

**Purpose:** Define how to start up, operate normally, and shut down the EUC while keeping the SIS in a known safe state.

**Key contents:**
- Pre-startup checklist (e.g., all SIS valves in correct position, all bypasses removed, all overrides cleared).
- Startup sequence with the order of valve openings, motor starts, and heater firing.
- Normal operating limits (e.g., pressure 1.0–2.0 bar, temperature 80–120 °C).
- Normal shutdown sequence.
- Emergency shutdown (ESD) sequence.
- Hand-off between shifts.

**KTU-relevant note:** The operating procedure must be **harmonized with the BPCS logic and the SIS logic** so that the operator's manual actions never fight the safety logic.

### 2.4.2 Alarm and Trip Response Procedures

**Purpose:** Tell the operator exactly what to do when a specific alarm or trip occurs.

**Structure (one procedure per trip, usually):**

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| Trip tag and description | e.g., "PAH-101, Reactor R-101 High Pressure Trip" |
| Trip setpoint | e.g., 4.5 barg |
| SIF involved | SIF-3 (SIL 2) |
| Possible operator actions | 1) Acknowledge; 2) Open vent valve; 3) Trip feed pump; 4) Notify supervisor |
| Do NOT actions | Do not bypass trip until SOP-207 is completed |
| Escalation | If pressure remains high after 5 min, call Emergency Response Team |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-out indication:** A modern SIS displays which trip fired first. The procedure must direct the operator to **read the first-out indicator before silencing the alarm** to preserve the diagnostic information.

### 2.4.3 Maintenance Procedures

**Purpose:** Govern the safe maintenance of SIS sensors, logic solvers, and final elements.

**Key contents:**
- Preventive maintenance schedule (e.g., quarterly, annually).
- Spare parts list with re-order levels.
- LOTO (Lock Out Tag Out) sequence.
- Megger testing of wiring.
- Replacement of sensor diaphragms, relays, solenoids.
- Re-instatement to service and test-after-repair.

### 2.4.4 Proof Test Procedures

**Purpose:** Periodically validate that each SIF still achieves its target **PFDavg** (average Probability of Failure on Demand) and therefore its target SIL.

**Key contents:**
- Test interval (e.g., 1 year, 2 years, 5 years).
- Test method (e.g., full stroke test, partial stroke test, sensor calibration check).
- Acceptance criteria (e.g., trip occurs within X seconds at setpoint Y).
- Failure reporting and follow-up.
- Calculation update: if a SIF fails a proof test, its PFDavg is recalculated for the next interval.

The relationship between **test interval (TI)**, **mean time to restoration (MTR)**, and **PFDavg** is captured in the standard SIL equations. For a 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) sensor architecture:

$$PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot (TI + MTR)}{2}$$

where:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (per hour) of the sensor
- $TI$ = proof test interval (hours)
- $MTR$ = mean time to restoration after a failure is detected (hours)

For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) redundant sensor architecture:

$$PFD_{avg,1oo2} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^{2} \cdot TI^{2}}{6}$$

**Procedurally**, the consequence of these formulas is simple: **the longer the test interval, the higher the PFDavg**, and therefore the procedure must specify the *maximum* test interval for each SIF.

### 2.4.5 Bypass / Override Procedures

**Purpose:** Provide a **strictly controlled** way to temporarily disable a SIF when necessary (e.g., during maintenance, commissioning, or unusual operational modes).

**Mandatory procedural controls:**

1. **Written authorization** by a designated person (e.g., Plant Manager).
2. **Time limit** – bypass must never be left in place indefinitely.
3. **Compensating measures** – additional manual checks, additional operators, temporary alarms.
4. **Removal of bypass** – procedure to remove the bypass, re-test the SIF, and document restoration.
5. **Audit trail** – log book entry, computer record, paper permit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common cause of major incidents:** An unremoved bypass left in place across a maintenance turnaround, then the plant starts up with one or more SIFs effectively disabled. Bypass procedures are the most safety-critical procedures in the entire SIS framework.

### 2.4.6 Commissioning Procedures

**Purpose:** Verify that every SIF is installed correctly, configured correctly, and performs to the SRS before the plant is released to operations.

**Key contents:**
- Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) and Site Acceptance Test (SAT) records.
- Loop check of every sensor and final element.
- Functional test of every SIF (simulate the initiating cause, confirm the final element moves).
- Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) checklist.
- Sign-off by operations, process safety, and management.

### 2.4.7 Management of Change (MOC) Procedures

**Purpose:** Ensure that *any* change to the process, the SIS, the procedure, or the organization is reviewed for its impact on safety before being implemented.

**Trigger conditions for MOC:**

- Change in process operating conditions (pressure, temperature, flow).
- Change in raw materials or product specifications.
- Change in SIS hardware (sensor, logic solver, valve replacement with different model).
- Change in SIS software (logic, setpoint, voting).
- Change in operating procedure.
- Change in personnel or organizational structure.
- Any temporary change (bypass) that exceeds 30 days.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "Why is MOC important?", the answer must include the phrase **"to ensure that the assumed PFDavg and SIL are still valid after the change."**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Procedure Development)

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda_D$ | Dangerous failure rate | failures per hour (fit = $10^{-9}$/h) |
| $TI$ | Proof test interval | hours |
| $MTR$ | Mean time to restoration | hours |
| $PFD_{avg}$ | Average probability of failure on demand | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| $PFD_{avg,1oo1}$ | PFDavg for 1oo1 architecture | $\approx \lambda_D \cdot TI/2$ |
| $PFD_{avg,1oo2}$ | PFDavg for 1oo2 architecture | $\approx \lambda_D^{2} \cdot TI^{2}/6$ |
| $SIL$ | Safety Integrity Level | 1, 2, or 3 (per IEC 61511) |
| $SIF$ | Safety Instrumented Function | identified by tag (e.g., SIF-04) |
| $PSSR$ | Pre-Startup Safety Review | mandatory before plant startup |
| $MOC$ | Management of Change | mandatory before any modification |
| $LOTO$ | Lock Out Tag Out | mandatory isolation procedure |
| $BPCS$ | Basic Process Control System | non-SIS control layer |
| $ESD$ | Emergency Shutdown | subsystem of the SIS |
| $PSD$ | Process Shutdown | subsystem of the SIS |
| $FAT/SAT$ | Factory/Site Acceptance Test | commissioning procedure |
| $SRS$ | Safety Requirements Specification | input document for procedures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Pitfall:** In the markdown table above, the symbol `vert` has been used (e.g., "0 to 1") instead of the vertical pipe character `|`, because the pipe character would break the KTU-compliant markdown table rendering. This is a structural safeguard, not a typographical error.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical refineries** use these procedures during turnarounds to test thousands of SIFs systematically.
- **Offshore oil platforms** use override procedures to manage gas detection bypasses during hot work.
- **Pharmaceutical plants** use commissioning procedures to validate clean-in-place (CIP) interlocks.
- **Power plants** use trip response procedures for boiler protection (e.g., furnace implosion, flame failure).
- **Nuclear industry** uses MOC procedures for any modification to reactor protection systems, with multi-level independent review.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

Because Procedure Development is fundamentally a **process-engineering and management discipline**, the "derivations" below are **logical and procedural derivations**, not algebraic ones. The one quantitative derivation — the PFDavg formula — is included because it is mandatory in any KTU question on proof-test procedures.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the 1oo1 PFDavg Formula (Proof Test Procedure Foundation)

We derive the standard IEC 61511 expression for the average probability of failure on demand of a single channel in a 1oo1 architecture.

**Step 1 – Define the unavailability function $U(t)$.**

The probability that a single channel with dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ is failed at time $t$ after a successful proof test, and has not yet been detected and repaired, is:

$$U(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t$$

This approximation is valid for $t$ much smaller than $1/\lambda_D$, which is the realistic case for SIS components (failure rates are typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$/h).

**Step 2 – Define the average unavailability over one test interval.**

We average $U(t)$ over the proof test interval $TI$, from $t = 0$ to $t = TI$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} U(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 – Substitute $U(t) = \lambda_D \cdot t$.**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D}{TI} \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{TI}$$

**Step 4 – Evaluate the limits.**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D}{TI} \cdot \frac{TI^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 – Simplify.**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2}$$

This is the textbook expression. For a more realistic case that includes a finite mean time to restoration $MTR$ after a proof-test failure is detected, the formula is extended to:

$$PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2} + \frac{\lambda_D \cdot MTR}{2}$$

**Procedural consequence:** The longer the test interval, the larger the PFDavg, the lower the achieved SIL. **Therefore, the proof test procedure must specify the maximum allowable test interval for each SIF.**

### 3.1.2 Logical Derivation: Why a Bypass Procedure Must Be Time-Limited

**Step 1 – State the safety function's purpose.**

A SIF exists to bring the process to a safe state when an abnormal condition occurs.

**Step 2 – State the effect of a bypass.**

A bypass disables the SIF, removing the safety protection layer for the duration of the bypass.

**Step 3 – State the risk created by a bypass.**

During the bypass, the residual risk equals the **pre-SIS process risk** (i.e., the risk before the SIS was installed).

**Step 4 – Conclude the procedural rule.**

Because the residual risk during a bypass is much higher than the mitigated risk, the procedure must enforce:

- (a) Approval at the highest practical authority.
- (b) A **maximum time limit** (commonly 24 h, 7 days, or 30 days depending on corporate policy).
- (c) Compensating measures during the bypass.
- (d) Mandatory removal and re-test at the end of the bypass window.

**Conclusion:** A bypass without a time limit is, by definition, an *uncontrolled* modification of the safety protection layer and constitutes a violation of IEC 61511 clause 16.4.

### 3.1.3 Logical Derivation: Why MOC Must Trigger for Procedure Changes

**Step 1 – A procedure is part of the safety barrier.**

Procedures are an *administrative barrier* that supports the *engineering barrier* (the SIS hardware and software).

**Step 2 – Modifying a procedure alters the human's role.**

A new procedure may require the operator to perform a new action, or may remove a check step.

**Step 3 – Human reliability changes with the procedure.**

The new task may have a higher probability of human error, or a lower one.

**Step 4 – Conclude the procedural rule.**

Because human reliability is a contributor to the overall SIL achievement, **any procedure change must be reviewed for its impact on safety**, which is exactly what MOC accomplishes.

**Conclusion:** Updating an operating procedure without MOC is a common procedural violation in real plants and is a frequent citation in KTU and CCPS audit reports.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Procedure Content Mapping

The following matrix maps **who** needs **which procedure** and **when**. This is a high-yield KTU topic and is often asked as "Compare and contrast operating, maintenance, and bypass procedures."

| Procedure Type | Trigger Event | Author | Approver | User | Documentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Procedure | Plant startup / new SOP issue | Process Engineering | Plant Manager | Operator | Logbook, shift handover |
| Alarm/Trip Response | New SIF or new alarm | Instrument Engineering + Operations | Plant Manager | Operator | Logbook, trip log |
| Maintenance Procedure | New equipment install or PM schedule | Maintenance Engineering | Maintenance Manager | Technician | CMMS work order |
| Proof Test Procedure | New SIF or revised TI | Safety Engineer | Plant Manager + Independent Reviewer | Technician + Operator | Test certificate |
| Bypass / Override | Plant maintenance / abnormal operation | Operations | Plant Manager (or higher) | Authorized operator | Bypass permit |
| Commissioning Procedure | New SIS installation or major modification | Project Engineering | Project Manager + Safety Engineer | Commissioning team | SAT report, PSSR |
| MOC Procedure | Any change to process, SIS, procedure, or organization | Originator (any discipline) | MOC Committee | All affected parties | MOC form, risk assessment |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example – Effect of Test Interval on PFDavg

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a 1oo1 architecture has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/h. The plant currently proof-tests it every 2 years. The procedure is being revised. Calculate the PFDavg for test intervals of 1 year, 2 years, and 5 years. Comment on the achieved SIL using the standard IEC 61511 table.

**Given:**

$$\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$

We assume $MTR \approx 0$ for simplicity (a typical assumption when the failure is detected immediately at proof test).

**Step 1 – Apply the 1oo1 formula:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2}$$

**Step 2 – Convert time intervals to hours.**

- 1 year = $8760$ h
- 2 years = $17520$ h
- 5 years = $43800$ h

**Step 3 – Compute PFDavg for each case.**

For $TI = 1$ year:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 8760}{2} = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $TI = 2$ years:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 17520}{2} = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $TI = 5$ years:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 43800}{2} = \frac{2.19 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 1.095 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 – Compare with IEC 61511 target ranges.**

| SIL | Target PFDavg range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

- 1 year interval: $PFDavg = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ → **SIL 2 ✓**
- 2 years interval: $PFDavg = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ → **SIL 2 ✓**
- 5 years interval: $PFDavg = 1.095 \times 10^{-2}$ → **SIL 1 only ✗ (lost one SIL level!)**

**Step 5 – Procedural recommendation.**

The proof test procedure must therefore specify a **maximum test interval of approximately 2 years** to maintain SIL 2, with a more conservative recommendation of **1 year** to provide a safety margin and to allow for the non-zero MTR in real plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic KTU numerical problem.** The student is expected to know the 1oo1 PFDavg formula, the SIL target ranges, and to translate the numerical result into a **procedural recommendation**. Memorize the formula and the SIL ranges.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 – Procedure Development Lifecycle (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the complete procedure development lifecycle, from SIF identification through to periodic review.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify SIF from SRS"] --> B["Identify Required Human Tasks"]
    B --> C["Perform Human Error Analysis"]
    C --> D["Draft Procedure in Standardized Format"]
    D --> E["Independent Review by Multi-Discipline Team"]
    E --> F["Approval by Designated Authority"]
    F --> G["Validation by Walk-through or Simulation"]
    G --> H["Distribute as Controlled Document"]
    H --> I["Train All Relevant Personnel"]
    I --> J["Periodic Use in Plant Operations"]
    J --> K{"Any Change to Process or SIS?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Trigger Management of Change MOC"]
    L --> M["Re-evaluate Procedure"]
    M --> D
    K -- No --> N{"Periodic Review Due?"}
    N -- Yes --> O["Re-validate Procedure"]
    O --> J
    N -- No --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The lifecycle is a continuous improvement loop. The **MOC trigger** is the most important branch; it ensures that no procedure ever silently becomes outdated.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 – Block-Level Functional Architecture of Procedure Types

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the seven categories of SIS procedures against the SIS components and the lifecycle phase they apply to.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseOp["Operation Phase"]
        OP1["Operating Procedure"]
        OP2["Alarm and Trip Response Procedure"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseMaint["Maintenance Phase"]
        MT1["Maintenance Procedure"]
        MT2["Proof Test Procedure"]
        MT3["Bypass and Override Procedure"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseComm["Commissioning Phase"]
        CM1["Commissioning Procedure"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseMod["Modification Phase"]
        MD1["Management of Change Procedure"]
    end
    subgraph SIS["SIS Architecture"]
        S1["Sensors 1oo1 or 1oo2"]
        S2["Logic Solver"]
        S3["Final Elements Valves Motors"]
    end
    S1 --> MT2
    S2 --> MT1
    S3 --> MT3
    OP1 --> OP2
    MT1 --> MT2
    MT3 --> MD1
    CM1 --> OP1
    MD1 --> OP1
    MD1 --> MT1
    MD1 --> MT2
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 – Bypass Procedure Decision Flow

The following Mermaid state diagram enforces the strict sequence of approvals and time limits required for a bypass procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Operator Identifies Need for Bypass"]) --> Form["Complete Bypass Request Form"]
    Form --> HAZOP["Identify Compensating Measures"]
    HAZOP --> RiskAsst["Perform Risk Assessment"]
    RiskAsst --> App1["Operations Supervisor Approval"]
    App1 --> App2["Plant Manager Approval"]
    App2 --> App3{"Time Greater than 30 Days?"}
    App3 -- Yes --> App4["HSE Director Approval"]
    App3 -- No --> SetTI["Set Bypass Time Limit"]
    App4 --> SetTI
    SetTI --> Log1["Log in Bypass Register"]
    Log1 --> Apply["Apply Bypass in SIS and BPCS"]
    Apply --> Comp["Apply Compensating Measures"]
    Comp --> Monitor["Operate Under Compensating Measures"]
    Monitor --> Warn{"Time Limit Approaching?"}
    Warn -- Yes --> Rem["Reminder to Operations"]
    Rem --> Monitor
    Warn -- Expired --> Forbid["FORBIDDEN Continue Without Removal"]
    Forbid --> Escalate["Escalate to Plant Manager Immediately"]
    Escalate --> Remove["Remove Bypass"]
    Remove --> ReTest["Re-test SIF"]
    ReTest --> Doc["Document Restoration"]
    Doc --> Close(["Close Bypass Permit"])
    Monitor --> PlannedEnd{"Planned Removal Time Reached?"}
    PlannedEnd -- Yes --> Remove
    PlannedEnd -- No --> Monitor
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the procedure has **two independent termination paths** (planned removal and emergency escalation) and a **FORBIDDEN state** that explicitly prohibits operating with an expired bypass. This is a hallmark of a robust bypass procedure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 – [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Q: Define "Procedure Development" in the context of a Safety Instrumented System and list any four categories of procedures required by IEC 61511.**

**Model Answer:**

Procedure Development is the systematic creation, documentation, validation, and maintenance of structured sets of instructions that govern the safe operation, maintenance, testing, and modification of safety instrumented functions (SIFs) throughout the operational phase of the safety lifecycle, in accordance with IEC 61511.

The four categories of procedures required by IEC 61511 are:

1. **Operating Procedures** – for normal and safe operation of the EUC.
2. **Alarm and Trip Response Procedures** – for operator action on detection of abnormal conditions.
3. **Proof Test Procedures** – for periodic verification of SIF integrity.
4. **Bypass / Override Procedures** – for the strictly controlled temporary disabling of a SIF.

(Other acceptable answers: Maintenance Procedures, Commissioning Procedures, Management of Change Procedures.)

### Question 2 – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Q: Why is a "Management of Change" (MOC) procedure mandatory before modifying an SIS?**

**Model Answer:**

A Management of Change (MOC) procedure is mandatory before modifying an SIS because any change — to the process, the SIS hardware, the SIS software, the operating procedure, or the organization — can alter the **average probability of failure on demand (PFDavg)** of the safety instrumented functions and therefore the achieved **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. MOC ensures that:

- The safety impact of the change is systematically assessed.
- The PFDavg is recalculated and the SIL target re-verified.
- All affected procedures, training, and documentation are updated.
- The change is reviewed and approved by a multi-disciplinary committee before implementation.

Without MOC, an apparently minor modification (e.g., changing a sensor model, adjusting a trip setpoint, removing a checklist step) can silently degrade the safety protection layer and lead to a major incident.

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 (Model)]

**Q(a) – 7 Marks:** List the major categories of procedures required in a Safety Instrumented System. Explain in detail the **Bypass / Override Procedure**, including its mandatory controls, the safety rationale for time-limiting it, and its relationship with the **Management of Change (MOC)** procedure.

**Q(b) – 7 Marks:** A pressure transmitter in a 1oo1 architecture has a dangerous failure rate of $4 \times 10^{-7}$ failures per hour. The plant currently proof-tests it every 18 months. Calculate the average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg). If the test interval is extended to 4 years, what is the new PFDavg? Using the IEC 61511 target ranges for SIL, comment on whether the achieved safety integrity level is preserved in both cases.

**Model Answer – Q(a):**

**Categories of procedures (1 mark):**

The major categories are: (i) Operating procedures, (ii) Alarm and trip response procedures, (iii) Maintenance procedures, (iv) Proof test procedures, (v) Bypass / Override procedures, (vi) Commissioning procedures, (vii) Management of Change procedures.

**Bypass / Override Procedure (4 marks):**

A bypass is the temporary disabling of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). Bypasses are necessary during certain activities (e.g., maintenance, commissioning, abnormal operation), but they remove the safety protection layer and elevate residual risk back to the pre-SIS level.

**Mandatory controls:**

1. **Written authorization** – a formal permit signed by an authorized person (typically the Plant Manager, or HSE Director for long-term bypasses).
2. **Time limit** – the bypass must be removed within a specified period (e.g., 24 hours, 7 days, or 30 days depending on the corporate policy).
3. **Compensating measures** – additional manual monitoring, additional operators, temporary alarms, or a fire-watch.
4. **Bypass register** – a log of all active bypasses, with date, time, SIF tag, reason, and removal deadline.
5. **Removal and re-test** – when the bypass is removed, the SIF must be re-tested to confirm it is fully functional.
6. **Audit trail** – all bypass events must be reviewed in a periodic safety audit.

**Safety rationale for time-limiting:**

The longer a bypass is in place, the higher the cumulative exposure to risk, and the higher the probability that an abnormal event occurs while the SIF is disabled. Time-limiting ensures that the period of elevated risk is short and controlled.

**Relationship with MOC:**

Any bypass that exceeds a short emergency duration (typically 30 days) is treated as a **modification** to the SIS and therefore triggers the MOC procedure. MOC requires a formal risk assessment, calculation update, and approval by a multi-disciplinary committee. In particular, **bypasses that are "forgotten" and left in place after the maintenance activity is completed** are one of the most common causes of major process industry incidents; this is why the MOC procedure must explicitly require re-validation of SIFs after the bypass is removed.

**Model Answer – Q(b):**

**Given:**

$$\lambda_D = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$

For a 1oo1 architecture, assuming $MTR \approx 0$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2}$$

**Case 1: $TI = 18$ months $= 18 \times 730 = 13140$ hours**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 13140}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{5.256 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.628 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Case 2: $TI = 4$ years $= 4 \times 8760 = 35040$ hours**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 35040}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1.4016 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 7.008 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Comparison with IEC 61511 SIL target ranges:**

| SIL | Target PFDavg range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

- 18 months: $PFDavg = 2.628 \times 10^{-3}$ → falls in the **SIL 2** band ✓
- 4 years: $PFDavg = 7.008 \times 10^{-3}$ → still falls in the **SIL 2** band, but is much closer to the SIL 1 boundary.

**Conclusion:**

Extending the test interval from 18 months to 4 years more than doubles the PFDavg, eroding the safety margin within SIL 2. The procedure should therefore specify a maximum test interval of approximately 18 months to maintain a comfortable SIL 2 margin. A 4-year interval is technically still within the SIL 2 range but leaves little allowance for the realistic non-zero MTR; it is **not recommended**.

### Question B – Alternative Choice (Same Marks, 14 Total)

**Q(a) – 7 Marks:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Procedure Development Lifecycle** from the identification of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) to the periodic review of the approved procedure. Highlight the role of the **Management of Change (MOC)** trigger in the loop.

**Q(b) – 7 Marks:** Explain the **Proof Test Procedure** in detail. Why is it considered the most important maintenance procedure in an SIS? Derive the 1oo1 PFDavg formula and explain its procedural implication for the choice of test interval.

**Model Answer – Q(a):**

The procedure development lifecycle consists of the following steps (block diagram is described textually as a Mermaid ASCII equivalent):

```
[ Identify SIF from SRS ]
            |
            v
[ Identify Human Tasks ]
            |
            v
[ Human Error Analysis ]
            |
            v
[ Draft Procedure in Standardized Format ]
            |
            v
[ Multi-Discipline Review ]
            |
            v
[ Approval by Designated Authority ]
            |
            v
[ Validation by Walk-through / Simulation ]
            |
            v
[ Distribute as Controlled Document ]
            |
            v
[ Train Personnel ]
            |
            v
[ Periodic Use ] <-----> [ Periodic Review ]
            |
            v
      { Any Change? }
       /          \
     Yes           No
     /               \
[MOC Trigger]    [Continue Use]
     |
     v
[Re-evaluate Procedure]
     |
     v
[Return to Drafting]
```

**Role of MOC:**

The MOC trigger is the safety valve of the lifecycle. It ensures that no change — to the process, the SIS, the procedure, or the personnel — is silently introduced. When triggered, the procedure is re-evaluated for its impact on the achieved SIL, and if necessary, re-drafted, re-reviewed, and re-approved. This keeps the safety case valid throughout the operational life of the plant.

**Model Answer – Q(b):**

The Proof Test Procedure governs the **periodic, controlled verification** that each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) still meets its target **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** and therefore its target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

**Why it is the most important maintenance procedure:**

A SIF is a *dormant* protection layer — it sits there silently for years, doing nothing, until a dangerous event occurs. The only way to know that it will still function on demand is to **test it**. If the proof test is missed, skipped, or poorly executed, the SIF may be in a failed state, and the operator (and the public) will not know until the dangerous event actually happens. Hence, proof test procedures are arguably the single most important administrative safeguard in the entire SIS framework.

**Derivation of 1oo1 PFDavg formula (4 marks):**

The dangerous unavailability of a 1oo1 channel at time $t$ after a successful proof test is:

$$U(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t$$

Averaging over the test interval $TI$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D}{TI} \cdot \frac{TI^{2}}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2}$$

**Procedural implication (3 marks):**

The PFDavg is **directly proportional to the test interval $TI$**. Therefore, the proof test procedure must specify a **maximum allowable test interval** for each SIF, calculated so that the resulting PFDavg falls within the target SIL band with a safety margin. In practice, the actual test interval is set to **50–70 %** of this maximum to allow for schedule slippage. The proof test record must include the **date of test, the result (pass/fail), the name of the tester, and any follow-up action**, and must be retained for the lifetime of the SIF.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse PFD with PFH.** PFDavg is for low-demand mode (used in process industries); PFH is for high-demand or continuous mode (used in machinery and aerospace). The 1oo1 PFDavg formula is **not** applicable to PFH calculations.
> 2. **Always state the MTR assumption.** If you set MTR = 0 in the derivation, say so explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if the MTR is silently ignored.
> 3. **Do not skip the procedural recommendation.** A numerical answer to a proof-test question without a procedural recommendation (i.e., "the test interval should be at most X months") is considered incomplete and will lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **List all seven procedure categories** when asked. Many students list only three or four and lose 1 mark per missing item.
> 5. **In bypass questions, always mention compensating measures.** This is the single most-examined detail in the bypass procedure topic.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU revision):**

- **Definition:** Procedure Development is the systematic creation and maintenance of instructions for safe operation, maintenance, testing, and modification of SIFs, per IEC 61511.
- **Seven categories of procedures:** Operating, Alarm/Trip Response, Maintenance, Proof Test, Bypass/Override, Commissioning, and Management of Change.
- **Anatomy of a good procedure:** Title, Purpose, References, Definitions, Roles, Pre-conditions, Step-by-step actions, Safety precautions, Hold points, Validation criteria, Records, Annexures.
- **Five-step development methodology:** Identify SIF → Identify human tasks → Human error analysis → Write in standard format → Review, approve, validate, distribute.
- **1oo1 PFDavg formula:** $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot TI / 2$ (valid for $MTR \approx 0$).
- **1oo2 PFDavg formula:** $PFD_{avg,1oo2} \approx \lambda_D^{2} \cdot TI^{2} / 6$.
- **SIL target ranges (low-demand mode):** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **Bypass procedure must have:** Written authorization, time limit, compensating measures, bypass register, removal and re-test, audit trail.
- **Bypass time limit:** A bypass exceeding ~30 days is treated as a modification and triggers MOC.
- **MOC triggers:** Process change, SIS hardware change, SIS software change, procedure change, personnel change, long-term bypass.
- **Proof test interval:** Must be specified per SIF; longer TI → higher PFDavg → lower achieved SIL.
- **First-out indication:** Always read before silencing the alarm; preserves diagnostic information.
- **PSSR:** Pre-Startup Safety Review is mandatory before plant startup after construction or major modification.
- **LOTO:** Lock Out Tag Out is mandatory during any maintenance activity on SIS components.
- **Why procedures matter:** They are the **administrative safeguard** that complements the engineering safeguards (sensors, logic solver, valves). They mitigate human error, which is the cause of **70–80 %** of process industry incidents.
- **Standard reference:** IEC 61511-1:2016, clauses 11.5 (Operation), 13 (Validation), 14 (Modification), 16 (Operation, Maintenance, Modification), 17 (Verification, Validation, and Assessment).
- **Examiner's mantra:** "*Structure your answer with headers and bullet points. Define every term. Always state the procedural recommendation after a numerical result. Never skip MOC.*"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction, Installation, and Commissioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction, Installation, and Commissioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** governed by **IEC 61511** (the functional safety standard for the process industry sector) and the **2024 KTU PEICT862** syllabus, **Construction, Installation, and Commissioning (CIC)** refers to the *realization phase* of the overall safety lifecycle. It is the set of engineering, procurement, and field-execution activities that convert a fully validated Safety Requirements Specification (SRS) into a **physically installed, verified, and operationally proven** safety system that achieves the required **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** in service.

According to **IEC 61511-1:2016 / ISA 84.00.01**, the CIC phase sits between detailed engineering (design) and operation & maintenance. It includes:

1. **Construction** – Physical fabrication, mounting, cabling, and assembly of SIS equipment in accordance with engineering drawings and standards.
2. **Installation** – Site erection, positioning, wiring, earthing, and environmental protection of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements.
3. **Commissioning** – The systematic, documented process of verifying that the installed SIS performs exactly as specified in the SRS, including loop checks, functional tests, and proof tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The construction, installation, and commissioning phase is treated as the *physical realization* of a *functionally designed* SIS. Examiners expect students to link every CIC activity back to the **Safety Lifecycle** phases (Phases 8, 9, 10, 11 of IEC 61511) and to emphasize **traceability, documentation, and competency** as the three pillars of compliance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital's **emergency power system** (the analogy for a Safety Instrumented System). The architect's blueprint (the SRS) says: "If the main power fails, the backup generator must start within 10 seconds, the life-support ventilators must remain running, and the alarm system must flash red in ICU."

- **Construction** is the activity of *building* the generator room, fabricating the switchgear panels, and laying the dedicated power cables according to the blueprint.
- **Installation** is the *physical placement* of the generator on its vibration pads, connecting it to the fuel line, and terminating the control wiring at the ATS (Automatic Transfer Switch).
- **Commissioning** is the *live test* where an engineer deliberately cuts the main power and **stops a stopwatch** to confirm the backup starts in ≤ 10 seconds, the ventilators stay ON, and the alarms trigger. If anything fails, it is corrected and re-tested — **with photographs and signed test sheets** as evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a hospital cannot accept an emergency power system that was *designed beautifully* but never physically tested, an SIS cannot be declared "in service" without rigorous, **documented** commissioning. The "test sheet" is, in many ways, more legally important than the design itself when an incident occurs.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standards

The following international standards, codes, and metrics govern SIS construction, installation, and commissioning:

| Standard / Metric | Role in CIC |
|---|---|
| **IEC 61511-1:2016** | Defines safety lifecycle phases 8 (installation), 9 (commissioning), 10 (safety validation) |
| **IEC 61508** | Underlying functional safety standard (for SIS manufacturers) |
| **ISA RP 84.00.04** | Part 2 — Guidance for installation, commissioning, and operation |
| **NFPA 72** | Fire alarm system installation (where applicable) |
| **IEC 60079 / IS/IEC 60079** | Explosive atmospheres — Ex d, Ex e, Ex i protection |
| **IP Code (IEC 60529)** | Ingress protection — typical SIS cabinets: **IP54 / IP65** |
| **EMC Directive 2014/30/EU** | Electromagnetic compatibility for industrial environments |
| **Earthing resistance** | Typically **< 1 Ω** for SIS, **< 5 Ω** for general plant |
| **Cable segregation** | SIS vs. non-SIS: minimum **100 mm** parallel run, **1 m** from high-power VFDs |
| **Loop test voltage** | Typically **500 V DC** for insulation resistance (≥ **100 MΩ**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Memorize the *three lifecycle phases* (Installation, Commissioning, Validation) and the *one key output of each* — Installation produces the *as-built documentation*, Commissioning produces the *SAT report*, Validation produces the *Safety Validation Report*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase 8: Installation — The Structured Logic of Field Execution

Installation is *not* simply "wiring things up." It is a controlled engineering activity that must preserve the assumptions made during the SIL verification. If installation deviates from the design (wrong cable type, longer cable length, higher ambient temperature), the **PFDavg (Probability of Failure on Demand average)** can change, invalidating the SIL.

The installation logic proceeds in **five disciplined steps**:

1. **Pre-Installation Review**
   - Confirm that engineering drawings, datasheets, and hook-ups are **Issued for Construction (IFC)**.
   - Verify that equipment is **SIL-rated** (manufacturer's certificate of conformity).
   - Confirm qualified personnel and PPE compliance.

2. **Mechanical Installation**
   - Mount sensors in correct orientation, with sufficient **straight pipe runs** upstream/downstream (e.g., orifice plates typically require **10D upstream / 5D downstream**).
   - Ensure logic solver (PLC/SIS controller) is mounted in a **dedicated cabinet** with proper ventilation, dust protection, and physical access control.
   - Final elements (e.g., shutdown valves) installed with correct **gasket, bolt torque, and actuator alignment**.

3. **Electrical Installation**
   - **Cable selection** – Use **screened, twisted-pair (STP)** or **armoured** cables; conductor size as per voltage drop and loop resistance calculations.
   - **Segregation** – SIS cables separated from non-SIS by minimum distances (see formula sheet).
   - **Earthing / grounding** – Single-point star ground at the SIS cabinet; shield grounded at one end only (or both ends with care to avoid ground loops).
   - **Surge protection** – On field cables entering the control room (especially for outdoor SIS).

4. **Identification & Labelling**
   - Every cable, terminal, junction box, and instrument tagged with a **unique SIS tag number**.
   - Colour coding: typically **red** for SIS field cables (KTU 2024 convention), **yellow** for non-SIS, **green/yellow** for protective earth.
   - Labels must be **mechanically engraved or printed**, not hand-written.

5. **As-Built Documentation**
   - All field deviations are recorded in **"red-line" markups** that are then converted into updated drawings.
   - A complete **As-Built dossier** is compiled: hook-up drawings, cable schedules, termination diagrams, loop drawings, instrument data sheets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (the "Why"):** SIL verification is performed on a *paper design*. If field installation introduces extra resistance, extra capacitance, higher ambient temperature, or wrong response time, the *actual* installed PFDavg may be **higher** (worse) than the design PFDavg. The SIS may be operating at a *lower* SIL than intended — this is a **functional safety violation** that audits will catch.

### 2.2 Phase 9: Commissioning — The Logical Verification Chain

Commissioning is the *proof* that the installed SIS matches the SRS. It is a **bottom-up** verification:

1. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** – Performed at the manufacturer's facility, before shipment. Validates the logic solver and panel wiring.
2. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** – Performed after installation at the site. Validates the *complete installed SIS* in its real environment.
3. **Pre-Commissioning Checks** (a subset of SAT, non-functional):
   - Visual inspection, dimensional checks, electrical continuity, insulation resistance (megger test), earthing resistance.
4. **Loop Checks** – End-to-end electrical verification from sensor, through cabling, to logic solver I/O, to final element.
5. **Functional Tests** – Proves that the SIS *performs its safety function* under simulated process conditions. Each safety instrumented function (SIF) is tested:
   - **SIF-001**: "If pressure in vessel V-101 exceeds X bar, then close block valve ESD-101 within Y seconds."
   - The actual response time is measured and compared to the SRS specification.
6. **Proof Tests** – Periodic test of the SIF to reveal hidden failures. For new commissioning, this is the **first proof test**; the **proof test interval (T_proof)** is logged.

### 2.3 Phase 10: Safety Validation

Although technically a *separate* phase, in KTU exam questions the validation step is often grouped with commissioning. **Safety Validation** confirms that the *installed and commissioned* SIS meets the SRS in the *real operating context* — including bypasses, overrides, alarms, and human factors.

The output is the **Safety Validation Report**, signed by an independent competent person.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Standard Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PFDavg upper bound for SIL | $10^{-1}$ (SIL 1) to $10^{-5}$ (SIL 4) | Probability of Failure on Demand |
| 2 | PFDsys for independent subsystems | $PFD_{SYS} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} PFD_i$ | Sum for low-demand mode |
| 3 | Insulation resistance (megger) | $R_{iso} \ge 100\ \text{M}\Omega$ at **500 V DC** | Between conductor & earth |
| 4 | Earth resistance (SIS reference) | $R_{earth} < 1\ \Omega$ (target) | Single-point star ground |
| 5 | Earth resistance (general plant) | $R_{earth} < 5\ \Omega$ | Per IS 3043 |
| 6 | Cable segregation distance | $d_{seg} \ge 100\ \text{mm}$ from non-SIS, $\ge 1000\ \text{mm}$ from VFD/HT | Parallel run |
| 7 | Minimum bend radius (SIS cable) | $R_{bend} \ge 8 \times D$ (D = cable OD) | Manufacturer's spec overrides |
| 8 | Max voltage drop on SIS loop | $V_{drop} \le 1\%$ of supply (signal), $3\%$ (power) | At full load |
| 9 | Proof test interval | $T_{proof}$ (hours or months) | Set so $PFD_{avg}$ remains within SIL target |
| 10 | Straight pipe length (orifice) | $10D$ upstream, $5D$ downstream | D = pipe ID |
| 11 | Ambient temp (SIS cabinet) | $T_{amb} \le 40^\circ\text{C}$ typical, $\le 60^\circ\text{C}$ max | Affects MTBF |
| 12 | Enclosure ingress protection | **IP54** (indoor), **IP65 / IP66** (outdoor/hazardous) | IEC 60529 |
| 13 | Test bypass duration | $T_{bypass} \le$ SRS-specified max | To avoid process risk during test |
| 14 | EMC test level (industrial) | **3 V/m** (IEC 61000-4-3) | Field immunity |
| 15 | Surge withstand (comms/IO) | **1 kV / 2 kV** (line-line / line-earth) | IEC 61000-4-5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These numbers and formulas appear in *every* KTU exam question on this topic. Examiner valuation specifically checks whether students *cite the standard* (e.g., "as per IEC 61511 clause 16") and *quantify* values (e.g., "$R_{earth} < 1\ \Omega$") rather than giving vague qualitative answers.

### 2.5 Engineering Field Application

In a real **hydrocarbon refinery** or **LNG plant**, the SIS typically protects against overpressure, overtemperature, gas detection, and fire scenarios. The CIC phase is the *most expensive* lifecycle phase (often 25–35% of total SIS project cost) because:

- It involves thousands of man-hours of skilled labour.
- It requires process **shutdowns or bypasses** to safely execute live tests.
- Documentation must be **legally auditable** for the next 20+ years of operation.

A poorly executed commissioning can result in:
- **Common-cause failures** that defeat redundancy.
- **Latent failures** that are not detected until the first real demand.
- **Legal liability** in the event of an incident, because the *Safety Validation Report* is the primary evidence in court.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping CIC Activities to Standards

This matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 tool that examiners reward because it shows *systematic understanding*:

| Activity | IEC 61511 Clause | Key Output Document | Competent Person? | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Construction review | Clause 5.2.6.1.5 | Inspection & Test Plan (ITP) | Yes | Verifies build conforms to spec |
| Cable laying | Clause 11.5.2 | Cable schedule + megger record | Yes | Detects insulation failure pre-energisation |
| Instrument mounting | Clause 11.5.3 | Hook-up as-built | Yes | Preserves process measurement accuracy |
| Logic solver installation | Clause 11.5.4 | Panel as-built + earth record | Yes | Prevents ground loops & EMC issues |
| Pre-commissioning | Clause 12.1 | Punch list (snag list) | Yes | Closes gaps before live tests |
| Loop checking | Clause 12.2 | Loop check sheets (one per loop) | Yes | End-to-end electrical integrity |
| Functional safety testing | Clause 12.3 | SAT report | Yes | Proves SIF performance vs. SRS |
| Proof test (initial) | Clause 16.2.1 | Proof test record | Yes | Sets the baseline for T_proof |
| Safety validation | Clause 13 | Safety Validation Report | Independent competent | Final go-live authority |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 1: Effect of Cable Length on Loop Response Time

**Problem statement:** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) requires a shutdown valve (ESD-101) to close within **2.0 seconds** of a high-high pressure trip signal from transmitter PT-101. The logic solver (a SIL 3 PLC) has a scan time of **50 ms**. The transmitter output is a 4–20 mA signal transmitted over a **shielded twisted-pair cable** of total length **350 m**. Determine if the cable propagation delay is acceptable.

**Given:**
- Cable length $L = 350\ \text{m}$
- Propagation speed of signal in cable: typically $v \approx 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ (≈ 2/3 speed of light for typical PVC-insulated copper cable)
- Logic solver scan time $t_{scan} = 50\ \text{ms} = 0.050\ \text{s}$
- Solenoid response time $t_{sol} = 200\ \text{ms} = 0.200\ \text{s}$
- Valve stroking time $t_{valve} = 800\ \text{ms} = 0.800\ \text{s}$
- Total allowed response time per SRS: $t_{SRS} = 2.0\ \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the cable propagation delay:**

$$
t_{cable} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{350}{2 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
t_{cable} = 1.75 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 1.75\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret:** This is **1.75 microseconds**, which is **negligible** compared to a millisecond-scale scan time. The cable delay is many orders of magnitude smaller than the mechanical response times.

**Step 3 — Compute the total measured response time:**

$$
t_{total} = t_{cable} + t_{scan} + t_{sol} + t_{valve}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 0.00000175 + 0.050 + 0.200 + 0.800
$$

$$
t_{total} = 1.05000175\ \text{s} \approx 1.05\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the SRS:**

$$
t_{total} = 1.05\ \text{s} < t_{SRS} = 2.0\ \text{s} \quad \checkmark\ \text{PASS}
$$

**Step 5 — Margin available for future degradation:**

$$
M = t_{SRS} - t_{total} = 2.0 - 1.05 = 0.95\ \text{s}
$$

This 950 ms margin is essential to absorb:
- Ageing of solenoid (slower pull-in)
- Increased valve friction over years
- Increased logic solver scan time under heavy CPU load

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This problem illustrates a key CIC principle — the *installed* response time must be **measured** during commissioning, not *assumed* from data sheets. If the measured $t_{total}$ exceeds $t_{SRS}$, the system **fails validation** and must be redesigned (faster valve, faster logic solver, etc.).

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 2: Proof Test Interval from PFDavg Constraint

**Problem statement:** A SIL 2 SIF consists of a single pressure transmitter (PFD = $4 \times 10^{-3}$), a logic solver (PFD = $1 \times 10^{-3}$), and a single shut-down valve (PFD = $5 \times 10^{-2}$). All failures are assumed to be detected only by proof testing. For a SIL 2 target, the maximum allowable $PFD_{SYS}$ is $10^{-2}$ (actually $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, with $10^{-2}$ used as the conservative upper bound for SIL 2). Calculate the **maximum allowable proof test interval**.

**Given:**
- PFD of transmitter: $PFD_{PT} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$
- PFD of logic solver: $PFD_{LS} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$
- PFD of valve: $PFD_{V} = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
- Target: $PFD_{SYS} < 1 \times 10^{-2}$

**Step 1 — Apply the 1001 architecture summation:**

For a low-demand mode, 1-out-of-1 (1001) architecture:

$$
PFD_{SYS} = PFD_{PT} + PFD_{LS} + PFD_{V}
$$

$$
PFD_{SYS} = 4 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{SYS} = 54 \times 10^{-3} = 5.4 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Check against target:** $5.4 \times 10^{-2} > 1 \times 10^{-2}$ → **FAILS SIL 2 target**.

**Step 3 — Engineering solution:** Two options are common:
- **Option A (architecture change):** Use a **1oo2 voting** for the transmitter and the valve. For 1oo2, $PFD_{1oo2} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2}{3}$ (much lower than 1oo1).
- **Option B (proof test interval reduction):** Reduce $T_{proof}$ to bring the average PFD down.

**Step 4 — For option B, recall the simplified PFD formula:**

$$
PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

For the valve (the dominant contributor), $PFD_V = 5 \times 10^{-2}$. Suppose $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{hr}$ (dangerous failure rate). Then:

$$
T_{proof} = \frac{2 \cdot PFD_V}{\lambda_D} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
T_{proof} = \frac{0.1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{4}\ \text{hr}
$$

Converting to years (8760 hr/year):

$$
T_{proof} = \frac{20{,}000}{8760} \approx 2.28\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 10% rule for $PFD_{SYS}$ to valve alone:** If we cap the valve's contribution at 50% of the SIL 2 budget (i.e., $\le 5 \times 10^{-3}$):

$$
T_{proof,\,new} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2{,}000\ \text{hr} \approx 88\ \text{days}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination pitfall:** Many students forget the factor of **2** in the $\frac{\lambda_D T}{2}$ formula, or confuse the 1oo1 vs. 1oo2 PFD formulas. The 1oo2 formula is $PFD_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D t)^2}{3}$, which is *quadratic* in the proof test interval — making it very favourable for high SIL.

### 3.4 Worked Derivation 3: Earth Resistance — Fall-of-Potential Method

**Problem statement:** During commissioning, an engineer measures the earth resistance of an SIS cabinet using the **fall-of-potential method**. The auxiliary electrode is placed at 40 m from the SIS earth electrode, the probe is moved along the line, and resistance readings are: at 20 m → 0.85 Ω, at 25 m → 0.92 Ω, at 30 m → 0.94 Ω, at 35 m → 0.93 Ω, at 40 m → 0.91 Ω. Determine the **true earth resistance** and compare with the standard.

**Step 1 — Identify the flat region:** In the fall-of-potential method, the true earth resistance corresponds to the **plateau** in the resistance-vs-distance curve. The values at 30, 35, 40 m are clustered around **0.91–0.94 Ω**.

**Step 2 — Take the average of the plateau:**

$$
R_{earth} = \frac{0.94 + 0.93 + 0.91}{3} = \frac{2.78}{3} = 0.927\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Round and report:**

$$
R_{earth} \approx 0.93\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with standard:** $R_{earth} = 0.93\ \Omega < 1\ \Omega$ → **PASSES** the SIS criterion.

**Step 5 — Action:** Record the value in the **Earth Test Record** (a required commissioning document), signed and dated. The test instrument (e.g., a Megger DET2/2 or equivalent) calibration certificate must also be filed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Examiners often test whether students can *interpret* field measurement data, not just recite standards. Always show the units, the comparison with the standard, and the **documentation step** — these are the three valuation triggers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB --> nodeC[Phase 6 7: SIS Design and Engineering]

    nodeE --> nodeF[Phase 10: Safety Validation]
    nodeF --> nodeG{All SIFs Validated?}
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH([Phase 11: Operation and Maintenance])
    nodeG -->|No| nodeI[Punch List and Rework]
    nodeI --> nodeD

    nodeD --- nodeD1[Pre installation review]
    nodeD --- nodeD2[Mechanical mounting]
    nodeD --- nodeD3[Cable laying and termination]
    nodeD --- nodeD4[Earthing and EMC]
    nodeD --- nodeD5[As built documentation]

    nodeE --- nodeE1[Pre commissioning checks]
```
