
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart shows the iterative nature of CIC — if validation fails, the system returns to installation and commissioning (the "Punch List" loop). This is exactly how a real SIS project operates.

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: SIS Block Diagram Showing CIC Touchpoints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIELD[Field Area - Hazardous Zone]
        PT[PT 101 Pressure Transmitter SIL 2]
        TT[TT 102 Temperature Transmitter SIL 2]
        FT[FT 103 Flow Transmitter SIL 2]
        XV[ESD 101 Shutdown Valve SIL 2]
    end

    subgraph JUNCTION[Junction Boxes - IP65]
        JB1[JB SIS 001 Red Tag]
        JB2[JB SIS 002 Red Tag]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Room - Safe Area]
        SISPLC[SIS Logic Solver PLC SIL 3 in Cabinet IP54]
        BCS[DCS Basic Process Control System]
        ENG[Engineering Workstation for SAT]
    end

    PT -->|STP cable 4 to 20 mA red conduit| JB1
    TT -->|STP cable RTD red conduit| JB1
    FT -->|STP cable HART red conduit| JB2
    JB1 -->|Multi core armoured red| SISPLC
    JB2 -->|Multi core armoured red| SISPLC
    SISPLC -->|Output 24 VDC solenoid| XV
    SISPLC ---|Ethernet Modbus TCP isolated| BCS
    SISPLC ---|Serial RS485 for SAT| ENG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Notice three design choices enforced at CIC: (1) the SIS field cables are **physically segregated** from the DCS, (2) all junction boxes are tagged with the *red* SIS colour code, and (3) the BCS (Basic Control System) is **isolated** from the SIS — the SIS must function *independently* even if the DCS fails.

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Commissioning Test Sequence for One SIF

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Eng as Commissioning Engineer
    participant PT as PT 101 Transmitter
    participant SIS as SIS Logic Solver
    participant XV as ESD 101 Shutdown Valve
    participant Rec as Test Record Sheet

    Note over Eng,Rec: Step 1 Pre Test Safety
    Eng->>Rec: Sign permit to work, attach bypass authorisation
    Eng->>PT: Apply calibrated pressure source 0 to 100 percent
    PT-->>SIS: 4 to 20 mA signal rises
    SIS-->>Rec: Log trip setpoint reached at 18.4 mA

    Note over Eng,Rec: Step 2 Functional Test
    Eng->>PT: Force pressure above HH setpoint
    PT-->>SIS: Signal exceeds HH threshold
    SIS->>XV: Energise solenoid 24 VDC
    XV-->>Rec: Valve stroke start time t0
    Note right of XV: Physical valve closes
    XV-->>Rec: Valve fully closed at t1
    Eng->>Rec: Compute t1 minus t0 equals stroking time
    Eng->>Rec: Compare against SRS limit 2.0 seconds

    Note over Eng,Rec: Step 3 Reset and Document
    Eng->>SIS: Reset trip via authorised key switch
    Eng->>XV: Confirm valve opens on reset
    Eng->>Rec: Sign and date, attach photographs, archive in SAT report
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence matters:** KTU 2024 examiners specifically test the *order* of commissioning steps. The required order is: **(a) safety permit, (b) bypass authorisation, (c) signal injection, (d) trip verification, (e) reset, (f) documentation**. Reversing this order is a *common student error* and loses marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
> *Define the term "Commissioning" in the context of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). List any **three** key outputs of the commissioning phase.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Commissioning, as defined in **IEC 61511-1:2016 (Clause 12)**, is the *systematic, documented process of verifying and validating* that the installed Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — including all sensors, logic solvers, final elements, and associated cabling — performs in accordance with the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)**. Commissioning encompasses pre-commissioning checks, loop checks, functional tests, and the initial proof test.

**Three key outputs (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT) Report** — signed and dated.
2. **Punch list / snag list** — closed before hand-over.
3. **Updated as-built documentation** and **loop check sheets** for every SIF.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
> *State **three** essential differences between Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) and Site Acceptance Test (SAT) for an SIS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | FAT | SAT |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Performed at the **manufacturer's facility**, before shipment | Performed at the **actual site**, after installation |
| 2 | Validates the **logic solver and panel** in isolation | Validates the **complete installed SIS** including field cabling, sensors, and final elements |
| 3 | Does **not** test the actual process response | Tests the **real installed response time** of the SIF against the SRS specification |
| 4 (bonus) | Witnessed by **client engineering team** | Witnessed by **client operations and safety team** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student mistake is to state that FAT and SAT are "the same test done twice." This is **wrong** — they test *different scopes*. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this confusion. The correct distinction is **scope** (panel vs. full loop) and **location** (factory vs. site).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Describe in detail the **phased activities of installation** of a Safety Instrumented System as required by **IEC 61511**. Mention at least **four** activities and the documents produced. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Explain the concept of **proof testing** in an SIS. A SIL 2 SIF has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 6 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{hr}$ and uses a 1oo1 architecture. Calculate the **maximum proof test interval** such that the PFDavg remains below $5 \times 10^{-3}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Installation Activities (7 marks):**

The installation phase (IEC 61511 Clause 11) consists of the following activities:

1. **Pre-Installation Review (1 mark):** Verify the *Issued for Construction (IFC)* drawings, check that the equipment is SIL-rated by the manufacturer, confirm that installation personnel are **competent** (per Clause 5.2.6.1.5), and confirm site readiness.
2. **Mechanical Mounting (1 mark):** Install sensors with correct orientation, sufficient straight pipe runs (e.g., 10D/5D for orifice), proper bracket supports, and ingress protection. Mount final elements (valves) with correct gasket, bolt torque, and actuator alignment.
3. **Electrical Cabling and Termination (2 marks):** Use **screened twisted-pair (STP) or armoured cables** for SIS, maintain physical segregation from non-SIS cables (≥ 100 mm parallel), terminate at dedicated **red-tagged junction boxes**, ensure shield earthing follows single-point star ground scheme, install surge protection for outdoor field cables.
4. **Identification and Labelling (1 mark):** Every cable, terminal, instrument, and junction box must carry a **unique SIS tag number**. Use **red colour coding** for SIS circuits. Use engraved labels (not handwritten).
5. **As-Built Documentation (1 mark):** All field deviations are recorded in *red-line markups* and converted into updated drawings. A complete As-Built dossier is handed over.
6. **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP) (1 mark):** A formal document that records each installation step, the standard reference, the acceptance criteria, and the signature of the competent person.

**Part (b) — Proof Test Calculation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** A proof test is a *periodic* test of a SIF to reveal **dangerous undetected failures** that would otherwise remain latent. The average probability of failure on demand, PFDavg, depends linearly on the proof test interval $T_{proof}$ for a 1oo1 architecture.

**Formula (2 marks):** For a 1oo1 architecture with periodic proof testing:

$$
PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Given (1 mark):** $\lambda_D = 6 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{hr}$, target $PFD_{avg} < 5 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
T_{proof} < \frac{2 \cdot PFD_{avg}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
T_{proof} < \frac{10^{-2}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.667 \times 10^{3}\ \text{hr}
$$

**Result in years (1 mark):**

$$
T_{proof} < \frac{1667}{8760} \approx 0.19\ \text{years} \approx 70\ \text{days}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The proof test interval must not exceed approximately **70 days** (≈ 10 weeks) to maintain SIL 2 compliance for this SIF.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Safety Instrumented System** and indicate which elements of the architecture are affected by the construction, installation, and commissioning phase. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Discuss the **role of documentation** in the construction, installation, and commissioning of an SIS. List **six** essential documents produced during this phase. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and CIC Touchpoints (7 marks):**

A typical SIS functional architecture (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram) consists of three layers:

1. **Sensor layer** (3 marks) — Field-mounted transmitters (pressure, temperature, flow, level, gas detectors) that detect the process deviation. *CIC touchpoints:* correct mounting orientation, proper cable routing, calibration verification, and loop check.
2. **Logic Solver layer** (2 marks) — The SIL-rated PLC / safety controller that processes the input signals and executes the safety logic. *CIC touchpoints:* cabinet installation in a dust-free, temperature-controlled room; proper earthing; EMC verification; FAT before shipment; SAT on site.
3. **Final Element layer** (2 marks) — The actuator and valve (or contactor, or pump shutdown) that physically brings the process to a safe state. *CIC touchpoints:* correct gasket and torque, alignment of actuator, stroking time measurement, end-to-end functional test.

The construction, installation, and commissioning phase touches **all three layers** plus the **cabling infrastructure** that links them.

**Part (b) — Role of Documentation and Essential Documents (7 marks):**

**Role of documentation (2 marks):** Documentation in CIC serves three critical purposes: (i) *legal evidence* in case of an incident, (ii) *traceability* of every design decision to the installed hardware, and (iii) *audit readiness* for regulators and certifying bodies.

**Six essential documents (5 marks — ≈ 0.8 each):**

1. **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** — Records every step of the construction process with acceptance criteria and signatures.
2. **Cable Schedule and Megger Test Records** — Lists every SIS cable, its route, length, and the insulation resistance measured.
3. **Earth Test Record** — Documents the earth resistance measured at every SIS cabinet and field instrument.
4. **Loop Check Sheets** — One per SIF loop, recording the end-to-end electrical verification.
5. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) Report** — Records all panel-level tests at the manufacturer's facility.
6. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT) Report** — Records all field-level functional tests, including measured response times vs. SRS.
7. **Safety Validation Report** — Final sign-off by an independent competent person, declaring the SIS ready for service.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):** When asked about CIC, students often *only* describe the *physical activities* (wiring, mounting) and *forget the documentation*. In the KTU 2024 scheme, documentation is **explicitly worth 4–5 marks** in any 14-mark question on this topic. A 14-mark answer that ignores documentation can score **at most 8–9 marks** — a guaranteed loss of 5+ marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4 (CIC):**

- ☐ **CIC = Phases 8, 9, 10** of the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle (Installation, Commissioning, Safety Validation).
- ☐ **Three pillars of CIC:** Traceability, Documentation, Competency of personnel.
- ☐ **Three logical layers of an SIS:** Sensor, Logic Solver, Final Element — CIC touches all three.
- ☐ **Cable segregation:** ≥ **100 mm** from non-SIS, ≥ **1000 mm** from VFD/HT lines; SIS cables are **red** colour coded.
- ☐ **Earth resistance target:** **< 1 Ω** at the SIS cabinet (single-point star ground), **< 5 Ω** general plant (per IS 3043).
- ☐ **Insulation resistance (megger):** ≥ **100 MΩ** at **500 V DC**.
- ☐ **Pre-commissioning** is *static* (visual, megger, earth); **commissioning** is *dynamic* (loop check, functional test).
- ☐ **FAT** validates the panel at the factory; **SAT** validates the complete installed loop at the site.
- ☐ **Proof test** reveals latent dangerous failures; $PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}}{2}$ for 1oo1.
- ☐ **SRS** is the *contract* — every CIC activity must be traceable to an SRS clause.
- ☐ **Safety Validation Report** is the *final go-live authority*, signed by an **independent competent person**.
- ☐ **The 1oo2 architecture** reduces PFD by a *square factor* of the failure rate; preferred for SIL 2 and above.
- ☐ **Commissioning sequence (memorise order):** Permit → Bypass → Inject → Trip → Measure → Reset → Document.
- ☐ **Cited standards in exam answers:** IEC 61511 (SIS sector), IEC 61508 (generic), IS 3043 (earthing), IEC 60529 (IP code), IEC 60079 (Ex protection).
- ☐ **Common cause failures** are defeated by *separation* — physical, electrical, and logical — all enforced at CIC.
- ☐ **Proof test intervals are set at commissioning** and must be logged for the entire 20+ year operational life.
- ☐ **Cable propagation delay is negligible** (µs scale) compared to mechanical stroking times (hundreds of ms).
- ☐ **The most expensive SIS lifecycle phase** is CIC (≈ 25–35% of total project cost).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Startup Acceptance Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Startup Acceptance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Startup Acceptance Testing (SAT)** is the formal, structured, and documented on-site verification procedure executed after the installation of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) at the plant site, intended to confirm that the system has been installed in accordance with the approved engineering design documents, that all individual components (sensors, logic solvers, final control elements) and integrated loops perform exactly as specified in the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS), and that the system is fully ready—safe, reliable, and compliant—to assume its designated safety instrumented function (SIF) prior to the introduction of hazardous process fluids during plant commissioning and start-up.

According to the **IEC 61511** and **ISA 84.00.01** standards referenced in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, SAT is a mandatory phase of the Safety Lifecycle that bridges the gap between hardware installation and live process operation. It is sometimes referred to as **Site Acceptance Test (SiteAT)** and is functionally distinct from, but complementary to, the Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) conducted at the vendor's premises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEICT862 Module 4):**
> Startup Acceptance Testing sits at **Phase 8 (Operation, Maintenance, and Modification) crossover** with **Phase 5 (Installation and Commissioning)** of the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle. SAT is the **final proof** that the SIS is mechanically, electrically, functionally, and documentationally ready for the introduction of process energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have just ordered a custom-built, fully automatic fire-fighting robot for your home (the **SIS**). Before the manufacturing company ships it, they run it in their factory with simulated smoke and fire to ensure every motor, sensor, and nozzle works correctly in isolation — that is the **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)**.

The robot is then delivered to your home. It must now be physically installed in your living room, connected to your home's water pipes, electrical supply, and Wi-Fi. Before you trust it with a real fire emergency (introduction of the hazardous process fluid), you want to test it **right there in your home**, using your real pipes and real power, with simulated smoke cans. You check whether the system as a whole—robot + your home's infrastructure—truly performs end-to-end. This on-site, end-to-end, integrated validation is exactly what **Startup Acceptance Testing** does for an industrial SIS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping Summary:**
> - **Custom Fire Robot** ↔ Safety Instrumented System (SIS)
> - **Factory Test by Vendor** ↔ Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)
> - **Testing in Your Home** ↔ Site Acceptance Test (SAT)
> - **Simulated Smoke Cans** ↔ Bypass of real hazardous process (introduced safely with test media like inert fluids or instrument air)
> - **Real Fire Emergency** ↔ Actual plant start-up with hydrocarbons

## 1.3 Standards, Metrics, and Reference Frameworks (Bolded Constants)

- **IEC 61511-1, Clause 5.2.6.1.3** — *Site Acceptance Test* requirements during the Installation phase.
- **ISA 84.00.01-2004 (IEC 61511 Mod)** — *Site Acceptance Testing* requirements equivalent to IEC 61511.
- **IEC 61508-2, Clause 7.5** — Safe failure fraction and diagnostic coverage validation expectations that SAT must indirectly verify.
- **Typical SAT Duration**: **5 % to 10 %** of total project man-hours (vendor benchmark figure for engineering reference).
- **Acceptance Pass Criterion**: **100 %** of functional tests must pass; deviations must be raised as **Punch List Items (PLI)** and resolved before plant start-up authorisation.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) Validation Target**: Must meet the value assumed during the **PFDavg (Average Probability of Failure on Demand)** calculation per **IEC 61511-1, Clause 11.5.2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Reality Check:**
> When a question asks, *"Why is SAT necessary if FAT was already done?"* — the answer is that **FAT tests the *hardware* against the design specification in isolation** at the factory, while **SAT tests the *integrated installation* against the *as-built, site-specific* conditions** (wiring, grounding, ambient temperature, EMC, cable segregation, loop integrity). The site environment cannot be simulated fully at the factory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of the SAT Process

The Startup Acceptance Testing phase is not a single event. It is an **orderly cascade of sub-tests**, each with its own objective. A KTU-level answer must show awareness of this hierarchy:

- **Stage A — Pre-SAT Planning and Documentation Review**
  - Review of approved **SAT Procedure Document** (often part of the *Commissioning and Start-Up Plan*).
  - Review of **Cause & Effect (C&E) Matrix**, **Logic Diagrams**, **P&IDs**, and **Loop Drawings**.
  - Confirmation that the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** baseline is locked.
  - Verification of calibration certificates for every field device.

- **Stage B — Mechanical and Electrical Completion Verification**
  - Verification of cable glanding, routing, segregation (signal vs power), and termination torque.
  - Verification of **earth/ground continuity** (typically **≤ 1 Ω** at the equipment earth bar).
  - Verification of **insulation resistance** (typically **≥ 100 MΩ** at 500 V DC for instrument loops).
  - Verification of **IP rating** integrity (e.g., **IP 65 / IP 66 / IP 67** for field enclosures per IEC 60529).

- **Stage C — Loop Checks (Individual Field Loops)**
  - **Sensor Loop**: Inject simulated process variable (pressure, temperature, level) at the field sensor and verify the corresponding input at the logic solver (PLC/SIS controller).
  - **Final Element Loop**: Energise the logic solver output and verify correct actuation at the final control element (block valve, solenoid, motor starter).
  - **End-to-End Loop Test**: Simulate the process condition at the sensor and verify correct valve closure/opening along the entire SIF chain.

- **Stage D — Functional Safety Tests (SIF-by-SIF)**
  - For each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), simulate the initiating event and verify:
    - Correct detection by sensor
    - Correct logic execution by logic solver
    - Correct final element action
    - Correct alarms, annunciations, and notifications to the control system
  - Test **each SIF at least once** in normal and bypassed maintenance modes.

- **Stage E — Diagnostic Coverage and Fault Injection Testing**
  - Inject simulated faults (short circuit, open circuit, out-of-range signal) and verify that diagnostic functions detect and annunciate the fault within the time specified in the SRS.
  - Verifies the **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** assumption.

- **Stage F — Power-Up, Power-Down, and Transient Behaviour Tests**
  - Verify that the SIS restarts correctly after power interruption.
  - Verify behaviour on loss of pneumatic supply to actuators (must fail-safe per design).

- **Stage G — Punch List Generation, Closure, and Sign-Off**
  - All deviations recorded as **Punch List Items (PLI)**.
  - Each PLI categorised as **A (blocking)**, **B (non-blocking, time-bound)**, or **C (cosmetic)**.
  - No **Category A** items may remain open at plant start-up.
  - **SAT Report** issued with signatures of Owner, EPC, Vendor, and Independent Safety Assessor (where mandated).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale Behind Each Stage

- **Why Stage A?** A test that is not planned on paper cannot be reliably reproduced or audited under **ISO 9001 / IEC 61511 documentation traceability**.
- **Why Stage B?** Field installation introduces risks (crosstalk, earthing, ingress) that never existed in the controlled factory environment.
- **Why Stage C?** A sensor correctly wired but with reversed polarity, or a valve wired correctly but with a stuck stem, would have been masked during FAT.
- **Why Stage D?** The SIL (Safety Integrity Level) claim is verified **only when the complete SIF chain behaves as modelled** in the SIL calculation.
- **Why Stage E?** Hardware fault tolerance (e.g., 1oo2D, 2oo3 voting) requires the diagnostic to function — otherwise the **PFDavg** figure assumed during design is invalidated.
- **Why Stage F?** A safety system that fails to restart safely after a power dip is itself a hazard — it can lead to a **dangerous failure on demand of the process**.
- **Why Stage G?** Legal and regulatory liability requires traceable, signed acceptance before hazardous introduction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| $PFD_{avg}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand of a SIF | $10^{-1}$ (SIL 1) to $10^{-5}$ (SIL 4) | IEC 61511-1, Table 2 |
| $SFF$ | Safe Failure Fraction of a subsystem | $\geq 60\%$ to $\geq 99\%$ (SIL dependent) | IEC 61508-2, Table 3 |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair (assumed in $PFD$ calc) | $\mathbf{8\ hours}$ (typical bench value) | IEC 61511-1, Clause 11.5.2 |
| $TI$ | Test Interval (proof test interval) | $\mathbf{1\ year}$ to $\mathbf{10\ years}$ (SIF specific) | IEC 61511-1, Clause 16.3.2 |
| $\lambda_D$ | Dangerous failure rate | $\text{fits per hour}$ | IEC 61508-6 |
| $\lambda_S$ | Safe failure rate | $\text{fits per hour}$ | IEC 61508-6 |
| $DC$ | Diagnostic Coverage | $\geq 60\%$ to $\geq 99\%$ | IEC 61508-2 |
| $R_{insulation}$ | Insulation resistance (loop check) | $\geq 100\ M\Omega$ at 500 V DC | Field practice / IEEE 43 |
| $R_{earth}$ | Earth continuity resistance | $\leq 1\ \Omega$ at equipment earth bar | IEEE 1100 / IEC 60364 |
| $V_{test}$ | Insulation test voltage | $500\ V\ DC$ for instrumentation | IEEE 43 |
| $T_{response,SAT}$ | Time for SAT completion per SIF | $4$ to $16\ hours$ per SIF | Vendor benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:**
> Many students write $PFD_{avg}$ without clearly stating it is a *dimensionless* probability (range $\mathbf{0}$ to $\mathbf{1}$, expressed in orders like $10^{-3}$ for SIL 2). Always quote the SIL level alongside the $PFD_{avg}$ value in your answer to fetch the **dedicated 1-mark credit** for linking design intent to verification.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Oil & Gas (Upstream, Midstream, Refinery)**: SAT is performed before hydrocarbons are introduced into a new platform or pipeline.
- **Petrochemicals**: Confirms interlock logic between reactors and emergency shutdown valves (ESDVs).
- **Power Generation**: Verifies turbine overspeed trip, boiler flame failure, and generator protection logic.
- **Pharmaceuticals and Nuclear**: SAT is paired with **Validation and Qualification (V&Q)** per **ISPE** and **NQA-1** standards.
- **Chemical and LNG plants**: Confirms **HIPPS (High Integrity Pressure Protection System)** logic before pressurisation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix, and Worked SAT Reasoning

This section deliberately converts what is often a "remember-the-list" topic into a **rigorous analytical procedure**, deriving each acceptance check as a logical consequence of the SIL claim being validated.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the SAT Verification Logic (Per SIF)

Consider a single Safety Instrumented Function $SIF_i$ that the designer has claimed to meet **SIL 2**. The standard formula for a low-demand SIF (per IEC 61511-1) is:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} PFD(t)\, dt
$$

For a 1oo1 (one out of one, non-redundant) architecture:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2} + \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot TI}{2}
$$

For a 1oo2 (one out of two, redundant) architecture:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI)^2}{3} + \lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR
$$

The full form recognised in KTU examinations is:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} PFD_{i} \quad \text{where} \quad PFD_{i} = \text{failure rate contribution of subsystem } i
$$

**SAT verification uses these equations in reverse.** Each test confirms an *assumption* embedded in the $PFD_{avg}$ calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Design Assumption} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{SAT verifies}}\; \text{Measured Reality} \\[2mm]
\lambda_D \text{ (from manufacturer)} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{Fault injection}}\; \text{Detected within diagnostic time} \\[2mm]
TI \text{ (assumed proof interval)} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{Proof test simulated}}\; \text{Valve actually strokes in } \leq 2\ \text{s} \\[2mm]
MTTR = 8\ \text{h} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{Spare swap simulation}}\; \text{Realistic replacement time} \\[2mm]
\text{Diagnostic Coverage } DC &\;\xrightarrow{\text{Fault simulation}}\; \text{Annunciator + safe state} \\[2mm]
\text{Architecture (1oo2, 2oo3)} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{Subsystem isolation test}}\; \text{Voting logic proven}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic (Exam Tip):**
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, every mention of **$PFD_{avg}$** in a 14-mark question must be tied back to a **specific SAT test step** that validates an assumption inside the $PFD$ equation. Marks are awarded for showing the *forward engineering chain*: SRS → SIL → Architecture → $PFD_{avg}$ → SAT Test Step.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SAT Test to Design Assumption Linkage

| SAT Test Step | Design Assumption Verified | IEC 61511 Reference Clause |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor calibration certificate review | $\lambda_{DU}$ (undetected dangerous failure) per sensor | 11.5.1 |
| Loop end-to-end test | Wiring continuity, correct sensor-to-valve pairing | 12.4.3 |
| Logic solver function test (ladder / Cause-&-Effect) | Logic equivalence to SRS | 11.5.2 |
| Valve stroke time measurement | Final element response within assumed time | 16.3.2.3 |
| Bypass switch operation test | Bypass does not defeat safety (auto-removal) | 11.6.2 |
| Loss of power / loss of air test | Fail-safe direction confirmed | 11.5.3 |
| Diagnostic fault injection | $DC$ (Diagnostic Coverage) assumption | 11.4.2 |
| Partial stroke test (PST) on ESD valves | Proof test interval $TI$ realistic | 16.3.2.4 |
| Earth continuity $\leq 1\ \Omega$ | EMC and surge immunity | 12.4.1 |
| Insulation $\geq 100\ M\Omega$ | No leakage path | 12.4.1 |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A vendor claims a single SIF has $PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ and meets SIL 2 (target range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$). The MTTR used in the calculation is 8 hours, and the proof test interval is $TI = 1\ year = 8760\ hours$. During SAT, the maintenance team physically replaces a transmitter and finds the actual MTTR achieved is 16 hours due to the spares warehouse being 2 hours away. Verify whether the SIL 2 claim is still valid for a 1oo1 architecture.

**Step 1 — Write the 1oo1 $PFD_{avg}$ formula with MTTR term:**

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2} + \lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR
$$

**Step 2 — Reason that for a 1oo1 architecture, a doubling of MTTR only affects the second term. Assume for simplicity the dangerous detected rate is $\lambda_{DD} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ fits/h.**

$$
\lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR_{new} = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 16 = 8 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the original term:**

$$
\lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR_{old} = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 8 = 4 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantify the increase:**

$$
\Delta PFD = 8 \times 10^{-5} - 4 \times 10^{-5} = 4 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion for the SAT report:** The PFD increase from MTTR alone is negligible compared to the overall $PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ (contribution $\approx 1.3\%$). The SIL 2 claim **remains valid**, but the SAT report must record the revised MTTR value and trigger a **Management of Change (MOC)** update to the SRS to use $MTTR = 16\ h$ going forward. This is a textbook **SAT-induced design feedback** scenario.

## 3.4 Conversion Logic Tables (Why Each SAT Check Exists)

| Question | Answer (KTU Marker-Friendly) |
|---|---|
| Why insulation resistance test? | Prevents undetected leakage paths that mimic $PFD$ increase. |
| Why earth continuity test? | Prevents common-mode voltage damage to logic solver inputs. |
| Why valve stroke time measurement? | Confirms assumed $TI$ and that the SIF meets the **process safety time** demand. |
| Why fault injection on sensors? | Confirms $DC$ and reveals dangerous undetected failures. |
| Why loop check before functional test? | Avoids misdiagnosing a wiring fault as a logic solver fault. |
| Why SAT after FAT, not instead of? | FAT verifies hardware design; SAT verifies **integrated as-built installation**. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SAT Workflow within the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 4 Detailed Engineering] --> B[Phase 5 Installation]
    B --> C[Pre-SAT Documentation Review]
    C --> D[Mechanical and Electrical Completion]
    D --> E[Individual Loop Checks]
    E --> F[SIF Functional Tests]
    F --> G[Diagnostic and Fault Injection Tests]
    G --> H[Power Loss and Fail Safe Tests]
    H --> I{Punch List Review}
    I -->|Category A Closed| J[SAT Report and Sign Off]
    I -->|Category A Open| K[Reject Plant Start Up Authorisation]
    J --> L[Phase 6 Pre Start Up Safety Review PSSR]
    L --> M[Plant Start Up with Hazardous Process]
    K --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Students:** Node `I` is the **decision gate**. If even one Category A (blocking) Punch List Item remains, the gate refuses to advance to `L`. This visual cue is a common 14-mark question requirement: *"Draw and explain the SAT sign-off gate."*

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — SAT Roles and Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OWN as Owner and Operator
    participant EPC as EPC Contractor
    participant VEN as SIS Vendor
    participant ISA as Independent Safety Assessor
    EPC->>OWN: Submit SAT Procedure Document
    OWN->>ISA: Review and Approve SAT Plan
    ISA-->>OWN: Approved SAT Plan Returned
    EPC->>VEN: Issue Purchase Order SAT Milestone
    VEN->>EPC: Execute Loop Checks Stage C
    EPC->>VEN: Execute SIF Functional Tests Stage D
    VEN->>EPC: Issue SAT Punch List
    EPC->>OWN: Submit SAT Report with Punch List
    OWN->>ISA: Independent Witness of Functional Tests
    ISA-->>OWN: SAT Witness Report
    OWN-->>EPC: SAT Acceptance Letter or Rejection Note
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — SAT Coverage of the SIS Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAYER1 [Sensors and Field Devices]
        F1[Pressure Transmitter]
        F2[Temperature Transmitter]
        F3[Level Switch]
        F4[Flame Detector]
    end
    subgraph LAYER2 [Logic Solver]
        L1[SIS PLC with Safety CPU]
        L2[Voting 1oo2 or 2oo3]
        L3[Diagnostic Engine]
    end
    subgraph LAYER3 [Final Elements]
        FE1[ESD Valve]
        FE2[Solenoid Trip Coil]
        FE3[Motor Starter]
    end
    subgraph LAYER4 [SAT Verification Layers]
        V1[Loop Check Stage C]
        V2[Functional Test Stage D]
        V3[Fault Injection Stage E]
        V4[Power Loss Test Stage F]
    end
    F1 --> V1
    F2 --> V1
    F3 --> V1
    F4 --> V1
    L1 --> V2
    L2 --> V2
    L3 --> V3
    FE1 --> V2
    FE2 --> V2
    FE3 --> V4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The **Field Devices Layer (LAYER1)** is verified primarily by **Loop Checks (V1)**.
> - The **Logic Solver Layer (LAYER2)** is verified by **Functional Tests (V2)** and **Fault Injection (V3)**.
> - The **Final Elements Layer (LAYER3)** is verified by **Functional Tests (V2)** and **Power Loss Tests (V4)**.
> - This block mapping directly answers KTU questions of the form *"Which SAT test verifies which layer of the SIS?"*.

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — SAT Pass or Fail Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[SIF Functional Test Initiated] --> check1{Loop Check Pass?}
    check1 -- No --> fail1[Reject Loop Mark Punch List Item A]
    check1 -- Yes --> check2{Logic Solver Response Correct?}
    check2 -- No --> fail2[Reject Logic Mark Punch List Item A]
    check2 -- Yes --> check3{Final Element Strokes Within Process Safety Time?}
    check3 -- No --> fail3[Reject Valve Mark Punch List Item A]
    check3 -- Yes --> check4{Diagnostic Detection Time Within Spec?}
    check4 -- No --> fail4[Reject Diagnostics Mark Punch List Item A]
    check4 -- Yes --> check5{Fail Safe Direction Confirmed?}
    check5 -- No --> fail5[Reject Fail Safe Mark Punch List Item A]
    check5 -- Yes --> pass[SIF Accepted Sign Off Stage G]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **Startup Acceptance Test (SAT)** as applied to a Safety Instrumented System. How is it different from a Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** Startup Acceptance Testing (SAT) is the formal on-site, post-installation verification activity that confirms a Safety Instrumented System is installed in accordance with the design documents, performs its Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) as specified in the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS), and is safe to operate with hazardous process fluids.
2. **FAT limitation (1 Mark):** Factory Acceptance Test is performed at the vendor's premises with simulated inputs and verifies the hardware against design specifications in isolation from the actual plant environment.
3. **SAT value-add (1 Mark):** SAT verifies the **integrated, as-built installation** including cabling, earthing, ambient conditions, loop continuity, and site-specific interfaces — thereby validating that the design intent (including $PFD_{avg}$ and SIL assumptions) survives real-world field conditions.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** List any **six** activities that are part of a SAT procedure for a typical SIS.

**Model Answer (6 bullet points for 3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. Review and approval of the **SAT Procedure Document** and Cause-&-Effect Matrix.
2. Verification of **insulation resistance** (≥ 100 MΩ at 500 V DC) and **earth continuity** (≤ 1 Ω).
3. **Loop checks** for every sensor-to-controller and controller-to-final-element pair.
4. **Functional test** of every Safety Instrumented Function in normal and bypassed modes.
5. **Fault injection** to verify diagnostic coverage and safe failure fraction.
6. **Power loss and air loss** tests to confirm fail-safe direction.
7. Generation and closure of **Punch List Items** with **SAT Report** sign-off.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *(a)* Explain in detail the **stages of Startup Acceptance Testing** for a Safety Instrumented System with reference to **IEC 61511 / ISA 84.00.01**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 Marks):**

1. **Stage A — Pre-SAT Planning (1 Mark):** Final review of SRS, C&E matrix, logic diagrams, P&IDs, calibration certificates, vendor manuals. Approval of the SAT plan with all stakeholders.
2. **Stage B — Mechanical and Electrical Completion (1 Mark):** Visual inspection of cable routing, segregation, termination, earthing (≤ 1 Ω), insulation (≥ 100 MΩ), enclosure IP integrity.
3. **Stage C — Loop Checks (1 Mark):** Sensor-to-controller and controller-to-final-element loop verification by signal injection and observation.
4. **Stage D — Functional SIF Tests (1.5 Marks):** For each SIF, simulate initiating event, observe detection, logic, and final element action. Includes bypass mode testing.
5. **Stage E — Fault Injection Tests (1 Mark):** Inject sensor short/open, valve stuck, communication loss faults to verify diagnostic coverage and safe-state activation.
6. **Stage F — Power and Air Loss Tests (0.5 Mark):** Verify fail-safe direction on loss of utilities.
7. **Stage G — Punch List Closure and Sign-Off (1 Mark):** Document all deviations, categorise, close all blocking items, issue SAT Report with multi-party signatures.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **significance of Punch List management** during SAT. Explain the typical **categories of Punch List Items** and their implications for plant start-up authorisation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 Marks):**

1. **Definition of Punch List (1 Mark):** A Punch List is a written list of deviations, non-conformities, or unfinished items identified during SAT that must be resolved before the SIS can be formally accepted.
2. **Documentation Value (1 Mark):** Provides auditable evidence under **ISO 9001 / IEC 61511** for management of change, regulatory inspection, and insurance.
3. **Category A — Blocking Items (2 Marks):** These are deviations that affect safety integrity, regulatory compliance, or design intent. **Zero Category A items** may remain open at the time of plant start-up authorisation. Examples: failure to achieve required SIL, valve not stroking within process safety time, missing earth connection.
4. **Category B — Non-Blocking with Time Limit (1.5 Marks):** Deviations with documented mitigation in place and a closure deadline (typically within 30–90 days of start-up). Examples: cosmetic panel labelling, spare parts list completion.
5. **Category C — Cosmetic or Administrative (1 Mark):** Items not affecting safety, function, or operation. Recorded for completeness.
6. **Closure Process (0.5 Mark):** Each item is verified closed by the originator, witnessed by the SAT lead, and formally signed off before final acceptance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (SAT Topic):**
> Students frequently lose marks because they confuse **FAT, SAT, and PSSR (Pre-Start-Up Safety Review)**. The correct sequence is: **FAT → Installation → SAT → PSSR → Plant Start-Up**. PSSR is a separate management-level review (often by the Owner), not a technical loop test. If a KTU question asks *"What is the difference between SAT and PSSR?"*, answer: **SAT verifies the technical SIS performance**; **PSSR verifies that management systems, procedures, training, and operating manuals are in place** before hazardous introduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** *(a)* Describe the **key documents required to plan and execute a SAT**. Explain how each document supports the SAT process. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 Marks):**

1. **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS) (1 Mark):** Baseline reference; defines the SIFs, SIL targets, and proof test intervals. SAT tests must trace back to each SRS clause.
2. **Cause & Effect Matrix (1 Mark):** Defines expected input-to-output behaviour. Used as the **functional truth table** during Stage D functional tests.
3. **SAT Procedure Document (1 Mark):** Specifies the exact sequence, test methods, acceptance criteria, responsibilities, and safety controls (e.g., bypasses) during the test.
4. **P&ID and Loop Drawings (0.5 Mark):** Provide physical context for test point identification.
5. **Logic Diagrams / Functional Safety Logic (1 Mark):** Allow tracing of input logic to output actions for each SIF.
6. **Calibration Certificates (0.5 Mark):** Verify field devices meet stated accuracy — a $PFD_{avg}$ input parameter.
7. **SAT Punch List Format and SAT Report Template (1 Mark):** Pre-approved to ensure consistent documentation.
8. **Risk Assessment / Job Safety Analysis (1 Mark):** Required because SAT often requires **bypassing** safety functions temporarily; the JSA ensures compensating measures are documented.

---

**(b)** A vendor claims a SIF achieves **SIL 2** ($PFD_{avg}$ target $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$) with the following data: architecture 1oo1, dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ fits/h, proof test interval $TI = 8760$ hours (1 year), MTTR = 8 hours. During SAT the team verifies all numbers except that the valve measured stroke time is **6 seconds**, whereas the SRS assumed **2 seconds**.

(i) Compute the $PFD_{avg}$ of the SIF and confirm whether SIL 2 is achieved.
(ii) Explain what action the SAT team must recommend. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**(i) $PFD_{avg}$ Calculation (3 Marks):**

For a 1oo1 architecture:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot TI}{2}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{0.1752}{2} = 0.0876
$$

Expressed in scientific form:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo1} = 8.76 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**SIL 2 verification (1 Mark):**
SIL 2 target range is $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ (i.e., $0.01$ to $0.001$). Since $PFD_{avg} = 8.76 \times 10^{-2}$, the SIF **fails to meet SIL 2** — it is closer to SIL 1 boundary. The SAT team must reject the SIL 2 claim.

**(ii) Recommendation (3 Marks):**

1. Raise a **Category A Punch List Item (blocking)** for the valve stroke time deviation.
2. **Reject plant start-up authorisation** until valve is rectified, replaced, or **process safety time** is re-evaluated.
3. Initiate **Management of Change (MOC)** per IEC 61511-1 Clause 5.2.6.1.5 to either:
   - Upgrade the valve to meet 2 s stroke time, **or**
   - Re-architect the SIF to 1oo2 to halve the dangerous failure contribution, **or**
   - Re-document the SRS to reflect realistic 6 s stroke time and re-verify the $PFD_{avg}$.
4. Issue an **amended SAT Report** with the deviation, action plan, and updated SIL claim before plant start-up clearance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Numerical Questions on SAT):**
> The most common mistake students make is **forgetting the factor of 1/2 in the 1oo1 $PFD_{avg}$ formula**. Always show the substitution step explicitly. A second common mistake is **assuming valve stroke time does not affect $PFD_{avg}$** — it does, because $TI$ and process safety time are interlinked. Marks are explicitly reserved in KTU 2024 scheme for *qualitative* reasoning linking measurement results to design assumptions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** SAT is the **on-site, post-installation, end-to-end verification** of the Safety Instrumented System, confirming installation, integration, and functional behaviour match the **SRS**.
- **Standards Governing SAT:** **IEC 61511-1 Clause 5.2.6.1.3**, **ISA 84.00.01**, with reference to **IEC 61508**.
- **Sequential Position:** FAT → Installation → **SAT** → PSSR → Plant Start-Up.
- **Seven SAT Stages:** (A) Planning; (B) Mechanical/Electrical Completion; (C) Loop Checks; (D) Functional SIF Tests; (E) Fault Injection; (F) Power/Air Loss Tests; (G) Punch List Closure and Sign-Off.
- **Key Acceptance Metrics:** Insulation $\geq 100\ M\Omega$ at 500 V DC; Earth continuity $\leq 1\ \Omega$; Valve stroke time within process safety time; Diagnostic coverage per SRS; $PFD_{avg}$ within SIL target band.
- **Punch List Categories:** A (blocking — must close), B (time-bound non-blocking), C (cosmetic).
- **Roles:** Owner, EPC, SIS Vendor, Independent Safety Assessor (when mandated).
- **Linkage to SIL:** Each SAT test validates an **assumption** inside the $PFD_{avg}$ equation (sensor $\lambda_D$, valve response, MTTR, TI, DC, architecture).
- **Outputs:** Signed **SAT Report**, closed Punch List, updated as-built documentation, MOC entries.
- **Most Exam-Likely Traps:** Confusing SAT with FAT; confusing SAT with PSSR; forgetting the $1/2$ factor in 1oo1 $PFD_{avg}$; missing the link between valve stroke time and SIL claim.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operations of Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)

**Formal Definition (IEC 61511-1, Clause 3.2.75):**
> "Operation" refers to all activities necessary to keep the SIS in a functional state, including startup, normal operation, shutdown, and bypass management, while ensuring the Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) remain available to perform their safety actions when demanded.

According to **IEC 61511:2016** (the primary governing standard for process industry SIS), operations cover the day-to-day activities performed on the Safety Instrumented System from the operator's console, including but not limited to:

- **Normal Operation** (the SIS remains in a quiescent, armed state)
- **Authorised Bypasses** (temporarily removing SIFs during maintenance or startup)
- **Manual Shutdown Actions** (operator-initiated safety actions)
- **Reset/Restart Procedures** after a trip
- **Alarm Acknowledgement** for diagnostic alerts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** In any KTU question on SIS operations, the examiner expects students to link the activity directly to the **Safety Lifecycle phase** (specifically Phases 5, 6, and 7 of IEC 61511) and explain how operations affect the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of the system.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Black Box" View

Imagine you are a **commercial pilot flying a Boeing 737**. The autopilot and the basic flight controls (BPCS) keep the plane flying smoothly. The **Safety Instrumented System** is the layer that acts *only when something goes terribly wrong* — the angle-of-attack sensor, the stall warning, the ground-proximity alert, and the autopilot's safety overrides.

- **Operations** = the *pre-flight checklist, the cockpit switch monitoring, the bypass of a faulty sensor before takeoff, the resetting of an alert after a near-stall event.*
- **Maintenance** = the *scheduled inspection of the pitot tube, the proof test of the stall warning system every 5,000 flight hours, the calibration of the altimeter.*

Just as a pilot must never let a sensor stay "bypassed" indefinitely, an SIS operator must strictly govern how long a SIF is bypassed, because **every hour of bypass increases risk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** The operations and maintenance phase directly influences the **PFDavg (Average Probability of Failure on Demand)** of the SIF. A poorly maintained valve that takes 8 hours to repair has 16× the failure exposure of one that takes 30 minutes.

---

## 1.2 Maintenance of Safety Instrumented Systems

**Formal Definition (IEC 61511-1, Clause 3.2.61):**
> "Maintenance" is the combination of all actions, including test, measurement, replacement, adjustment, and cleaning, intended to retain an item in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function.

**Key Maintenance Activities in SIS:**

| Activity | Purpose | Standard Reference |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| **Proof Testing** | Detect hidden/dangerous failures that BPCS could not catch | IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.4 |
| **Calibration** | Restore measurement accuracy of sensors | IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.3 |
| **Preventive Maintenance** | Replace items before they fail dangerously | IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.1 |
1. **Corrective Maintenance** — Repair after a detected failure
2. **Bypass Management** — Coordinated with maintenance windows
3. **Documentation Update** — Update safety manuals and as-built drawings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Maintenance of a SIS is fundamentally different from maintenance of a BPCS. In BPCS, a small failure is acceptable (a single failure does not cause a hazard). In SIS, an undetected dangerous failure *is the hazard*, so maintenance becomes the *primary defence* against dangerous undetected failures.

### Maintenance Phases (IEC 61511 Lifecycle Mapping)

The maintenance phase is **Phase 8 of the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle**, which includes the following sub-phases:

- **Phase 8.1 — Maintenance Procedures and Plans**
- **Phase 8.2 — Implementation of Maintenance**
- **Phase 8.3 — Audit and Modification Records**

> [!NOTE]
> The objective of SIS maintenance is to **detect and correct dangerous undetected failures** before they can combine with a process demand to cause a hazardous event. The interval between these tests is called the **Proof Test Interval (PTI)**.

---

## 1.3 Bypass Management — A Critical Operations Activity

A **bypass** is a temporary action that removes a SIF (or part of it) from active service. It is one of the most dangerous SIS operations because the protective layer is removed.

**Authorised Bypass Categories (per IEC 61511):**

1. **Maintenance Bypass** — Required to perform proof testing without causing nuisance trips.
2. **Operational Bypass** — Required during specific process states (e.g., startup, shutdown, or special batch operations).
3. **Compensating Measure Bypass** — Used when an alternative protective measure is put in place during the bypass.

**Key Operational Rule:**
> A bypass must **NEVER** be implemented without: (a) written authorisation, (b) a defined time limit, (c) compensating measures in place, and (d) a clear record in the bypass log.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "bypass" with "override" or "inhibit". An **override** forces a specific output state, while an **inhibit** prevents a specific action. A **bypass** removes the entire SIF or SIF element. These are three distinct actions with different procedural requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of SIS Operations

SIS operations can be decomposed into a structured set of activities. The **IEC 61511 Clause 5** (Operation of the SIS) explicitly requires the following procedural framework:

### Step 1 — Operating Procedures Development
- Develop written operating procedures for **each SIF** within the SIS.
- Procedures must be **available, current, and accessible** to all relevant personnel.
- Address: normal operation, startup, shutdown, bypass, manual shutdown, reset, and response to demand.

### Step 2 — Operating Authorisation
- Only **authorised personnel** are permitted to operate the SIS.
- Authorisation must be documented and tied to **role-based access control**.
- Operators must be **trained and competency-verified** (per IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.6.1.4).

### Step 3 — Bypass Management
- Establish a **bypass management procedure** with strict controls.
- Maintain a **bypass register** documenting every bypass event.
- Apply **engineering and administrative controls** before granting bypass.

### Step 4 — Abnormal Situation Management
- When a SIF demands action (trips), the operator must follow the **"trip response procedure"**.
- Investigate the cause of the demand (process upset or spurious trip).
- **Reset** the SIS only after the cause is identified and the process is restored to a safe state.

### Step 5 — Operating Records and Audit
- Maintain logs of: bypass events, trips, spurious trips, maintenance activities, and demand events.
- These records feed into the **SIF proof test history** and inform PFDavg calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Operations Matter Mathematically:** The **PFDavg** formula contains a term for the **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR)**, which is determined by the speed of operations response. Faster operator action after a trip reduces risk.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework of SIS Maintenance

The **IEC 61511 Clause 16** (SIS Installation, Commissioning, and Validation) and the maintenance requirements establish a structured framework:

### Step 1 — Maintenance Planning
- Develop a **maintenance plan** for each SIF.
- The plan must address: **what**, **when**, **how**, **by whom**, and **with what resources**.
- Maintenance must be **scheduled to minimise the duration of unrevealed dangerous failures.**

### Step 2 — Maintenance Categorisation
- **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance** — Performed at fixed intervals (e.g., proof testing).
- **Predictive Maintenance** — Triggered by condition monitoring data.
- **Corrective (Unscheduled) Maintenance** — Triggered by a detected failure.
- **Shutdown Maintenance** — Performed during process unit turnarounds.

### Step 3 — Proof Test Design
- A **proof test** is a periodic test to reveal dangerous undetected failures in a SIF.
- Test coverage (often denoted **β₁ or PTC**) determines the fraction of failures the test can detect.
- **Proof Test Interval (PTI)** is the time between successive proof tests.
- A 100% effective proof test restores the system to "as good as new" condition.

### Step 4 — Calibration and Adjustment
- Sensors must be **calibrated** to maintain the stated accuracy.
- Final elements (valves) must be **stroke-tested** to verify operation.
- Calibration intervals must be **justified** by historical drift data.

### Step 5 — Documentation and Records
- Every maintenance activity must be **recorded** with date, time, result, and personnel.
- Records must be **retained** for the **entire operational life** of the SIF.
- Maintenance records feed into the **calculation of the achieved PFDavg** for the SIF.

### Step 6 — Competency Management
- Personnel performing SIS maintenance must be **trained, experienced, and competency-verified**.
- Competency includes: understanding the SIF, understanding the safety implications, and familiarity with the procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The proof test interval directly controls the PFDavg. If the PTI is too long, the SIF will fail to meet its required SIL. If it is too short, the cost of ownership becomes prohibitive. The PTI is therefore a **design parameter**, not a maintenance parameter.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 1: Core Reliability and Safety Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1, idealised, where $\lambda_{DU}$ is dangerous undetected failure rate per hour, TI is test interval in hours) | Dimensionless; values typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| 2 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2}{3}$ | PFDavg for 1oo2 voted architecture | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 2oo2}} = 2 \times \lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}$ | PFDavg for 2oo2 architecture | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 2oo3}} = 6 \times (\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2$ | PFDavg for 2oo3 architecture (ideal) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System Availability (A) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure Rate (λ) | failures/hour (or /year) |
| 8 | $\text{MTTR} = \text{MDT} + \text{MAT}$ | Mean Time To Repair = Mean Down Time + Mean Active Repair Time | hours |
| 9 | $\text{SIL} \leftrightarrow \text{PFD}_{\text{avg range}}$ | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Low demand mode (per IEC 61511 Table 3) |
| 10 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage — fraction of failures detected by automatic diagnostics | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 11 | $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,\text{common cause}}}{\lambda_{DU,\text{total}}}$ | Beta-factor for common cause failures | Dimensionless (0 to 1); typically 0.01 to 0.10 |
| 12 | $\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}}{\lambda_{DU}}$ | Maximum allowable proof test interval for 1oo1 SIF | hours |
| 13 | $\text{PTC} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,\text{not covered}}}{\lambda_{DU,\text{total}}}$ | Proof Test Coverage (PTC) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 14 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1, full}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2} + \lambda_{DU} \times \text{MTTR} + \beta \times \lambda_{DU} \times \dfrac{\text{TI}}{2}$ | Full PFDavg for 1oo1 with MTTR and β-factor | Dimensionless |

### Table 2: SIL-to-PFDavg Mapping (Low Demand Mode)

| SIL | PFDavg Target Range | RRF Target | Example Application |
|-----|--------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | Low-pressure alarm shutdown |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | Burner management system (BMS) |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | Reactor overpressure protection |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | Nuclear reactor shutdown (rare in process) |

### Table 3: Proof Test Coverage Categories

| PTC Range | Category | Typical Use |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| $\text{PTC} \geq 90\%$ | High coverage | Routine operational proof testing |
| $60\% \leq \text{PTC} < 90\%$ | Medium coverage | Partial stroke testing of valves |
| $\text{PTC} < 60\%$ | Low coverage | Diagnostic checks only (insufficient for SIL 3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by Safety Engineers to **justify the proof test interval** during the design phase (SIL verification per IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.6.1.6) and to **measure achieved risk reduction** during the operations phase using actual failure data.

---

## 2.4 Impact of Operations and Maintenance on Safety Integrity

The following causal chain is critical for KTU:

**Ineffective Operations/Maintenance → ↑ PFDavg → ↓ Risk Reduction Factor → ↑ Process Risk → Potential Incident**

Specifically:

- **Long MTTR** (e.g., due to slow maintenance response or unavailable spares) increases PFDavg.
- **Long PTI** (e.g., due to deferring proof tests) increases PFDavg.
- **Poor bypass management** (uncontrolled or undocumented bypasses) effectively reduces the achieved SIL.
- **Poor proof test coverage** (e.g., testing only one part of a redundant SIF) leaves dangerous failures undetected.
- **Inadequate training** (e.g., operators who do not understand the SIF) leads to inappropriate manual overrides.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PFDavg for 1oo1 Architecture (Effect of Proof Test Interval)

### Objective
To derive the average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg) for a single-channel (1oo1) sensor SIF as a function of the proof test interval, MTTR, and failure rate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the dangerous undetected failure rate.**
Let $\lambda_{DU}$ represent the dangerous undetected failure rate of the channel, in failures per hour. This represents failures that occur but are not detected by any automatic diagnostic.

**Step 2 — Set up the time-varying unavailability function.**
For a 1oo1 channel that is proof-tested at interval $\text{TI}$, the unavailability $P(t)$ varies over the test interval. Immediately after a successful proof test, the channel is "as good as new" with $P(0) = 0$. As time progresses, the unavailability grows linearly because failures are assumed to be random (Poisson process) with constant rate $\lambda_{DU}$.

Therefore, the unavailability at time $t$ after the last proof test is:

$$
P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \times t}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simplification.**
For low failure rates (which is the practical case for safety instrumentation, since $\lambda_{DU} \times t \ll 1$), the exponential can be expanded using the first-order Taylor series:

$$
e^{-\lambda_{DU} \times t} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} \times t
$$

Therefore, the unavailability simplifies to:

$$
P(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \times t
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average unavailability over the interval [0, TI].**
The average value of $P(t)$ over the proof test interval is:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{TI}} \int_{0}^{\text{TI}} P(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and integrate.**
Substituting the linear expression for $P(t)$:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{TI}} \int_{0}^{\text{TI}} \lambda_{DU} \times t \, dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral.**
The integral of $t$ from 0 to TI is $\dfrac{\text{TI}^2}{2}$. Therefore:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{TI}} \times \lambda_{DU} \times \dfrac{\text{TI}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the result.**
The TI terms cancel, leaving:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The PFDavg grows linearly with the proof test interval. **Doubling the PTI doubles the PFDavg**, which means a SIF that was certified for SIL 2 may silently drop to SIL 1 if proof tests are deferred.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: System Availability from MTBF and MTTR

### Objective
To derive the steady-state availability of a repairable system, which is the operational foundation for understanding why MTTR matters for SIS.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the system states.**
A repairable system has two states: **UP** (operational) and **DOWN** (failed and under repair). Let $T_{\text{UP}}$ be the mean time the system spends in the UP state, and $T_{\text{DOWN}}$ be the mean time the system spends in the DOWN state.

**Step 2 — Express in standard reliability terms.**
By definition:
- $T_{\text{UP}} = \text{MTBF}$ (Mean Time Between Failures)
- $T_{\text{DOWN}} = \text{MTTR}$ (Mean Time To Repair)

**Step 3 — Define availability.**
Availability is the fraction of time the system is operational:

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{UP}}}{T_{\text{UP}} + T_{\text{DOWN}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the reliability terms.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in terms of failure rate.**
Using $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$:

$$
A = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \times \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the typical safety instrument domain assumption.**
Since $\lambda \times \text{MTTR} \ll 1$ for safety instruments (low failure rate, short repair), the denominator can be expanded using the binomial series, and the first-order approximation gives:

$$
A \approx 1 - \lambda \times \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 7 — Relate to PFDavg.**
The PFDavg contribution from MTTR for a 1oo1 channel is approximately $\lambda_{DU} \times \text{MTTR}$ during the period the channel is in failed state. This shows that even short MTTRs add a non-negligible term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A SIL 3 SIF with $\text{TI} = 8760 \text{ hours}$ and $\lambda_{DU} = 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$ gives $\text{PFD}_{\text{TI}} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ (which is SIL 2). Adding an MTTR contribution of $\text{MTTR} = 24 \text{ hours}$ adds $24 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$, which is small but non-negligible for the SIL budget.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Allowable Proof Test Interval (PTI_max)

### Objective
To derive the maximum PTI that maintains the required SIL for a 1oo1 SIF, which is essential for operations and maintenance planning.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the PFDavg formula for 1oo1.**
For simplicity, neglect MTTR and β-factor (these are small additive corrections handled later):

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the constraint.**
The PFDavg must be less than the PFDavg target for the required SIL. Let $\text{PFD}_{\text{req}}$ be the maximum allowed PFDavg for the SIL:

$$
\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for TI.**

$$
\text{TI} \leq \dfrac{2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}}{\lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the maximum PTI.**

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}}{\lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
For a SIL 2 SIF ($\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = 10^{-2}$) with $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$:

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4 \times 10^{4} \text{ hours}
$$

Converting to years (dividing by 8760):

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{40000}{8760} \approx 4.57 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The SIF must be proof-tested at least once every **4.57 years** to maintain SIL 2. Operations and Maintenance must plan this within turnaround schedules. Missing this interval will cause the SIF to fail its SIL requirement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operations & Maintenance Decision Matrix

This matrix maps typical operations/maintenance scenarios to their **impact on safety integrity** and **KTU-recommended response**.

| Scenario | Operational Symptom | Impact on PFDavg / SIL | Required Response per IEC 61511 |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Proof test overdue | PTI exceeded | PFDavg > target | Restore SIF immediately; perform proof test |
| Spurious trip | Process shutdown without demand | No PFD impact, but lost production | Investigate cause; check for common cause |
| Bypass without authorisation | SIF disabled | PFDavg effectively = 1 | Halt process until SIF restored; document incident |
| Calibration overdue | Sensor drift | Increased systematic error | Recalibrate; verify trip setpoint |
| Bypass with time limit exceeded | SIF still bypassed | PFDavg = 1 for bypassed element | Force process shutdown; perform proof test |
| Inadequate MTTR | Long repair times | PFDavg contribution rises | Improve spares management; train maintenance crew |
| Multiple BPCS failures | Increased demand rate | SIF demand frequency rises | Verify SIF sizing; risk re-assessment |

### Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A 1oo1 SIF has $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$ and $\text{MTTR} = 8 \text{ hours}$. The proof test interval is $1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ hours}$. Verify the SIL achieved.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the PFDavg contribution from proof testing:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{TI}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the PFDavg contribution from MTTR:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{MTTR}} = \lambda_{DU} \times \text{MTTR} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} + 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 8.776 \times 10^{-3} \approx 8.78 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with SIL ranges.**
From the table:
- SIL 1 range: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ (i.e., $0.01$ to $0.1$)
- SIL 2 range: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ (i.e., $0.001$ to $0.01$)

The calculated PFDavg of $8.78 \times 10^{-3}$ falls within $10^{-3} \leq 8.78 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$, which is the **SIL 2 range**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The SIF achieves **SIL 2**. The MTTR contribution is small (only 0.18% of the total), confirming that for 1oo1 channels, the proof test interval is the dominant driver of PFDavg.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEC 61511 Operations and Maintenance Workflow (Block Architecture Flow)

This diagram shows the **functional workflow** of Operations and Maintenance activities within the SIS Safety Lifecycle, mapping each activity to its corresponding IEC 61511 clause.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[PHASE 5: SIS REALIZATION] --> A2[PHASE 6: SIS OPERATION]
    A2 --> A3[PHASE 7: SIS MAINTENANCE]
    A3 --> A4[PHASE 8: SIS MODIFICATION]
    A4 --> A5[PHASE 9: DECOMMISSIONING]

    A2 --> B1[Normal Operation Procedures]
    A2 --> B2[Bypass Management Procedure]
    A2 --> B3[Manual Shutdown Procedure]
    A2 --> B4[Reset and Restart Procedure]
    A2 --> B5[Alarm Management]

    A3 --> C1[Preventive Maintenance Plan]
    A3 --> C2[Proof Test Procedure]
    A3 --> C3[Calibration Procedure]
    A3 --> C4[Corrective Maintenance]

    C1 --> D1[Document Maintenance]
    C2 --> D2[Update SIF Records]
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2

    B2 --> E1[Bypass Register]
    B4 --> E1
    C2 --> E1

    D2 --> F1[CALCULATE ACHIEVED PFDavg]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> F2{ACTUAL SIL MEETS REQUIREMENT?}
    F2 -->|YES| G1[Continue Operation]
    F2 -->|NO| G2[Trigger SIL Verification + Corrective Action]
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**

- The **left spine** (A1 → A5) represents the **IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle** phases.
- **Phase 6 (Operation)** generates the procedures shown in the B-cluster.
- **Phase 7 (Maintenance)** generates the activities in the C-cluster.
- All records (D2) and bypass events (E1) feed into the **achieved PFDavg calculation (F1)**, which acts as the **ongoing SIL verification** of the operational SIF.
- The decision node **F2** is the **operational safety check**: if the achieved PFDavg exceeds the target, the SIF is no longer safe and corrective action is triggered.

---

## 4.2 Proof Test Interval vs. PFDavg — Causal Relationship

This block diagram explains the **interactions** between maintenance activities and the PFDavg of a SIF.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Proof Test Interval TI] --> node2[PFDTI = lambda_DU x TI / 2]
    node3[Mean Time To Repair MTTR] --> node4[PFDMTTR = lambda_DU x MTTR]
    node5[Beta Factor beta] --> node6[PFDBeta = beta x lambda_DU x TI / 2]
    node2 --> node7[SUM PFDavg = PFDTI + PFDMTTR + PFDBeta]
    node4 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8{COMPARE WITH SIL TARGET}
    node8 -->|IN RANGE| node9[SIL ACHIEVED]
    node8 -->|OUT OF RANGE| node10[SIL NOT ACHIEVED - CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    node9 --> node11[Continue as is]
    node10 --> node12[Reduce TI / Reduce MTTR / Improve beta]
```

**Interpretation:**

- Three independent **operations and maintenance parameters** (TI, MTTR, β-factor) feed into the PFDavg calculation.
- The **decision node (node8)** validates the achieved SIL against the design SIL.
- The **remediation path (node10 → node12)** shows the operations team's options: tighten the proof test schedule, speed up repairs, or improve common cause failure resistance.

---

## 4.3 Bypass Management Decision Tree

This block-level schematic shows the **decision logic** of an authorised bypass.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Bypass Request Initiated] --> q1{Is the bypass justified?}
    q1 -->|NO| stop1[Reject Bypass Request]
    q1 -->|YES| q2{Are compensating measures in place?}
    q2 -->|NO| stop2[Implement Compensating Measure First]
    q2 -->|YES| q3{Is the bypass time-bounded?}
    q3 -->|NO| stop3[Set Time Limit Before Proceeding]
    q3 -->|YES| q4{Is there written authorisation from the safety authority?}
    q4 -->|NO| stop4[Obtain Written Authorisation]
    q4 -->|YES| action1[Implement Bypass and Log in Bypass Register]
    action1 --> timer1[Monitor Time Limit]
    timer1 --> q5{Time limit reached?}
    q5 -->|NO| timer1
    q5 -->|YES| action2[Remove Bypass Automatically or Force Shutdown]
    action2 --> verify1[Verify SIF is Functional]
    verify1 --> close1[Close Bypass Record]
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **bypass decision tree** enforces the **IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.6.1.5** requirements for authorised bypasses.
- Notice that there is **no direct path** from "request" to "implement" — every intermediate step is a **gate** that must be cleared.
- The **timer and auto-removal** at the end prevent "bypass drift" where a SIF is left bypassed indefinitely.

---

## 4.4 SIS Operations & Maintenance — Master Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PROC[PROCESS UNDER CONTROL] --> BPCS[Basic Process Control System BPCS]
    PROC --> SIS[Safety Instrumented System SIS]
    BPCS --> OP[OPERATIONS LAYER]
    SIS --> OP
    OP --> OPCON[OPERATING PROCEDURES]
    OP --> BYP[BYPASS MANAGEMENT]
    OP --> ALM[ALARM MANAGEMENT]
    SIS --> MAINT[MAINTENANCE LAYER]
    MAINT --> PM[PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    MAINT --> PT[PROOF TESTING]
    MAINT --> CAL[CALIBRATION]
    MAINT --> CM[CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    OP --> DOC[OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE RECORDS]
    MAINT --> DOC
    DOC --> VER[ONGOING SIL VERIFICATION]
    VER --> SILD{ACHIEVED SIL COMPLIANT?}
    SILD -->|YES| CONT[CONTINUE OPERATION]
    SILD -->|NO| CORR[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The left side shows the **process** with the two control layers (BPCS and SIS). The right side shows the **human activity layers** (Operations and Maintenance) that interact with both. The **right-most spine** is the **verification and feedback loop** that ensures the SIS continues to meet its SIL throughout its operational life.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Bypass" in the context of SIS operations. List any four procedural requirements for an authorised bypass. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO4]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Bypass** is a temporary, authorised action that removes a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) or a portion of a SIF from active service, while maintaining the rest of the process operating.

The four mandatory procedural requirements for an authorised bypass are:
1. **Written Authorisation** from the designated safety authority before implementation.
2. **Defined Time Limit** specifying the maximum duration of the bypass.
3. **Compensating Measures** in place (e.g., manual watch, alternative protection, reduced throughput).
4. **Bypass Register** entry recording the event with date, reason, time limit, and personnel responsible.

---

### Question 2: What is a "Proof Test" in a Safety Instrumented System? Why is the Proof Test Interval (PTI) critical for SIL compliance? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | CO4]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Proof Test** is a periodic, scheduled test performed on a SIF to detect dangerous undetected failures that are not caught by the BPCS or by automatic diagnostics. It restores the SIF to a state considered "as good as new."

The **Proof Test Interval (PTI)** is critical for SIL compliance because the PFDavg of a SIF is **directly proportional** to the PTI (per $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2}$ for 1oo1). If the PTI exceeds the maximum allowed interval calculated during SIL verification, the achieved PFDavg rises above the target range, causing the SIF to **fail its SIL requirement** and creating a hidden safety risk.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: (a) Discuss the operational requirements of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) as per IEC 61511. [7 Marks]
**(b) A 1oo2 SIF has two identical sensors with $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$ each. The proof test interval is 1 year. Calculate the PFDavg and identify the SIL achieved. Given: 1 year = 8760 hours. [7 Marks]**

**[14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 + CO5]**

---

#### (a) Operational Requirements as per IEC 61511 [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction to SIS Operations (1 Mark):**
SIS operations refer to all activities performed during the operational phase of the safety lifecycle (Phase 6 of IEC 61511). These activities ensure the SIS remains functional and available to respond to process demands without compromising the Safety Integrity Level.

**Step 2 — Operating Procedures (1.5 Marks):**
Written operating procedures must be developed for each SIF. These procedures must address:
- Normal operation
- Startup and shutdown sequences
- Bypass management
- Manual shutdown (operator-initiated safety action)
- Reset and restart after a trip
- Response to spurious trips

**Step 3 — Bypass Management (1.5 Marks):**
Bypasses are critical operations activities. The IEC 61511 clause 5.2.6.1.5 requires that:
- A bypass management procedure must be in place.
- Each bypass must be authorised in writing.
- A bypass register must be maintained.
- Time limits must be enforced.
- Compensating measures must be active.

**Step 4 — Operator Training and Authorisation (1 Mark):**
Only personnel who are trained, competency-verified, and authorised may operate the SIS. Operators must understand the safety implications of their actions, including manual overrides and bypasses.

**Step 5 — Trip Response and Reset (1 Mark):**
When a SIF trips, the operator must:
- Acknowledge the trip and take safe action.
- Investigate the cause (process upset or spurious trip).
- Document the event in the trip log.
- Reset the SIF only after the process is in a safe state.

**Step 6 — Operating Records (1 Mark):**
Records of operations activities (bypasses, trips, alarms, manual shutdowns) must be maintained. These records support the **achieved PFDavg** calculation during the operational phase.

---

#### (b) Numerical Problem — PFDavg for 1oo2 SIF [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 Mark):**
For a 1oo2 architecture (either sensor can detect the demand), the simplified PFDavg formula is:

$$
