\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (2 Marks):**
Given: $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$ and $\text{TI} = 8760 \text{ hours}$.

Calculate $\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}$ first:

$$
\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 2.628 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Square the result (1 Mark):**

$$
(\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI})^2 = (2.628 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 6.906 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 3 (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{6.906 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = 2.302 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the SIL (2 Marks):**
Compare with the SIL ranges:
- SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
- **SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$**
- SIL 5? No — the standard stops at SIL 4.

The calculated PFDavg of $2.302 \times 10^{-6}$ is below the SIL 4 lower bound. Therefore, the SIF achieves **SIL 4** (with significant margin).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** Students often write $\text{PFD} = \lambda \times \text{TI}$ instead of $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2}{3}$ for 1oo2. The squared term is **mandatory** for redundant architectures because **both** sensors must fail dangerously for a 1oo2 system to fail. Marks will be **deducted** for using the 1oo1 formula.

---

### Question B: (a) Explain the maintenance requirements for a Safety Instrumented System per IEC 61511. [7 Marks]
**(b) Calculate the maximum allowable Proof Test Interval (PTI) for a SIL 2 SIF with a 1oo1 sensor having $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$. Also, briefly explain the effect of doubling the PTI on the achieved SIL. [7 Marks]**

**[14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO4 + CO5]**

---

#### (a) Maintenance Requirements as per IEC 61511 [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
SIS maintenance is the combination of all actions (test, measurement, replacement, adjustment, cleaning) intended to retain or restore the SIS to a state where it can perform its required safety function. Maintenance is covered by IEC 61511 Clause 16 and corresponds to **Phase 7 of the Safety Lifecycle.**

**Step 2 — Maintenance Planning (1.5 Marks):**
A maintenance plan must be developed for each SIF, addressing:
- **What** to maintain (sensor, logic solver, final element)
- **When** to maintain (proof test interval, calibration interval)
- **How** to maintain (procedure)
- **By whom** (competency requirements)
- **With what resources** (spares, tools, test equipment)

**Step 3 — Proof Testing (1.5 Marks):**
A proof test is a periodic test to detect dangerous undetected failures. Requirements:
- Proof test interval must be calculated during SIL verification.
- Proof test coverage (PTC) must be documented.
- The test must be performed by competent personnel.
- The result must be recorded in the SIF history file.

**Step 4 — Calibration (1 Mark):**
Sensors must be calibrated at intervals determined by:
- Manufacturer's recommendations
- Historical drift data
- Process criticality

**Step 5 — Corrective Maintenance and Spare Parts (1 Mark):**
Failures must be repaired within an acceptable MTTR. Spare parts must be available to minimise repair time. A spare parts inventory linked to the failure rates is recommended.

**Step 6 — Documentation and Records (1 Mark):**
All maintenance activities must be recorded with:
- Date and time
- Personnel involved
- Test/calibration results
- Any deviations found
- Corrective actions taken

These records support the calculation of the achieved PFDavg and the audit of the SIS.

---

#### (b) Maximum Allowable PTI for SIL 2 SIF [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the PTI formula (1 Mark):**
For a 1oo1 SIF, the maximum PTI is derived from the SIL constraint:

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}}{\lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify PFDreq for SIL 2 (1 Mark):**
For SIL 2, the PFDavg must lie within the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$. To maintain the SIL 2 boundary at the upper end (worst case), $\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = 10^{-2}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the values (1 Mark):**
Given $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}$:

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the result (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4 \times 10^{4} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{40000}{8760} \approx 4.57 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 6 — Effect of Doubling the PTI (2 Marks):**
If the PTI is doubled to $2 \times \text{PTI}_{\text{max}}$, the new PFDavg becomes:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, new}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times 2 \times \text{PTI}_{\text{max}}}{2} = 2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = 2 \times 10^{-2}
$$

This is **outside the SIL 2 range** (which ends at $10^{-2}$). The SIF would now be classified as **SIL 1**. Therefore, doubling the PTI causes the SIF to **drop one SIL level**, which is a significant degradation of safety integrity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** Students often forget to **convert the result into years** or to **explicitly state the SIL boundary used for PFDreq**. Marks are reserved for both the calculation and the **interpretation** of the result. Always state the SIL boundary used (e.g., "PFDreq = 10⁻² corresponds to the worst-case SIL 2 limit").

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost on This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Operations and Maintenance" in SIS:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Bypass with Override or Inhibit** — These are three different actions with three different procedures. Always use the correct term.
> 2. **Forgetting the MTTR contribution** — Many students calculate PFDavg using only the TI term, omitting the $\lambda_{DU} \times \text{MTTR}$ contribution. For MTTR > 8 hours, this is significant.
> 3. **Wrong architecture formula** — Using 1oo1 formula for 1oo2 or 2oo3 will give answers that are **orders of magnitude wrong**.
> 4. **Not linking operations to SIL** — Pure procedural answers without mentioning PFDavg, RRF, or SIL will score partial credit only.
> 5. **Ignoring the β-factor** — Common cause failures are real. In redundant SIFs, the β-factor contribution is often the dominant failure mode and must be included.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operations of SIS:**
> - SIS operations = Phase 6 of IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle.
> - **Operating procedures** must address: normal operation, startup, shutdown, bypass, manual shutdown, reset.
> - **Bypass management** requires written authorisation, time limit, compensating measures, and a bypass register.
> - **Bypass ≠ Override ≠ Inhibit** (three distinct terms).
> - Only **authorised, trained, and competency-verified** personnel may operate the SIS.
> - **Operating records** (bypasses, trips, alarms) feed into achieved PFDavg calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance of SIS:**
> - SIS maintenance = Phase 7 of IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle.
> - Maintenance activities include: **proof testing, calibration, preventive maintenance, corrective maintenance.**
> - **Proof Test Interval (PTI)** is the **primary lever** controlling PFDavg.
> - **Proof Test Coverage (PTC)** must be high (≥ 90% for SIL 3).
> - All maintenance activities must be **documented** in the SIF history file.
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) directly impacts the achieved SIL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formulas to Memorise:**
> - 1oo1: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times \text{TI}}{2}$
> - 1oo2: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2}{3}$
> - 2oo3: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 6 \times (\lambda_{DU})^2 \times \text{TI}^2$
> - $\text{PTI}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{req}}}{\lambda_{DU}}$
> - $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
> - $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **SIL Boundaries (Low Demand Mode):**
> - SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1}$
> - SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$
> - SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$
> - SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-4}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insights:**
> - **Doubling the PTI doubles the PFDavg** for a 1oo1 SIF → can drop one SIL level.
> - **Bypass effectively makes PFDavg = 1** for the bypassed SIF element.
> - **Operations and maintenance are not separate** from safety; they **directly determine** the achieved safety integrity.
> - **The β-factor contribution** for common cause failures must be included for redundant SIFs.
> - **Spurious trips are a maintainability issue** (not a safety issue per se), but they cause operators to distrust and bypass the SIF, which is a safety issue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard References (No Fabricated Citations):**
> - **IEC 61511:2016** — Functional safety — SIS for the process industry sector (primary governing standard).
> - **IEC 61508:2010** — Functional safety of electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems (parent standard).
> - **ISA 84.00.01** — Application of SIS for the process industries (harmonised with IEC 61511).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management of Change
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT862 - SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management of Change

# 🛡️ Management of Change (MOC) — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Management of Change (MOC)** is a formal, documented, and systematic review-and-approval procedure used in process industries to evaluate, authorize, implement, and verify any proposed modification to process equipment, procedures, raw materials, operating conditions, software, or organizational structure that falls outside the established design and operational baseline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (ISA-84 / IEC 61511 Aligned):**
> *Management of Change is a Safety Lifecycle activity that ensures all changes — whether temporary or permanent — are reviewed for their impact on Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), process hazards, and the Safety Integrity Level (SIL), prior to implementation.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MOC is one of the seven mandatory procedures (the "7-M" framework) required for Safe Operation of a process plant:

| # | Procedure | Focus Area |
|---|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Operating Procedures | Daily operations |
| 2 | Training | Personnel competency |
| 3 | Mechanical Integrity | Equipment health |
| 4 | Pre-Startup Safety Review | Commissioning |
| 5 | Hot Work Permit | Welding/cutting safety |
| 6 | Management of Change | Modification control |
| 7 | Emergency Response | Incident handling |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Renovating a House" 🏠

Imagine you live in a **high-rise apartment with 200 families**. One resident decides to remove a load-bearing wall (a "change"). Without a formal process:
- The building structural engineer isn't consulted.
- Fire exits might get blocked.
- Electrical load capacity changes.
- The neighbor below could face ceiling collapse.

So the Housing Society mandates: **"Any structural modification requires (1) application, (2) engineer review, (3) society committee approval, (4) supervised implementation, and (5) post-modification inspection."**

> That formal workflow is exactly **Management of Change** — applied to a chemical plant, refinery, or any process facility.

## 1.3 Why MOC is a KTU High-Weightage Topic

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places MOC inside Module 4 of **PEICT862 — Safety Instrumentation System** because it forms the **operational bridge** between the design-time SIS (built during the Safety Lifecycle) and the run-time plant reality. Without MOC, the carefully engineered SIL could degrade silently — a phenomenon known as **"SIL Decay."**

### Core Drivers for MOC

- **Regulatory Compliance** — OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (l), EPA RMP, IEC 61511 Clause 5.16.
- **Process Safety** — Prevents unauthorized changes that could bypass safety interlocks.
- **Asset Integrity** — Keeps P&IDs, Cause-&-Effect matrices, and SIS logic in sync with the physical plant.
- **Knowledge Preservation** — Captures the *why* behind a change for future operators.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in MOC (Bold for Highlighting)

- **MOC Approval Cycle Time** — Typical industrial benchmark: **≤ 14 working days** for non-emergency changes; **≤ 48 hours** for emergency changes.
- **Risk Reduction Threshold** — Changes must **not degrade** the existing **SIL** of any Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
- **Documentation Retention Period** — Minimum **5 years** post-decommissioning (per OSHA PSM standard).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — The MOC Engine

## 2.1 Trigger Conditions: When MUST MOC Be Invoked?

A change requires formal MOC initiation if it affects **any one** of the following:

- ⚙️ **Process Parameters** — temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition setpoints outside the design envelope.
- 🔧 **Equipment / Hardware** — replacing a pump with a different characteristic, changing valve trim, modifying relief devices.
- 💻 **Software / Logic** — editing the SIS application logic in a Safety PLC (e.g., changing a trip delay timer in a Siemens / Honeywell / Yokogawa SIS).
- 📋 **Procedures** — bypassing an established operating step, changing start-up/shut-down sequences.
- 🧪 **Raw Materials** — introducing a new catalyst, new feedstock, or new supplier.
- 👥 **Organization** — reducing operator staffing, merging control rooms, reassigning safety-critical roles.
- 🌍 **Regulatory / Environmental** — new emission limits, new zoning law, new hazardous area classification.
- 🛠️ **Temporary Changes** — bypass lines, jumper wires, disabled alarms, manual override of an interlock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Hot-Point:** Students often assume MOC applies *only* to permanent changes. **Wrong!** Per IEC 61511, *temporary changes must also be MOC-reviewed* and have a defined **expiry date** to ensure they are not forgotten.

## 2.2 The 6-Phase MOC Lifecycle

```text
Phase 1 → Request & Initiation
Phase 2 → Technical & Safety Review
Phase 3 → Approval Authorization
Phase 4 → Implementation (Controlled)
Phase 5 → Verification & Documentation
Phase 6 → Closure & Archival
```

### Phase 1 — Request & Initiation
- The **originator** (any employee) raises a formal MOC request using an **MOC Form**.
- Must include: *Reason for change, scope, affected systems, proposed date, risk classification.*

### Phase 2 — Technical & Safety Review
- A **multi-disciplinary team** reviews the change. Mandatory participants:
  - Process Engineer
  - Instrumentation & Control Engineer
  - Safety Engineer / Process Safety Specialist
  - Operations Supervisor
  - Maintenance Lead
  - Environmental / Health representative (if applicable)

### Phase 3 — Approval Authorization
- Authority level depends on **risk classification** (see table below).
- Low-risk changes may be approved by an Engineer; high-risk changes require **Plant Manager / HSE Director** signature.

### Phase 4 — Implementation (Controlled)
- Carried out per the approved plan.
- **Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO)** procedures apply if maintenance is involved.
- SIS logic changes require **bypass management** with time limits.

### Phase 5 — Verification & Documentation
- **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** confirms the plant is ready.
- Updated P&IDs, Cause-&-Effect, SIS logic diagrams, and alarm database are released.
- Operators are trained on the change before resuming production.

### Phase 6 — Closure & Archival
- All documentation is archived in the **Plant Master File**.
- The change becomes the **new baseline**.
- A **post-implementation audit** may be scheduled after 90 days.

## 2.3 Risk-Based Classification of Changes

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasizes the **risk-matrix approach** to grade MOC requests. This is a frequent 3-mark question topic.

### KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Risk Class | Definition | Approval Authority | Cycle Time |
|------------|------------|--------------------|------------|
| **Class A — Emergency / Emergency MOC** | Unforeseen, immediate safety threat | Plant Manager + HSE Head (verbal OK, written within 24 h) | **≤ 48 hours** |
| **Class B — High Risk** | Affects SIS, fire & gas, or relief systems | Plant Manager + Multi-disciplinary team | **≤ 14 days** |
| **Class C — Medium Risk** | Affects process optimization or non-SIS instrumentation | Process + I&C + Safety Engineer | **≤ 7 days** |
| **Class D — Low Risk** | Like-for-like replacement with same spec | Single discipline reviewer | **≤ 3 days** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Relationship | Units / Boundary |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Probability of Failure on Demand — Post-Change** | $PFD_{avg,new} \leq PFD_{avg,old}$ | Must not increase |
| **SIL Verification Constraint** | $SIL_{new} \geq SIL_{old}$ | Integer level 1–3 |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Combined SIF PFD (1oo2 architecture)** | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(PFD_{single})^2}{3}$ | For identical channels |
| **MOC Cycle Time (days)** | $t_{MOC} = t_{review} + t_{approval} + t_{implement}$ | Working days |
| **Hazard Frequency (per year)** | $f_i$ from HAZOP/LOPA | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ |
| **Tolerable Risk Frequency** | $f_{tolerable} \leq 10^{-5}$ | Per OSHA, for public fatality |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use** vertical pipe symbol **\|** inside markdown tables. KTU 2024 evaluation scripts will break the rendering. Use **`\vert`** or **`\mid`** instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** use MOC to control catalyst changes in hydrocrackers.
- **Power Plants** use MOC before retuning turbine governors.
- **Pharma Plants** use MOC for batch recipe modifications under FDA scrutiny.
- **Offshore Platforms** use MOC to manage subsea control system software updates.

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — MOC Worked Analysis

## 3.1 Case Study: SIS Logic Timer Change

> A KTU-style numerical/analytical problem commonly tests whether a proposed change **preserves or violates** the existing SIL.

### Problem Statement
A SIF (Safety Instrumented Function) is currently operating at **SIL 2** with a **1oo1 (one-out-of-one)** architecture.

- Sensor: Pressure Transmitter, $\lambda_{DU,sensor} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per hour}$
- Logic Solver: Safety PLC, $\lambda_{DU,PLC} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per hour}$
- Final Element: Solenoid-operated Valve, $\lambda_{DU,valve} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{per hour}$
- Proof Test Interval: $T_{proof} = 8760\ \text{hours}$ (1 year)

A proposed MOC changes the proof test interval from **1 year** to **2 years (17520 h)** for economic reasons.

**Question:** Evaluate the impact on SIL. Should the MOC be approved?

### Step 1 — Recall the PFD Formula for 1oo1 Architecture

For a single-channel 1oo1 subsystem:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Subsystem PFD with $T_{proof} = 8760\ \text{h}$

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219
$$

$$
PFD_{PLC} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{PLC} = \dfrac{0.00876}{2} = 0.00438
$$

$$
PFD_{valve} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{valve} = \dfrac{0.1752}{2} = 0.0876
$$

### Step 3 — Sum for Total SIF PFD

$$
PFD_{SIF,old} = 0.0219 + 0.00438 + 0.0876
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF,old} = 0.11388 \approx 0.114
$$

### Step 4 — Compute New PFD with $T_{proof} = 17520\ \text{h}$

$$
PFD_{sensor,new} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.0438
$$

$$
PFD_{PLC,new} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.00876
$$

$$
PFD_{valve,new} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.1752
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF,new} = 0.0438 + 0.00876 + 0.1752 = 0.22776 \approx 0.228
$$

### Step 5 — Compare Against SIL 2 Target Band

| SIL Level | Target PFD Range |
|-----------|-------------------|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **SIL 2** | **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$** |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

Now check $PFD_{SIF,old} = 0.114 \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-1}$ → This already sits in **SIL 1**, not SIL 2.

$$
PFD_{SIF,new} = 0.228 \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-1}
$$

### Step 6 — Risk Reduction Factor Comparison

$$
RRF_{old} = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{SIF,old}} = \dfrac{1}{0.114} \approx 8.77
$$

$$
RRF_{new} = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{SIF,new}} = \dfrac{1}{0.228} \approx 4.39
$$

$$
\Delta RRF = \dfrac{RRF_{old} - RRF_{new}}{RRF_{old}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta RRF = \dfrac{8.77 - 4.39}{8.77} \times 100\% = 49.9\%
$$

### Step 7 — MOC Decision

| Criterion | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| Existing SIL preserved? | ❌ No — was already SIL 1 |
| PFD increased? | ✅ Yes — doubled |
| RRF decreased by ≥ 50%? | ✅ Yes |
| SIS logic affected? | ✅ Yes |
| **MOC Recommendation** | **REJECT — Return for redesign** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The proposed change must be **REJECTED**. To compensate, the plant must either:
> (a) add a **1oo2 redundancy** to the valve, or
> (b) install **partial-stroke testing** on the valve, or
> (c) restore the **1-year proof test interval**.

## 3.2 Summary Decision Matrix for MOC Approvals

| Question the Reviewer MUST Ask | Required Answer |
|--------------------------------|-----------------|
| Does it affect any SIF? | If yes → mandatory LOPA re-validation |
| Does it introduce new hazards? | If yes → re-run HAZOP |
| Does it change the Safe Operating Limits? | If yes → update Operating Procedures |
| Does it affect SIS software? | If yes → Functional Safety Assessment |
| Does it require operator retraining? | If yes → schedule training before startup |
| Does it have a defined expiry (if temporary)? | If no → not approved |

# 🗺️ Structural Diagram — MOC Process Architecture

## 4.1 End-to-End MOC Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Originator identifies Need for Change] --> B[Submit MOC Request Form]
    B --> C{Risk Classification}
    C -->|Low Risk Class D| D1[Single Engineer Review]
    C -->|Medium Risk Class C| D2[Process and I and C Review]
    C -->|High Risk Class B| D3[Multi Disciplinary Team Review]
    C -->|Emergency Class A| D4[Verbal Plant Manager OK within 48 h]
    D1 --> E[Approval Authority Signs]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Implement Change with LOTO and Bypass Management]
    F --> G[Pre Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    G --> H{Verification Passed?}
    H -->|No| X1[Rollback to Baseline] --> Z[Archive and Close MOC]
    H -->|Yes| I[Update Documentation]
    I --> J[Operator Training]
    J --> K[90 Day Post Implementation Audit]
    K --> L[Archive in Plant Master File]
    L --> Z
    Z --> M[New Baseline Established]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

> All seven artifacts above are **mandatory outputs** for any high-risk (Class B) MOC. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to recall at least **four** of these in 3-mark answers.

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Management of Change (MOC) as applied to a Safety Instrumentation System. List any four triggers that necessitate an MOC.**

**Model Answer:**

Management of Change is a formal, documented procedure to review, approve, implement, and verify any modification to process design, equipment, software, or procedures that may affect process safety or the integrity of a Safety Instrumented System.

**Four Triggers:**
1. Change in process operating setpoints outside the design envelope.
2. Modification or replacement of SIS hardware (sensor, logic solver, final element).
3. Editing of SIS application logic or trip setpoints.
4. Introduction of new raw materials or new operating procedures.

*(Examiner may also accept: temporary bypass installation, organizational/staffing change, regulatory change.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Emergency MOC and Routine MOC. State the typical approval cycle time for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Emergency MOC | Routine MOC |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Trigger | Sudden safety threat, equipment failure | Planned improvement, optimization |
| Cycle Time | **≤ 48 hours** | **7 to 14 days** |
| Approver | Plant Manager + HSE Head (verbal OK, written follow-up) | Multi-disciplinary review committee |
| Documentation | Retroactive (within 24 h) | Proactive (before implementation) |
| Examples | Bypassing a faulty sensor temporarily | Installing a new level transmitter |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the six phases of the Management of Change lifecycle with a suitable flowchart. State the role of each stakeholder.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SIF operates as 1oo2 voting with two identical pressure transmitters feeding a safety PLC driving a single shut-down valve.

Given:
- $\lambda_{DU,sensor} = 3 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per hour}$
- $\lambda_{DU,PLC} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per hour}$
- $\lambda_{DU,valve} = 1 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{per hour}$
- Proof test interval $T_{proof} = 17520\ \text{h}$

Compute the PFD of the SIF and verify whether the SIF meets **SIL 2**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Phase 1 — Request & Initiation:** Originator raises MOC form with reason, scope, and risk class.

**Phase 2 — Technical & Safety Review:** Multi-disciplinary team (Process, I&C, Safety, Operations, Maintenance, Environment) reviews for hazards, SIS impact, and regulatory compliance.

**Phase 3 — Approval Authorization:** Risk-class-appropriate authority signs the MOC.

**Phase 4 — Implementation:** Change executed under controlled conditions with LOTO, bypass management, and time limits for temporary changes.

**Phase 5 — Verification & Documentation:** Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) confirms readiness; P&IDs, C&E, SIS logic, and operating procedures are updated.

**Phase 6 — Closure & Archival:** Documents filed in Plant Master File; new baseline established; 90-day post-implementation audit scheduled.

**Stakeholder Roles:**

| Stakeholder | Role |
|-------------|------|
| Originator | Identifies need, raises MOC form |
| Process Engineer | Reviews process implications |
| I&C Engineer | Reviews instrumentation and SIS logic |
| Safety Engineer | Reviews hazard and SIL impact |
| Operations | Reviews operability and training |
| Plant Manager | Final approval for high-risk MOC |
| Commissioning Team | Implements the change |
| Quality / Document Control | Updates master documents |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Recall PFD for 1oo2 Voting (identical channels):**

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(PFD_{single})^2}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute single-channel PFD for each subsystem:**

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.02628
$$

$$
PFD_{PLC} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.00876
$$

$$
PFD_{valve} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 17520}{2} = 0.0876
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 1oo2 formula to the sensor pair:**

$$
PFD_{sensor,1oo2} = \dfrac{(0.02628)^2}{3} = \dfrac{0.0006906}{3} = 0.0002302
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all subsystem PFDs:**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor,1oo2} + PFD_{PLC} + PFD_{valve}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 0.0002302 + 0.00876 + 0.0876 = 0.09659
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with SIL 2 band:**

| SIL | PFD Range |
|-----|-----------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **2** | **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$** |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

$$
PFD_{SIF} \approx 9.66 \times 10^{-2}
$$

This value **just falls in SIL 1** (since $9.66 \times 10^{-2} > 10^{-2}$), narrowly missing SIL 2.

**Conclusion:** The SIF **does NOT meet SIL 2** with the given configuration. Recommend either shortening the proof test interval or upgrading valve architecture.

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of Management of Change in preventing **SIL Degradation**. With a diagram, show how MOC interacts with the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plant proposes an MOC to install a **bypass line** around a level-control valve for a duration of 90 days to allow maintenance. The original SIF was SIL 2 with $PFD_{SIF} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$. During the bypass, the SIF is rendered **ineffective**. Evaluate the MOC from a safety standpoint.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**SIL Degradation:** Over time, the actual SIL of a SIF drifts downward from its original design level due to unauthorized or poorly-managed changes. Common causes include:
- Modified trip setpoints without LOPA re-validation.
- Disabled alarms.
- Bypassed interlocks left in place.
- Proof test interval extensions.

**Role of MOC in Preventing SIL Decay:**
1. **Captures** every change in a documented register.
2. **Triggers** mandatory Functional Safety Assessment for any SIS-impacting change.
3. **Enforces** re-validation of LOPA when a change alters the risk-reduction credit.
4. **Maintains** synchronization between design intent and field reality.

**Mermaid — MOC Interaction with IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concept and Scope] --> B[Hazard and Risk Analysis]
    B --> C[SIF Design]
    C --> D[Detailed SIS Engineering]
    D --> E[Installation and Commissioning]
    E --> F[Operation and Maintenance]
    F --> G[MOC Triggered Here]
    G --> G1{Re Validate SIL?}
    G1 -->|Yes| H[LOPA Re validation]
    G1 -->|No| X[Reject MOC]
    H --> I[Update Design Baseline]
    I --> F
    F --> J[Decommissioning]
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Recognize Loss of Risk Reduction:**

During the bypass, the SIF is **fully ineffective**, meaning:

$$
PFD_{SIF,bypass} = 1.0
$$

The risk reduction factor drops to:

$$
RRF_{bypass} = \dfrac{1}{1.0} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Exposure Duration:**

$$
t_{exposure} = 90\ \text{days} = 90 \times 24 = 2160\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cumulative Risk Exposure:**

Let the hazardous event frequency (without SIF) be $f_i = 1 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{per year}$ (typical from HAZOP).

Without MOC, expected number of hazardous events during bypass:

$$
N_{hazard} = f_i \times \dfrac{t_{exposure}}{8760}
$$

$$
N_{hazard} = 10^{-2} \times \dfrac{2160}{8760} = 2.466 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Mitigation Hierarchy (per IEC 61511):**

| Mitigation Option | Recommendation |
|--------------------|----------------|
| Add temporary additional layer of protection (e.g., manual monitoring every 2 h) | **Mandatory** |
| Reduce inventory of hazardous material during bypass | **Required** |
| Limit bypass duration to ≤ 30 days with re-approval | **Required** |
| Implement continuous gas/level detection with independent alarm | **Required** |

**Step 5 — MOC Decision:**

| Criterion | Status |
|-----------|--------|
| SIL degradation? | ✅ Total — SIL drops from 2 to 0 |
| Compensating measures in place? | ❌ Not mentioned in proposal |
| **MOC Recommendation** | **CONDITIONAL APPROVAL** — only if (a) manual round checks every 2 hours, (b) bypass shortened to 30 days, (c) re-MOC review before each extension |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in MOC Questions:**
> 1. Forgetting to **update the P&ID, Cause-&-Effect, and SIS logic diagram** — these three documents are *always* marked separately.
> 2. Treating **temporary changes** (bypasses) as exempt from MOC — they are NOT exempt.
> 3. Confusing **MOC with MoC (Modification of Code)** — in plant practice, MOC is the umbrella term covering hardware, software, and procedures.
> 4. Computing PFD for 1oo2 architecture using the **sum** instead of the **square/3** formula — this is the single most common calculation error.
> 5. Failing to mention **operator training** as a mandatory post-MOC step.
> 6. Forgetting the **expiry date** for temporary changes — KTU examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **MOC is mandatory** for any change affecting design basis, SIS, or operating procedures.
- 🔁 **Six-phase lifecycle:** Initiate → Review → Approve → Implement → Verify → Close.
- ⚠️ **Temporary changes are NOT exempt** — they require an expiry date.
- 📊 **Risk-based approval** — Class A (Emergency) → Class D (Low Risk).
- 🛡️ **SIL must not degrade** after MOC; $PFD_{new} \leq PFD_{old}$.
- 📚 **Mandatory outputs** — Updated P&ID, C&E, SIS logic, alarm DB, operating procedure, training, and LOPA.
- 🧮 **Key formulas** — $PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2}$; $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(PFD_{single})^2}{3}$; $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$.
- 👥 **Multi-disciplinary review** is the cornerstone — no MOC is approved by a single individual.
- 🏛️ **Standards referenced** — IEC 61511 Clause 5.16, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(l), ISA-84.00.02.
- 📁 **Documentation retention** — minimum 5 years (often plant life + 5 years for SIS).
- 🔍 **Audit cadence** — 90-day post-implementation review is the industry benchmark.
